If account_id does not exist (e.g. a race where the account was deleted, or a wrong id passed), the UPDATE affects 0 rows but rowcount is not checked, and the function still returns True. The caller (router.complete_session) treats True as 'credited' and mints a signed order receipt claiming runs_credited=N. Result: receipt asserts N runs credited, but the balance was never incremented — a receipt that lies about settlement. The fulfillment row is also now recorded as done, so a retry will NOT re-credit. The UPDATE rowcount should be asserted > 0 inside the same transaction.

findings.title: 60s stale-claim re-grant of an idempotency key can double-execute a non-idempotent action (acp.create mints a duplicate, orphaned session)
findings.description: When an idempotency key is in status 'processing' and more than IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s, line 103) has elapsed since created_at, idempotency_begin rebinds the row to the new request_hash and returns {'state':'new'} (lines 337-344), letting the caller proceed. The comment assumes the original holder crashed. But the timeout cannot distinguish a crashed request from a legitimately slow in-flight one. Two concrete double-execute paths: (1) acp.create -- create_session (router 183) generates a FRESH session id via _new_session_id() on every 'new' return. If the original create is still running at 60s (e.g. blocked on the serialized lock above, or slow body parse), a retry re-claims the key and creates a SECOND checkout session. The two sessions get different ids; whichever idempotency_complete (line 189) runs last overwrites the stored response, so the buyer's retry sees session B while session A is orphaned in acp_sessions -- and either session can later be completed/charged independently. (2) acp.complete -- if a /complete is genuinely slow past 60s, the retry re-claims 'new' and re-enters the charge+fulfill path. Here the session-keyed Stripe idempotency_key (router 265) and fulfill_once (store 365) DO dedup the money, so no double charge -- but the 60s window is the wrong guard: it should be much larger than any plausible request latency, or the reclaim should be gated on additional liveness signal. Net: the value of the timeout is real (prevents permanent 409 DoS) but 60s is aggressive enough that on the lock-serialized hot path a backlog could legitimately exceed it, defeating the at-most-once guarantee for the not-otherwise-deduped acp.create/update/cancel scopes.

findings.location: mark_event_processed 253-260 + api/billing.py 191-203 (separate commits on shared connection)
findings.title: Webhook dedup mark and the handler's side-effect writes are not in one transaction, so a crash between them re-processes the event (or marks-then-loses it)
findings.description: In billing.py the flow is: is_event_processed -> _handle_event (which performs its own committed set_subscription/create_account writes) -> mark_event_processed (a separate INSERT+commit). Each store method commits independently on the single shared connection; there is no enclosing transaction spanning the side effects and the dedup record. If the process crashes (or set_subscription's transaction is left dangling per the other finding) after _handle_event commits but before mark_event_processed commits, Stripe redelivers and _handle_event runs again. While set_subscription is mostly idempotent, _resolve_account -> create_account is NOT fully idempotent (it can create a second account when the customer mapping is not yet linked), so the re-run can fork accounts. The store offers no transactional API to bundle the dedup insert with the effect, which defeats the 'never double-grant or double-credit' guarantee the module docstring claims. Round 1's store.py findings do not mention mark_event_processed or its non-atomicity with the handler.

findings.title: combined_text space-joins sender and subject, letting subject tokens satisfy sender/domain-anchored patterns (the .gov-anchor root cause)
findings.description: combined_text returns f"{self.sender} {self.subject}".lower() — sender and subject flattened into ONE string with a single space separator. The categorization engine matches LABEL_RULES regex patterns against exactly this shape (core/rules.py:1007-1008 builds the identical f"{sender} {subject}".lower() and core/rules.py:1032 does re.search(pattern, combined_text)). Because there is no boundary marker between the sender field and the subject field, a pattern intended to anchor on a *sender domain* (e.g. a '.gov' or 'bank.com' substring) can be satisfied by text appearing only in the SUBJECT — an attacker or a quirky subject line ('Re: my new bank.com password', 'forwarded from irs.gov') can cause mis-categorization into a higher-trust/priority label. This is a model-level expression of the same root cause behind the .gov-anchor finding: the single combined string conflates two semantically distinct fields. Note also the rules engine duplicates this string inline rather than calling msg.combined_text, so any hardening of the property would not propagate. Fix: match sender and subject separately (or insert a separator the patterns can anchor against / restrict domain-anchored patterns to the sender field only).

findings.title: IMAP archive COPY+\Deleted+EXPUNGE per message calls mailbox-wide expunge in a loop; unchecked STORE and expunge can delete unrelated flagged mail or leave duplicates
findings.description: When UID MOVE is unsupported, the fallback does COPY -> 'STORE +FLAGS \Deleted' -> imap.expunge(). expunge() permanently removes EVERY message flagged \Deleted in the selected mailbox, not just this uid — if any other message in INBOX already carries \Deleted (or a prior iteration flagged one whose COPY later failed), it is destroyed. Called once per archivable uid inside the loop (main lines 161-165), so it's repeated mailbox-wide expunges (slow and broad). The STORE result is not checked; if +\Deleted fails, the message is COPIED to Archive but remains in INBOX (duplicate). Safer: collect uids, COPY all, then a single UID EXPUNGE (RFC 3501 UIDPLUS) scoped to those uids, or rely on MOVE only. The protected gate itself looks correct (fail-closed on empty From).

findings.title: COPY+Delete+EXPUNGE fallback expunges entire mailbox, not just the message
findings.description: When UID MOVE is unsupported, the fallback does `COPY` then `STORE +FLAGS \Deleted` then `imap.expunge()`. expunge() (non-UID) permanently removes ALL messages in the selected mailbox that carry the \Deleted flag — not just this uid. If any other message in INBOX already has \Deleted set (e.g. from a prior interrupted run or another client), this loop will permanently delete those too. Should use UID EXPUNGE (RFC 3501 UID EXPUNGE / `imap.uid('EXPUNGE', uid)` where supported) or batch the expunge once at the end with explicit awareness. Potential unintended permanent deletion.

findings.location: triage tool lines 109-127 (dry_run default True, no auth/metering)
findings.title: MCP triage tool exposes destructive mailbox mutation (dry_run=False) with no authentication, metering, or credit consumption
findings.description: The MCP 'triage' tool, when called with dry_run=False, drives service.run_triage which actually archives/moves mail. The MCP endpoint is mounted at /mcp with only DNS-rebinding host protection (no bearer/auth, no plan check, no consume_credit). This is the MCP-layer instance of the round-1 'triage endpoints unauthenticated/unmetered' finding (which cited api/app.py), but it is a distinct, separately-reachable surface: an agent reaching /mcp can mutate the server operator's connected mailbox and consume provider quota for free. Reported as a NEW location of the same class.

findings.title: Standard-IMAP archive does COPY then unconditional +FLAGS \\Deleted but never EXPUNGE; message remains until expunge and is destructive without confirmation
findings.description: For non-Gmail IMAP, archive copies to 'Archive' then sets \Deleted on the original. (1) It never EXPUNGEs, so the message stays in INBOX marked deleted — not actually archived from the user's view until something expunges; and many clients hide \Deleted, making it look gone while still present. (2) If a later expunge runs (by this or another client/session), the original is permanently destroyed; combined with the COPY-status-only check, if COPY reported OK but the copy is incomplete the original could be lost. Data-loss-adjacent behavior on the archive path. Also 'Archive' folder is assumed to exist (no ensure_label_exists call) — COPY to a nonexistent folder returns NO and archive returns False, but the message was not deleted (safe) — still, behavior is inconsistent with the gmail branch.

findings.location: apply_label / move script line 263-270; get_message_details 211-228; star 295-301; unstar 311-317; mark_read 360-366; mark_unread 376-382
findings.title: AppleScript injection / breakage via unescaped message_id interpolated as a raw literal
findings.description: message_id is f-string interpolated directly into AppleScript as `whose id is {message_id}` (an unquoted token) across apply_label, get_message_details, star, unstar, mark_read, mark_unread. Although Mail.app message ids are normally integers, the value originates from list_messages parsing (msg_id = parts[0] of a tab-split osascript line) and is typed str throughout EmailMessage.id with no validation. If an id ever contains non-numeric content (e.g. a sender field bled into the id column on a malformed/blank line, or a future provider id scheme), the script either errors or executes attacker-influenced AppleScript. Because it is placed as a bare expression (not a quoted string), any whitespace/operators in the value change the script's meaning. This is the classic AppleScript-injection vector the task calls out. Validate that message_id matches ^[0-9]+$ before interpolation.

findings.title: AppleScript injection via unescaped mailbox/label/account names with embedded double-quotes
findings.description: label, mailbox, and self.account are interpolated into AppleScript inside double quotes (e.g. `mailbox "{label}"`, `of account "{self.account}"`, `make new mailbox with properties {{name:"{label}"}}`). A label or account name containing a double-quote or backslash terminates/escapes the AppleScript string literal, breaking the script or allowing arbitrary AppleScript to be appended. Labels come from the rule taxonomy and config (`vip_senders`/custom rules can introduce arbitrary label strings), and account names are user config. No escaping of `"` or `\` is performed anywhere before interpolation.

findings.location: list_messages, lines 428-434 ($orderby 'receivedDateTime desc' combined with arbitrary $filter)
findings.title: Graph rejects $orderby + $filter on different properties with HTTP 400 — the primary 'isRead eq false' pending/list query fails
findings.description: list_messages always sets $orderby=receivedDateTime desc AND, when query is non-empty, $filter=<query>. Microsoft Graph imposes the constraint that when $orderby and $filter are combined, the $orderby properties must also appear (in order) at the start of the $filter, otherwise it returns 400 'The restriction or sort order is too complex...'. The cli passes query='isRead eq false' for Outlook pending (cli.py:642) and 'has:nouserlabels' as the default for all providers (round 1 #299). 'isRead eq false' + '$orderby receivedDateTime desc' is exactly the unsupported combination and will 400. The broad except at 436-440 then swallows it and returns an EMPTY result, so Outlook pending/filtered listing silently returns nothing. Round 1 flagged the $top concern (635) and the empty-on-error swallow (628) but not the structural $orderby+$filter 400 incompatibility that makes any filtered Outlook listing fail.

findings.title: ruff and mypy run advisory-only (set +e) — lint/type regressions can never fail CI
findings.description: Both the 'Lint with ruff' (lines 33-47) and 'Type check with mypy' (lines 49-63) steps begin with `set +e` and only emit a `::warning::`/step-summary on nonzero exit — the step always exits 0. So a type error or a lint failure (including the kind of 3.10+ typing slip the missing-floor matrix would otherwise need to catch) never blocks a merge. Combined with the 3.11/3.12-only matrix, there is effectively no automated gate that would catch a 3.9 typing/syntax regression: pytest doesn't run on 3.9, and mypy/ruff are non-blocking. If the team intends ruff/mypy as real gates, drop `set +e` (and pin ruff/mypy versions); if they are intentionally advisory, that should be a conscious decision because right now the only hard gate is pytest on two never-3.9 interpreters.

findings.title: Cloudflare smoke test validates only a thin happy-path slice and would not catch the method/route/contract divergences
findings.description: The post-deploy smoke test checks only: /health contains status ok, /app/ returns 200, /v1/billing/plans contains the substring '"plans"', and /v1/senders/check for courts.ca.gov returns '"protected":true'. It uses `curl -fsS` so it only asserts a 2xx and a substring. It does NOT exercise: a non-protected sender (would catch the 1password substring/dead-chase logic and the unused PROTECTED set), wrong-method handling (405 fall-through), /v1/triage vs preview divergence, /v1/audit/{run_id} (the advertised verifiable-receipt surface), the discovery routes the frontend links to (/.well-known/agent.json, /server.json, /openapi.json) which all 404, or malformed-body handling. A regression in any of those ships green. The substring grep for '"plans"' also passes regardless of the metered/credit_packs/currency omission.

findings.title: AGENTS.md describes only the legacy Gmail scripts and omits the entire unified CLI / multi-provider / API architecture
findings.description: AGENTS.md's Project Structure and Build/Test/Dev sections describe the repo as if it were the original single-file Gmail labeler: it names gmail_labeler.py as 'core labeling logic', final_sweep.py, recount.py, and the AppleScript tools, and the install command is `pip install google-api-python-client google-auth-httplib2 google-auth-oauthlib`. It says 'No unit test suite' (line 26) although a tests/ directory with pytest config (pyproject.toml) and a CI pytest gate now exist. It does not mention cli.py, the core/ + providers/ module structure, the FastAPI api/, MCP, or ACP. README.md and CLAUDE.md describe the current architecture; AGENTS.md contradicts them by presenting the superseded legacy layout as the project. An agent onboarding via AGENTS.md would be pointed at the wrong entry points and would be told there are no tests.

findings.title: Doc/install mismatch: CLAUDE.md tells users to check 'launchctl list | grep com.user.gmail_labeler' but deploy installs com.user.mail_automation
findings.description: CLAUDE.md's Scheduling section says 'launchctl list | grep com.user.gmail_labeler' to check scheduler status, but deploy.sh installs and bootstraps com.user.mail_automation (deploy.sh:60-62) and README.md:553 correctly greps mail_automation. A user following CLAUDE.md will see no match and conclude the scheduler failed to install. The label name drifted when the scheduler was renamed from gmail_labeler to mail_automation but CLAUDE.md was not updated.

findings.title: Dockerfile COPY . . ships logs, state, caches, and any local secrets into the image
findings.description: Line 12 does a blanket `COPY . .` with no .dockerignore present in the repo root (only .gitignore exists). The repo working tree contains multi-MB runtime logs (gmail_labeler.log ~3.8MB, auto_drain*.log ~1MB each, launchd.stderr.log ~80KB), *_state.json runtime state (labeler_state.json), __pycache__, .venv, .git, .ruff_cache/.pytest_cache, and potentially credentials.json/token caches if a developer ever created them locally. Without a .dockerignore these all get baked into the image: bloated layers, and a real secret-leak risk if credentials.json / *_token_cache.json / .env files are present at build time (CLAUDE.md lists credentials.json and ~/.outlook_token_cache.json as gitignored secrets, but .gitignore does NOT stop Docker COPY). Add a .dockerignore covering .git, .venv, *.log, *_state.json, __pycache__, *cache*, credentials.json, client_secret_*.json, *token_cache*.

findings.location: line 2 (Python-3.10+ badge) and line 241 (Prerequisites: Python 3.10 or later) vs CLAUDE.md:278 and api/app.py:34 / requirements-mcp.txt:2-3 / Dockerfile:7
findings.title: Three-way contradiction on the minimum Python version (3.9 vs 3.10)
findings.description: The declared minimum Python version is inconsistent across the repo: README.md badge (line 2) and Prerequisites (line 241) say 'Python 3.10+', while CLAUDE.md:278 says 'Python 3.9+' and the entire MCP-isolation architecture (api/app.py:34 'the core API stays importable on 3.9', requirements-mcp.txt:2-3, Dockerfile:5-7) is justified by a 3.9 floor. There is no `[project] requires-python` in pyproject.toml to pin it authoritatively (pyproject.toml only has `[tool.pytest.ini_options]`). Effect: a 3.9 user trusts CLAUDE.md, installs, and may hit an untested import path; or the team believes 3.9 is supported and keeps paying the lazy-import complexity cost for a floor that the README itself disavows and CI never tests. Pick one floor, set `requires-python` in pyproject.toml, and make the docs agree.

findings.location: build_feed lines 33-64 (and committed acp/product_feed.json); no test references build_feed
findings.title: No test asserts feed price equals the amount actually charged — a price/unit drift between feed and /complete charge would ship undetected
findings.description: build_feed() and the committed product_feed.json are the buyer-facing price declaration; complete_session charges models.grand_total(line_items) which derives from pack['amount_cents']. There is no test that exercises build_feed or asserts feed.products[i].price corresponds to grand_total for the same pack/quantity. The other review round explicitly flagged that the charged AMOUNT on the money path is never verified (findings.json:1217). Because feed price and the charge are both pulled from amount_cents but through two independent code paths (feed multiplies nothing; charge multiplies by quantity), a future edit (e.g. adding tax to line items, or changing the feed to render dollars) silently desynchronizes the advertised price from the charged price with no failing test.

findings.title: Pydantic body validation runs before the auth/version gate, so malformed bodies bypass the gate and return a non-spec error envelope
findings.description: FastAPI parses the `body: models.CheckoutCreate/Update/Complete` parameter BEFORE the function body executes, i.e. before `_gate(...)` is called. A request with a malformed/oversized body (e.g. missing payment_data on /complete, item id > 128 chars, quantity > 1000) returns FastAPI's default 422 `{"detail": [...]}` shape and never reaches `_gate`. Two problems: (1) spec conformance — the ACP spec wants the `{type,code,message,param}` error envelope (register_acp_handlers wires it only for ACPError, not RequestValidationError), so clients get an off-spec error; (2) ordering — body content is validated and echoed back (pydantic error includes offending input) to an UNAUTHENTICATED caller who never passed the Bearer/API-Version gate. Register a RequestValidationError handler that emits the spec envelope, and/or gate before model binding (e.g. parse body manually inside the handler).

findings.title: Only fetches up to SAMPLE_SIZE but messages.list maxResults=2000 exceeds Gmail's 500 cap, silently returning fewer
findings.description: `messages().list(..., maxResults=SAMPLE_SIZE)` with SAMPLE_SIZE=2000 (line 175). Gmail API caps maxResults at 500; values above are clamped server-side, so the 'statistically significant 2000 sample' silently becomes at most 500 with no pagination to fetch more. The report then claims a 2000-message analysis it never performed.

findings.title: Receipts are persisted (and run_ids minted) even for dry_run previews, mixing speculative previews into the durable audit ledger
findings.description: _run is called by both triage_preview (dry_run=True) and triage. For a dry-run preview it still mints run_id and calls receipts.persist(), writing a signed receipt row with dry_run=1 into the receipts table. Combined with the lack of auth (account_id is always None here — persist() is called without account_id), every anonymous preview writes an unbounded number of receipt rows keyed by random run_id with account_id NULL. This is an unauthenticated write-amplification / storage-growth vector against the SQLite store and pollutes the ledger with throwaway preview receipts that can never be listed (list_receipts filters by account_id).

findings.title: current_period_end is read but a None/expired value never downgrades entitlements — and _period_end only set on subscription.* events, never on invoice.paid
findings.description: NEW angle complementing round1: invoice.paid (265-270) calls set_subscription(status='active') with NO current_period_end, so an account reactivated by invoice.paid keeps whatever stale period_end it had (often None). entitlements_for (plans.py 184-186) treats status 'active' as fully entitled and never consults current_period_end, so even when period_end IS stored it is dead data. The two facts together mean billing-period expiry is structurally unenforceable: there is no path where a passed current_period_end revokes access.

findings.title: entitlements_for trusts account['run_credits'] via int(...) without guarding non-int/None stored values, and is dead code on the live path
findings.description: entitlements_for is never called at runtime (no callers outside tests/docs gen), so the entitlement computation it represents is effectively dead code with respect to enforcement — reinforcing the critical 'credits never consumed' finding. Separately, int(account.get('run_credits', 0)) will raise if run_credits is ever stored as a non-numeric (the schema defaults it to 0 NOT NULL so currently safe), and status normalization lowercases account.get('status') but the canceled/past_due statuses written by the webhook are already lowercase, so the active set ('active','trialing') correctly excludes them. Flagging primarily that this resolver exists but no endpoint consults it — the limits it computes are advertised to buyers (GET /v1/billing/plans) yet never applied.

findings.title: monthly_run_cap is consumed only by the marketing-artifact generator, never by runtime enforcement
findings.description: grep 'monthly_run_cap' shows it read at scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:46 (renders 'unlimited'/'N/mo' into pricing.md marketing copy), inside public_dict()/entitlements_for() in plans.py (the public catalog + the dead entitlements function), and the plan definitions. There is no code that counts a customer's runs in a calendar month and compares against this cap. Confirmed there is also no usage-accounting storage: api/store.py schema (41-98) has accounts.run_credits and current_period_end (subscription expiry) but NO per-month run counter, runs_used, reset_at, or usage table (grep for usage/run_count/monthly/reset_at/period_start in store.py => exit 1). So even if someone tried to enforce monthly_run_cap, the data needed to do so is not recorded anywhere.

findings.title: Metered per-run billing advertised but no Stripe Meter event is ever emitted
findings.description: METERED_ADDON declares meter_event_name='triage_run' and 'unit_amount_cents': 1, presenting a usage-based pay-per-run path. grep shows no code anywhere emits a Stripe Billing Meter event (no stripe.billing.meter_events / 'triage_run' reporting call exists in api/service.py, api/app.py, or mcp_server/server.py). So the metered add-on is also non-functional: triage runs are neither counted toward a meter nor reported to Stripe for invoicing. The whole 'agents pay $0.01/run' revenue mechanism is unimplemented at the code level.

findings.location: entitlements_for return dict, line 192 (run_credits) — combined with no caller
findings.title: entitlements_for surfaces run_credits but, being uncalled, the credit balance is read by nothing for any access decision — deeper instance than round 1's consume_credit note
findings.description: Round 1 flagged that consume_credit is never called (credits never spent) and entitlements_for is dead. The deeper instance: entitlements_for is the ONLY function that reads run_credits for an entitlement context (line 192, `int(account.get('run_credits',0))`), and it is uncalled. So the read side of the credit balance is as dead as the debit side: the balance is write-only (added by fulfill_once) and read by nothing in any request path. Even if a developer wired only consume_credit (the debit), they would still have no place that READS the balance to decide 'metered vs subscription vs cap' — entitlements_for, the intended decision function, would still need wiring. The two halves (read in plans.entitlements_for, debit in store.consume_credit) are both orphaned, so fixing one without the other yields no enforcement.

findings.title: run_triage is the single triage chokepoint and accepts no caller identity — enforcement has no insertion point that is actually used
findings.description: run_triage takes provider/query/limit/dry_run/... but no account_id, api_key, or entitlements argument, and never consults api.store. Both the HTTP path (api/app.py:_run) and the MCP path (mcp_server/server.py:_triage) call it with no identity. Because it is the only chokepoint, the absence of any account parameter here is the structural reason the cap/credit checks are dead: there is literally no place on the live path where the caller is known, so consume_credit/entitlements_for cannot be invoked without a signature change. This is the root-cause locus for the unenforced-paid-model finding (fix bases not outputs: wiring auth+metering belongs here, not bolted onto each endpoint).

findings.title: run_credits is a write-only balance — granted by ACP fulfillment and add_credits, never read for an access decision
findings.description: run_credits is incremented by add_credits (223) and fulfill_once (384) and surfaced by entitlements_for (192, dead) but no code reads it to decide whether a triage may proceed. The schema has no mechanism to ever reduce it on use (consume_credit exists but is uncalled). This makes the column an inert ledger entry: ACP purchases inflate it, nothing draws it down, and it does not influence any runtime path. Combined with the auth gap on /v1/triage and /mcp, the commercial value proposition ('pay-per-run for MCP/ACP', api/plans.py:137) is structurally unimplemented.

findings.title: Single shared connection serialized by one RLock negates WAL read concurrency (docstring claim is false)
findings.description: The module docstring (lines 21-24, 116) claims "WAL mode keeps reads concurrent with the writer." That is not achievable with this design: there is exactly ONE sqlite3 connection (self._conn, line 127) and every read goes through _fetch_one/_fetch_all which acquire the SAME self._lock RLock (lines 426, 435) that every write also holds. WAL only lets reads proceed concurrently with a writer when they are on SEPARATE connections; with one connection guarded by one mutex, all access is fully serialized regardless of journal mode. Effect: under any concurrent FastAPI threadpool load (sync endpoints dispatched on Starlette's threadpool, which the code at lines 124-126 explicitly anticipates with check_same_thread=False), a slow read (e.g. a large list_receipts) blocks every other read AND every write for its full duration. This is a throughput/latency defect, not data corruption, but it makes the WAL pragma pure overhead and contradicts the documented rationale. Severity is medium because writes are described as tiny/infrequent, but reads (list_receipts with LIMIT, get_session, audit lookups) are on the request hot path.

findings.title: No PRAGMA busy_timeout set: any second connection to the DB file fails immediately with 'database is locked'
findings.description: Only journal_mode=WAL is set (line 134); there is no `PRAGMA busy_timeout`. SQLite's default busy_timeout is 0, meaning a connection that cannot immediately acquire the needed lock returns SQLITE_BUSY ('database is locked') instantly rather than waiting and retrying. The current single-process/single-connection design hides this, but the docstring (lines 16-19) explicitly advertises this store as the migration seam and notes the DB is a single file. The moment ANY second connection touches data/app.db -- a second worker process (gunicorn/uvicorn --workers >1), a backup/copy tool, a sqlite3 CLI inspection, a future read-replica, or a WAL checkpoint contending with the writer -- that connection gets an immediate OperationalError instead of waiting. For a commerce store backing billing/ACP credit grants, an unhandled 'database is locked' surfaces as a 500 and, on a webhook path, can cause Stripe to retry (mostly safe due to event-id dedup) but on the ACP /complete path can interact badly with the idempotency 'processing' state. Fix: self._conn.execute('PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000;') in __init__. Low-cost, high-value hardening.

findings.title: No foreign-key constraints or enforcement: receipts/acp_sessions/acp_fulfillments accept arbitrary account_id, allowing orphaned ledger and credit rows
findings.description: receipts.account_id, acp_sessions.account_id, and acp_fulfillments.account_id are plain TEXT columns with no REFERENCES accounts(id) constraint, and PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON is never issued (SQLite defaults FKs OFF even when declared). fulfill_once(session_id, account_id, runs) blindly INSERTs a fulfillment row and then UPDATEs accounts for that id; if account_id is bogus the fulfillment row is recorded (consuming the exactly-once slot for that session) while the credit UPDATE matches zero account rows -> the buyer is permanently never credited yet the session is marked fulfilled. There is no DB-level guard that account_id is real. Round 1 flagged that fulfill_once does not verify the account exists; this finding is the schema-level root cause (no FK + FKs disabled) that also affects receipts and sessions, not just fulfill_once.

findings.location: build_agent_manifest lines 17-51 (protocols/agentic_commerce, oauth_scopes); cf. api/plans.py METERED_ADDON line 130, gen_commerce_artifacts.py
findings.title: Agent discovery manifest advertises a metered/credit-pack commerce surface and verified-safe paid runs that the engine never meters or debits — false self-description to autonomous agents
findings.description: /.well-known/agent.json and llms.txt (built by build_agent_manifest / build_llms_txt and committed via gen_commerce_artifacts.py) advertise to autonomous agents: an agentic_commerce checkout (manifest line 33-37), pricing at /v1/billing/plans which returns METERED_ADDON '$0.01/triage run' + CREDIT_PACKS (billing.py:97-102), and 'This is how AI agents pay for verified-safe triage' (plans.py:137). But (a) the metered Stripe Meter event 'triage_run' (plans.py:136) is emitted nowhere, (b) ACP-purchased run_credits are never consumed by any triage (acp/router.py credits via fulfill_once; mcp_server/server.py _triage and api/service.run_triage take no account/credit), so an agent that BUYS a credit pack gets a balance that is never spent and an agent that simply calls the MCP `triage` tool runs unlimited for free. The machine-readable self-description therefore makes a commercial promise the runtime structurally cannot keep — a B2A integrity defect, not merely an internal dead-code issue. Also note oauth_scopes is advertised as [] (line 45) while ACP and MCP both require/accept bearer credentials, understating the auth surface to integrators.

findings.title: Bare try with no on-error swallows all move failures silently
findings.description: The per-message `try ... end try` has no `on error` handler and no logging. If `move msg to targetMailbox` fails (e.g. target mailbox does not exist, account offline, permission), the failure is silently discarded with zero diagnostics, so the user believes archiving succeeded when messages remain. This also masks the case where `targetMailbox` (resolved at lines 9-13) is invalid.

findings.title: Secret passed as 1Password CLI argument is exposed in process listing / shell history
findings.description: op_item_edit builds the command as ["op", "item", "edit", item, f"{field}={value}"] where `value` is the secret (e.g. the compacted Gmail OAuth token JSON written back by store_json_secret / gmail_auth.store_token_info). Command-line arguments are world-readable via `ps`, /proc/<pid>/cmdline, process accounting, and audit logs on a multi-user host, and the token can land in shell history if ever invoked manually. The matching gmail_auth.py _op_item_edit (lines 56-63) has the identical pattern. 1Password supports reading the value from stdin (e.g. via a fields assignment that avoids argv) to avoid this. The `sensitive=True` flag only suppresses stderr in error text; it does not prevent argv exposure.

findings.title: Secret value passed as a command-line argument is exposed in process listing / argv
findings.description: op_item_edit builds `op item edit item field=<secret-token-json>` and _run_op executes it via subprocess.run with the full argv. While the command runs, the secret token JSON is visible in the process table (ps auxww, /proc/<pid>/cmdline on Linux, Activity Monitor) to any local user/process. For a credential-storage boundary this is a real leak vector: prefer passing the secret via stdin or an op assignment that reads from a file/env, not as an argv element. The corresponding read paths (op read) are fine since the secret only comes back on stdout, but the write path embeds the plaintext token in argv.

findings.title: No test coverage for the credential write/store boundary (store_json_secret, op_item_edit, FileNotFound/CalledProcessError paths)
findings.description: There is no tests/test_onepassword.py anywhere in the repo (confirmed: tests/ has billing/mailapp/web/receipts/state/store/audit/config/models/api/acp/protected_enforcement/mcp/rules but nothing for onepassword/auth). The store_json_secret success path, its 'not configured' RuntimeError fallthrough (line 274), op_item_edit argument construction, and _run_op's FileNotFoundError (line 40) and CalledProcessError sensitive/non-sensitive branches are entirely untested. This is the credential boundary; a regression in arg construction (e.g. the field=value split, account/vault flag ordering) or error handling would not be caught. Recommend adding tests that mock subprocess.run and assert exact argv, the 'not configured' raise, JSON-decode failure, and that sensitive=True suppresses stderr.

findings.location: extract_domain() line 61-64; classify_domain() lines 66-88; callback line 121-129
findings.title: Bare 'except:' swallows all errors in domain extraction and batch callback, plus a too-loose keyword classifier
findings.description: extract_domain uses a bare 'except: return None' (catches KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit too) hiding any parse error. The batch callback does 'if exception: return' silently dropping any message whose metadata fetch failed — those messages are then never classified or moved, but the loop's emptiness check at the top can still see them, risking the 'No moves performed' branch (lines 190-196) being hit while the bucket is non-empty, which the code itself notes 'might loop forever'. classify_domain matches KEYWORDS substrings against combined subject text where Marketing includes the literal '%' and 'off' and 'invite' — 'off' matches 'office', 'kickoff', etc., and '%' matches any percent — so legitimate mail is mis-binned. Worse, the keyword loop returns the FIRST KEYWORDS dict key that matches in dict-iteration order, not the highest-priority/most-specific, so 'account' (Finance/Banking) wins over a security 'verify' if Banking is iterated first. This script archives between labels (misroute risk) per its own header.

findings.title: Bare except: swallows all errors and the domain split is fragile against display-name From headers
findings.description: extract_domain does `sender.split('@')[-1].strip('>]').lower()` inside a bare `except:` (line 63) which catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit. For a From like 'Name <user@bank.com>' it returns 'bank.com>' before strip, then strips '>]' → 'bank.com' (ok), but for 'Name <a@b> via list <relay@x.com>' or addresses with trailing params it returns garbage. There is no MIME/relay decode (unlike core.normalize_sender), so an iCloud Hide-My-Email relay sender is keyed by 'icloud.com' and ALL relayed mail collapses into one domain bucket and is bulk-moved together — including a relayed protected sender. The bare except also masks real bugs.

findings.title: batchModify and list calls have no error handling, no pagination, and an unchecked remove-label can erase intent
findings.description: run_sweep calls service.users().messages().list(...).execute() and batchModify(...).execute() with NO try/except — any HttpError (rate limit, transient 5xx) aborts the whole sweep mid-rule with a traceback and no state saved. The 'while True' loop checks 'if "nextPageToken" not in results: break' but never actually USES the page token in the subsequent list() call (it re-runs the same query), so it relies on the modify having removed the label to make progress; if remove_id is None (label not found) the messages keep matching the query forever => infinite loop. get_label_id indexes results['labels'] (KeyError if 'labels' key absent) rather than results.get('labels', []). No rate-limit backoff at all unlike the main GmailProvider.

findings.title: --limit counts built actions, not messages scanned; vip-only/protected/skip runs fetch far more than limit
findings.description: The loop guard is `while processed_this_run < limit` and `processed_this_run += len(actions)` (line 293), but `actions` excludes protected senders (line 225 continue), non-VIP skips in vip_only mode (line 230 continue), and None details (line 207 continue). So when many messages are filtered out, len(actions) << len(fetched messages). `batch_limit = min(limit - processed_this_run, 100)` keeps requesting full batches and pagination keeps advancing, so the run can scan vastly more than `limit` messages (and make far more API calls / sleep cycles) before processed_this_run reaches limit. The docstring says limit is 'Max messages to process per run'. With --vip-only over a large unfiltered mailbox this effectively walks the entire mailbox instead of stopping at limit. Likely off-by-semantics rather than off-by-one, but it diverges from documented behavior and can cause runaway API usage.

findings.title: health connect/disconnect not in try/finally; failure after connect leaks the connection
findings.description: cmd_health calls provider.connect() then health_check() then provider.disconnect() inside a try, but if health_check() raises, control jumps to the except (line 515) and provider.disconnect() is never called — the connection (IMAP socket / OAuth session) is leaked. Unlike other commands which use the `with provider:` context manager (which guarantees __exit__/disconnect), health does manual connect/disconnect without try/finally. Should use `with provider:` or wrap disconnect in finally.

findings.title: cmd_pending omits VIP config loading, so pending output's is_vip/tier reflects only built-in VIPs, inconsistent with other commands
findings.description: cmd_label (422-423), cmd_summary (522-524), cmd_vip (717-719), cmd_escalate (827-829) all call load_config() + apply_vip_senders_from_config(config) before categorizing. cmd_pending (622 onward) does NOT load config. So when listing pending items, cat_result.is_vip / tier_name (lines 671-674) are computed without the user's config-defined VIP senders applied — a sender that is VIP per config will show is_vip=False in `pending` but is_vip=True in `summary`/`vip`. Inconsistent categorization across subcommands for the same mailbox.

findings.title: cmd_escalate trusts age_hours=0 without detecting that the provider supplied no date
findings.description: The escalate loop computes `age_hours = calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date)` (line 883) and passes it straight into escalate_by_age (line 886) with no check for whether msg.date was actually available. For Gmail/IMAP/Mail.app msg.date is always None, so age_hours is always 0 and no escalation ever occurs — but the command still prints a confident 'Messages checked: N / Messages escalated: 0' summary (lines 936-937), giving the operator false assurance the inbox was correctly triaged. The command should detect msg.date is None (no provider date support) and either warn 'age-based escalation unsupported for provider X' or skip with a logged reason, rather than silently reporting zero escalations.

findings.title: cmd_pending age column is always 0h and sort key is degenerate for non-Outlook providers
findings.description: cmd_pending computes `age_hours = calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date)` at line 667. For Gmail/IMAP/Mail.app msg.date is always None, so every pending item gets age_hours=0 and the displayed age is uniformly '0h' (lines 694, 704) — misleading the user about how stale flagged items are. Additionally the sort key `(x["tier"], -x["age_hours"])` (line 680) becomes effectively a sort by tier only, because -age_hours is 0 for all items, so the intended secondary 'oldest first within tier' ordering silently does nothing on these providers.

findings.title: Type confusion: corrupt state with non-int total_processed crashes run on first batch
findings.description: run_labeler does `total_processed = state.get_total()` (line 173) then `total_processed += len(actions)` (line 295). get_total() returns `self.state.get('total_processed', 0)` with no type coercion, so a corrupt-but-valid state file storing total_processed as a string (e.g. "42") yields a str; `str += int` raises TypeError mid-run (verified). The default fallback (0) only applies when the key is absent, not when it is present-but-wrong-type. Combined with the _load schema gap, this turns a slightly malformed state file into a hard crash on the first processed batch.

findings.title: courts.ca.gov protection is spoofable via substring — 'x@courts.ca.gov.evil.com' matches includes() and is wrongly reported protected
findings.description: Line 106 uses value.includes('courts.ca.gov'), an unanchored substring match against the whole lowercased sender string. 'phish@courts.ca.gov.attacker.com' contains the substring 'courts.ca.gov' and so returns protected:true with label Personal/Government tier 1 — a spoofed sender gets the highest-trust verdict. The Python engine's _domain_matches (core/rules.py:659-663) explicitly kills exactly this left-/right-embed spoof ('purchase.com' != 'chase.com', '.com.attacker.example' rejected). Round 1 flagged only the PROTECTED-set/fallthrough inconsistency and the broad .gov suffix, not this substring-spoofability of the configured domain.

findings.title: chase.com protected only as a substring — 'support@chase.com.phish.net' reported protected, and any display name containing 'chase.com' triggers tier-1 Finance verdict
findings.description: Line 128 value.includes('chase.com') returns protected tier-1 Finance/Banking for any sender string containing the substring 'chase.com' anywhere (e.g. 'alerts@chase.com.evil.com', or a display name 'Chase.com Security <x@phish.net>'). No domain-boundary anchoring (the engine uses _domain_matches). This is a separate defect from round-1's note that line 153's fall-through chase.com check is dead — here the LIVE line-128 check itself is substring-spoofable on the shipped demo.

findings.title: /v1/audit returns identical 'protected_held:2, archived:1' counts for every run_id — receipts are not tied to any run and any enumerated id returns a 200 success receipt
findings.description: runId is parsed from the path but never looked up; the body is a hardcoded constant. Any GET /v1/audit/<anything> returns a 200 with the same fabricated audit counts (total 3, protected_held 2, archived 1). There is no 404 for unknown ids (unlike api/receipts.py:123-124 which 404s on missing receipts), so the surface cannot distinguish a real run from a fabricated one and the counts carry zero information about any actual triage. Combined with the missing signature, the audit surface is purely decorative.

findings.location: triagePreview lines 172-198; reached from /v1/triage and /v1/triage/preview lines 255-263
findings.title: triagePreview fabricates archived/protected/labeled counts unrelated to any mailbox — the live 'triage' endpoints invent results from the limit parameter alone
findings.description: triagePreview computes archived = capped>0?1:0, protectedHeld = capped>1?2:..., total = max(capped,3), and a fixed receipt string, derived solely from the request's `limit` value with no mailbox access whatsoever. Both /v1/triage and /v1/triage/preview route here. A caller sending limit:500 still gets total:3 (max(min(500,50),3)=50 -> actually 50; with limit absent total=3). The numbers are theatre. The Python run_triage (api/service.py) actually connects to a provider and reports real counts. A demo visitor or agent calling the live endpoint receives invented audit numbers presented as a real triage result. Round 1 reported no triagePreview finding.

findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml lines 108-119 (smoke test) vs worker behaviors above
findings.title: CI smoke test only asserts /health, /app/, plans-present, and one courts.ca.gov sender — none of the worker's protection/receipt divergences are caught
findings.description: The post-deploy smoke test (ci.yml:113-119) checks /health status ok, /app/ reachable, /v1/billing/plans contains 'plans', and that {sender:'clerk@courts.ca.gov'} returns protected:true. It never tests an apple.com/irs.gov/docusign sender (would reveal the 3-domain gap), never tests a spoofed substring sender (would reveal the includes() spoofability), never fetches /v1/audit/{id} (would reveal the unsigned fake receipt), and never checks that /v1/triage differs from preview. The single positive courts.ca.gov assertion passes precisely because the substring match happens to be true, masking every defect. So CI green-lights a demo whose safety core silently diverges from the engine. Round 1 flagged the smoke test only for propagation/flakiness (ci.yml lines 108-119), not for failing to cover protection correctness.

findings.location: senderCheck lines 82-170 vs core/rules.py is_protected_sender (787-805), _decode_mime (624-633), _idna_decode (636-649), relay handling (676+)
findings.title: Worker omits MIME-decode, IDNA/punycode normalization, iCloud-relay recovery and self-mailbox match — homoglyph/relay/encoded protected senders are unprotected on the live site
findings.description: The Python gate RFC2047-decodes encoded From words BEFORE matching (so an encoded From can't hide the real address), punycode-decodes IDN labels (homoglyph defense), recovers the real domain from iCloud privaterelay local-parts, and applies a Gmail-canonical self match. The worker does none of this — it lowercases the raw string and substring-checks 3 domains. So '=?utf-8?B?...?=' encoded protected senders, xn-- homoglyph lookalikes of courts.ca.gov/chase.com, and relayed protected mail all evade protection on the deployed demo. This is the gap between the marketed safety core and the JS reimplementation; round-1 worker findings did not cover the missing normalization layers.

findings.title: Agent-discovery surfaces /.well-known/agent.json, /server.json, /pricing.md 404 on the Cloudflare share demo
findings.description: The worker only hardcodes /llms.txt (L307-318); every other non-API path falls through to serveApp() -> env.ASSETS.fetch (L235). The ASSETS binding directory is './web' (wrangler.toml L9-11), and web/ contains ONLY index.html. The repo's agent-discovery artifacts — .well-known/agent.json, server.json, pricing.md, and even llms.txt — live at the REPO ROOT, not under web/. DEPLOY.md L65 advertises 'GET /.well-known/agent.json, GET /llms.txt' as the agent-discovery surface and L78 lists server.json/pricing.md as GTM artifacts. On the Cloudflare share demo at uma.4444j99.dev, /.well-known/agent.json, /server.json, and /pricing.md will return 404 (ASSETS has no such files). Only the hardcoded /llms.txt responds, and it is a worker-inlined copy that can silently drift from the root llms.txt. The FastAPI/Docker deployment serves these correctly via well_known.router + StaticFiles, so the two deploy targets have divergent discovery contracts.

findings.title: Worker senderCheck protected-sender logic is partially dead/incorrect vs documented protection set
findings.description: Line 1 declares `const PROTECTED = new Set(["courts.ca.gov", "chase.com", "1password.com"])` but PROTECTED is never referenced anywhere in the file (dead constant). The actual protection is computed inline: the generic fall-through at lines 152-153 returns `protected: value.includes("chase.com") || value.includes("1password.com")`, but `chase.com` is already caught and returned early at line 128, so the `chase.com` term here is dead code. Net effect: the demo only protects `.gov`/`courts.ca.gov` (line 106), `chase.com` (line 128), and `1password.com` (line 153). README/llms.txt claim the gate 'never archives government, financial, legal, or platform mail'; the demo protects no 'legal' senders beyond courts.ca.gov and only two financial/platform domains, whereas the canonical core/rules.py PROTECTED_SENDERS has ~40 domains (stripe.com, robinhood.com, irs.gov, ssa.gov, login.gov, apple.com, google.com, anthropic.com, example-lawfirm.com, etc.). The demo's safety story is materially narrower than what the prose advertises.

findings.title: Wrong-method requests to API routes fall through to the static asset server instead of returning 405
findings.description: Every `/v1/*` route is guarded by `&& request.method === "POST"` (or `=== "GET"`). A request with the wrong method (e.g. GET /v1/senders/check, POST /v1/billing/plans, GET /v1/triage) does NOT match its branch and falls through to `return serveApp(request, env)` at line 320, which calls `env.ASSETS.fetch(request)`. The client receives the static SPA HTML (200) or a 404 HTML page for an API JSON endpoint — never a proper 405 Method Not Allowed with an Allow header. The real FastAPI app (api/app.py) returns 405 with a JSON body and an `Allow` header for these cases. An agent or test that probes the method contract gets HTML where it expects JSON, and a 200 status masks the misuse.

findings.title: /v1/audit/ with an empty run_id fabricates a receipt for the literal id 'demo'
findings.description: `const runId = url.pathname.split("/").pop() || "demo"`. For `/v1/audit/` (trailing slash, empty final segment) `.pop()` returns `""`, so runId defaults to `"demo"` and the worker returns a 200 receipt `"Signed receipt for demo"` with fabricated audit counts. There is no 400/404 for a missing run id. Worse, `/v1/audit/a/b/c` returns a receipt for `c` (the last segment) while ignoring the rest of the path, so any nested path under /v1/audit/ resolves to *some* fabricated receipt. The real API treats the entire `{run_id}` as a single path param and looks it up in a ledger (404 on miss). The worker minting a 200 'signed receipt' for arbitrary/empty ids is a trust-surface lie distinct from the round-2 'unsigned fabricated audit' finding because it concerns path-parse robustness.

findings.title: Worker /v1/billing/plans omits metered/credit_packs/currency that the real API returns
findings.description: The real `GET /v1/billing/plans` (api/billing.py list_plans) returns `{plans, metered, credit_packs, currency}`. The worker's `billingPlans()` returns only `{plans: [...]}`. An agent or client that reads `data.metered`, `data.credit_packs`, or `data.currency` (advertised in plans.py as the single source of truth and surfaced for ACP credit-pack purchases) gets `undefined` against the share demo. Silent contract divergence between the two HTTP surfaces that both claim to be the same product API.

findings.title: readJson swallows all parse errors to {} so malformed/empty/non-JSON POST bodies return a 200 'success' instead of 400
findings.description: `readJson` catches every error from `request.json()` and returns `{}`. A POST to /v1/senders/check, /v1/triage, or /v1/triage/preview with a malformed body, no body, or a non-JSON content-type therefore proceeds with `body = {}`: senderCheck(undefined) returns protected:true (line 84-104 empty branch), and triagePreview("demo", NaN) returns a fabricated 200 receipt. The real FastAPI app validates the body against Pydantic schemas and returns 422 for malformed input. The worker never returns a 4xx for bad input — it always fabricates a plausible 200. A client cannot distinguish 'I sent garbage' from 'the server protected an empty sender'.

findings.title: Preview always returns run_id 'demo_preview', but the UI/llms.txt promise 'each run gets a run_id' fetchable at /v1/audit/{run_id}
findings.description: triagePreview hardcodes `run_id: "demo_preview"`. The site copy (index.html line 642) and worker llms.txt say each run gets a run_id you can fetch at /v1/audit/{run_id}. Fetching /v1/audit/demo_preview on the worker returns a receipt for run_id 'demo_preview' but with audit numbers (total 3, protected_held 2, archived 1) that do NOT necessarily match the preview's own numbers (which scale with the limit, e.g. total=Math.max(capped,3)). So the 'verifiable receipt' for a preview run can report different counts than the preview itself returned — the audit lookup is not derived from or consistent with the preview it claims to certify.

findings.title: Two plists invoke the same run_automation.sh at the same 9:00 time — duplicate/overlapping schedule risk if both loaded
findings.description: diff shows the two plists are byte-identical except Label (com.user.gmail_labeler vs com.user.mail_automation) and RunAtLoad (true vs false). Both set ProgramArguments to /bin/bash run_automation.sh and StartCalendarInterval Hour=9 Minute=0, and both write to the SAME log files (/Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/launchd.std{out,err}.log). If both LaunchAgents are ever bootstrapped, the full multi-provider automation runs twice concurrently at 09:00, double-processing Gmail/Outlook/iCloud and interleaving writes to identical log paths. They are redundant: gmail_labeler is the legacy label and should not coexist as a second copy of the universal runner.

findings.title: Non-standard log directory (~/System/Logs) hardcoded — launchd will not create it and will fail to start the job if missing
findings.description: StandardOutPath/StandardErrorPath point at /Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/. macOS convention for per-user logs is ~/Library/Logs; ~/System/Logs is non-standard. More importantly, launchd does NOT create the parent directory for StandardOut/ErrorPath — if the directory does not exist, the job fails to launch (errno on the redirect). deploy.sh:38 does 'mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"' (= $HOME/System/Logs/mail_automation), so the supported deploy path creates it for the deploying user — but the directory is absolutized to /Users/4jp in the plist, so on any other user the plist points at a dir deploy.sh created under a different $HOME. The two only line up for user 4jp. Same in com.user.gmail_labeler.plist.

findings.location: lines 197-210 (record/_append entry), redact gating at 209-210; docstring 40-42
findings.title: Redacted ('shareable/committable') audit JSONL still writes raw provider message_id on every line
findings.description: The docstring (lines 40-42) promises that redact=True yields 'domain-only lines (no local-part) for a receipt you can share or commit.' But the only field gated by `if not self.redact` is `sender` (lines 209-210). The entry dict ALWAYS includes `"message_id": message_id` (line 200) regardless of redact. So a redacted JSONL produced by `cli.py --redact-audit` (whose stated purpose at cli.py:380 is 'a shareable domain-only receipt') still embeds the raw provider message identifier on every line. For Gmail these are opaque hex ids, but for IMAP they are mailbox UIDs and for Mail.app/Outlook they can encode account/store/message paths — internal identifiers the user is being told are safe to commit. The redaction promise ('no local-part') is satisfied for sender but the message_id channel is unredacted, defeating the stated 'committable' guarantee.

findings.title: BATCH_SIZE env var crashes on non-integer value
findings.description: int(os.getenv(f'{prefix}BATCH_SIZE')) is called unguarded. If MAIL_AUTO_BATCH_SIZE is set to a non-numeric string (e.g. '100 ', 'large', or empty-after-whitespace), int() raises ValueError that propagates out of load_config() and aborts the entire CLI before any work. Config loading should be resilient to bad env input (try/except with a logged warning, or validation), consistent with the lenient handling everywhere else in this module.

findings.title: YAML batch_size/port/throttle_seconds assigned verbatim with no type validation or coercion
findings.description: batch_size (194-195), imap.port (217-218), and throttle_seconds (196-197) are copied straight from yaml.safe_load output into the typed dataclass fields with no int/float coercion or validation. YAML happily parses `batch_size: "100"` as a string, `batch_size: 100.5` as a float, `port: high` as a string, etc. The values then flow downstream (batch_size into provider batch API calls, port into imaplib connect, throttle into time.sleep) where a string or wrong type produces a TypeError deep in the provider rather than at config load, or silently mis-sizes batches. Unlike the env path there is not even an int() attempt — the raw YAML type is trusted.

findings.title: default_provider taken verbatim from YAML/env with no validation against the known provider set
findings.description: default_provider is assigned from untrusted YAML (line 189) and env (266-267) with no check against {gmail, imap, mailapp, outlook}. A typo like `default_provider: gmial` is accepted silently. Whatever consumes it later either KeyErrors or silently falls through. Worse, the field is effectively dead in the CLI: cli.py selects the provider only from `--provider` (args.provider), never from config.default_provider, so the documented config knob does nothing and the typo is never surfaced. Either way the user's intent is silently lost.

findings.title: log_level loaded but never applied, and not validated against valid logging levels
findings.description: config.log_level is populated from YAML (191) and env (268-269) but the CLI never reads it: logging is configured by a hardcoded logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO) at cli.py:48 and only overridden to DEBUG by the --verbose flag (cli.py:1194-1195). So `log_level: ERROR` or `log_level: WARNING` in config.yaml is silently ignored — a real behavioral surprise documented in CLAUDE.md and the sample config. Additionally, even if it were applied, the value is unvalidated: a typo like `log_level: WARN` (not a valid Python level name) or `log_level: verbose` would later fail in setLevel/getLevelName or set a numeric level of 'Level WARN'. Dead config knob plus missing validation.

findings.title: custom_rules loaded into Config but never applied to the rules engine (dead config knob)
findings.description: config.custom_rules is populated from YAML (248-250) and documented in CLAUDE.md and the sample config (388-394) as 'override default LABEL_RULES', but nothing ever merges it into core.rules.LABEL_RULES. grep across the repo shows custom_rules is only assigned in config.py and never read by cli.py or core/rules.py. Unlike vip_senders (which has apply_vip_senders_from_config), there is no apply_custom_rules_from_config. So users who add custom_rules to their config get zero effect and no warning — their categorization customizations are silently dropped. When/if it were wired up, note it is also assigned verbatim from untrusted YAML with no schema/regex validation, repeating the vip_senders problem (each rule's 'patterns' would be re.search'd unvalidated).

findings.title: calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 for None date, conflating 'unknown age' with 'brand new' and masking the provider gap
findings.description: When email_date is None, the function returns 0 (lines 1173-1174). 0 means 'just received now', which is the maximally-wrong default for an escalation feature: it guarantees the `email_age_hours < 24` no-escalation branch in escalate_by_age (core/rules.py:1122) is always taken. Because 3 of 4 providers (Gmail/IMAP/Mail.app) always pass None (see companion findings), this fallback silently disables the entire age-based escalation feature on those providers instead of surfacing a 'date unavailable' condition. A safer design would raise or return a sentinel (e.g. float('nan') or None) that the caller could detect and warn on, or callers should skip/flag messages with no date rather than treating them as age 0. This is the root-cause enabler that turns a missing-field bug into an invisible feature outage.

findings.title: Tech/Security greedy .* patterns elevate ordinary marketing mail to Tier 1 Critical
findings.description: Tech/Security is tier 1 (Critical, starred, kept-in-inbox), priority 9. Its patterns use unanchored greedy '.*' across the combined sender+subject text: r'new.*device' matches 'new mobile device unboxing review' (a gadget marketing blast), r'login.*detected' and r'verification.*code' similarly match benign phrasing. Verified re.search(r'new.*device','new mobile device unboxing review') -> True. Because priority 9 beats Marketing/Shopping/Notification, a promotional email is wrongly elevated to Critical and starred. Round 1 flagged AI/brand and Finance substrings but not these Tech/Security .* patterns.

findings.location: Personal/Government patterns lines 472-478 (r'passport', r'dmv', r'social.*security', r'state\.fl\.us')
findings.title: Government Tier-1 patterns 'passport'/'dmv'/'social.*security' bare-match generic subjects
findings.description: Personal/Government is tier 1 Critical. 'passport' matches 'a passport to better savings' / 'passportphotos r us'; 'dmv' matches any 'dmv*' substring ('admvise', 'dmvxyz'); 'social.*security' (greedy) matches 'social media security: protect your account' and 'social security number protection guide'. Verified categorize_with_tier('x@y.io','a passport to better savings') -> Personal/Government tier 1; ('x@y.io','social security number protection guide') -> Personal/Government tier 1. Marketing copy gets elevated to Critical/starred. Round 1 flagged only the r'\.gov$'/r'@.*\.gov' anchors and the Personal family/mom/dad keywords, not these Government keyword patterns.

findings.title: State save failure is logged and swallowed; corrupt state file falls back to default silently, risking reprocessing or skipped resumption
findings.description: save() catches all exceptions, logs, and returns — so a failed state write (disk full, permission) is invisible to the caller; the run continues and on the NEXT run resumes from a stale/earlier page token, reprocessing already-handled messages (with archive/move side effects re-attempted). _load() catches JSONDecodeError and generic Exception and returns _default_state() — a corrupt/truncated state file (e.g. from a crash mid-write, since save() is not atomic: open('w') truncates then dumps, so a crash leaves a zero/partial file) silently resets next_page_token to None, causing the run to restart from the beginning rather than resume. save() should write to a temp file and os.replace() for atomicity; load() should distinguish 'absent' from 'corrupt'.

findings.title: State file written non-atomically; crash mid-write corrupts resume state
findings.description: save() opens the state file in 'w' (truncating) and json.dumps directly to it. A crash, disk-full, or signal during the write leaves a truncated/partial JSON file. On next run _load() catches JSONDecodeError and silently resets to default state (next_page_token=None) — meaning the run restarts from the beginning and re-processes everything, or loses progress, with only a logged error. Write to a temp file in the same dir then os.replace() for atomic replacement. (In scope as state-file-corruption.)

findings.title: last_run uses naive local time while the rest of the codebase is UTC-aware
findings.description: save() sets `last_run = datetime.now().isoformat()` — a naive timestamp in the machine's local timezone with no offset/Z suffix. core/audit.py:198 uses `datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()` (UTC, offset-aware) and the escalation/age logic in core/rules.py works in UTC. Mixing a naive local timestamp into a UTC-oriented system means: (1) consumers comparing last_run against UTC `now` get an error of up to the local UTC offset (hours), (2) any later `datetime.fromisoformat(last_run)` produces a naive datetime that cannot be subtracted from an aware UTC datetime without raising 'can't subtract offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes', (3) the value is ambiguous around DST transitions. Should be `datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()` for consistency.

findings.title: Non-atomic write: a crash or kill during save() can leave a truncated/corrupt state file
findings.description: save() opens the destination file directly in 'w' mode, truncating it before the new content is written. If the process is killed (SIGKILL, jetsam, power loss) between truncation and json.dump completing/flushing, the on-disk state file is left empty or partially written. On the next run _load either hits the JSONDecodeError path (loses all progress -> restart from scratch) or, if the partial content happens to parse, feeds malformed state into the run. Since this module exists specifically for crash recovery, a write strategy that itself corrupts state on crash defeats its purpose. The fix is write-to-temp + os.replace (atomic rename), which the audit module's append-with-newline at least partially sidesteps but state.py does not.

findings.title: Smart mailbox ships with literal placeholder 'your-name' — 'Needs Action' view is broken/empty unless hand-edited
findings.description: condition type:sender does contain value:"your-name" uses the literal placeholder. Run as-is (the script has no prompt or guard), the '1. Needs Action' smart mailbox filters for senders literally containing 'your-name', matching essentially nothing. The comment says to replace it but nothing enforces that, so the headline view silently does not work.

findings.title: CTA "View on GitHub" link points to stale/renamed org (organvm-iii-ergon) that only resolves via GitHub's rename redirect
findings.description: The only outbound/actionable link on the pitch page is `View on GitHub`. The repo's actual canonical org is `a-organvm` (git remote is `https://github.com/a-organvm/universal-mail--automation.git`; server.json declares `io.github.a-organvm/...`). I confirmed via the GitHub API and a redirect-following curl: `https://github.com/organvm-iii-ergon/universal-mail--automation` currently 301-redirects to `https://github.com/a-organvm/universal-mail--automation` (final HTTP 200), i.e. the org was renamed and the link only works because GitHub forwards the old name. This is fragile: GitHub release the old org name once a new owner claims it (GitHub frees a renamed org/user name once the legacy redirect is broken or after re-creation), so a third party could register `organvm-iii-ergon` and silently capture this CTA's destination. Even barring takeover, it is the wrong canonical URL and contradicts every other link surface (README badges also still use the stale `organvm-iii-ergon`, but the deployed git remote is `a-organvm`). Fix the base generator so the pitch CTA uses the live org `a-organvm` (or the live product domain `uma.4444j99.dev`).

findings.title: Pitch page shows placeholder marketing copy that contradicts the shipped, detailed pricing and feature set
findings.description: The pitch landing page presents only stub copy: `#market` says "Business model details coming soon." with badges "subscription / pre-launch" (lines 200-204), `#features` shows a single card "In Development — Feature list coming soon." (line 177), `#solution` and `#hero` just repeat the meta description "Email workflow automation and scheduling" (lines 142, 166, 240), and `#positioning` declares "No declared edges / standalone" (line 217). Meanwhile the repo has a concrete, shipped pricing model and feature set: pricing.md and api/plans.py define Free $0 / Pro $19-mo / Business $49-mo tiers, a $0.01/triage metered rate, and ACP credit packs ($1.00 / $9.00); README.md ships a full product description (multi-provider Gmail/Outlook/iCloud triage, Eisenhower tiers, escalation). A prospect/agent reading this pitch sees "pre-launch, coming soon" for a product that actually has live pricing and a deployed worker at uma.4444j99.dev. This is stale/incorrect product claims contradicting pricing.md / api/plans.py. Root cause is the auto-generator (header comment `ORGANVM:PITCH:AUTO v1.0`) emitting an empty template; the fix belongs in the generator/seed that feeds it, not this rendered file.

findings.title: Important-sender match uses substring `contains` on full From string — over-matches and is display-name spoofable
findings.description: is_important uses `if theSender contains s` (line 5) where `theSender` is the full From string ('Display Name <addr>') and s is an entry like 'boss@company.com' or 'familymember@example.com'. Two issues distinct from round 1 (which only reported the placeholder list and the bare-try swallow): (1) substring (not boundary) matching — 'boss@company.com' as a substring would also match 'notboss@company.com.evil.com' or any string embedding it; there is no anchoring like the Python gate's _domain_matches boundary check. (2) Because the match is against the full From string including the display name, a malicious or coincidental display name containing 'boss@company.com' flags arbitrary mail. The canonical Python VIP path (core/rules.check_vip_sender, core/config.apply_vip_senders_from_config) uses validated regex patterns against the parsed address; this AppleScript shares none of that and silently diverges. Result: VIP flagging is unreliable and spoofable, while the Python engine's hardened VIP logic is bypassed entirely.

findings.title: Refresh catches only RefreshError; transient network errors crash and a post-refresh write-back failure is not isolated
findings.description: The try wraps both creds.refresh(Request()) AND store_token_info(creds), but only `except RefreshError`. (1) A transient network failure during refresh (google.auth.exceptions.TransportError, requests ConnectionError/Timeout) is NOT a RefreshError subclass, so it propagates as an unhandled crash instead of degrading to interactive consent. (2) If refresh SUCCEEDS but store_token_info raises (op CLI unavailable, RuntimeError 'Token storage not configured', subprocess failure), that exception is also not RefreshError, so it crashes the whole call — after a valid refresh — and the refreshed token is lost / not persisted, forcing a fresh interactive flow next time. The write-back should be in its own try with a warning-and-continue.

findings.location: _init_labels lines 120-122; process_batch lines 187, 224-226; categorize via core returns 'Misc/Other'
findings.title: Dead 'Uncategorized' removal logic — core categorizer returns 'Misc/Other', not 'Uncategorized'
findings.description: gmail_labeler.py imports categorize_message from core.rules, which returns labels from core's taxonomy where the catch-all is 'Misc/Other'. But _init_labels only caches 'Uncategorized' (lines 120-122) and process_batch builds removal logic around uncategorized_id = self.label_cache.get('Uncategorized') (line 187) with the guard label_name != 'Uncategorized' (line 225). Since core never emits 'Uncategorized', this branch effectively only ever removes a literally-named 'Uncategorized' label that the categorizer will never assign, and the intended 'remove stale catch-all when re-categorizing' behavior for 'Misc/Other' is never wired up here. The removal of the new 'Misc/Other' catch-all is only reachable via --remove-label. This is stale code that no longer matches the core taxonomy it depends on; the comments at lines 209-211 still reference 'Misc/Other' but the variable is 'Uncategorized'.

findings.location: LABEL_RULES line 282; categorize_email line 364; label_all_unlabeled_emails lines 386,430
findings.title: Legacy catch-all label is 'Uncategorized' while core uses 'Misc/Other' (taxonomy divergence)
findings.description: The legacy module defines its own catch-all as 'Uncategorized' (priority 999) and categorize_email returns 'Uncategorized' as the fallback, whereas core/rules.py defines the catch-all as 'Misc/Other'. Running the legacy entry point therefore creates and applies an 'Uncategorized' label that is inconsistent with every other path (cli.py, gmail_labeler.py, imap_rules.py) which produce 'Misc/Other'. This splits the unmatched bucket across two label names, breaks bulk_sweeper SWEEP_RULES queries (which target 'label:Misc/Other'), and produces an inconsistent taxonomy in the user's mailbox.

findings.location: LABEL_RULES (whole dict, lines 39-283) vs core/rules.py LABEL_RULES
findings.title: Legacy LABEL_RULES is a stale fork missing many categories and hardened patterns
findings.description: The embedded LABEL_RULES diverges substantially from core/rules.py: legacy lacks Dev/GameDev, Finance/Tax, Tech/Google, Personal/Health, Social/LinkedIn, Tech/Storage, Personal/Government, Professional/Legal categories, and lacks all the boundary-anchored hardened patterns (e.g. core's r"(^|@|\.)gemini\.com\b" vs legacy bare r"gemini" which mislabels Google Gemini AI as Finance/Banking; core's anchored r"(^|@|\.)citi(bank|cards|group)?\.com\b" vs legacy bare r"citi" which matches 'publicity'/'felicity'; core's r"(^|@|\.)ally\.com\b" vs legacy bare r"ally" which matches 'really'). Legacy 'AI/Services' keeps priority 4 (wins over Tech/Security at 9), while core de-ranked AI/Services to priority 12 specifically so login/security alerts win. Anyone invoking the legacy entry point gets materially worse categorization and the bare-substring over-matching bugs that core already fixed.

findings.location: LABEL_RULES lines 60, 95, 235, 239, 273 (patterns r"render\\.com", r"self\\.inc", r"builtin\\.com", r"e\\.godaddy\\.com", r"ibo\\.org")
findings.title: Double-backslash regex patterns match a literal backslash, so these senders never categorize
findings.description: In the legacy file these raw-string patterns use DOUBLE backslash: r"render\\.com", r"self\\.inc", r"builtin\\.com", r"e\\.godaddy\\.com", r"ibo\\.org". A raw string r"render\\.com" is backslash-backslash-dot-c-o-m, i.e. regex for a literal backslash followed by any char + 'com'. Verified with re: it does NOT match 'render.com' and only matches text containing an actual backslash. So Dev/Infrastructure (render), Finance/Banking (self.inc), Professional/Jobs (builtin.com), Services/Domain (e.godaddy.com), and Marketing (ibo.org) silently fail to fire for these senders, dumping them into Uncategorized. The refactored core/rules.py fixed these to single-backslash; this is unrepaired drift in the still-runnable legacy entry point (gmail_labeler_legacy.py is documented as a supported legacy command).

findings.location: main, slice line 144: uids = uids[max(0, len(uids) - args.start - args.limit) : len(uids) - args.start]
findings.title: Paging slice computes a negative end index when start > len(uids), selecting wrong (oldest) messages
findings.description: When args.start exceeds the number of matched UIDs, len(uids) - args.start is negative and is used as the slice END. Python interprets a negative slice end as an offset from the end, so instead of yielding an empty page (the correct behavior when paging past the end) it selects from the beginning. Verified: with n=10 uids, start=15, limit=5 the slice returns [0,1,2,3,4] (the 5 OLDEST messages) rather than []. This silently processes/labels the wrong messages when paging beyond the available range. The end should be clamped: max(0, len(uids) - args.start).

findings.title: All advertised endpoints use placeholder host mail.example.com, not the live uma.4444j99.dev
findings.description: Every agent-facing URL (/mcp, /acp/checkout_sessions, /acp/feed.json, /v1/audit/{run_id}, /v1/billing/plans, /v1/senders/check, /v1/triage/preview) points at https://mail.example.com. The actual deployed host (wrangler.toml route) is uma.4444j99.dev. An agent consuming llms.txt to discover the service would hit a non-existent host and every integration fails.

findings.title: Headline trust claim ('signed audit receipt', 'never archives government/financial/legal/platform mail') contradicts the live worker.mjs
findings.description: llms.txt advertises 'an independent, signed audit receipt that refuses to report success if a protected sender left the inbox' and a gate that 'never archives government, financial, legal, or platform mail'. The live cloudflare/worker.mjs fabricates an UNSIGNED hardcoded receipt and its PROTECTED set is only 3 domains with spoofable substring matching (see R2 worker findings). The agent-facing descriptor overstates the guarantee the deployed surface actually provides.

findings.title: mail_export.tsv is git-tracked; currently header-only but the schema is sender/subject PII and the file is the AppleScript export target
findings.description: mail_export.tsv is TRACKED. Right now it contains only the header row (mailbox_name, message_id, sender, subject, date_received, read, flagged, size_bytes) and zero data rows, so no PII is leaked TODAY. But the file is the exact output target of export_mail_snapshot.applescript (writes to ~/mail_automation/mail_export.tsv) and the columns are real per-message PII (sender, subject, message_id). Committing the empty-but-tracked file means a real export run that lands here would be a one-command 'git add' away from publishing the inbox. It should be removed from tracking and gitignored (same class as the labeler_state.json finding).

findings.title: Docstring says '>30 days' but query uses 'older_than:7d' (also comment says 7 days) — stale/inconsistent threshold
findings.description: Module docstring states 'Mark old (>30 days) emails ... as READ'. The actual query is `f"label:{category} is:unread older_than:7d"` (line 41). The two disagree (30 vs 7 days). Operator running based on the docstring would mark far more recent mail as read than expected — a behavior surprise for a bulk mutation.

findings.title: MCP triage tool does not bound/validate query and remove_label inputs
findings.description: The REST layer validates triage inputs via Pydantic (api/schemas.py: query max_length=2048, remove_label max_length=512, provider max_length=64). The MCP triage and triage_preview tools bypass those schemas entirely — they accept raw str args and pass query, remove_label, and provider straight into service.run_triage with no length or format bound. Only `limit` is clamped (_clamp_limit) and only check_protected_sender truncates its inputs ([:4096]).
