An agent (especially a prompt-injected one, which the module docstring explicitly anticipates) can supply an arbitrarily large `query` or `remove_label`, driving unbounded provider work / regex scanning, exactly the DoS vector schemas.py line 12-16 was written to prevent. Inconsistent with the security posture the file claims.

findings.location: _triage() lines 129-146 and triage()/triage_preview() return paths; cf. api/app.py:_run lines 124-130
findings.title: MCP triage returns no persisted/signed run_id receipt — the advertised 'verifiable audit receipt' is unverifiable on the MCP surface
findings.description: The module docstring (lines 5-6) and INSTRUCTIONS (line 63-64) promise that 'every triage returns a verifiable audit receipt.' But _triage returns TriageResponse(**result) where result comes straight from service.run_triage, which provides only audit.receipt_line() (a plain human string, core/audit.py:261) and audit.summary() (self-reported counts). Unlike the HTTP path (api/app.py:_run lines 124-130) the MCP path never mints a run_id nor calls receipts.persist(), so no signed, fetchable, independently re-derivable receipt (GET /v1/audit/{run_id}) is ever produced for MCP-driven runs and TriageResponse.run_id is always None. The agent receives an unsigned string it cannot independently verify, contradicting the surface's central trust claim. NEW: round 1's MCP findings are about auth/metering/input bounds, not the missing signed receipt.

findings.title: Non-rate-limit error from batch.execute() is unhandled and crashes the entire triage/summary/escalate run
findings.description: batch_get_details wraps batch.execute() in _execute_with_backoff, which only swallows/retries 403/429 rate-limit HttpErrors and RE-RAISES any other HttpError (e.g. a 400 malformed-request, a 500, or an auth 401). There is no try/except around line 248, so a single non-rate-limit failure of the whole batch GET aborts batch_get_details with an exception, propagating up and crashing cmd_label/cmd_summary/cmd_pending/cmd_escalate mid-run. Round 1 covered the per-message callback swallowing failures (567) and whole-batch retry double-processing, but not the uncaught non-rate-limit batch.execute exception that takes down the whole run. The per-message get_message_details path catches 404 specifically, but the batch path has no equivalent guard.

findings.location: apply_label() / remove_label() / star() / mark_read() etc., lines 277-283, 301-307, 328-344, 361-377
findings.title: IMAP X-GM-LABELS / FLAGS STORE operations return True without checking the IMAP response status
findings.description: For the Gmail-extensions path, apply_label does self._connection.uid('STORE', ..., '+X-GM-LABELS', ...) and immediately 'return True' WITHOUT inspecting the (typ, data) tuple imaplib returns. imaplib does not raise on a 'NO'/'BAD' server response — it returns it. So a server that rejects the label store (e.g. label quota, invalid label, mailbox not selected) reports SUCCESS to the caller and to the audit (labels_added recorded as applied). The same unchecked-return pattern is in remove_label (X-GM-LABELS), star/unstar (+/-FLAGS \Flagged), and mark_read/mark_unread (+/-FLAGS \Seen). Contrast the COPY path at line 288 which DOES check res=='OK'. These should check the returned status before returning True.

findings.location: star line 331, unstar line 340, mark_read line 364, mark_unread line 373
findings.title: IMAP star/unstar/mark_read/mark_unread ignore STORE response and report success on server rejection
findings.description: Each method calls self._connection.uid('STORE', message_id, '+/-FLAGS', ...) and returns True in the try block without checking the returned status tuple (imaplib returns 'NO'/'BAD' rather than raising). A server-rejected flag change is reported as success, so a star/flag that did not actually apply is counted as applied. Lower severity than the label/archive path because these do not move mail out of the inbox, but it still produces wrong results on a common path.

findings.title: Tab/newline in sender or subject corrupts TSV parsing of osascript output
findings.description: The list-messages script emits `msgId & "\t" & msgSender & "\t" & msgSubject & "\t" & ...` joined by linefeed. Senders/subjects are arbitrary email content. If a subject contains a literal tab, `line.split("\t")` produces extra columns and the read/flagged flags shift to the wrong positions (or the row gets `len(parts) >= 5` true with wrong fields). If a subject contains a newline, the row splits into multiple `lines`, and the continuation fragment is silently parsed as a malformed message (likely dropped by the `len(parts) >= 5` guard, but a tab-containing subject is silently mis-parsed, yielding wrong is_read/is_starred for that message). Delimiter collision with unescaped content is a real correctness bug.

findings.title: Timeout/osascript failure in list_messages silently returns empty result, masking errors and ending pagination early
findings.description: _run_applescript raises RuntimeError on timeout or non-zero exit. list_messages catches RuntimeError and returns `ListMessagesResult(messages=[], next_page_token=None)`. A transient AppleScript failure (timeout on a big mailbox, Mail busy) is therefore indistinguishable from 'no more messages' — the caller stops paginating and the sweep silently processes nothing/partial data with no error surfaced upward. health_check would still pass on a small limit. Inappropriate silent fallback on a common path.

findings.title: OData $filter injection / breakage on folder names containing a single quote
findings.description: The folder-existence fallback builds an OData filter via f-string interpolation: params={"$filter": f"displayName eq '{part}'"}. If a label segment contains a single quote (e.g. a folder named "O'Brien" or any user-supplied label with an apostrophe), the resulting filter string is malformed OData and the Graph request will fail (400), which is then swallowed by the bare-except at 648 leading to the RuntimeError at 649. OData requires single quotes inside string literals to be doubled (''). Beyond breakage, this is an injection vector: a crafted label could alter the filter semantics. Since labels can come from config (core/rules.py LABEL_RULES / config.yaml vip label_override), this is reachable from user-controlled data.

findings.title: Read-modify-write of categories races and silently drops categories when the GET fails
findings.description: apply_category reads current categories (347), appends, and PATCHes the full list (356-359). Two problems: (1) If the GET at 347 throws, the except at 349 sets current_cats=[] and then PATCHes {"categories": [category]} — this OVERWRITES and DELETES any categories already on the message, silent data loss of metadata. (2) Even on success this is a non-atomic read-modify-write; concurrent runs (the CLI/launchd job overlapping) can clobber each other's category additions. Graph has no add-to-collection for categories, but the failure-path overwrite (1) is the clear defect: a transient read error becomes destructive.

findings.title: Folder cache pagination not followed ($top 100 only) — mailboxes with >100 folders miss entries, causing duplicate folder creation
findings.description: _init_folder_cache fetches only the first 100 top-level folders ($top:100) and never follows @odata.nextLink. Same for childFolders. A mailbox with more than 100 folders at a level will have missing cache entries. ensure_label_exists then treats those folders as nonexistent and attempts to CREATE them (618-630); the create may 409/400 (then the swallowed fallback runs). This can create duplicate folders or fail. The list_messages path correctly returns nextLink, so the inconsistency shows the omission is a bug, not intentional.

findings.title: No handling of HTTP 429 throttling / Retry-After; raise_for_status turns throttling into a hard error with no backoff
findings.description: All three request helpers call response.raise_for_status() with zero retry, no honoring of the Retry-After header, and no handling of Graph's 429 (throttling) or transient 503/504. Bulk relabeling of large mailboxes is exactly the workload Graph throttles. A single 429 raises HTTPError; in list_messages it is swallowed into an empty result (silent early stop), and in apply_* it propagates to apply_actions and is counted as a per-message error. The provider has no rate-limit resilience despite being designed for bulk operations.

findings.title: Bare 'except: pass' silently produces an incomplete folder cache, causing duplicate folders / wrong moves
findings.description: _fetch_child_folders wraps the recursive Graph call in 'except Exception: pass' with the comment 'Ignore errors for child folders'. Any error (auth expiry, throttling/429, transient network) silently aborts caching that subtree. Because ensure_label_exists() consults _folder_cache to decide whether a folder already exists, a silently-incomplete cache means apply_label() will attempt to CREATE a folder that actually exists, or fail to find the correct destination — leading to duplicate folders or messages moved to the wrong/new folder. A 429 here is swallowed rather than retried. Errors should at minimum be logged with the parent folder context.

findings.title: Outlook due-date strftime hard-codes 'Z' (UTC) and zeroes the clock, mislabeling naive/local or non-UTC due_date at the wrong UTC date
findings.description: star() formats any incoming due_date as `due_date.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z")` and then sets `"timeZone": "UTC"`. This takes the date components of `due_date` verbatim (in whatever zone the datetime carries — including a naive local datetime, the common case since core/rules.py and the CLI never construct aware UTC due_dates) and asserts they are UTC midnight. So a due_date that is, e.g., 2026-06-01 23:00 local in UTC-7 (which is 2026-06-02 06:00 UTC) is written as `2026-06-01T00:00:00Z` — wrong calendar date AND wrong time. strftime silently ignores tzinfo here, so even an aware non-UTC datetime is corrupted: the wall-clock fields are emitted with a forced Z. The Microsoft To Do reminder lands on the wrong day. No round-1 finding covers due_date formatting (round 1 only noted Gmail/IMAP/Mail.app star ignore due_date).

findings.location: _api_get/_api_post/_api_patch 213-232; no 429 Retry-After handling on mutation calls
findings.title: No 429/Retry-After backoff on apply_label/apply_category/star mutation calls — bulk tier-routing aborts each item on throttle with no retry
findings.description: Round 1 (#632) noted _api_get/_api_post/_api_patch have no 429 handling and raise_for_status turns throttling into a hard error. The NEW, more severe consequence specific to the mutation path: during a bulk tier-routing run (apply_label move + apply_category PATCH + star PATCH = 3 Graph writes per message), Graph aggressively throttles writes (429 with Retry-After). Each throttled write raises HTTPError, is caught by the per-method broad except, and returns False — so the message is silently skipped (move not done, category not applied) with no retry and no backoff, and the run continues hammering Graph (amplifying throttling). Unlike Gmail's _execute_with_backoff, Outlook has zero retry, so a throttled bulk run drops a large fraction of mutations silently. This is the write-path amplification angle distinct from round 1's general 429 note on reads.

findings.title: OData injection in folder lookup ($filter displayName eq '{part}')
findings.description: When folder creation fails, the fallback issues a Graph GET with params `{"$filter": f"displayName eq '{part}'"}` where `part` is a label path segment interpolated raw into the OData filter. A folder/label name containing a single quote (or OData operators) corrupts the filter or changes its meaning — at minimum a label with an apostrophe (e.g. "O'Brien") raises a 400 and the folder is silently lost; at worst the filter is broadened. Single quotes must be doubled per OData escaping rules.

findings.title: pyproject.toml has no [project] table / build backend / console_scripts — package is not installable
findings.description: pyproject.toml contains only [tool.pytest.ini_options] (testpaths, pythonpath). There is no [project] table, no [build-system] backend, no version, no dependencies, and no [project.scripts]/console_scripts. There is also no setup.py or setup.cfg (verified absent). Consequences: (1) `pip install .` / `pip install -e .` cannot build a distribution; (2) the documented invocations `python -m mcp_server` and `uvicorn api.app:app` only work from a checked-out working tree with cwd on sys.path (pytest adds '.' via pythonpath, but a pip install would not) — there is no packaged console entry point such as `universal-mail` or `universal-mail-mcp` despite mcp_server/server.py defining a main()/argparse CLI (prog='universal-mail-mcp', L155). For the Docker/Render deploy this is benign (it runs `uvicorn api.app:app` from /app with code COPYed in), but the project is not distributable/installable and the Python version floor (the documented 3.9 core vs 3.10 mcp split) is documented only in prose, never enforced via requires-python.

findings.title: server.json advertises a PyPI package universal-mail-automation 0.1.0 that the repo cannot build (pyproject.toml has no [project] metadata)
findings.description: server.json declares packages[0] = {registryType: pypi, identifier: 'universal-mail-automation', version: '0.1.0', transport stdio} and the agent.json/llms.txt advertise 'stdio: python -m mcp_server'. But pyproject.toml contains ONLY a [tool.pytest.ini_options] table — there is no [project] name/version, no build-system, no packaging metadata. The repo therefore cannot produce the PyPI distribution that the MCP registry manifest (server.json) promises, so any agent that resolves the package via pypi identifier 'universal-mail-automation' @ 0.1.0 will fail (no such published, buildable artifact from this source). This is a cross-file consistency bug between the advertised install surface and the actual packaging.

findings.location: lines 2-11 (all `>=` lower bounds, no upper caps); also requirements-api.txt:3-8 (fastapi>=0.110, uvicorn>=0.29, httpx>=0.27, pydantic>=2.0)
findings.title: Dependency floors are unpinned (>= only) with no lockfile — an upstream minor can break the build silently
findings.description: requirements.txt pins only lower bounds: google-api-python-client>=2.0.0, google-auth-httplib2>=0.1.0, google-auth-oauthlib>=0.5.0, msal>=1.25.0, requests>=2.28.0, pyyaml>=6.0. requirements-api.txt likewise: fastapi>=0.110, uvicorn[standard]>=0.29, httpx>=0.27, pydantic>=2.0. Only stripe (>=15.2,<16) and mcp (>=1.2,<2) have upper caps. There is NO lockfile/constraints file anywhere (verified: no poetry.lock, Pipfile.lock, uv.lock, constraints.txt). CI installs the latest satisfying version every run (`pip install -r ...`, and `cache: 'pip'` only caches the wheel download, not a resolved set). A pydantic, fastapi, starlette, or google-client minor/major bump can change behavior or break the build with zero warning, and because nothing is reproducible, a green build today can be a red build tomorrow with no code change. Recommend a compiled constraints/lock (pip-tools/uv) checked in, or at least upper caps on the framework deps (fastapi, pydantic, uvicorn) the way stripe/mcp already have.

findings.title: Domain match runs against full From string including display name — display-name spoofing routes legitimate mail
findings.description: `sender of msg` in Mail.app returns the full From header form 'Display Name <addr@domain>' (confirmed by providers/mailapp.py:150,160,190 which parses `sender of msg` as the whole From column). `theSender contains ("@" & dom)` and `theSender ends with dom` therefore match against the DISPLAY NAME too, not just the address. A sender with display name 'Your mailchimp.com rep' <person@bank.com> would match dom='mailchimp.com' via `contains` and be moved out of the inbox. The Python gate deliberately parses via parseaddr so 'the display NAME never feeds the gate' (core/rules.normalize_sender docstring line 746). The AppleScript performs no such address extraction, so display-name content can both cause false-positive moves of protected/important mail and be used to spoof routing. Not covered by round 1.

findings.location: module docstring lines 5-11 and main() lines 96-109 (writes pricing.md, llms.txt, .well-known/agent.json, server.json — but NOT acp/product_feed.json)
findings.title: Commerce-artifact generator does not regenerate acp/product_feed.json — the feed is on a second, separate generation path and can drift
findings.description: gen_commerce_artifacts.py claims to generate "the committed commerce / go-to-market artifacts from the canonical catalog" so "the artifacts never drift", and writes pricing.md, llms.txt, .well-known/agent.json, and server.json. But the committed ACP product feed (acp/product_feed.json) is NOT written here — it is regenerated by an entirely separate entrypoint, `python -m acp.feed` (acp/feed.py write_static_feed). A maintainer changing CREDIT_PACKS in api/plans.py and re-running the documented `python scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py` will refresh agent.json/server.json/pricing.md while leaving product_feed.json stale (wrong runs/price/title) until they separately remember to run the other script. This split-source setup defeats the stated single-source-no-drift goal and is the exact mechanism by which the feed's prices/titles can silently diverge from plans.py.

findings.title: Fake payment client ignores amount/currency/token/idempotency_key — charged amount never asserted
findings.description: _OKPay.charge accepts amount/currency/token/idempotency_key but returns a fixed ChargeResult without inspecting any of them. No test asserts that the router charges the correct grand_total (200 minor units for 2x pack_100) or passes idempotency_key='acp-charge:{session_id}'. The receipt summary asserts runs_credited==200 (the fulfillment count) but the charged AMOUNT on the money path is never verified. A bug that charges the wrong amount (e.g. per-run price vs pack total, or qty not multiplied) would pass every test here. This is a coverage gap on the literal money path.

findings.title: Test encodes the arbitrary-bearer-funding defect as intended behavior, locking in the broken identity model
findings.description: test_complete_success_credits_and_emits_signed_order_receipt asserts get_store().get_account_by_api_key('testkey')['run_credits'] == 200 where 'testkey' is the literal bearer from _headers(). The test thereby certifies that credits land on an account keyed by an arbitrary caller-supplied string with no issued/verified key — the very anti-pattern that makes cross-caller balance sharing and unredeemable credits possible. test_complete_is_idempotent (153) and test_complete_retry (162) reinforce it by manually create_account(api_key='testkey'). This is a test-quality finding: the suite green-lights the auth-bypass identity model, so the defect cannot be caught by regression and any fix will require rewriting these assertions. allow-secret

findings.location: whole file — no POST /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} update test (grep confirms only create/get/complete/cancel exercised)
findings.title: update_session (a price/items-mutating, money-relevant endpoint) has zero test coverage
findings.description: The ACP test suite exercises create, get, complete, cancel, idempotency, and the 402 path, but never POSTs to /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} to update items or buyer. update_session is the endpoint that re-derives line_items/total_runs and can re-price a session after it became READY, and it is the desync entry point (lines 217-225). It is shipped with no regression test for: re-pricing, the items=None branch's two-source read, the invalid-state guard (lines 211-214), or that grand_total stays consistent with total_runs after an update. A money-mutating path with no test is a quality gap that lets the desync regress silently.

findings.title: Success-charge fake ignores the amount argument, so no test asserts the charged amount equals grand_total / equals the credited runs' price
findings.description: _OKPay.charge (lines 26-27) returns ok regardless of amount, and the success test only asserts runs_credited==200 and run_credits==200 (lines 133, 138). No test captures or asserts the 'amount' that complete_session passes to charge() (router.py line 263), so nothing verifies that the money charged equals grand_total(line_items) NOR that it corresponds to total_runs at the correct pack price. The single most important money invariant of the whole ACP surface (charge amount == credited-runs price) is unasserted; the desync described in the residual-gap scope would pass the entire test suite green.

findings.title: Docstring claims 'real signature path' but every grant test monkeypatches construct_event away
findings.description: The module docstring asserts the webhook tests 'exercise the real signature path ... and the event-handling logic (via a monkeypatched construct_event)'. In practice only test_webhook_bad_signature_is_400 exercises real signature verification (it asserts 400). Every test that actually GRANTS access (subscription grant, checkout.session.completed, retry/dedup) monkeypatches stripe.Webhook.construct_event to return a hand-built event dict, so the genuine HMAC signature verification over the raw body is never exercised on any success path. A regression that silently accepts an unsigned/forged payload on the grant path (e.g. verifying re-serialized JSON, or skipping verification when the event type is handled) would not be caught by these tests. The mock replaces exactly the security primitive the webhook's fail-closed posture depends on.

findings.location: line 7 (import stripe) and line 14 (client = TestClient(app)); same module-level TestClient in test_api.py:10, test_acp.py:11, test_receipts.py:8, test_web.py:7
findings.title: Test modules construct TestClient(app) and import stripe at import time with no importorskip guard — pytest collection hard-crashes when optional API deps are absent
findings.description: Five API test modules build `client = TestClient(app)` at module scope (collection time), which constructs the full FastAPI app, and test_billing.py additionally does a bare top-level `import stripe`. None of them guard with `pytest.importorskip(...)` the way tests/test_mcp.py:11 guards `mcp`. If fastapi/httpx/stripe are not installed (verified absent in this environment), importing these modules raises ModuleNotFoundError during collection, which pytest reports as a collection ERROR and fails the whole run rather than skipping. The test suite is therefore not robust to a partial install (e.g. running core/provider tests without requirements-api.txt); only CI's full install masks it. Fix: gate each with `fastapi = pytest.importorskip('fastapi')` / `pytest.importorskip('stripe')` before constructing the client, matching test_mcp.py.

findings.title: Tests exercise consume_credit/add_credits in isolation, masking that no production path calls them
findings.description: tests/test_store.py asserts consume_credit and add_credits behave correctly at the Store level (39-43), and tests/test_acp.py asserts ACP fulfillment credits run_credits (133,153,173). These unit tests give green coverage to the metering primitives while there is zero integration test asserting that a triage request actually debits a credit or is rejected when over-cap (grep of tests/ for monthly_run_cap/entitlements_for/429/quota => exit 1, no hits). The test suite thus reinforces a false sense that metering works end-to-end; the gap (no caller debits) is invisible to the tests because the only callers are the tests themselves.

findings.title: Pricing displayed on the web app diverges from the source-of-truth plans.py ($19/mo Pro, $49/mo Business) — customers see wrong prices
findings.description: plans.py (the documented single source of truth) and cloudflare/worker.mjs both list Pro at $19/mo and Business at $49/mo. web/index.html hardcodes a DIFFERENT, much higher pricing ($29-$49 for pro, $149-$299 for business) with different names ('Inbox safety audit', 'Assisted cleanup'). Since plans.py docstring asserts 'A price change is one edit here — never a hunt across code, docs, and the website', this is exactly the drift it warns against: the website advertises prices that do not match the Stripe-backed catalog the API serves at /v1/billing/plans. A buyer can be quoted one price and charged another, or be confused at checkout. (Note: web/index.html is modified per git status, so this divergence is live.)

findings.location: static sample table line 522-525 (founder@client.example -> 'Stays') vs localSenderCheck lines 751-762
findings.title: Static sample-audit table disagrees with the live sender-check tool on the same demo senders
findings.description: The hard-coded sample inbox (added in the diff) shows founder@client.example as 'Stays' and admin@new-domain.example / billing@unknown-vendor.example as 'Needs review'. But the live 'Check a sender' tool's offline fallback (localSenderCheck) classifies founder@client.example as NOT protected ('Can move', line 753/818) because it matches none of the .gov/chase.com/1password.com patterns, and has no 'needs review' state at all. A visitor who types one of the sample addresses into the tool gets a verdict that contradicts the curated table sitting directly above it, undermining the demo's credibility on the exact trust claim it is selling.

findings.title: Smoke test runs immediately after wrangler deploy with no propagation wait — flaky failures
findings.description: The 'Smoke test live share demo' step curls the live custom domain `https://uma.4444j99.dev` immediately after the `cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4` deploy step in the same job. Worker/route propagation and edge cache invalidation are not instantaneous; `curl -fsS` with no retry/backoff (the step uses `set -euo pipefail`) will fail the job if the new deployment has not yet propagated to the edge node the runner hits, producing intermittent red CI on otherwise-good deploys. No `--retry`/sleep/poll loop is present.

findings.location: line 30 (single combined `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt`)
findings.title: The 3.9-isolation contract is undermined by always installing requirements-mcp.txt in CI
findings.description: requirements-mcp.txt is documented as 'ISOLATED... install ONLY where you run the MCP server' precisely because `mcp` needs >=3.10, the explicit purpose being to keep the core suite green on a 3.9 floor (also stated in tests/test_mcp.py:3-5 which uses importorskip('mcp')). But ci.yml line 30 always installs all three requirements files together on every matrix interpreter. This means: (a) if 3.9 were ever added to the matrix, the install step itself would fail at `mcp>=1.2,<2` resolution, defeating the importorskip safety net; and (b) the CI never validates the supposedly-supported 'core-only on 3.9 without mcp' install path that the architecture and README/Dockerfile all advertise. The isolation contract is asserted but never exercised. If 3.9 support is real, add a matrix leg that installs only requirements.txt (+ optionally -api) on 3.9 and asserts the core imports and test_mcp skips.

findings.location: feed (lines 67-69) -> build_feed (33-64); also router.py _links (125-130) and permalink_url (321)
findings.title: Host header reflected into absolute feed/checkout/seller URLs (Host-header injection / cache-poisoning of the agent discovery surface)
findings.description: The live feed endpoint builds every absolute URL from `request.base_url` (feed.py:69), which is derived from the client-supplied Host header. build_feed embeds this into `checkout_url`, `seller_url`, product `url`, and `image_url` (feed.py:47-62). An attacker who can set the Host header (or poison a shared cache) makes the feed advertise an attacker-controlled `checkout_url`, redirecting an ACP agent's purchase flow to a hostile endpoint. The same reflection occurs in router `_links` (terms/privacy URLs) and the order `permalink_url` (router.py:321), which an agent may follow to verify the receipt. Mitigation: derive base URLs from a configured, trusted public origin (env var) rather than the request Host, or validate Host against an allowlist.

findings.location: build_line_items lines 86-96 (base = int(pack['amount_cents']); subtotal = base * qty; total_runs += int(pack['runs']) * qty)
findings.title: Line-item subtotal and total_runs are unbounded int*qty products with no per-session amount cap before charging
findings.description: subtotal = base * qty and total_runs += runs * qty, with qty bounded only at <=1000 per Item and an unbounded number of distinct items in the list. A create/update body with many max-quantity line items produces a grand_total of arbitrary size that is passed straight to PaymentClient.charge(amount=...) and stored as the order amount. There is no sanity cap (e.g. max charge ceiling) on the aggregate money amount before the Stripe PaymentIntent is created. Stripe will reject amounts above its own per-charge maximum, but the failure surfaces as a generic 'charge failed' (payment.py:89-91) rather than a clean pre-charge invalid_items, and a malicious agent can force large charge attempts. The money amount on the charge path has no upper bound validation.

findings.location: grand_total lines 100-101 and build_line_items lines 86-94 (base_amount/subtotal/total all integer cents); build_totals 104-111
findings.title: grand_total sums line_items 'total' which equals subtotal (tax always 0) — totals math diverges from build_totals and bakes in a no-tax assumption on the charged amount
findings.description: Per-line `total` is set to `subtotal` (tax hard-coded 0) at models.py:92-94, and grand_total() sums those line totals. Separately build_totals() computes total = sub + tax. Today tax is always 0 so the two agree, but the amount actually CHARGED (complete_session uses grand_total of line_items, router.py:257) is computed from the line-item totals path, NOT from build_totals(). If tax is ever introduced in build_totals (the natural place — it returns a 'tax' row), the displayed session 'total' in `totals` would include tax while the charged grand_total(line_items) would not, charging the customer the pre-tax amount while showing a tax-inclusive total. Two independent money-summation paths on the same session is a latent off-by-tax defect on the charged amount.

findings.location: Item.quantity Field le=1000 line 27 + build_line_items line 96 (total_runs += runs*qty); plans.CREDIT_PACKS pack_1000 line 146-147
findings.title: Single ACP session can legitimately bill ~$9,000 / credit 1,000,000 runs with no high-value confirmation or per-session cap
findings.description: Item.quantity is bounded 1..1000 (models.py line 27) and CheckoutCreate.items is an unbounded List[Item] (line 43). A single valid item {id: pack_1000, quantity: 1000} yields total_runs = 1000 runs/pack * 1000 qty = 1,000,000 runs and amount = 900 cents * 1000 = 900,000 cents = $9,000 (build_line_items lines 85-96). Multiple distinct line items multiply this further (no cap on number of line items, no cap on grand_total). There is no high-value-purchase confirmation, no per-session monetary ceiling, and no anomaly guard before charge() at router.py line 262. A confused or prompt-injected agent (or a replayed/forged SPT) can be driven to a five-figure single charge that the fail-closed ethos elsewhere in the codebase would otherwise demand a guard for.

findings.location: build_line_items line 79 (valid = bool(items)) + router.py create line 176/188 (_persist with total_runs)
findings.title: Empty-items create yields valid=False but is still persisted; downstream relies solely on status guard, not on amount/runs being zero
findings.description: build_line_items sets valid=bool(items) (line 79), so an empty items list -> valid=False, total_runs=0, empty line_items. create_session persists this as STATUS_NOT_READY (router line 176) which /complete refuses (line 252). The protection is entirely the status string check; there is no defense-in-depth guard that amount>0 or total_runs>0 before charge. If any path ever flips a zero-runs/zero-amount session to READY (e.g. a future update bug, or the desync), charge(amount=0) is sent to Stripe and fulfill_once credits 0 runs while still minting an order receipt claiming a purchase. The amount>0 / total_runs>0 invariant is asserted nowhere on the charge/fulfill path.

findings.title: ACP charge swallows all exceptions into a generic 'charge failed', masking declines vs network vs config errors and any settlement ambiguity
findings.description: StripeSPTPaymentClient.charge wraps payment_intents.create in a bare except Exception that logs and returns ChargeResult(ok=False, error='charge failed'). This collapses card declines, network timeouts (where the charge MIGHT have actually been created/captured at Stripe), idempotency conflicts, and config errors into one opaque failure. A network timeout after Stripe created the PaymentIntent is the classic settle-without-confirm hazard: the client returns ok=False (so no credit/receipt), the session stays READY, the agent retries — and only the per-session idempotency key saves it from a double charge, while the first (possibly successful) intent is never reconciled. There is no distinction that would let the caller decide to verify the intent status out-of-band. Broad exception swallowing on the charge path hides the ambiguous-settlement case.

findings.location: 67-97 (no idempotent re-read of an existing intent)
findings.title: Charge retry under same idempotency_key returns Stripe's replayed intent but never reconciles a previously-succeeded-then-uncredited charge
findings.description: When /complete is retried after a crash between charge-success and fulfill, charge() is called again with the same idempotency_key acp-charge:S. Stripe replays the original PaymentIntent (status succeeded) — good, no double charge. But charge() has no path that distinguishes 'this is a replay of an already-succeeded charge' from a fresh success; it just returns ok=True with payment_id. That is fine in isolation, but combined with the router's fulfill_once being the ONLY guard, if the original crash happened AFTER fulfill_once committed but BEFORE save_receipt/_persist, the retry re-charges (replayed, ok) and fulfill_once returns False — so the retry produces a 200 with no order (see router 323-330). There is no Stripe-side reconciliation that the money already mapped to a credited fulfillment. This makes the 'crash-retry-safe' docstring claim (282-285) false for the post-fulfill/pre-receipt window.

findings.title: Re-completing an already-completed session via a fresh Idempotency-Key skips charge but the early-return path also stores the completed response under the new key without re-validating ownership
findings.description: When a session is already STATUS_COMPLETED, /complete returns `current` and calls `_complete_idempotency(ctx, current)` (lines 248-251). This stores the prior buyer's completed session (including their order/permalink/buyer email) under the NEW caller's idempotency key, and returns it to the new caller. Combined with the lack of api_key binding (see other findings), a second party calling /complete on a known session id with any bearer receives the first buyer's full completed session including PII and the order permalink. Even ignoring the disclosure, persisting another party's response under an unrelated idempotency key is semantically wrong. Bind completion responses to the originating account.

findings.title: On idempotent re-completion where fulfilled=False and a prior order exists, no order is re-attached but response still reports status completed — order may be dropped from the response
findings.description: In the post-charge fulfillment block, `order = current.get('order')` (line 293). If fulfill_once returns False (already fulfilled) AND current has no stored order yet (the elif at line 323, order is None), an info message is emitted but `order` stays None, so the final response (line 332-336) carries no order object even though credits were genuinely fulfilled earlier. This happens if the FIRST /complete crashed AFTER fulfill_once committed but BEFORE _persist saved the order into the session (lines 290 vs 318-337). A subsequent /complete then finds fulfilled=False (credit already applied) and no stored order, so it returns completed WITHOUT an order/permalink — the buyer's purchase has no retrievable order receipt despite being charged and credited. The order id minted on the first attempt is lost (it was random and never persisted). This is a durability gap in the order-receipt issuance for the crash-after-credit case.

findings.title: Bearer credential length is unbounded and unvalidated; arbitrary attacker-chosen strings become account api_keys
findings.description: _gate accepts any non-empty Bearer value as ctx.api_key with no length cap, charset check, or format validation (lines 79-83). complete_session then uses it directly as the api_key to look up or CREATE an account (router.py:287-289). An attacker can supply an arbitrarily long or structured bearer that is persisted into the accounts.api_key UNIQUE column (store.py:50,160-163). No bound is enforced (unlike billing models which cap field lengths). This enables unbounded-size inserts and account-namespace squatting (claiming api_keys that collide with the server's own uma_-prefixed format). Validate bearer format/length and never auto-create accounts from an unauthenticated bearer.

findings.title: ACP /complete auto-creates an account from the bearer token, so any unauthenticated bearer can mint an account and be credited paid runs after a (configured) charge
findings.description: After a successful payment.charge, store.get_account_by_api_key(ctx.api_key) is looked up and, if None, store.create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key) is called — i.e. the caller-supplied Bearer token becomes an API key for a brand-new account with no verification that the token is a real, issued uma_ key. The _gate only checks the header is non-empty 'Bearer <x>'. So credits are granted to an account keyed by an arbitrary attacker-chosen string. With the NullPaymentClient (default, unconfigured) result.ok is False so no fulfillment happens — but once Stripe SPT is configured, a charge that succeeds will credit runs to an account the caller fully controls the key for. That may be intended for agent flows, but there is no binding between the delegated payment token's payer and the api_key, so credits can be steered to any key. Worth confirming this is the intended trust model. allow-secret

findings.title: Failed-charge 402 response is cached under the Idempotency-Key, locking out retries with that key
findings.description: On a failed charge the handler builds a 402 response and calls _complete_idempotency(ctx, resp) at line 279, persisting that 402 body as the DONE idempotency result for ctx.idempotency_key. The inline comment says 'the session stays completable on retry,' which is true for the SESSION, but any retry that reuses the same Idempotency-Key will hit _begin_idempotency, see state 'replay', and get the cached 402 returned verbatim (with Idempotent-Replayed: true) — it will never re-attempt the charge even if the payment method is now good. The client must know to mint a fresh Idempotency-Key after a failure, which is non-obvious and contradicts the usual idempotency contract (same key -> retry the same operation). For a transient decline this means a paying agent is stuck at 402 until it changes the key. Borderline correctness/UX defect on the pay path; not money loss but a conversion-blocking footgun.

findings.title: Charge happens before account resolution; a post-charge failure path credits nothing but already captured funds
findings.description: complete_session charges the token (router.py:262-266) and only AFTER a successful charge resolves/creates the account and calls fulfill_once (287-290). If get_account_by_api_key or create_account raises (e.g. DB error, or a UNIQUE collision on api_key from a concurrent create), the exception propagates after money was already captured at Stripe, and fulfill_once never runs — the buyer is charged with no credits and no order receipt. The endpoint has no try/except around the post-charge fulfillment to compensate or to record the orphaned charge. Because the per-session charge key makes the charge idempotent, a retry would re-enter, re-confirm (dedup) and then fulfill — but only if the same Idempotency-Key is NOT reused (see the cached-response finding) and the account-create race resolves. The ordering (charge -> then persist fulfillment) leaves a window where settled money has no corresponding credit/receipt and no reconciliation record.

findings.title: Idempotency-Key is global (PRIMARY KEY on key alone), not scoped per api_key — cross-tenant key collision can leak another caller's response
findings.description: idempotency_keys.key is the sole PRIMARY KEY (store.py:73-80) and _begin_idempotency claims on (key, scope, request_hash) but the uniqueness is on key only. The Idempotency-Key is fully client-chosen and not namespaced by api_key/bearer. If two different callers (different bearer tokens) happen to use the same Idempotency-Key value with the same scope and an identical request body (request_hash matches), the second caller gets state='replay' and receives the FIRST caller's stored response_json — which for acp.complete includes the order, buyer, and permalink of someone else's purchase. Same-body collision across tenants is unlikely with random keys, but nothing prevents a caller from choosing a predictable key. Idempotency scope should include the authenticated principal. Cross-tenant response disclosure / mis-attribution risk.

findings.title: TOCTOU race between get_account_by_api_key and create_account can raise an unhandled IntegrityError (500)
findings.description: complete_session does account = store.get_account_by_api_key(ctx.api_key); if account is None: account = store.create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key, plan='free'). accounts.api_key has a UNIQUE constraint (store.py:50). The check and the insert are not in one transaction (each goes through its own lock acquisition), so two concurrent /complete calls with the same fresh bearer (e.g. a client retry with a new Idempotency-Key, or two agents sharing a key) can both see None and both attempt the INSERT; the second raises sqlite3.IntegrityError, which is not caught here and surfaces as an uncaught 500 rather than the spec ACPError envelope. create_account also does not use INSERT OR IGNORE / ON CONFLICT, so there is no recovery. Fix: catch IntegrityError and re-fetch, or use an upsert (get-or-create) primitive under a single lock. allow-secret

findings.title: Buyer is charged before the account is resolved/created and before fulfill_once — a non-fulfilling state path can charge money without minting a retrievable order
findings.description: The Stripe SPT charge (payment.get_payment_client().charge, lines 262-266) executes BEFORE get_account_by_api_key/create_account (287-289) and fulfill_once (290). If fulfill_once returns False (session already fulfilled by a prior call) the money path is fine because the charge is session-idempotent (key f'acp-charge:{session_id}'). However, the ordering means a real charge has already been attempted/settled against the SPT for a bearer the server never verified (finding 1) and an account it then auto-creates. If create_account were to fail (the TOCTOU IntegrityError in finding 3), the code raises a 500 AFTER the charge has succeeded at Stripe — the buyer is debited but complete_session returns an error and no order receipt is persisted (save_receipt at 313 is never reached). The charge-idempotency key protects a retry from double-charging, but the failed-path leaves a charged-but-unfulfilled-and-unrecorded state that depends on the client retrying with the same session. Consider resolving/creating the account and claiming fulfillment before (or transactionally with) the charge.

findings.title: Account auto-provisioned at /complete is keyed only by api_key with no email/customer linkage, so ACP-purchased credits are orphaned from the Stripe-billing identity
findings.description: create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key, plan='free') creates a brand-new account with no email and no stripe_customer_id. The subscription/billing webhook path (api/billing.py _resolve_account) keys accounts by client_reference_id / stripe_customer_id, never by api_key. So a buyer who already has a billing account (created via /v1/billing/checkout) and then performs an ACP purchase under a bearer the server has never seen will get a SECOND, disjoint account holding the run_credits, with no reconciliation between the two identity spaces. entitlements_for (api/plans.py:175-193) reports run_credits from whichever account is looked up, so the credits can be effectively invisible to the buyer's real account. This is a direct consequence of using an unvalidated bearer as the sole identity key. allow-secret

findings.location: complete_session lines 257, 262-266, 304-310 (amount = grand_total; receipt summary 'amount': amount + 'currency': CURRENCY)
findings.title: Charged amount is taken from the stored session line_items, which an update_session call can re-price between create and complete with no re-binding to the original quoted price
findings.description: complete_session computes `amount = models.grand_total(current['line_items'])` from the stored session row. update_session (lines 217-230) lets a caller resubmit `items` and re-derive line_items/total_runs, persisting a new amount onto the same session id, as long as status is still READY. The Stripe charge idempotency key is `acp-charge:{session_id}` (line 265), so the FIRST charge for that session id wins at Stripe regardless of the current stored amount — but the order receipt summary and the credited total_runs are taken from the CURRENT (possibly re-priced) session, not the amount Stripe actually settled. An agent that creates at pack_100 ($1.00), then updates to pack_1000 before /complete, gets the receipt/credits for whichever line_items are stored at completion time while Stripe may dedup to a different earlier amount — the persisted/displayed amount and credited runs can diverge from the money actually captured.

findings.location: complete_session lines 257-266 (amount = grand_total(...); charge(amount=amount)) — no amount > 0 guard before charging
findings.title: No positive-amount guard before charging: a READY session whose line_items sum to 0 charges/fulfills $0 (deeper instance — a future zero-priced or mis-edited pack)
findings.description: amount = models.grand_total(current['line_items']) is charged with no check that amount > 0. A session reaches READY only when build_line_items returns valid=True, which is true whenever every item names a known pack (models.py:79-84) — it never re-validates that the resulting amount is positive. CREDIT_PACKS currently has positive amount_cents, but a pack edited/added with amount_cents=0 (or a quantity*0 edge) yields a READY session with grand_total 0. StripeSPTPaymentClient would send amount=int(0) (Stripe rejects, surfacing as generic 'charge failed'), but the injected/Null test clients and any non-Stripe processor would 'succeed', fulfilling total_runs credits for a $0 charge and minting a signed receipt for amount=0. The money path should reject amount <= 0 before charging rather than relying on the catalog never holding a zero price. (Round 1 noted 'no amount<=0 guard' generically at acp/payment.py charge and router complete_session 257-266; this names the concrete catalog-edit trigger and the test-client fulfillment-for-$0 consequence.)

findings.title: Payment-failed re-persist drops account_id binding via INSERT OR REPLACE
findings.description: On the payment-failed branch (273-280) _persist(session_id, resp, total_runs) is called WITHOUT account_id, so save_session (store.py 407 INSERT OR REPLACE) writes account_id=NULL onto the acp_sessions row. The success path (337) writes account_id=account['id']. Because save_session is a full-row replace, any prior account_id binding on the row is silently nulled whenever a later /complete attempt fails payment, or whenever update/cancel re-persist (which also omit account_id). The acp_sessions.account_id column — the only durable link from a session to the account it was fulfilled to — therefore becomes unreliable for reconciliation/audit. Round 1 noted last-write-wins resurrecting READY over COMPLETED but not the account_id un-binding caused by the partial _persist signature.

findings.title: Stripe charge idempotency keyed on session_id, but fulfill dedup also keyed on session_id — an update that re-prices then re-completes cannot re-charge the delta
findings.description: The charge idempotency key is `acp-charge:{session_id}` (265) and fulfill_once dedups on session_id (290). Consider: session created with pack A (amount X), /complete charges X via key acp-charge:S and credits runs_A. If a buyer instead first /completes is blocked because update only allowed pre-READY... but the inverse hole exists: if a /complete fails at fulfill (returns False on a *partial earlier* fulfillment for the same session under a different account), Stripe still has charged once for session S. More concretely, because BOTH the charge dedup and the fulfill dedup are keyed solely on session_id with no amount component, a session whose line_items/total were mutated by update_session between two /complete attempts will, on the second attempt, reuse the SAME Stripe idempotency key acp-charge:S — so Stripe replays the FIRST charge amount, not the new amount, while fulfill_once (already fulfilled) credits nothing new. The charged amount and the credited runs can therefore reflect different snapshots of the session with no error surfaced. Round 1 flagged update-before-complete repricing generally; this is the specific charge-key-collision-across-amounts consequence.

findings.title: Re-completing a COMPLETED session under a NEW Idempotency-Key returns the stored completed response but never charges — yet a session marked completed with fulfilled=False (credit-applied-but-not-persisted) is indistinguishable
findings.description: The early-return at 248-251 trusts current['status']==COMPLETED to mean 'fully fulfilled, do nothing'. But the COMPLETED status is only persisted at 337, AFTER fulfill_once (290) and the receipt mint (312-317). If a crash occurs after fulfill_once but before the final _persist, the session row is still STATUS_READY (the last persisted state was create/update), so the early-return does NOT trigger — instead a retry re-enters the charge path. Conversely there is no state that records 'credit applied, completion not finalized', so the system cannot tell a genuinely-completed session from a partially-completed one purely by status. The status field is set last in a multi-write sequence with no transaction, so it is not a reliable completion sentinel. Round 1 covered the already-completed early-return shaping; this is the NEW observation that status is the final write in a non-atomic sequence and therefore an unsound completion marker.

findings.title: create_account at /complete uses account['id'] without handling a concurrent-insert IntegrityError or None return
findings.description: If get_account_by_api_key returns None, create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key) is called. accounts.api_key is UNIQUE, so two concurrent /complete calls with the same fresh bearer race: the loser's INSERT raises sqlite3.IntegrityError, which is unhandled and surfaces as a 500 (and, because it fires after payment.charge succeeded at line 262, money may already be captured while the completion 500s). Round 1 noted a generic TOCTOU at 287-289; the NEW specific is that create_account performs NO duplicate-handling (store.py 158-164 has a bare INSERT with no OR IGNORE / retry), so the second concurrent buyer's charged-but-failed completion is a concrete, reproducible failure mode. account['id'] is then dereferenced at 290/337 assuming a dict. allow-secret

findings.location: complete_session line 257 vs acp/models.py build_totals line 104-111 / build_session line 138
findings.title: Buyer-displayed totals.total and the amount actually charged are derived from two different code paths and never compared
findings.description: The session object the buyer/agent inspects exposes a human-facing 'totals' array whose total is build_totals(line_items) -> sum(subtotal)+sum(tax) (models.py lines 104-111). But /complete charges models.grand_total(current['line_items']) = sum(li['total']) (router.py line 257). These are two distinct summations over the same line_items computed at different times and places. Today they coincide because tax is always 0 and total==subtotal, but there is no assertion that the figure the buyer agreed to (totals.total) equals the figure charged (grand_total). A future tax/discount line, or any line_item whose 'total' field diverges from subtotal+tax, makes the displayed price and the charged price silently differ — the agent consents to one number and is billed another. The charge should be reconciled against (or derived from) the same totals the session advertises.

findings.location: update_session lines 217-225 (else branch keeps current['line_items'] but pulls total_runs from row['data']['total_runs'])
findings.title: update_session without items reads line_items and total_runs from two different stored locations, then re-shapes — desync entry point
findings.description: When body.items is None, update_session sets line_items = current['line_items'] (from data['response']) but total_runs = row['data']['total_runs'] (from the sibling top-level field) — two separately-persisted sources (lines 223-224). It then re-shapes and _persist's both. This is the concrete code path that can desync the charge basis from the credited runs: the response's line_items and the stored total_runs are never validated to still agree, and re-shaping recomputes totals from line_items while total_runs is carried verbatim. Combined with the missing reconciliation at /complete, an update that mutates only the buyer (no items) re-persists a response whose grand_total and total_runs are only as consistent as they happened to be at the last items-bearing write — there is no re-derivation against CREDIT_PACKS to re-establish the invariant.

findings.location: complete_session line 290 fulfill_once + lines 295-317 order minting (order_id minted only when fulfilled=True, but charge already succeeded)
findings.title: Charge succeeds then fulfill_once True but save_receipt/save_session can raise, leaving funds captured with no retry path that re-mints the order
findings.description: After a successful charge (line 267 passes), fulfill_once credits runs and returns True (line 290). The code then signs and save_receipt (lines 312-317) and finally _persist (line 337). If save_receipt or _persist raises (e.g. UNIQUE/DB error, the disk-full or locked-DB conditions round 1 noted for the single-connection store), the request 500s AFTER the charge captured money AND after fulfill_once already marked the session fulfilled. A retry now finds fulfilled=False (acp_fulfillments row exists) and takes the 'already_fulfilled' branch (lines 323-330) returning completed with NO order/receipt — the verifiable order receipt (the product's headline trust artifact) is permanently lost for a paid, credited purchase, with no path to re-mint it. Distinct from round-1 'charge succeeds while persist throws leaves session READY' because here fulfillment DID persist; it's the receipt/order that is irrecoverably dropped.

findings.location: lines 38-45 (try/except around `from mcp_server.server import http_app, mcp`)
findings.title: MCP import failure (including a missing/renamed `mcp` internal symbol) silently downgrades a paid `/mcp` endpoint to 404 at INFO log level
findings.description: The MCP app is imported under `except Exception` and on ANY failure sets `_MCP_AVAILABLE=False`, logging only at `logger.info`. mcp_server/server.py:32 imports a narrow internal path `from mcp.server.streamable_http import TransportSecuritySettings`. The requirements floor `mcp>=1.2,<2` spans many 1.x minors; this internal class/module path is not part of a stability contract and can move/rename across 1.x releases. If it does — or if `mcp` is simply not installed (e.g. a 3.9 host, or a deploy that forgot requirements-mcp.txt) — the broad except swallows the ImportError and the `/mcp` endpoint that the README advertises as a product surface silently vanishes (mount at line 143 is skipped). Because it is only an INFO log and the app still boots healthy (200 on /health), the outage is invisible to the Render healthcheck and to operators. A paid/advertised endpoint disappearing with no error signal is a silent-failure dependency hazard.

findings.title: persist() always called with account_id=None on the public triage path, so every triage receipt is unattributed and list_receipts can never return them
findings.description: _run mints run_id and calls receipts.persist(run_id, result) with no account_id (defaults None). save_receipt stores account_id=NULL. list_receipts(account_id) filters WHERE account_id = ?, so a paying customer's hosted-receipt-history feature (sold on Pro/Business per plans.py) can never surface any triage receipt — all are orphaned. The retained-receipt-days entitlement is therefore unfulfillable through this path. Distinct from round1's general 'entitlements unenforced' findings: this is the receipt-attribution gap specifically.

findings.title: Webhook references stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError without guaranteeing the attribute exists across Stripe SDK major versions
findings.description: The except clause catches (ValueError, stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError). In recent stripe-python (v8+/v12+) the error module path and import surface changed; stripe.error may not be the canonical location (it can be stripe.SignatureVerificationError). If the installed SDK does not expose stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError, evaluating the except tuple raises AttributeError at exception-handling time, which would escape as an unhandled 500 and, worse, could mask a signature failure as a server error rather than the intended 400 'invalid signature'. Pin/verify the exception import against the SDK version in requirements.

findings.title: Event marked processed only after success, but a handler that raises 500 leaves the read-gate open while a redelivery may already be mid-flight
findings.description: When _handle_event raises, the code re-raises HTTP 500 (line 200-202) and never marks the event processed — correct for retry. But because the dedup is a separate read (is_event_processed) rather than the atomic mark return, the window between the failed attempt and Stripe's redelivery can overlap with a concurrent in-flight handler that has NOT yet marked. Combined with the TOCTOU above, a partially-applied handler (e.g. set_subscription for checkout.completed succeeded, then a later step threw) can be replayed and re-apply state. set_subscription is mostly idempotent, but _resolve_account's create_account branch is not (new account each time it cannot resolve). Recommend making the whole handle+mark a single atomic unit.

findings.title: status from obj.get('status') may be None and is written verbatim; entitlements then treat unknown/None status oddly
findings.description: For customer.subscription.created/updated, status = obj.get('status'). If Stripe omits status (malformed/partial event, or a future API shape), status is None and is passed to store.set_subscription(status=None). set_subscription only writes non-None fields, so status is left unchanged — but plan_id may already have been overwritten with the price-derived plan. Result: plan upgraded to 'pro' while status retained an old value, or plan written without a matching status. Worse, on a real cancel/unpaid where status is one of the canceled set, plan is forced to 'free' (good), but for any other non-active status (e.g. 'past_due', 'incomplete') the paid plan_id is still written and entitlements_for only downgrades based on status being non-(active|trialing). If status was left stale-active by a prior event, the account keeps paid limits despite a failed payment. The plan/status pair should be written atomically and consistently.

findings.location: _handle_event() lines 248-262, plan_id_for_price + 'unknown price -> keep current plan'
findings.title: Unknown Stripe price maps to plan_id=None, which is then written as the account plan, demoting a paying customer
findings.description: price_id = _first_price_id(obj); plan_id = plans.plan_id_for_price(price_id). plan_id_for_price returns None when the price matches no env-configured plan (e.g. STRIPE_PRICE_PRO not set on this host, or a price created in the dashboard but not wired into env). The comment at line 249 says 'unknown price -> keep current plan', but the code does NOT keep the current plan: unless status is in the canceled set, plan_id stays None and is passed to set_subscription(plan=None). set_subscription skips None fields, so it happens to keep the current plan ONLY by accident of that None-skip. However if status IS canceled/unpaid, plan_id is set to 'free' — fine. The real bug: if env price vars are unset (common in the 'billing not fully configured' state the module advertises) every subscription.updated resolves plan_id=None and the account plan is never actually advanced past whatever checkout.session.completed wrote — and checkout._checkout_plan_id also falls back to plan_id_for_price(None)=None when metadata plan is absent, so a real purchase can land with plan=None written to the row.

findings.title: invoice.paid forces status='active' but never refreshes plan, so a downgraded plan stays downgraded while invoice.paid re-activates
findings.description: invoice.paid sets status='active' for the customer's account but passes no plan. If a prior subscription.updated had (incorrectly per other findings) written plan=None or 'free', invoice.paid re-activates the account at the wrong plan tier. Conversely, invoice.paid blindly setting active could re-activate an account that subscription.deleted just set to free/canceled if events arrive out of order (Stripe does NOT guarantee event ordering). Out-of-order invoice.paid after a cancellation re-grants active. Subscription status should be sourced from the subscription object, not toggled by invoice events without re-reading current subscription state.

findings.title: current_period_end stored as raw Stripe epoch int with no timezone/units validation; entitlement code never enforces it
findings.description: current_period_end is captured as an int epoch (Stripe sends UTC seconds). It is stored but plans.entitlements_for never consults current_period_end — so an account whose period has lapsed but whose status row still says 'active' (e.g. because subscription.deleted was missed/raced) retains paid entitlements indefinitely. The field is recorded but is dead for access control, defeating its purpose as a settlement boundary.

findings.title: Webhook _resolve_account silently creates a brand-new free account when metadata account_id does not exist
findings.description: _resolve_account: if account_id is provided but store.get_account(account_id) returns None (e.g. the account was deleted, or the metadata carries a stale/foreign id), and no customer mapping exists, it falls through to store.create_account(account_id=account_id, plan='free') and grants against that fresh account. A checkout.session.completed or subscription.updated whose metadata account_id points at a non-existent account will therefore mint a NEW account and apply the subscription to it, rather than failing/alerting. The paid grant lands on an account the customer cannot reach (they have no api_key/email tied to it from their side), effectively losing the entitlement the customer paid for, and creating orphan accounts. Should at least reconcile via customer_id first or refuse to fabricate an account from attacker/forge-able metadata. Note metadata is set by this server at checkout, so forgery requires webhook-signature bypass, but stale-id and deleted-account cases are real.

findings.title: Real subscription on an unconfigured/unmatched price silently leaves the plan unchanged with no log
findings.description: For a genuine subscription whose price id is not in the env-configured mapping (price not set on this host, a newly added price, or a multi-item subscription whose items[0] is not the plan price), _first_price_id returns a real id but plans.plan_id_for_price (plans.py:164-172) returns None. plan_id is then None, set_subscription only writes non-None fields (store.py:213), so the plan column is left at its previous value. A legitimate upgrade/downgrade therefore silently no-ops the plan while still writing status='active'. There is no warning logged for the 'matched a price we don't recognize' case, so a price-id misconfiguration produces wrong entitlements with zero signal. The broad except (KeyError,IndexError,TypeError) in _first_price_id additionally cannot distinguish 'subscription genuinely has no item' from 'unexpected payload shape' — both become None.

findings.title: Stripe status written verbatim with no validation; non-active states (incomplete/paused) keep the paid plan stored
findings.description: status = obj.get('status') is written straight into accounts.status with no enum check, and the drop-to-free guard at line 253 only covers ('canceled','unpaid','incomplete_expired'). A subscription that is 'incomplete' (payment not yet succeeded), 'paused', or any future/unknown Stripe status keeps the matched paid plan_id (e.g. 'pro') in the plan column while status reflects the non-paying state. entitlements_for (plans.py:184-186) currently treats anything outside {active,trialing} as the Free floor, so live entitlement is safe TODAY — but the stored plan column is now inconsistent (says 'pro' for a never-paid subscription). Any future consumer that reads account['plan'] directly instead of going through entitlements_for would grant paid features to an unpaid/incomplete subscription. The data should be validated/normalized rather than trusted verbatim.

findings.location: _handle_event invoice.paid 265-270 and subscription events 240-263; no event ordering guard
findings.title: Out-of-order or replayed events (invoice.paid after subscription.deleted) can re-activate a canceled account
findings.description: Stripe delivers webhooks with at-least-once, NOT in-order semantics. Idempotency is enforced only per event id (store.is_event_processed), not per subscription state/timestamp. An invoice.paid (lines 265-269 sets status='active' unconditionally for any account matching the customer) or an older customer.subscription.updated (status active) that arrives or is redelivered AFTER a customer.subscription.deleted will flip a correctly-canceled account back to status='active'. For invoice.paid the plan is left untouched (so plan='free' + status='active' — internally inconsistent but currently benign via entitlements), but for a reordered subscription.updated the account regains plan='pro'/status='active' after cancellation. There is no comparison against current_period_end or an event/created timestamp to reject stale transitions.

findings.title: checkout.session.completed reads obj.get('subscription') which is None for one-time/payment-mode or async-payment sessions, writing subscription_id=NULL while granting active
findings.description: _handle_event for checkout.session.completed pulls subscription_id = obj.get('subscription') (228) and passes it to set_subscription. For a checkout.session.completed where payment is async (payment_status='unpaid', e.g. delayed payment methods) or where mode isn't a fully-provisioned subscription yet, obj['subscription'] can be absent/None — set_subscription then skips the subscription_id write (store.py 213) but STILL writes status='active' (236, hard-coded). The account is granted active access with no stripe_subscription_id recorded, so a later customer.subscription.deleted (matched by subscription_id) or portal action cannot reliably tie back. Round 1 flagged the hard-coded active without payment_status verification; the NEW angle is the silently-dropped subscription_id leaving an active grant with no subscription linkage for later revocation.

findings.title: On cancel/unpaid the handler sets plan='free' but does NOT clear stripe_subscription_id/customer_id, and status may still be a verbatim non-canceled Stripe value
findings.description: For customer.subscription.updated where Stripe status is e.g. 'incomplete' or 'paused' (not in the canceled/unpaid/incomplete_expired set at 253), plan_id is NOT forced to free, status is written verbatim (260), and the paid plan stays. The branch only floors to free for three exact statuses. Statuses like 'paused' or 'incomplete' (which mean access should not be granted) leave the paid plan stored with a non-active status. While round 1 noted 'non-active states keep paid plan stored', the NEW specific gap is that the deleted/canceled path floors plan to free but never nulls stripe_subscription_id, so get_account_by_customer/subscription lookups still resolve a 'free' account to a live (now-canceled) subscription id — a stale linkage that a subsequent invoice.paid (line 269, matched by customer) would re-activate to status='active' on the free plan.

findings.title: A handler that partially succeeds then raises leaves a partial DB mutation but the event un-marked, so the full retry re-applies the already-applied partial writes
findings.description: _handle_event performs set_subscription writes (each its own committed statement, store.py autocommit-per-statement) and can raise mid-sequence (e.g. the checkout branch resolves+creates an account, set_subscription commits, then a later line raises). The webhook catches, 500s, and does NOT mark_event_processed (203 skipped). Stripe redelivers; on retry is_event_processed is still False, so _handle_event runs the WHOLE branch again — re-resolving/re-creating accounts and re-writing subscription state that the first attempt already committed. Because set_subscription is idempotent-ish but _resolve_account can mint a second orphan account if the first attempt's create committed but a later step's failure left customer linkage unset, retries can fan out duplicate accounts. Round 1 flagged the TOCTOU on the dedup read and orphan-account creation separately; this is the NEW partial-commit-then-full-replay interaction across the multi-statement handler.

findings.title: invoice.* events with no resolvable customer silently no-op, so a paid invoice for an account whose customer link was never written never activates
findings.description: Both invoice.paid (266-270) and invoice.payment_failed (272-277) look up the account ONLY via get_account_by_customer(customer_id) and do nothing if customer_id is missing or unmatched. Unlike the subscription branch, there is no metadata.account_id fallback and no _resolve_account. If an account was created via checkout.session.completed but the stripe_customer_id write was skipped (e.g. obj['customer'] was None on that earlier event, see the dropped-subscription_id finding), then a subsequent invoice.paid cannot find the account and silently returns — the customer pays but is never set to active. A money-in event with no state effect and no log. Round 1 covered invoice.paid resurrecting canceled subs and the customer-as-dict type confusion, but not the silent no-op when the customer link is absent.

findings.title: Subscription events read metadata.account_id off the subscription object, but checkout-set metadata lives on subscription_data — orphan-account creation on real flows
findings.description: For customer.subscription.created/updated/deleted the handler reads meta_account = (obj.get('metadata') or {}).get('account_id'). The checkout flow sets account_id metadata under subscription_data.metadata (create_checkout line 133), which Stripe DOES propagate to the subscription's metadata — but only for subscriptions created via that checkout. A subscription created/edited directly in the Stripe dashboard or via the customer portal carries no account_id metadata, so meta_account is None and _resolve_account falls through to creating a brand-new free orphan account (line 218) unless the customer_id already maps. Combined with the round-1 orphan-account findings, the NEW point is the specific reliance on metadata being present on every subscription event, which is not guaranteed for portal/dashboard-originated changes — a common real path.

findings.title: subscription.updated/created can move a Stripe customer_id onto a different account, and resolution prefers metadata account_id over the existing customer mapping
findings.description: _resolve_account prefers the (attacker-influenceable) metadata account_id over the stripe_customer_id mapping. If a subscription's metadata.account_id points at account A but the customer_id is already linked to account B, set_subscription writes customer_id=B's-customer onto account A (and the schema's UNIQUE(stripe_customer_id) then makes a later write to B fail). This is a NEW angle: round1 flagged the orphan-create and the UNIQUE-constraint 500 separately, but not that the metadata-over-customer precedence lets one Stripe customer's subscription state be redirected onto a different local account whenever metadata and the existing customer link disagree.

findings.title: Expanded Stripe objects: obj.get('customer'/'subscription') may be a dict (when expanded), used as a string id in get_account_by_customer/set_subscription
findings.description: NEW concrete confirmation of round1's note (line 126): when a webhook's API version or event config returns expanded objects, obj['customer'] is a full customer dict, not an id string. get_account_by_customer then runs `WHERE stripe_customer_id = ?` with a dict param (sqlite raises 'unsupported type'), 500-ing the handler and wedging the event into Stripe's retry loop; or for set_subscription, a dict is stored into a TEXT column. The handlers never coerce obj.get('customer') via `.get('id') if isinstance(...,dict)`.

findings.location: cross-ref store.run_credits (ledger) vs entitlements_for line 192 — credits exposed but triage never debits
findings.title: entitlements_for returns run_credits (line 192) but no triage path reads or debits it; the number is reported to clients as a balance that never decreases
findings.description: entitlements_for surfaces run_credits to any consumer (plans.py line 192), presenting it as the account's purchasable-run balance. But the triage execution path (service.run_triage / app._run) never calls consume_credit and never reads run_credits (round-1 established consume_credit is dead). The NEW angle: entitlements_for actively advertises this balance as a meaningful entitlement field, so a client/agent reasonably treats it as spendable and depleting, while it only ever grows. This is a correctness contract violation on the reported entitlement, not merely dead code — the API tells the buyer they have N runs that are in fact unmetered/unlimited and the N is decorative.

findings.title: entitlements_for downgrades to free only on non-(active|trialing) status but never checks current_period_end, and defaults missing status to 'active' — an account row with no/blank status keeps paid plan
findings.description: status = (account.get('status') or 'active').lower(). If an account's status is None/empty (e.g. created via create_account default 'active', or never updated), it is treated as active and the account's plan (which could be 'pro'/'business' from a stale or mis-applied webhook) yields full paid entitlements. Combined with the webhook findings (plan can be written without a consistent status, and current_period_end is ignored), an account can hold paid limits without an active paid subscription. The fail-safe intent ('unpaid never honored') is undercut by defaulting blank status to active.

findings.title: Account with NULL/unknown subscription status defaults to 'active', so an incomplete/unset-status subscription can be honored as paid
findings.description: entitlements_for treats a missing status as active: `status = (account.get('status') or 'active').lower()` (plans.py:184). In billing._handle_event, customer.subscription.created/updated writes `status = obj.get('status')` (billing.py:248) which may be None or a non-terminal Stripe status like 'incomplete'/'incomplete_expired'; set_subscription only writes non-None values (store.py:213), so a None status leaves the column at its default 'active' (store.py:48). Net effect: a subscription that was created but whose payment never completed (status incomplete/None) can resolve to plan limits above the Free floor because the status fell back to 'active'. The downgrade list at billing.py:253 covers 'incomplete_expired' but not a None/absent status reaching plans.py with the 'active' default. Treat unknown/None status as NOT active (fail-closed to Free).

findings.location: METERED_ADDON lines 130-139 (unit_amount_cents=1, '$0.01 / triage run') vs CREDIT_PACKS lines 144-147
findings.title: Metered price ($0.01/run) and credit-pack prices are inconsistent: pack_1000 charges $9.00 for 1000 runs ($0.009/run) but metered advertises $0.01/run — overlapping price surfaces disagree
findings.description: The catalog advertises two simultaneously-available pay-per-run mechanisms for the same product (the blurb at plans.py:137-138 explicitly says metered covers 'ACP credit-pack purchases'). Metered unit is $0.01/run (unit_amount_cents=1). pack_100 = 100 cents / 100 runs = $0.01/run (consistent). pack_1000 = 900 cents / 1000 runs = $0.009/run (a 10% volume discount), which is fine as a discount but means the two advertised price surfaces (metered vs pack_1000) quote different effective per-run prices for the same triage run with no stated relationship. More importantly, NO Stripe Meter event is ever emitted for metered usage (no consume/meter call anywhere on the triage path), so the $0.01/run metered price is advertised and listed in GET /v1/billing/plans but is unbilled — a price that is published but never charged.

findings.title: Ephemeral signing key fallback means receipts signed before RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is set verify as valid against a different (random) key, and silently become unverifiable after restart — a tamper-evidence gap
findings.description: When RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is unset, a per-process random key is generated and used to sign AND verify. This makes verify() return True in-process even though no durable key exists, giving a false sense of tamper-evidence: a client cannot independently verify (the key never leaves the process and changes every restart), yet the server's own verify() passes. For the headline 'independent, third-party-verifiable receipt' product claim, signing with an ephemeral key in production (if the env var is accidentally unset) produces receipts that are NOT independently verifiable, while still being emitted as if they were.
