There is no hard failure in non-dev; only a logged warning. A money/trust artifact should fail closed (refuse to sign) when no durable key is configured in production.

findings.title: Ephemeral per-process signing key makes receipts unverifiable after restart and differ across replicas
findings.description: If RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is unset, sign() uses a per-process random key (secrets.token_bytes(32)) generated once per process. The code warns, but the consequence on the money/trust path is concrete: (1) receipts signed before a restart fail verify() after restart (the headline 'independently verifiable receipt' silently becomes unverifiable); (2) in any horizontally-scaled deploy (the MCP server explicitly advertises stateless_http for horizontal scaling), each replica has a DIFFERENT ephemeral key, so a receipt signed by replica A cannot be verified by replica B's /v1/audit endpoint, and the exported verifier will reject genuine receipts. For ACP order receipts this means a purchased order's signature may not verify depending on which process serves the GET. This is a fail-open-ish trust gap when the env key is forgotten in production. Consider refusing to sign (or loudly degrading) rather than fabricating an unstable key.

findings.title: Published verification recipe omits ensure_ascii, breaking independent verification of receipts containing non-ASCII (e.g. the audit's ⚠ violation marker, unicode receipt_line)
findings.description: `_canonical` uses `json.dumps(body, sort_keys=True, separators=(',',':'))` — Python's default `ensure_ascii=True` escapes non-ASCII to \uXXXX. The GET endpoint publishes the verification recipe as 'HMAC-SHA256 over JSON.dumps(signed_body, sort_keys=True, separators=(',',':')) with the server's RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY' — it does NOT mention ensure_ascii. An independent verifier in another language (or a Python verifier that serializes without ASCII-escaping) reconstructs different canonical bytes whenever the body contains non-ASCII and gets a verification FAILURE on a genuine receipt. This is reachable: `core/audit.py` `receipt_line()` appends the literal '⚠' character on any gate violation (line 272), and the signed body's `receipt_line` / `summary` can carry unicode. The whole stated point of exporting `verify` is standalone/open-source verification; the recipe as documented is incomplete and will produce false 'tampered' verdicts on legitimate receipts. (Note the inconsistency with core/audit.py line 225 which deliberately uses ensure_ascii=False.)

findings.title: Receipt persistence is fully best-effort: a ledger write failure means the run returns 200 with a run_id whose GET will 404, with no signal to the client
findings.description: `persist` swallows any store exception (`except Exception ... logger.warning`) and still returns the signature. In app.py `_run` (124-129) the run_id is set on the result and returned to the client regardless of whether `save_receipt` actually committed. So on a transient DB error the API returns 200 with `run_id`, the client records it as their verifiable receipt id, and a later `GET /v1/audit/{run_id}` returns 404 'receipt not found' — the advertised independent-verification path is silently broken for that run with no error surfaced at triage time. The design comment justifies this as 'ledger is a convenience, not the safety boundary', but for a product whose pitch is the verifiable receipt, a silently-missing receipt is a degraded-trust outcome the client is never told about.

findings.title: Two independent receipt signing paths can diverge: ACP order receipt body shape (router 297-311) is hand-built and not produced by signed_body(), risking signature/verify mismatch if signed_body() ever changes
findings.description: receipts.signed_body() (77-85) defines the canonical receipt body shape used by the triage path and is the shape the GET /v1/audit verifier reconstructs from DB columns (run_id, provider, dry_run, summary, receipt_line). The ACP path builds its OWN dict literal (router 297-311) with the same keys today, signs it, and stores summary=order_receipt_body['summary']. These are two parallel definitions of the signed body. If signed_body() is updated (e.g. to add a field or reorder/normalize), the ACP-minted receipts will be signed over a different body than the verifier reconstructs, and every ACP order receipt will fail verification — silently, since nothing tests cross-path body equivalence. The money/trust artifact for agent purchases is signed by a forked, drift-prone code path. Round 1 noted receipts sign the self-reported summary; this is the distinct two-implementations-of-the-signed-body divergence risk.

findings.title: Idempotency claim has a check-then-insert TOCTOU race under the RLock-but-multi-statement design only protects within a process; cross-process it is unsafe, and even in-process the 'new' path is fine but stale-reclaim rebinds without verifying status
findings.description: idempotency_begin does SELECT (nolock helper) then conditionally INSERT, all under self._lock, so within one process it is serialized correctly. However: (1) The store explicitly assumes a single process ('the API is a single process') — if ever run under multiple workers (gunicorn -w N, multiple uvicorn workers), the SELECT+INSERT is not atomic across processes and two requests can both see existing is None and both INSERT, causing an IntegrityError on the second (uncaught -> 500) rather than a clean conflict/processing result. The PRIMARY KEY would catch it but the code does not handle the IntegrityError. (2) The stale-reclaim branch (line 337) rebinds request_hash for a 'processing' entry older than the timeout but does not reset status, which is fine, yet it returns {'state':'new'} allowing a second concurrent in-flight request to also proceed once the first one's claim ages out, potentially double-charging if combined with a slow /complete. Worth flagging given this is the ACP money path.

findings.title: create_account does not handle UNIQUE constraint violations (stripe_customer_id / api_key), surfacing IntegrityError as an unhandled 500
findings.description: accounts.stripe_customer_id and api_key are UNIQUE. create_account does a bare INSERT with no ON CONFLICT / try-except. In the ACP path (acp/router.py line 289) and the billing _resolve_account path, create_account can be called with an api_key or implicitly during concurrent webhook redeliveries; if two flows race to create an account for the same customer or api_key, the second INSERT raises sqlite3.IntegrityError which propagates as an unhandled 500 instead of resolving to the existing account. get_account_by_api_key exists but is not consulted before create in _resolve_account.

findings.title: Idempotency 'processing' stale-timeout rebinds the key to a new payload but a slow original request can still complete and overwrite the rebind, allowing two operations under one key
findings.description: If an original request takes longer than IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s), a second request re-claims the key (state 'new', rebinds request_hash) and proceeds. The original request, when it finally finishes, calls idempotency_complete(key, response) unconditionally — overwriting status to 'done' with the ORIGINAL response, even though the second request also proceeded and may have charged/fulfilled. For the ACP /complete path this means two distinct completions can both run under one Idempotency-Key (the charge is deduped per-session by Stripe, but fulfill_once is per-session too, so credits are safe — yet the response stored becomes whichever finishes last, and a third replay returns a stale/foreign response). The 60s abandon heuristic does not prevent concurrent execution, only relieves a permanent lock; the comment frames it as crash-recovery but it fires on slow (not crashed) requests too.

findings.title: API keys stored in plaintext and matched by direct SQL equality (no hashing, not constant-time)
findings.description: API keys (uma_<token>) are stored verbatim in the accounts.api_key column and looked up with `SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE api_key = ?`. Two weaknesses: (1) the secret is at rest in plaintext in data/app.db, so any read of that file (backup, the Dockerfile COPY issue above, a SQL-injection elsewhere, an ops dump) yields live credentials — best practice is to store only a hash (e.g. sha256) of the key and compare hashes. (2) SQLite string equality in an index lookup is not a constant-time comparison, so it is theoretically timing-observable, though SQLite/btree indexing makes this low practical risk. The plaintext-at-rest issue is the substantive one for a credential store. allow-secret

findings.title: Stale 'processing' idempotency reclaim rebinds the key without resetting status to 'processing', and the 60s timeout can let a still-running original double-execute
findings.description: When a 'processing' entry is older than IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s), the code UPDATEs request_hash+created_at and returns {'state':'new'} to let the retry re-claim it. Two problems: (1) it does not reset/confirm status stays 'processing' (it remains 'processing', which is fine), but it never guards against the ORIGINAL request still being alive — a slow-but-not-crashed first request (e.g. a Stripe charge taking >60s) will have its key reclaimed and the SECOND request will proceed to charge/fulfill in parallel, defeating idempotency precisely on the money path (ACP /complete). The charge dedup via per-session Stripe key mitigates double-charge, but fulfill_once is keyed by session and is atomic, so credits are safe; however two concurrent /complete bodies could still both reach payment.charge. (2) 60s is short for a payment round-trip. Consider a longer timeout and/or tying reclaim to a heartbeat.

findings.location: Store single shared connection + RLock, class-level; idempotency_begin lines 322-353
findings.title: Single global SQLite connection serialized by one RLock makes all DB writes a process-wide bottleneck and INSERT race window in idempotency_begin spans a non-transactional read+insert
findings.description: idempotency_begin does _fetch_one_nolock(SELECT) then conditionally INSERT, all inside the RLock — so within ONE process it is atomic. BUT the store explicitly documents 'single process'; with multiple workers (uvicorn --workers>1 or multiple Render instances pointing at the same MAIL_DB_PATH), each process has its OWN connection+lock and the SELECT-then-INSERT is NOT atomic across processes — two workers can both SELECT none, both INSERT (one hits PRIMARY KEY -> IntegrityError, uncaught, 500). The INSERT in idempotency_begin is not wrapped to catch the unique-violation. The whole design assumes single-process which is not enforced; a multi-worker deploy breaks idempotency and surfaces unhandled IntegrityError on the money path.

findings.title: Stale 'processing' idempotency entry is re-claimed as 'new', allowing the protected operation to re-run
findings.description: When an idempotency key is in 'processing' and older than IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s), idempotency_begin rebinds the row to the new request_hash and returns {'state':'new'} (store.py:337-344), letting the request proceed as if first-time. For acp.complete this means a /complete that genuinely crashed mid-charge can be retried after 60s and will re-enter the charge+fulfill path. Double-charge is mitigated by the per-session Stripe idempotency_key f'acp-charge:{session_id}' (router.py:265) and double-credit by fulfill_once, so the design is defensible — BUT it depends on those two downstream guards being present and correct. With NullPaymentClient or a test/fake client that does NOT honor idempotency_key (the injected _OKPay in tests ignores it), the 60s reclaim plus a non-idempotent charge client would re-charge. Also, 60s is short relative to a slow Stripe call, so a still-in-flight legitimate request can be reclaimed concurrently. Flagging the coupling: correctness here is load-bearing on Stripe honoring the per-session key, which is unverifiable in this repo.

findings.title: ACP update_session can mutate a session's items/total without re-binding to the original idempotent create, enabling price/quantity change before complete
findings.description: update_session (router.py:200-232) re-derives line_items, total_runs and status from body.items for any non-terminal session and persists them. There is no check that the updater is the same party/api_key that created the session (the gate accepts any bearer token), so any caller who knows the session_id can rewrite the cart (e.g. inflate quantity to 1000 via Item.quantity le=1000, or swap to pack_1000) before /complete. Because amount and credited runs are taken from the stored line_items at complete time, a third party who learns a session_id could alter what gets charged/credited. Combined with the missing ownership check on the gate, this is an integrity gap on the cart. Mitigated by unguessable session ids, but there is no authorization tying update to the creator.

findings.title: create_account silently accepts an arbitrary externally-supplied api_key, conflating server-issued credentials with caller-asserted strings
findings.description: create_account(api_key=...) writes whatever string the caller passes straight into accounts.api_key with no validation that it matches the uma_ issuance format (new_api_key produces 'uma_' + token_urlsafe(32)). This is the store-level enabler of the auth-bypass above: there is no notion of 'this key was issued by us'. get_account_by_api_key returns None for unknown keys (correct), but nothing prevents an unknown key from later being inserted as a real account. A safer store API would separate issue_account() (server-minted key only) from any externally-keyed insert, or reject non-uma_-prefixed keys. As written, the store cannot distinguish a forged bearer from a legitimate one once the ACP path provisions it. allow-secret

findings.title: idempotency_complete writes status='done' + response unconditionally — a stale-reclaimed key lets a late original writer clobber the retry's result (last-writer-wins on a rebound key)
findings.description: idempotency_complete (lines 355-362) does UPDATE ... SET status='done', response_json=? WHERE key=? with NO guard on the current status or on which request 'owns' the key. Combine with the stale-reclaim path (337-344): suppose request A claims key K ('processing'), stalls >60s; request B (a retry, possibly with a DIFFERENT payload since the reclaim rebinds request_hash) re-claims K as 'new', proceeds, and calls idempotency_complete writing B's response and status='done'. Then the original A finally finishes and ALSO calls idempotency_complete, overwriting with A's (now stale) response. A subsequent replay of K returns A's response even though B was the request that actually completed and was acknowledged to the client. Because there is no fencing token / no compare-and-set on status, the two in-flight requests silently race and the loser's result is what callers later replay. For ACP, where the stored response carries the order id / session status, a client could replay and receive a session shape inconsistent with what was charged/fulfilled. Fix: complete should only transition from 'processing'->'done' for the row whose created_at/request_hash matches the claim it was issued (a claim token), and the reclaim should invalidate the prior holder.

findings.title: Default autocommit isolation_level left implicit; explicit per-method commit() means multi-statement methods are NOT atomic if a statement raises mid-way
findings.description: The connection uses sqlite3's default isolation_level (deferred), and each method manually calls self._conn.commit(). In fulfill_once (lines 374-389) two statements run before a single commit: the INSERT OR IGNORE into acp_fulfillments and the balance UPDATE. The docstring claims they 'run in one locked transaction' (line 368). They are in one lock, but if the second statement (the UPDATE) raises (e.g. SQLITE_BUSY from an external connection per the busy_timeout finding, or a disk-full error) AFTER the INSERT succeeded and BEFORE commit, no explicit rollback is issued — the next operation on this connection will implicitly start fresh, but the pending INSERT may be committed by a later commit() of an UNRELATED method, recording a fulfillment with NO corresponding credit (a silent under-credit / lost grant). There is no try/except with rollback around any multi-statement method. Same shape applies to the stale-reclaim UPDATE+commit and idempotency_begin's INSERT+commit. Fix: wrap multi-statement mutations in explicit `with self._conn:` (which rolls back on exception) or add try/rollback. Confidence medium because the current single-process design rarely raises mid-transaction, but the failure mode (fulfillment recorded without credit) is a money-path correctness bug.

findings.location: line 337 (if _now() - existing["created_at"] > IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT) with _now()=time.time() at line 106-107
findings.title: Idempotency 'processing' abandonment window uses non-monotonic wall clock (time.time), so a clock step can prematurely free or permanently wedge a key
findings.description: The 60s idempotency-processing timeout is computed as `_now() - existing["created_at"]` where both are `time.time()` (wall clock, REAL columns). Wall-clock time is not monotonic: an NTP correction or manual clock adjustment between the claim and the retry can (a) make the delta negative or huge, prematurely re-claiming a key that is still legitimately processing (double execution / double credit debit on the same idempotency key) or (b) push the entry past the window so it never expires (self-DoS the comment at lines 100-103 explicitly tries to prevent). Same wall-clock basis also governs `created_at` ordering of receipts. For an interval/timeout decision a monotonic source is correct. Round 1 flagged `_period_end` epoch-units but not this monotonic-vs-wallclock timing class.

findings.title: idempotency_complete silently no-ops if the key row is absent (rowcount unchecked), so a lost claim leaves the key permanently un-finalized
findings.description: idempotency_complete issues UPDATE ... WHERE key = ? and ignores rowcount. If the key row was deleted, never inserted (e.g., the begin INSERT failed cross-process and was swallowed upstream), or reclaimed/rewritten, the UPDATE matches zero rows and commits successfully with no error. The router (_complete_idempotency) then believes the response was persisted. A subsequent replay either finds no row (re-executes the non-idempotent action) or finds a stale 'processing' row (returns 409). Round 1 flagged the stale-reclaim last-writer-wins clobber and the NULL-response replay, but not the zero-row silent no-op when the key is simply missing.

findings.title: idempotency stale-reclaim updates request_hash/created_at but NOT status, so a crashed 'processing' key can be reclaimed yet still read as processing by a racing third request
findings.description: On stale-timeout reclaim the UPDATE sets request_hash and created_at and returns {'state':'new'} but leaves status='processing'. A third concurrent request arriving microseconds later sees status=='processing' with the NEW (matching) request_hash and is told {'state':'processing'} (409) even though the reclaiming request is the legitimate owner — or, worse, two requests both pass the >timeout check before either UPDATE commits and both get {'state':'new'} (the rebind is not atomic compare-and-set). This is a NEW angle beyond round1's noted stale-timeout overwrite: the status field is never transitioned during reclaim.

findings.title: Manifest/llms.txt reflect the raw request base_url (Host header) — Host-header spoofing makes the self-describing surface advertise attacker-controlled URLs
findings.description: build_agent_manifest(str(request.base_url)) and build_llms_txt take the base URL straight from the request. Starlette derives base_url from the Host header (and forwarded headers if a proxy middleware is trusted). Without a trusted-host allowlist, an attacker can send a forged Host so the served manifest advertises checkout_url / mcp / receipt_verification endpoints on an attacker domain. An agent that fetches /.well-known/agent.json from the victim and trusts the returned checkout_url could be redirected to an attacker's checkout — a phishing/SSRF-adjacent vector on the agent-commerce discovery path. Pin allowed hosts (TrustedHostMiddleware) or a configured public base URL for these surfaces.

findings.title: Discovery endpoints reflect request base_url into manifest URLs with no Host allowlist (cache-poisoning / link-spoofing)
findings.description: build_agent_manifest/build_llms_txt embed str(request.base_url) (derived from the Host header / X-Forwarded-* if trusted) directly into advertised checkout_url, mcp url, receipt_verification, etc. With no Host validation, a forged Host header makes the server emit a manifest pointing agents at an attacker domain for checkout and receipt verification — a classic Host-header injection feeding a B2A discovery surface that agents are meant to trust. Round1 flagged one well_known item; this is the SSRF/host-trust angle on the agent-facing URLs.

findings.title: Mutating inbox collection while iterating skips messages (move during repeat-over-messages)
findings.description: The script binds `set inboxMessages to messages of inbox` (line 8) then iterates that reference and calls `move msg to targetMailbox` inside the loop. In Mail.app AppleScript, `messages of inbox` is a live reference; moving a message out of the inbox shifts the remaining indices, so the `repeat with msg in inboxMessages` loop skips every other matching message on a single pass. Common workaround is to snapshot into a static list first, or iterate in reverse by index. As written, a single run leaves roughly half the >90-day messages un-archived, silently (the surrounding try swallows any error).

findings.title: Resolving Archive mailbox can throw before the loop with no handling
findings.description: Lines 9-13 resolve `mailbox targetMailboxName` (default "Archive") with no try wrapper. If a mailbox literally named "Archive" does not exist (Mail.app commonly names it per-account, or it may be absent), this line raises an unhandled AppleScript error and the script dies before processing anything. The default `targetAccountName` of "" relies on a top-level mailbox named exactly "Archive", which is not guaranteed to exist.

findings.title: HttpError handler breaks the category loop on a transient error, silently skipping remaining work
findings.description: On any HttpError (rate limit, transient 5xx) the handler logs a warning and `break`s out of the inner while (line 130), abandoning the rest of that category with no retry/backoff. A single 429 ends archiving for that label silently (only a WARNING). Other categories continue, so the run reports success-ish while having done partial work.

findings.location: senders_for() callback lines 61-68; archive_loop pagination lines 92-131
findings.title: Archive pagination re-runs the same query and breaks on len(ids)<1000, so >1000 protected-heavy pages can loop or miss messages; HttpError aborts the whole category
findings.description: archive_loop loops while True but never uses a page token: it re-lists 'label:X label:INBOX' each pass and relies on the INBOX removal to shrink the result. Two issues: (1) when ALL of a 1000-result page is protected (archivable empty), the code does 'if len(ids) < 1000: break; continue' — it continues, re-fetching the SAME 1000 protected messages forever (infinite loop) because nothing changed. (2) On HttpError it 'break's out of the whole category, abandoning remaining messages silently (logged as warning only). The senders_for callback fails closed correctly (empty From => protected), which is good, but a batch.execute() that raises is uncaught inside senders_for (no try) and will propagate to the per-category HttpError handler, abandoning the category. allow-secret

findings.location: archive_loop lines 90-130 (query line 90); senders_for batch.execute line 79
findings.title: Gmail query uses unescaped category in label: and batch.execute has no error handling
findings.description: `query = f"label:{category} label:INBOX"` interpolates category from a static list (safe today), but the pattern would break/inject if categories ever contained spaces or query operators. More concretely, senders_for() calls `batch.execute()` with no try/except: an HttpError there (rate limit/network) propagates out of senders_for into archive_loop's try, which catches HttpError and `break`s the per-category loop — silently abandoning that category's archiving with only a warning. Also the pagination relies on len(ids) < 1000 to stop, but list() with maxResults=1000 returns at most 1000 and the loop re-queries the same query expecting prior batchModify to have removed INBOX; if batchModify is skipped because all were protected (archivable empty) and len(ids)==1000, it `continue`s and re-fetches the same 1000 protected messages forever (infinite loop).

findings.title: Unhandled batch.execute() exception aborts protected-sender gate before INBOX removal
findings.description: senders_for() calls batch.execute() (line 79) with NO try/except. The googleapiclient batch.execute() raises on a transport/HTTP error before any per-message callback runs, so the {id: From} map is never populated and the exception propagates up into archive_loop's try block. archive_loop only catches HttpError (line 128); a non-HttpError (socket timeout, BatchError, SSLError, JSON decode) escapes the loop entirely and crashes the run. More importantly, if a partial batch fails, some ids never get a callback and thus are absent from `out`; `senders.get(i, '')` returns '' which is_protected_sender treats as protected (fail-closed), so those are skipped from archiving (safe). But the per-callback design relies on every id getting a callback to populate out — confirm the fail-closed default actually holds for ids whose request silently never fires. Net: the gate's correctness depends on batch.execute() either fully succeeding or fully raising; partial completion is the dangerous middle.

findings.title: Plaintext secret passed as a command-line argument to `op` (visible via process listing)
findings.description: store_token_info / store_json_secret serialize the OAuth token (including refresh_token) and invoke `op item edit <item> "<field>=<compact_json_secret>"`. The secret value is an argv element, so for the lifetime of the `op` subprocess it is visible to any local user via `ps auxww`, /proc/<pid>/cmdline, or process accounting/audit logs. 1Password's CLI supports reading assignment values from stdin / files specifically to avoid this; passing the secret on the command line leaks it. Same pattern in both auth/onepassword.py and gmail_auth.py.

findings.title: op item edit assignment arg breaks when field name contains '=' or value contains '='
findings.description: The command is built as `op item edit item f"{field}={value}"`. The `op` CLI parses the first '=' in this token as the field/value separator. If `field` itself contains an '=' (unlikely but possible from a misconfigured op_ref/field_env), or more importantly if the *value* is fine but the field assignment semantics depend on exactly one '=', op may mis-split. Worse: op assignment syntax also supports `field[type]=value` and section-qualified `section.field=value`; a field name derived from parse_op_ref (which can contain '/' joined path segments, e.g. field='Field/Sub') is passed verbatim. op item edit does not interpret '/' as a section separator, so a multi-segment field from an op:// ref with >3 path parts will create/edit a field literally named 'a/b' rather than the intended nested field, silently writing the token to the wrong field. Since this is the token write-back path, a wrong-field write means the next read (op_item_get with the real field) returns the old/empty token -> auth silently breaks.

findings.location: op_item_edit, line 111; op_item_get, line 85; op_read, line 60
findings.title: User/env-derived item names or values starting with '-' can be parsed by op as flags (argv option injection)
findings.description: cmd lists place item names and the `field=value` token directly in argv with no `--` end-of-options sentinel. If `item`, `ref`, or the `field=value` token begins with a leading '-' (e.g. an item literally named '--force' or a value/field starting with '-'), op's argument parser may interpret it as an option/flag rather than a positional. There is no shell, so this is not shell injection, but it is classic argv option injection: a maliciously- or accidentally-named item/field could toggle op behavior or cause an error. Add `--` before positional/assignment arguments (e.g. `op item edit -- item field=value`) to neutralize leading-dash parsing. Same applies to `op read -- ref` and `op item get -- item`.

findings.title: parse_op_ref maps op:// fields with extra '/' segments into a single slash-joined field, which op edit cannot address as a nested field
findings.description: For ref 'op://Vault/Item/Section/Field' the parser yields field='Section/Field' (parts[2:] joined by '/'). op read accepts this path form, but op item edit (used by store_json_secret -> op_item_edit) expects section-qualified fields in 'section.field=value' (dot) form, not 'section/field'. So a ref that reads correctly via op_read will, on write-back, create/edit a literal field named 'Section/Field' rather than the nested field — the read and write are asymmetric for sectioned references. Result: token write-back lands in the wrong field; subsequent reads of the original ref miss it. Read-path callers won't notice; the bug only manifests on refresh/store with sectioned refs.

findings.title: Gmail `from:{domain}` query may not match the raw extracted domain, causing zero moves (and feeding the infinite-loop)
findings.description: The bulk move query is `f"from:{domain} label:{TARGET_SOURCE}"` where `domain = sender.split('@')[-1].strip('>]').lower()`. The extracted token can include trailing display-name junk, angle brackets only partially stripped, or be a subdomain (e.g. 'email.marketing.brand.com') that Gmail's `from:` operator treats differently from how it was captured, so the search returns no messages even though the sample clearly contained that sender. When that happens for the dominant domains, no moves occur, reinforcing the runaway loop noted separately. Also `extract_domain` strips only `>` and `]` but not a trailing `"` or whitespace inside the address, which can corrupt the query token.

findings.title: batchModify failure only sleeps 5s and continues, double-counts moves, and pagination uses a fresh search each loop causing potential infinite loop
findings.description: Inside the bulk move-while-loop: on HttpError the code logs and time.sleep(5) but does NOT break or retry the same batch — it falls through to the nextPageToken check. Since the FAILED batch's messages still carry the source label, the very next 'service.users().messages().list(q=query)' returns the same messages again (query is 'from:domain label:Misc/Other' and the failed modify left them labeled), so the loop can spin indefinitely on a permanently-failing batch (e.g. a label-permission error), repeatedly sleeping 5s. moves_performed += len(batch_ids) is incremented BEFORE confirming success only in the success branch (ok) but the failure path leaves the same page to be refetched. There is no max-iteration / no-progress guard. The outer while True (line 100) similarly has only the empty-sample exit.

findings.title: Gmail search-query injection via sender-derived domain
findings.description: `domain` is derived from the raw From header (`extract_domain` splits on '@' and strips '>]'), then interpolated into a Gmail search query: `query = f"from:{domain} label:{TARGET_SOURCE}"`. A crafted From header could inject Gmail search operators (e.g. a domain like `x OR label:INBOX` or `x in:anywhere`) that broaden the search beyond the Misc/Other bucket. Because the result IDs are then bulk-batchModify'd (addLabelIds target / removeLabelIds source), a query that matches messages outside Misc/Other would mis-move them. No protected-sender gate runs in this script (documented LEGACY), so a broadened query could move mail it should not. Severity bounded by it being label moves not archive, but it is a real query-injection -> wrong-target mutation.

findings.location: drain_loop lines 100-199 (outer while True), 159-187 (inner while True), callback line 121
findings.title: Potential infinite loop and dropped errors in inbox drain
findings.description: Two concerns: (1) The inner bulk-move loop `while True` breaks only when search returns no messages OR no nextPageToken; but it re-issues the SAME query `from:{domain} label:Misc/Other` each iteration and relies on the prior batchModify having removed the source label. If batchModify fails (HttpError is caught, logged, sleep 5, then continues) the same page is fetched again forever -> infinite loop hammering the API. (2) The batch-get `callback` does `if exception: return`, silently dropping messages that fail metadata fetch so they are never classified/moved (they remain in Misc/Other), and the outer loop may then spin with moves_performed==0. The `service` is never closed/disconnected either.

findings.location: run_sweep line 91 (`if not add_id`) vs lines 88-89, 114
findings.title: Missing 'remove' label is silently tolerated, leaving source label attached forever
findings.description: get_label_id returns None when a label is not found (line 79). run_sweep only guards add_id (line 91 `if not add_id: ... continue`), but never guards remove_id. When the 'remove' label ('Misc/Other') resolves to None, the batchModify body sends removeLabelIds: [] (line 114), so messages get the new label added but are NEVER stripped of 'Misc/Other'. They therefore still match the rule['query'] ('... label:Misc/Other') on the next list() call, and combined with the non-advancing pagination (separate finding) this can loop indefinitely re-adding the same label or at minimum never make progress. At least a warning should be logged as is done for add_id.

findings.title: SWEEP_RULES target label names diverge from core taxonomy ('Work/Dev/Infrastructure', 'Work/RealEstate' not in LABEL_RULES)
findings.description: SWEEP_RULES add labels 'Work/Dev/Infrastructure' (lines 30,37,67) and 'Work/RealEstate' (line 60), and 'Education/Research' (line 54). core/rules.py defines 'Dev/Infrastructure' (no 'Work/' prefix) and has no 'Work/RealEstate' category at all; 'Education/Research' does exist. get_label_id (line 88) will return None for any of these that don't already exist as Gmail labels, and run_sweep skips the rule with only a warning (line 91-93). So the Notion/Backblaze/Termius/Majestic sweeps are no-ops unless those exact differently-named labels were manually created — silent divergence from the canonical taxonomy. The hierarchical names also don't match the 'Dev/Infrastructure' produced by every categorizer, fragmenting the label tree.

findings.title: SWEEP_RULES route by bare 'from:<domain>' with no protected-sender gate; safe today but a one-line edit ships unprotected mutations
findings.description: Every SWEEP_RULE moves `from:<domain> label:Misc/Other` from Misc/Other into a category label (relabel, no INBOX removal) — currently inbox-internal so not a never-archive breach. The concern is that the queries trust the configured domain verbatim and there is no is_protected_sender choke point; if a future rule's 'remove' is set to 'INBOX' or a destructive target (the data structure permits it), it becomes a label-only archive with zero sender protection. Per the gate contract in core/rules.py this should route through is_protected_sender at the choke point, not rely on the author never adding an INBOX-removing rule.

findings.location: default --query 'has:nouserlabels' (label_parser line 1014) used for all providers
findings.title: Gmail-specific default query passed verbatim to IMAP/Outlook/Mail.app providers
findings.description: The label subcommand default query is the Gmail search operator `has:nouserlabels` (line 1014, help text even says '(default: has:nouserlabels for Gmail)'). cmd_label passes args.query straight to get_provider/run_labeler for ALL providers without remapping. For imap/outlook/mailapp this string is not a valid query and will either match nothing, error, or (worst case) be interpreted unexpectedly by the provider's search backend. A user running `cli.py label --provider outlook` with no --query gets a Gmail-only default silently. The other commands (summary/escalate/vip) default to '' which is safer; label is inconsistent.

findings.title: Global --verbose flag is defined on the top-level parser only and is shadowed/unreachable when a subcommand is given
findings.description: `-v/--verbose` is added to the root parser (line 968) BEFORE subparsers. With argparse, a global option declared before the subparser must appear before the subcommand on the command line (e.g. `cli.py -v label ...`), not after (`cli.py label -v ...`). Worse, the shared provider_group declares `--provider -p` but there is no -v on subparsers, so `cli.py label --verbose` raises 'unrecognized arguments'. The documented/intuitive `cli.py label --provider gmail --verbose` will error. Verbose is effectively only usable in the rarely-typed prefix position, making the flag practically inaccessible.

findings.title: pending uses sender-side 'is:starred' query for gmail but 'isRead eq false' for outlook, then filters on msg.is_starred — outlook path yields zero pending items
findings.description: For gmail the query is 'is:starred' and the post-filter is `if msg.is_starred` (line 665) — consistent. For outlook the query is 'isRead eq false' (unread, line 642) but the post-filter is STILL `if msg.is_starred`. Unread Outlook messages are not necessarily starred/flagged, and Outlook's is_starred mapping (if it maps flagStatus) won't correlate with isRead. So for outlook, cmd_pending fetches unread messages then discards all that aren't starred — almost always producing an empty 'pending' list, contradicting the command's purpose. For non-gmail/non-outlook providers query='' fetches everything then keeps only starred, which at least matches the filter but ignores 'pending/due' semantics entirely.

findings.location: run_labeler, lines 172, 186-190 (page_token = state.get_token(); passed to provider.list_messages)
findings.title: Malformed page token from state is replayed unvalidated to the provider
findings.description: page_token is read straight from get_token() (no type/shape check) and passed into provider.list_messages(page_token=...). A corrupt-but-valid state file storing next_page_token as a dict, number, or other non-string is replayed to the live provider API. Depending on the provider this either raises an opaque API error, silently returns the wrong page, or (Gmail) sends a malformed pageToken parameter. There is no validation that a resumed token is a string (or None) before it is trusted as a resumption cursor. is_resumable() (state.py:131-133) likewise only checks `is not None`, so a non-string token reports resumable=True.

findings.title: Worker .gov protection uses endsWith('.gov') on the full From value, accepting spoofs the real API rejects
findings.description: Line 106 protects when `value.includes("courts.ca.gov") || value.endsWith(".gov")`. `value` is the entire lowercased From string, not a parsed domain. (a) `value.endsWith(".gov")` is satisfied by `attacker@evil.gov` (any TLD-less '.gov' suffix) AND fails for a normal address with a trailing display element; more importantly it does NOT replicate the API's _gov_protected which parses the real domain and anchors to the TERMINAL label, explicitly rejecting `irs.gov.attacker.com` and `service.gov.uk`. The worker would mark `service.gov.uk`... actually .uk fails endsWith('.gov'), but `x.gov.attacker.gov` passes, and `irs.gov.attacker.com` fails (good) only by luck of suffix. The substring `value.includes("courts.ca.gov")` matches `courts.ca.gov.evil.com`. This is the parsed-domain-vs-raw-string class of bug for the .gov path specifically (distinct from round-2's general substring note in that it pinpoints the endsWith vs terminal-label divergence and the courts.ca.gov.evil.com pass-through).

findings.title: Overwrites live Apple Mail plist with only a single .backup that is clobbered on every run
findings.description: BACKUP_PATH is a fixed `.backup` (line 18). Each run does `shutil.copy2(PLIST_PATH, BACKUP_PATH)` (line 103) overwriting the previous backup, then rewrites the live plist. If the script is run twice (e.g. once with bad SELF_NAME, then again), the second run backs up the already-corrupted plist, destroying the only good copy. There is no dry-run, no timestamped backup, and no validation that the rewritten plist is well-formed before replacing. This mutates a real macOS Mail data file.

findings.title: Non-atomic plist write can corrupt SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist on interrupt
findings.description: The plist is written by opening PLIST_PATH 'wb' and plistlib.dump directly onto the live file (lines 123-124). If the process is interrupted mid-write (crash, Ctrl-C, disk full), the live Mail plist is left truncated/corrupt. Safe practice is to write to a temp file and os.replace() atomically. The .backup mitigates only if the user notices and restores manually.

findings.title: Plist read-modify-write not atomic; backup overwritten each run
findings.description: main() copies PLIST to .backup (overwriting any prior backup), then loads, mutates, and writes back via plistlib.dump to the SAME path with a plain open('wb'). A crash during dump corrupts the user's live SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist, and since the backup is overwritten at the start of every run, a second run after a corrupted first run would back up the corrupted file, destroying the good backup. Also there is no locking against Mail.app concurrently reading the plist. Write to temp + os.replace and use a timestamped/once backup.

findings.title: Protected-sender disposition over-reported as protected_held even when message left the inbox (count vs violation mismatch)
findings.description: When is_protected but left_inbox is True (a real gate breach), the code appends to self.violations AND sets disposition=PROTECTED_HELD, incrementing counts[PROTECTED_HELD]. So summary()['protected_held'] and receipt_line()'s 'N protected held in inbox' count INCLUDES messages that actually left the inbox. The receipt then simultaneously claims the message was 'held in inbox' (in the protected_held tally) while the violation warning says it left — an internally contradictory receipt. The disposition recorded in the JSONL line is also 'protected_held' for a message that was archived/moved, so the persisted audit record misstates the actual disposition (the very thing the module's docstring says it must record faithfully). A violated message should arguably get a distinct disposition (e.g. 'protected_violated') so the held count stays truthful.

findings.title: _append catches only OSError; a serialization error in json.dumps escapes into the apply path
findings.description: The docstring and code comment promise receipt-keeping 'must NEVER raise into the apply path'. But the try block also contains json.dumps(entry,...). If entry contains a non-serializable value (e.g. labels_added carrying a non-str object, or a future field holding a datetime/bytes), json.dumps raises TypeError — which is NOT an OSError and therefore is NOT caught, so it propagates out of record() and aborts the run mid-batch, exactly the outcome the module claims to prevent. The except should be broadened to Exception (with the same in-memory-degrade behavior) or json.dumps should be hardened, to honor the stated invariant.

findings.title: Audit silently downgrades to non-protected when rules import/parse fails
findings.description: _independently_protected swallows ALL exceptions and returns False on failure. Combined with record()'s `is_protected = bool(protected) or _independently_protected(sender)`, if core.rules fails to import (e.g. during a partial deploy or a circular-import edge) the independent cross-check silently returns False for EVERY sender. The audit then relies solely on the gate's own `protected` flag — collapsing the 'independent observer' guarantee the module's docstring sells, with no warning logged. The gate-stopped-recognizing-a-sender scenario the module exists to catch becomes uncatchable, silently. At minimum a one-time logger.warning when the import path fails would surface the degradation.

findings.title: The independent audit's protection re-derivation swallows ALL exceptions and returns False (fail-OPEN for the cross-check)
findings.description: The whole point of _independently_protected is to catch a gate that stopped recognizing a protected sender. But its except clause returns False on ANY exception (import error, regex error, parse error). So if is_protected_sender itself starts raising (e.g. a regression that throws on a malformed From, or a config regex that triggers re.error), the audit silently treats the sender as NOT protected and will not flag a violation even if that sender left the inbox — the independent safety check fails open precisely when the rules engine is broken. The docstring acknowledges 'declines to assert a protection it cannot compute' as intentional, but for a fail-CLOSED product the safer choice on an exception is to treat as protected (or to surface the compute failure as its own violation/warning), not silently False. Same swallow in _domain_of (cosmetic there).

findings.location: summary() lines 250 -> api/receipts.py signed_body 83 / persist 94-105 -> GET /v1/audit/{run_id} 114-142 (unauthenticated)
findings.title: Receipt body schema is designed to carry raw violation message_ids to an unauthenticated endpoint; only the fail-closed assert keeps it empty
findings.description: summary() returns `"violations": list(self.violations)` (line 250) which holds RAW provider message_ids (appended at line 184). That summary becomes `triage_result['audit']` (api/service.py:133), which `signed_body()` copies verbatim into the receipt `summary` field (api/receipts.py:83), persisted to the ledger and served by the UNAUTHENTICATED `GET /v1/audit/{run_id}` (api/receipts.py:114-142). app.py:117-122 takes deliberate care to keep these exact message_ids server-side ('so internal identifiers do not leak to the client'), but the receipt path would publish them. Today the leak is masked only because `assert_no_violations()` (service.py:127) raises before `_run` reaches `receipts.persist`, so a non-empty violations list never gets persisted. That is a fragile single point of defense: any future change that persists a receipt before asserting, adds a 'soft/warn-only' violation mode, or surfaces the summary on a path that does not re-assert, immediately leaks internal message ids to an anonymous caller. The redaction in core/audit (lines 209-210) does NOT cover this array. Defense-in-depth: strip/redact `violations` ids from the shared receipt body rather than relying on the upstream raise.

findings.location: _independently_protected lines 110-114 and _domain_of lines 84-92 (both import core.rules and both bare-except)
findings.title: A single core.rules import failure collapses BOTH the cross-check AND the receipt's domain field at once, with the receipt still emitted as if trustworthy
findings.description: _independently_protected (110-114) and _domain_of (84-92) each independently `from core.rules import ...` inside a bare `try/except Exception`. A failure of core.rules to import or a throw in normalize_sender/is_protected_sender (e.g. the naive-datetime / regex class of bug already flagged in rules.py) trips BOTH simultaneously for the same message: the cross-check silently degrades to trusting only the gate's own `protected` flag (independence collapses to one source) AND the entry's `domain` field falls back to the divergent `rpartition('@')` parser (line 92) that does not match the canonical normalize_sender. The record is still written and summary() still reports 'no violations' with full confidence — there is no field on the entry or in summary() signalling 'independent re-derivation unavailable / degraded.' The module's headline promise (docstring 20-38: caught 'even [if] a gate stops recognizing a protected sender') silently evaporates exactly when the rules layer is broken, and nothing — not the receipt, not assert_no_violations, not the caller — is told the second source went dark. (Round 1 noted each function fails open in isolation; this is the compound, signal-less degradation of the whole independence guarantee plus the simultaneously corrupted domain field.)

findings.title: No type validation of YAML values before assigning to typed config fields
findings.description: Values pulled from YAML (batch_size, throttle_seconds, dry_run, port, scopes, custom_rules, vip_senders, extra_*_labels) are assigned directly to config fields with zero type checking. A YAML file with `batch_size: "100"` (string) or `throttle_seconds: fast` flows straight into config.batch_size/throttle_seconds and only blows up later in arithmetic/sleep calls far from the config layer, making the error hard to trace. Similarly custom_rules/vip_senders are assigned wholesale (config.custom_rules = data['custom_rules']) even if the YAML author wrote a list or scalar there, so downstream .items() iteration (apply_vip_senders_from_config line 313) raises AttributeError. The `if ... isinstance(..., dict)` guard is applied to provider sub-blocks but NOT to custom_rules/vip_senders/extra lists.

findings.title: VIP entry missing 'pattern' is silently dropped without warning
findings.description: The loop only processes entries where isinstance(vip_data, dict) and 'pattern' in vip_data. A VIP sender the user configured but where they typo'd the key (e.g. 'patern') or omitted 'pattern' is silently skipped — no log, no count, no error. The user believes a protected/priority sender is configured when it is not, which for a product whose headline guarantee is 'protected senders are never archived' is a meaningful silent failure. Should at least logger.warning on a skipped malformed VIP entry.

findings.title: Broad except Exception swallows YAML parse errors as empty config
findings.description: Any failure loading/parsing the YAML (malformed YAML, permission error, decode error) is caught by `except Exception`, logged only at warning level, and returns {} so the system silently runs on pure defaults. A user with a syntactically broken config file (e.g. bad indentation) gets the default Gmail behavior with no clear failure — their custom rules, VIP senders, and provider overrides all silently vanish. yaml.YAMLError vs OSError should be distinguished, and a malformed (vs absent) config arguably should be a hard error rather than a silent fallback to defaults.

findings.title: merge() silently merges actions for DIFFERENT message_ids; only the docstring says 'assumed'
findings.description: merge() builds the result with message_id=self.message_id and never verifies self.message_id == other.message_id. The docstring (line 96) only states 'same message_id assumed'. If a caller ever merges two LabelActions for different messages (e.g. an accumulation/dedup pass keyed wrongly), the result silently adopts self's message_id and carries other's add_labels/remove_labels/archive/star/target_folder/category — meaning labels/archives/moves intended for message B get applied to message A. Given that archive/star/target_folder propagate via merge (lines 102-104), a mis-keyed merge could move/label the wrong message. There is no production caller today (merge appears only in tests/test_models.py:97-127), but the method is public API on a money-path/data-path model and a future caller has nothing guarding the invariant. Fix: assert self.message_id == other.message_id (or raise ValueError).

findings.title: merge() can resurrect a non-empty sender from `other`, weakening the fail-closed protected-sender guarantee
findings.description: The protected-sender gate is fail-closed: a blank sender is treated as PROTECTED / never-archived (core/rules.py:797-798, providers/base.py:297 'A blank sender fails closed (protected)'). merge() computes sender = self.sender or other.sender (line 99). If self.sender is '' (the fail-closed/protected sentinel) but other.sender is a non-empty address, the merged action adopts other's sender. Combined with archive=self.archive or other.archive (line 102) and remove_labels propagation, a merge of a blank-sender action (which the gate would have protected) with another action could yield a non-blank sender plus archive=True — the gate would then evaluate the (other) sender and could archive a message whose original action was deliberately blank/protected. This is the inverse of the model docstring's intent (lines 71-74: sender REQUIRED for the protected gate, blank = fail closed). No production caller merges today, but the public API undermines the headline never-archive invariant if used. Fix: do not coalesce sender across messages; require equal message_id (and ideally equal sender) before merging.

findings.title: VIP tier and tier_config can disagree when VIP tier is outside 1-4, yielding wrong folder/star/inbox behavior
findings.description: For a VIP, `tier = vip.tier` is taken verbatim and `tier_config = PRIORITY_TIERS.get(tier, PRIORITY_TIERS[1])`. If a VIP is configured with an out-of-range tier (e.g. 0, 5, or negative), result.tier carries the bad value (5) while tier_config silently falls back to the tier-1 config (folder Action/Critical, star=True). Reproduced: add_vip_sender('boss', tier=5) -> result.tier=5 but result.tier_config.number=1. Downstream code that trusts tier_config.folder/star will treat a 'tier 5' email as Critical, and code that branches on result.tier (e.g. escalation, reporting) sees an undefined tier. add_vip_sender does not validate the tier argument. (Same get(..., PRIORITY_TIERS[4]) fallback at line 1013 masks an unknown rule tier instead of surfacing it.)

findings.location: lines 1176-1182 (calculate_email_age_hours: local import + replace(tzinfo=utc)) vs line 8 module-level import
findings.title: calculate_email_age_hours assumes naive provider dates are UTC, but providers populate naive LOCAL dates (e.g. email Date: header parse), inflating/deflating age by the UTC offset
findings.description: When `email_date.tzinfo is None`, the function does `email_date.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)` — i.e. it ASSUMES naive means UTC. But the realistic source of a naive date in this codebase is a future provider parsing an RFC822 `Date:` header or an IMAP INTERNALDATE that has been stripped to naive *local* time, or the macOS Mail.app AppleScript path (which returns local-zone dates). Treating a naive *local* timestamp as UTC skews the computed age by the machine's UTC offset (up to ±14h), which can flip an email across the 24h/72h escalation boundaries in escalate_by_age. The assume-UTC is documented as intentional in the test (test_naive_datetime_assumed_utc) but the assumption is wrong for the local-time providers; the safe behavior is to localize using the machine tz or to require providers to attach tzinfo. Round 1 covered the None->0 conflation and negative-age cases but not the naive-LOCAL-mislabeled-as-UTC skew.

findings.title: Failed state save is swallowed; run continues believing progress is persisted
findings.description: The except Exception around the write only logs an error and returns None. Callers (cli.py, gmail_labeler.py) get no signal that the checkpoint failed to persist. The run proceeds consuming pages from the provider while the on-disk token is stale, so a later crash resumes from a much earlier point and reprocesses (or skips, if total_processed got out of sync) a large range of messages. A broad except also masks programming errors (e.g. a non-serializable object in history). At minimum the failure should propagate or be returned to the caller.

findings.title: _load does not validate that loaded JSON is a dict with expected keys
findings.description: json.load() can return any JSON type (a list, a string, an int) if the state file was overwritten with valid-but-wrong JSON. _load returns it verbatim without checking it is a dict. Subsequent self.state.get(...) calls and self.state['next_page_token'] = ... assignments will raise AttributeError/TypeError (e.g. on a loaded list) on the next save/getter, crashing rather than falling back to defaults. Also, a partially-written file that happens to parse as valid JSON (e.g. truncated to a smaller valid object) would silently load a stale/incomplete token. Recommend isinstance(data, dict) check and merge against _default_state() to guarantee all keys exist.

findings.title: Overly broad except in _load masks non-JSON load errors and silently discards prior state
findings.description: _load catches `Exception` (in addition to JSONDecodeError) and returns _default_state() on ANY error — including a programming bug, an unexpected encoding error, or a permission error reading an otherwise-valid file. The effect is that a transiently unreadable but valid state file (e.g. momentary permission/lock issue) is silently treated as 'no prior state', discarding all saved progress and restarting from scratch, with only a logger.error that a non-interactive scheduled run will not see. A read failure on an existing file should arguably be distinguished from 'file genuinely corrupt' so resumable progress is not thrown away on a transient error.

findings.title: RESET_STATE default 'true' wipes GmailLabeler resume state, and save(None,0,{}) may not match how run() reads token
findings.description: By default RESET_STATE is true, so `app.state_manager.save(None, 0, {})` is called, overwriting the shared gmail_state.json (the same STATE_FILE the main labeler uses) with empty state. If a user has a long-running labeler pass in progress with a saved page_token, running final_sweep.py silently discards that resume token. The two scripts share one state file; there is no per-query state isolation, so cross-contamination between the 'Uncategorized' sweep and the main 'has:nouserlabels' pass is real (the comment at line 8 acknowledges 'stale page tokens from other queries' as the very risk being papered over).

findings.title: Headless/hosted credential acquisition silently blocks on a local-server OAuth flow
findings.description: When no valid token exists (no stored token, or refresh fell through), get_credentials unconditionally calls InstalledAppFlow.run_local_server(port=0), which opens a browser and blocks waiting for an interactive consent redirect. In the MCP/REST hosted context (mcp_server/server.py http_app, api/service.py run_triage -> get_provider -> connect), there is no browser and no human; the call will hang/block the request thread (and likely fail in a container with no display). There is no env guard (e.g. an interactive=False / headless mode) to make this fail fast with a clear error instead of blocking. Combined with provider connect() wrapping, this can manifest as an opaque hang rather than a clean 503.

findings.title: OAuth refresh-token written to 1Password via command-line argument 'field=value' — secret exposed in process args
findings.description: store_token_info builds `op item edit item field=value` where value is the full compact token JSON (contains the refresh_token). Passing a secret as a command-line argument exposes it to any local process that can read /proc or `ps` for the lifetime of the op subprocess. 1Password CLI supports reading secrets from stdin/files for exactly this reason. The refresh token is long-lived; leaking it grants persistent mailbox access.

findings.title: Page-token resumption with a draining/destructive query can skip messages or resume against a shifted result set
findings.description: When --remove-label is used (or any query whose match-set shrinks as the run modifies messages), the result set shifts under pagination. For the default 'has:nouserlabels' query the code saves and resumes via page tokens (lines 285-286, 321-322). But labeling unlabeled messages REMOVES them from the 'has:nouserlabels' result set, so a saved nextPageToken from a prior run points into a result set that has since shifted — Gmail may skip an unknown number of messages between the last processed page and the token's position, leaving some unlabeled messages permanently unprocessed across resumed runs. The author's own comment (lines 272-282) acknowledges page tokens are 'dangerous' for draining queries yet the code still persists and reuses them for the default query, which IS a draining query. Result: silent incomplete coverage on resume.

findings.title: Legacy categorize_email uses unguarded header['name']/['value'] and a catch-all label 'Uncategorized' that is never created elsewhere consistently
findings.description: categorize_email (line 345-350) does header['name'] / header['value'] direct subscript inside the loop — a malformed header dict KeyErrors and is caught only by the broad per-email except at line 441 (which prints and continues, silently dropping the message). Also the catch-all is 'Uncategorized' (line 282) here whereas core/rules.py renamed it to 'Misc/Other'; run_automation.sh / cli.py sweep on 'label:Misc/Other'. If a user runs the legacy gmail_labeler.py directly, unmatched mail lands in 'Uncategorized', which the modern sweep query never targets — orphaned, never re-swept. Taxonomy split-brain between legacy and core.

findings.title: COPY failure returns False but message is left in place — but STORE/EXPUNGE results unchecked on success path
findings.description: After COPY succeeds, the code issues STORE +FLAGS \Deleted and expunge() without checking their return codes (lines 75-76). If STORE fails (e.g. read-only selection, server error) the message was already COPIED to Archive, so a failed delete leaves a duplicate: one in Archive and one still in INBOX, while the function returns True (reported as 'moved'). Silent duplication / inconsistent state.

findings.title: If COPY succeeds but STORE/EXPUNGE step errors, message is duplicated or the function returns True without confirming deletion
findings.description: After a successful COPY (line 72-74), the code does STORE +\Deleted and expunge (lines 75-76) but checks NEITHER return status, then unconditionally `return True` (line 77). If the STORE or EXPUNGE fails (e.g. transient error), the original remains in INBOX while a copy now exists in Archive — a duplicate — yet archive_uid reports success (moved += 1). The caller's success count is then wrong and the user believes the inbox was drained when it was only copied. No verification that the source actually left INBOX.

findings.title: Folder/label names interpolated unquoted/unescaped into IMAP commands (folders with spaces or quotes break or inject)
findings.description: apply_label builds raw IMAP arguments by f-string interpolation: imap.uid('STORE', uid, '+X-GM-LABELS', f'"{label}"') and imap.uid('COPY', uid, f'"{label}"'). Labels from LABEL_RULES contain '/' and spaces (e.g. 'Awaiting Reply', 'AI/Data Exports', 'Misc/Other'). Wrapping in double quotes handles spaces but a label containing a double-quote or backslash would break the IMAP literal/quoting and could corrupt the command. ensure_label (line 98) passes the bare label to imap.create() with no quoting at all, so 'Awaiting Reply' (with a space) is sent unquoted and will likely fail or create the wrong mailbox. Current LABEL_RULES names are controlled, but this is a latent injection/quoting bug if names ever include user input or special chars, and 'Awaiting Reply' / 'AI/Data Exports' already contain spaces that the unquoted create() mishandles.

findings.title: Messages are marked \Seen as a side effect of labeling, and STORE/COPY results are never checked
findings.description: apply_label unconditionally marks every processed message as read (`imap.uid('STORE', uid, '+FLAGS', r'(\Seen)')`, line 110) for every categorized message, including ones that just got copied to a folder. The default --query is 'ALL' (line 116) and default mailbox INBOX, so a routine run will mark the entire inbox as read — a surprising, hard-to-undo side effect that is not mentioned in the CLI help. Additionally the return tuples from imap.uid('STORE'/'COPY') are never inspected, so a failed COPY (e.g. nonexistent folder) or failed STORE is silently ignored — the script reports success in stats regardless of whether the IMAP operation actually applied.

findings.title: apply_label ignores IMAP command status and force-marks \Seen on every processed message
findings.description: apply_label issues imap.uid('STORE'/'COPY', ...) and never checks the (res, data) return — a COPY to a non-existent folder or a STORE failure is silently ignored, so a message may be marked \Seen (line 110) without actually being labeled/copied, losing the unread state for mail that was never filed. ensure_label treats both 'OK' and 'NO' as success (line 99) but a 'NO' can mean a real create failure (permissions, invalid name) not just 'already exists', after which COPY to that folder fails silently. Marking \Seen unconditionally on triage is also a behavior change the user may not expect (it clears unread badges for everything scanned).

findings.title: Advertises /v1/audit/{run_id} 'receipt verification' that the live worker returns unsigned
findings.description: Listing receipt verification as an agent capability while the live endpoint returns a fabricated, unsigned, hardcoded body means any agent that verifies receipts is misled into trusting an unverifiable artifact.

findings.title: Unbounded while-loop relies on each batchModify shrinking the result set; no nextPageToken and no progress guard
findings.description: The loop lists `maxResults=1000` unread items, removes UNREAD, then `if len(ids) < 1000: break`. It depends entirely on removing UNREAD shrinking the `is:unread` query result. This generally works, but if a batchModify partially fails or Gmail eventual-consistency keeps returning the same 1000 IDs, the loop re-issues the identical query and never terminates. There is no max-iteration cap, no dry-run, and no protected-sender consideration (acknowledged in docstring as marking READ only).

findings.title: Pagination loop never advances pageToken — relies on side-effect of removing UNREAD to terminate
findings.description: The while loop calls messages().list(q='label:X is:unread older_than:7d', maxResults=1000) repeatedly WITHOUT a pageToken. It terminates only because each batchModify removes UNREAD so the same query returns fewer results next pass. If a batchModify partially fails or a message can't lose UNREAD (e.g. system constraint), the identical query keeps returning the same >=1000 ids and the loop spins forever. The `if len(ids) < 1000: break` is the only secondary guard and is bypassed whenever exactly 1000 are returned. No error handling around batchModify.

findings.title: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS entries with a URL scheme produce malformed origins (http://https://host) and never match
findings.description: Lines 51-53 treat each MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS entry as a bare hostname and unconditionally build origins as f'http://{h}' and f'https://{h}'. The SDK requires exact origin/Host matches. If an operator follows the natural reading and supplies a value with a scheme (e.g. MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='https://app.example.com', which is how an Origin/URL is usually written), then: (1) the host entry 'https://app.example.com' can never equal a real Host header (which is hostname[:port] only), so Host validation 421s; and (2) the derived origins become the malformed 'http://https://app.example.com' / 'https://https://app.example.com', which never match a legitimate Origin header, so Origin validation 403s. There is no normalization or validation of the supplied tokens, turning a plausible mis-entry into a total endpoint outage with a confusing error. NEW relative to round 1 (which only flagged the '*' disable case).

findings.location: check_protected_sender line 94 (sender[:4096], subject[:4096]) vs api/schemas.py SenderCheckRequest max_length=4096
findings.title: MCP check_protected_sender truncates to 4096 chars silently rather than rejecting, diverging from the API's validation contract
findings.description: The HTTP API bounds sender/subject with Pydantic max_length=4096 and REJECTS longer input (schemas.py lines 29-30). The MCP tool instead silently truncates sender[:4096], subject[:4096] (server.py line 94) before passing to service.check_sender. The same logical input therefore yields a 422 on the REST surface but a (truncated, possibly wrong) 'protected' answer on the MCP surface — two surfaces over the same engine give different protection verdicts for an over-length sender. Silent truncation of the value feeding the protected-sender gate can also drop the very domain that would have matched a .gov/financial anchor, weakening the safety verdict for that input.

findings.title: INBOX strip in _drop_if_protected matches only 'INBOX'/'\\INBOX' but remove path matches the same; a lowercase or aliased inbox label could leak past the gate
findings.description: _drop_if_protected strips remove_labels where lbl.upper() in ('INBOX','\\INBOX'). The audit/leave-inbox detection in apply_actions (line 351) uses the same comparison. This covers Gmail INBOX and IMAP \Inbox. But for a provider that names the inbox differently (e.g. Outlook 'Inbox' folder, or a localized inbox name), a remove_labels entry for that inbox would NOT be stripped for a protected sender, so the protected message could be moved out of inbox. The gate is inbox-name-specific and not capability-aware. Folder providers rely on action.archive (which IS cleared) rather than remove_labels, so the practical risk is limited, but the gate's coverage is narrower than its docstring's 'NEVER moved out of inbox' claim. Medium-confidence invariant gap.

findings.title: remove_label falls back to raw label NAME as label ID on cache miss, sending an invalid removeLabelIds value
findings.description: In remove_label: `label_id = self._label_cache.get(label) or label`. When a hierarchical user label (e.g. 'Misc/Other') is not in the cache, the code falls back to using the human-readable NAME ('Misc/Other') as the Gmail label ID. Gmail's modify/batchModify API requires the opaque label ID (e.g. 'Label_42'), not the display name, for user labels; passing the name yields HttpError 400 invalidArgument. System labels (INBOX, STARRED, UNREAD, etc.) happen to use their name as the ID so they work, but user labels do not. This means a remove of an uncached user label silently fails (caught and logged at 328-330, returns False) or aborts a whole batchModify chunk (apply_actions:469-472). Since the label cache is only populated at connect() and on create, removing a pre-existing user label that wasn't created this session can fail. Medium/high-confidence correctness bug on the remove path.

findings.title: apply_label single-path and apply_actions batch-path enforce protected gate inconsistently
findings.description: apply_label() (single) performs NO protected-sender gate — it just applies the label. The protected gate lives only in apply_actions() and base._drop_if_protected. Since Gmail labels are additive this is acceptable for label adds, BUT apply_label is also reused by star() (label STARRED) and indirectly nothing stops a caller from invoking apply_label/remove_label('INBOX')/archive() directly to archive a protected sender, bypassing the gate entirely. The headline invariant ('protected sender NEVER archived') is only enforced at the apply_actions chokepoint, not at archive()/remove_label() which are public methods. archive() -> remove_label('INBOX') has no protected check. Medium-confidence invariant gap (depends on whether callers ever bypass apply_actions; cli.py uses apply_actions, but escalate/other paths may call archive directly).

findings.title: remove_label falls back to using the raw label string as a label ID when not in cache, which can no-op or error silently
findings.description: remove_label does 'label_id = self._label_cache.get(label) or label'. If the human label name (e.g. 'Misc/Other') is not cached, it sends the literal NAME as a removeLabelIds value. Gmail's batchModify/modify expects label IDs, not names, for user labels — removing by name silently does nothing (no error for a non-matching id in removeLabelIds) so the remove_label intent is lost while the call still 'succeeds' (returns True). Only works by accident for system labels whose id == name (INBOX, STARRED). This means a --remove-label of a user label that wasn't pre-cached is a silent no-op.

findings.title: Gmail batchModify is treated as all-or-nothing, but invalid ids within a chunk fail the whole call, marking valid messages in the chunk as errored
findings.description: batchModify with a chunk of up to 1000 ids is a single call; if ANY id in the body is invalid (e.g. a message deleted between list and modify, or a malformed id), Gmail returns a 400 for the entire batchModify, not a per-item result. The except at 469-472 then counts the ENTIRE chunk as error_count and none of the chunk's messages are recorded as applied (succeeded set unchanged) — so a single bad id strands up to 999 valid messages as 'failed'/unprocessed in this run, and the audit at 487-495 records all of them archived=...and False (applied=False), i.e. labels_added=[]. There is no per-id isolation or fall-back to smaller chunks. Round 1 covered the late-binding body closure and dry-run accounting but not the per-chunk poison-pill behavior where one bad id discards a whole 1000-message batch.

findings.location: apply_actions pending dict lines 434/484-487 (keyed by action.message_id)
findings.title: Gmail audit recording keyed by message_id collides on duplicate actions, dropping audit records for all but the last action on a given id
findings.description: `pending` is a dict keyed by `action.message_id` (line 434). If `actions` contains more than one LabelAction for the same message_id (e.g. when LabelAction.merge did not coalesce them — round 1 already flagged merge's weak message_id handling in core/models.py), the later assignment overwrites the earlier, and the post-execution loop (lines 483-495) records exactly one disposition per unique message_id via `pending.get(action.message_id)`. The audit therefore under-counts: it cannot witness two distinct operations applied to the same message, and if one of the colliding actions was the protected/violating one and the other overwrote it in `pending`, the violation disposition can be lost. The 'every action gets a receipt' independence claim assumes one record per applied operation, but this path emits at most one per message_id. The base.py provider loop (which records per-action) does not have this collision, making Gmail's batch override inconsistent with the base behavior.

findings.title: IMAP SEARCH query not encoded/quoted; multi-word criteria and non-ASCII break or inject extra search tokens
findings.description: The query is passed as a single positional arg to imaplib's uid('search', None, query). imaplib splits/handles args literally; a query containing spaces (e.g. 'FROM example.com SUBJECT "x"') is sent as one literal token rather than properly tokenized criteria, and there is no CHARSET handling for non-ASCII search terms (charset arg is None). This produces wrong search results or protocol errors. No quoting of values containing spaces.

findings.title: Pagination offset math is inconsistent between the slice and the next_token, causing skipped/overlapping pages
findings.description: The slice `uids[max(0, total-start-limit):total-start]` returns the most-recent window working backward by `start`. But `next_token = start + limit`. On page 1 (start=0) it returns uids[total-limit:total] (newest `limit`). next token = limit. Page 2 (start=limit) returns uids[total-2*limit:total-limit]. That is internally consistent for walking backward. HOWEVER the returned slice size is `limit` only when `start` is a multiple of `limit`; if a caller passes an arbitrary page_token (or limit changes between calls) the window and the token diverge and messages are skipped or repeated. Also when `start>=total` the slice `uids[max(0,neg):negative]` yields empty correctly, but `total_estimate` and token semantics are not robust to limit changes. Fragile pagination contract. allow-secret

findings.title: Slice stop index total-start can become negative when start>total, silently yielding wrong/empty window
findings.description: `uids[max(0, total - start - limit):total - start]`. The start index is clamped with max(0,...) but the STOP index `total - start` is not clamped. If a stale/large page_token makes start>total, the stop becomes negative, so the slice is `uids[0:negative]` = all-but-last-|negative| items rather than empty — returning unintended messages instead of an empty page. Negative stop index is a real off-by/sign bug under bad tokens.

findings.title: fetch HEADER parsing assumes data[0] is a 2-tuple; raw/atom responses raise TypeError unhandled
findings.description: `email.message_from_bytes(data[0][1])` after only checking `data[0] is None`. For some servers/responses data[0] may be a bytes literal (not a (b'...', b'...') tuple), making data[0][1] an int/index error, or data may contain multiple parts. Unlike the X-GM-LABELS/FLAGS parsing below (which guards `isinstance(label_data[0], tuple)`), the header fetch does not guard the tuple shape, so a non-tuple response raises an unhandled exception that propagates out of get_message_details (which the base batch_get_details does not catch per-item).

findings.title: X-GM-LABELS value not IMAP-quoted/escaped; labels with spaces, quotes, or backslashes corrupt the STORE command
findings.description: The Gmail label is interpolated as `f'"{label}"'`. If a label contains a double-quote it breaks the quoting; labels with spaces are handled by the quotes but labels containing IMAP-special chars or backslashes (e.g. system labels like \Inbox vs user labels) are not escaped. Notably archive() calls remove_label(message_id, "\\Inbox") which becomes the literal STORE value `"\Inbox"` — Gmail expects the system label token, and double-quoting a backslash-prefixed system label may not match. Quoting/escaping of label values is not robust.

findings.title: X-GM-LABELS parsing regex `\(([^)]*)\)` is wrong for labels containing parentheses or quoted spaces; mis-splits labels
findings.description: The regex `re.search(r'\(([^)]*)\)', raw_str)` grabs the first parenthesized group, then `match.group(1).split()` splits on whitespace and strips quotes. This breaks for: (a) labels that themselves contain spaces inside quotes (split on whitespace shatters them into multiple labels), (b) the IMAP UID being inside its own parentheses earlier in the response (the regex may match the wrong group), and (c) labels containing ')' (the `[^)]*` stops early). Label detection (incl. \Starred) is therefore unreliable.

findings.title: IMAP command/argument injection via unescaped label and mailbox names
