findings.description: Label/folder names are interpolated into IMAP commands as quoted literals via f-strings without escaping the quote or backslash: `self._connection.uid('STORE', message_id, '+X-GM-LABELS', f'"{label}"')` and `self._connection.uid('COPY', message_id, f'"{label}"')`. A label containing a double-quote or CRLF can terminate the quoted-string argument and inject additional IMAP command tokens (IMAP literal/quoted-string injection), potentially altering the command (e.g. applying a different label, or smuggling a second command via CRLF where the server tolerates it). `self._connection.create(label)` and `select(mailbox)` pass names unquoted/unescaped too. message_id is also passed raw to STORE/COPY/FETCH. Labels are currently from a static taxonomy, but the code accepts arbitrary strings; an attacker-influenced folder name (synced from server) reaching these paths is exploitable. Use imaplib's proper quoting or validate/escape names.

findings.title: IMAP pagination slice can drop/duplicate messages and mis-handle offsets
findings.description: Pagination computes `uids = uids[max(0, total - start - limit):total - start]` to take most-recent-first. When `start` exceeds `total` (e.g. caller passes a stale large page_token), `total - start` is negative and the slice `uids[...: negative]` indexes from the end, returning an unintended window of messages rather than an empty page. Also next_token is computed as `start + limit` compared to total, independent of how many were actually returned, so it can return a non-None next token that then yields the negative-index window. This is an off-by-context pagination bug that can re-process or skip messages.

findings.title: IMAP STORE/COPY return code not checked; broad except returns success/failure inconsistently
findings.description: Several IMAP operations ignore the result tuple: e.g. star/unstar/mark_read/mark_unread call `self._connection.uid('STORE', ...)` inside try and return True unconditionally even if the server returns 'NO'/'BAD' (only an exception is caught; imaplib returns ('NO', ...) without raising for command-level failures in some cases). So a failed flag/label op reports success. archive() in standard-IMAP mode does `uid('COPY', ...)` then on OK marks Deleted and returns True, but does not expunge — leaving the message both in source and flagged deleted (inconsistent with icloud_triage which expunges). These cause over-reporting of success.

findings.title: Standard-IMAP archive sets \Deleted without EXPUNGE and reports success, leaving the message in the inbox flagged-deleted
findings.description: For non-Gmail IMAP, archive() does COPY to 'Archive' then STORE +FLAGS (\Deleted) on the original, returning True if the COPY succeeded. It never EXPUNGEs, so the original remains in the selected mailbox (merely flagged deleted, typically still listed) — the message has NOT actually left the inbox, yet archive() returns True and base.apply_actions records did_leave_inbox=True (ARCHIVED) for the audit. This both mis-reports the disposition and risks unexpected permanent deletion later if any other code path issues an EXPUNGE on that mailbox. Also the COPY target is a hardcoded literal 'Archive' that may not exist on arbitrary servers.

findings.title: IMAP folder/label names interpolated unquoted-or-naively into IMAP commands (command injection / breakage)
findings.description: X-GM-LABELS and COPY/CREATE use f'"{label}"' (Gmail) or pass label directly (CREATE). A label containing a double-quote or CRLF is not escaped, so it can break the IMAP literal or inject a second IMAP command on the wire. Labels here can be hierarchical category names; a crafted name corrupts the protocol stream. Standard IMAP CREATE passes the raw label with no quoting at all.

findings.title: 'first message whose id is' searches only current/default mailbox context, may target wrong or missing message
findings.description: `first message whose id is {message_id}` in get_message_details, star, unstar, mark_read, mark_unread, and apply_label does not scope by mailbox or account. In Mail.app `message` without an explicit container resolves against an ambiguous default scope; ids are not globally unique across accounts/mailboxes, so 'first message whose id is X' can match a different message than the one listed (list_messages scoped to a specific mailbox/account), or raise if not in the default scope. This can cause operations to hit the wrong message or fail silently (returns False/None). apply_label additionally only scopes the destination mailbox by account, not the source message lookup.

findings.title: Mailbox marked as created even when AppleScript fails, caching a false 'exists' state
findings.description: In ensure_label_exists, `self._created_mailboxes.add(label)` runs unconditionally right after `_run_applescript`, but if creation actually failed the except branch logs the error AFTER the add already... actually the add is inside the try before the if, so on a RuntimeError the add never runs (good). However, on the 'exists' path AND the 'created' path the label is cached; if the make-mailbox silently produced a different effective name (hierarchical '/' names in Mail.app create literal-named mailboxes, not nested), subsequent move-to-mailbox by the same '/' name may not resolve. Hierarchical label names like 'Work/Dev/GitHub' are passed verbatim as a single mailbox name; Mail.app does not interpret '/' as nesting, so apply_label's `mailbox "Work/Dev/GitHub"` and the created mailbox may mismatch behavior expectations.

findings.title: Per-page O(n) message indexing causes quadratic cost and re-fetch of full message list each page
findings.description: The script does `set allMsgs to messages of targetMailbox` then `item i of allMsgs` per page. Fetching `messages of targetMailbox` materializes the entire mailbox each call, and pagination re-runs this for every page — O(total) per page, O(total^2) overall for a full sweep. For large mailboxes this is a severe performance problem and can hit the 30s osascript timeout, after which list_messages returns an empty result (silent truncation of the sweep).

findings.title: get_message_details TSV parse mis-handles sender/subject containing tabs (same delimiter-collision as list_messages)
findings.description: The detail script returns sender & '\t' & subject & '\t' & ... and python does output.split('\t') then `len(parts) < 5` guard. A sender or subject containing a tab shifts fields, so is_read/is_flagged/mailbox map to wrong tokens, producing wrong flags and wrong 'labels' set. Same unescaped-delimiter root cause as the list path.

findings.location: apply_label line 266, ensure_label_exists lines 338/342, list_messages account_filter line 133/261/332/409
findings.title: AppleScript injection: label/account/message_id interpolated unescaped into osascript
findings.description: Multiple f-string AppleScript bodies interpolate values directly into the script that is passed to `osascript -e`, e.g. `set targetMailbox to mailbox "{label}" {account_filter}` and `make new mailbox with properties {{name:"{label}"}}`, and `account_filter = f'of account "{self.account}"'`. A label or account name containing a double-quote (or AppleScript metacharacters) breaks out of the string literal and injects arbitrary AppleScript that runs with the user's Mail.app/automation privileges. `label` originates from the categorization taxonomy (mostly trusted) but `self.account` comes from config/CLI (`--account`) and labels can be supplied via custom_rules/config; message_id is interpolated as a bare numeric token (line 265/297/313) and is provider-supplied. This is the AppleScript analogue of shell/SQL injection and is not escaped anywhere. Note this is also reachable from the API path (mailapp provider -> service.run_triage).

findings.title: AppleScript tab/linefeed-delimited parsing breaks on senders/subjects containing tabs or newlines
findings.description: list_messages emits records joined by tab and split by '\t', and lines split by '\n'. An email subject or sender containing a literal tab or newline (legal in display names / RFC2047 folded headers that AppleScript returns) will produce extra fields, so `parts[:5]` mis-aligns sender/subject/read/flagged, yielding wrong is_read/is_starred and garbled sender — which then feeds the protected-sender gate. Silent data-corruption rather than a crash. The `---TOTAL:` sentinel could also collide with a subject literally starting with '---TOTAL:'.

findings.title: 30s osascript timeout with no recovery; TimeoutExpired loses partial output and orphan process risk
findings.description: subprocess.run(..., timeout=30) on a large mailbox (list_messages iterates messages of a mailbox in AppleScript) can routinely exceed 30s; on TimeoutExpired the function raises RuntimeError('AppleScript timed out') and the partially-run AppleScript may have already mutated state (for move/flag scripts) with no record of what completed. list_messages catches RuntimeError and returns an empty result, which the caller may interpret as 'mailbox empty' / 'done', silently truncating processing.

findings.title: Inner find-folder failure raises a misleading 'Failed to create or find folder' for the whole label, not the failing segment
findings.description: The inner `except Exception: raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to create or find folder: {label}")` at 648-649 only fires when the _api_get itself throws. But when _api_get succeeds and simply returns an empty value list (folder genuinely not found and not created), no error is raised at all — the loop proceeds with stale parent_id. So a genuine 'cannot find/create' condition does NOT raise (it silently degrades per the previous finding), while a transient GET error DOES raise. The error-handling logic is inverted relative to intent: the real failure path is silent and the transient path is fatal.

findings.title: $top passed unbounded to Graph; values >1000 are rejected/clamped, and large $top with $orderby+$filter can 400
findings.description: $top is set directly to `limit` with no clamp. Microsoft Graph caps $top at 1000 for messages; a caller passing limit>1000 (e.g. `escalate --limit 5000` in the CLI) yields a 400 InvalidRequest. Additionally, combining $orderby with certain $filter expressions on messages requires special handling and can return 400 unless the filtered/sorted property matches; the code always sends $orderby=receivedDateTime desc together with an arbitrary $filter query, which Graph rejects for some filter properties. Both failure modes are swallowed by the except at 438-440 returning an empty ListMessagesResult, which the CLI treats as 'No more messages' (cli.py 192-194) — a silent early termination rather than an error.

findings.title: Unbounded recursive child-folder fetch with no depth limit or cycle/duplicate-name guard; deep trees risk perf/stack and name collisions overwrite cache entries
findings.description: _fetch_child_folders recurses into every child folder with $top:100 and no depth cap. A deeply nested or very wide mailbox produces N API calls serially (slow, and each swallows errors at 273 so partial failures are invisible). Folder full_name keys are built as parent_name/child (270); two different folders sharing the same path string (possible across separate top-level trees, or duplicate displayNames) overwrite each other in _folder_cache, so ensure_label_exists/get_message_details may resolve to the wrong folder ID. Also $top:100 with no nextLink follow means mailboxes with >100 top-level or >100 child folders silently truncate (pagination not followed here, unlike list_messages).

findings.title: Token cache file written/read with default (world-readable) permissions — OAuth refresh token at rest is not protected
findings.description: The MSAL SerializableTokenCache is written via open(self.token_cache_path, 'w') with no mode restriction (167) and read with plain open (153). On a multi-user system the cache (default ~/.outlook_token_cache.json) contains refresh/access tokens and is created with the process umask (commonly 0644 = world-readable). Best practice is to chmod 0600. The file also is not written atomically (no temp-file-then-rename), so an interrupted write (crash/kill mid-serialize) can corrupt the token cache, forcing re-auth or — worse — leaving a truncated JSON that deserialize() at 154 will choke on (raising and aborting _get_msal_app on the next run).

findings.title: acquire_token_silent error result not surfaced; falls through to interactive prompt in headless/automated runs
findings.description: acquire_token_silent can return a dict with an 'error' (e.g. invalid_grant / interaction_required) rather than None. The code only checks `if result and 'access_token' in result` (178); any error result falls through to acquire_token_interactive (184). For the documented launchd/automation use case (run_automation.sh, daily 9 AM), there is no interactive console/browser, so acquire_token_interactive will block waiting on a browser/redirect that never comes, hanging or failing the scheduled job rather than logging a clear 'token expired, re-auth needed' error. No device-code or silent-only mode for headless operation.

findings.title: tier_routing target_folder is never honored by Outlook move path — tier folder routing is effectively a no-op for the actual move
findings.description: Tier routing sets action.target_folder = tier_config.folder (cli.py 257), but apply_actions (base.py) only iterates action.add_labels and calls apply_label(message_id, label) — it never reads target_folder. For Outlook, apply_label moves the message to the folder named by `label` (the categorization label like 'Work/Dev/GitHub'), NOT to the tier's Action/Critical|Important|Delegate folder. So the documented 'Action folders' behavior (CLAUDE.md tier table: 'Action/Critical folder' etc.) does not actually move messages to Action folders on Outlook — only the category color is applied. This is a cross-file gap but it makes the outlook tier-routing feature partially non-functional.

findings.title: MSAL OAuth token cache written to disk with default (potentially world-readable) permissions
findings.description: The Outlook provider serializes the MSAL token cache (containing the OAuth access AND refresh tokens for the user's mailbox) to self.token_cache_path (default ~/.outlook_token_cache.json) via `open(path, 'w')` with no os.chmod to 0600 and no umask hardening. On a shared/multi-user host or a misconfigured umask, the refresh token — which grants long-lived mailbox access — can be readable by other local users. A grep confirmed there is no chmod/0o600/umask anywhere in the codebase. The Gmail flow avoids this by storing in 1Password, but the Outlook path persists secrets to a plaintext file with inherited permissions.

findings.title: Folder create/find fallback can silently return the parent or raw label as the destination id, risking a move to the wrong folder
findings.description: In the per-segment loop, on create failure it tries to find the existing folder by $filter displayName eq '{part}'. The $filter value interpolates 'part' unescaped — a folder/label name containing a single quote breaks the OData filter (ODATA injection / 400). If the find also fails or returns empty, the inner 'except Exception: raise RuntimeError' only fires for the GET exception; if result.get('value') is empty (no match, no exception) the loop continues WITHOUT updating parent_id, and the final 'return self._folder_cache.get(label, label)' returns the raw LABEL STRING as the folder id. apply_label then POSTs move with destinationId=<label string>, which Graph will reject (caught -> False) — so it fails safe-ish, but a partially-created hierarchy can leave parent_id pointing at an intermediate folder and a subsequent segment created under the wrong parent. Unescaped OData $filter is the concrete defect.

findings.title: apply_category swallows the read-current-categories error and proceeds with empty list, silently dropping existing categories
findings.description: Before PATCHing categories it GETs current ones; on any exception 'current_cats = []'. It then appends the new category to that empty list and PATCHes {categories: [new]} — which OVERWRITES and thus REMOVES all of the message's pre-existing categories whenever the read failed (throttling, transient). The read failure is silently swallowed (bare 'except Exception: current_cats = []' with no log). So a transient GET error causes silent data loss of other categories on the message. Should at least log, and ideally abort the patch rather than overwrite with a partial set.

findings.title: Token cache file written without restrictive permissions and silent failure modes around interactive auth in a server context
findings.description: _save_token_cache writes the MSAL token cache (contains refresh tokens) with a plain open(path,'w') and no chmod 0600 — on a shared host the OAuth refresh token is world-readable per the process umask. Additionally _acquire_token falls back to app.acquire_token_interactive (opens a browser) when no cached token — in the hosted API/MCP context (run_triage -> connect) this would block/hang on a headless server rather than failing cleanly; it is only mapped to ProviderUnavailable if it raises, but an interactive prompt may hang instead. The deserialize at lines 152-154 reads the cache with no error handling — a corrupt cache file raises and is uncaught in _get_msal_app.

findings.title: Bare/broad except: pass swallows folder-fetch and create errors silently
findings.description: _fetch_child_folders wraps the recursive fetch in `except Exception: pass`, silently ignoring all errors (auth expiry, rate-limit/429, network) so the folder cache is silently incomplete; later apply_label may then create duplicate folders or move to the wrong place. ensure_label_exists similarly swallows create errors then tries a lookup whose inner except also raises a generic RuntimeError losing the original cause. 429/throttling responses are treated as 'folder probably exists' which can corrupt the folder hierarchy.

findings.title: OAuth token cache written world-readable with no restrictive permissions
findings.description: The MSAL serialized token cache (contains refresh/access tokens) is written with plain `open(self.token_cache_path, 'w')`, inheriting the process umask (commonly 0644 -> world/group readable). A long-lived OAuth refresh token on disk should be 0600. Also the file is read/written without a lock; concurrent runs could corrupt it. Recommend os.open with mode 0o600 or os.chmod after write.

findings.title: Outlook silently swallows date-parse failures, falling back to None and disabling escalation for that message
findings.description: Outlook is the only provider that populates date, but it parses receivedDateTime with `datetime.fromisoformat(...replace('Z','+00:00'))` inside a try/except ValueError that on failure leaves `received = None` (lines 457-458, 508-509). If Microsoft Graph ever returns a fractional-second precision or offset format that fromisoformat (esp. on Python 3.9-3.10, where fromisoformat is stricter than 3.11+) cannot parse, the date is silently dropped, msg.date becomes None, and that message reverts to the same age=0 no-op as the other providers — without any log line. Given CLAUDE.md states Python 3.9+, fromisoformat rejecting some valid ISO-8601 variants on 3.9/3.10 is a realistic failure that would silently degrade escalation on the one provider that supports it. Should log a warning on parse failure.

findings.title: datetime.fromisoformat on Graph receivedDateTime rejects 7-digit fractional seconds on Python <3.11, silently dropping the date
findings.description: Graph API `dateTimeOffset` values are frequently returned with 7 fractional digits (e.g. `2026-01-15T10:00:00.1234567Z`). `datetime.fromisoformat` only accepts 3- or 6-digit fractional seconds before Python 3.11; on 3.9/3.10 (CLAUDE.md states the project targets 'Python 3.9+') the parse raises ValueError, which is caught and swallowed (received=None). The escalation/age pipeline then silently treats the message as ageless (calculate_email_age_hours returns 0). Round 1 flagged the generic silent-swallow-to-None at these lines but not the specific Python-version / fractional-precision defect class that makes the swallow fire systematically on a whole API-version cohort. CI only tests 3.11/3.12 so this is invisible in CI.

findings.title: response.json() on 202/204 (empty body) move/patch responses raises JSONDecodeError, turning a successful move into a reported failure
findings.description: _api_post is used by apply_label's /move and archive's /move. Graph's /move returns the moved message body (200), but some Graph mutation endpoints return 202 Accepted or 204 No Content with an EMPTY body; _api_patch on flag/isRead can return 200 with body, but Graph occasionally returns 204 for PATCH with no representation. response.json() on an empty body raises requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError, which is NOT an HTTPError, so raise_for_status passed but json() throws. apply_label/archive/star/mark_read all catch broad Exception and return False, so a SUCCEEDED mutation is reported as failed (False) — the caller may retry the move (duplicate move attempt) or count it as an error. Round 1 covered broad-except swallowing of errors (628) but not the success-with-empty-body path being misclassified as failure via json() on 204/202.

findings.title: datetime.fromisoformat fails on Graph timestamps with sub-second precision beyond 6 digits or fractional 'Z', silently disabling escalation
findings.description: received = datetime.fromisoformat(msg['receivedDateTime'].replace('Z','+00:00')). On Python 3.9-3.10, datetime.fromisoformat does NOT accept all ISO-8601 forms Graph emits: Graph returns timestamps like '2024-01-01T12:00:00.1234567Z' (7-digit fractional seconds) which fromisoformat rejects on <3.11 (it only accepts 3 or 6 fractional digits). The .replace('Z','+00:00') leaves the 7-digit fraction intact, so fromisoformat raises ValueError, caught at 457-458/508-509 and silently swallowed to date=None — disabling age-based escalation for those messages. Round 1 (#646) noted the swallow-to-None; the NEW root cause is the specific 7-digit-fraction incompatibility with fromisoformat on the documented 3.9 floor, which makes this fire systematically (not just on rare malformed dates).

findings.title: Token bound into the requests.Session Authorization header at first use is never refreshed even though MSAL silent-refresh exists
findings.description: Round 1 (#626) noted the access token is never refreshed mid-run. The NEW mechanism worth calling out precisely: even though _acquire_token() does call acquire_token_silent (which CAN mint a fresh token from the refresh token), it is only invoked once in connect(). _get_session() bakes f'Bearer {self._access_token}' into the Session headers at first call (207-209) and caches the Session, so even if _acquire_token were re-called, the cached Session header would not update. The two caches (self._access_token and self._session.headers) make refresh structurally impossible without disconnect()/reconnect(). A sweep longer than the ~1h access-token lifetime gets 401s that the broad excepts convert to silent per-item False, dropping all remaining mutations.

findings.title: Outlook folder lookup builds an OData $filter via f-string with an unescaped folder name (OData injection / breakage)
findings.description: In the create-or-find fallback, the displayName filter is built as f"displayName eq '{part}'" where part is a label segment derived from the categorization taxonomy / user config. A folder name containing a single quote (e.g. a custom label like "Bob's mail") breaks the OData query (400 from Graph) and a crafted value could alter the filter semantics. This sits on the protected-gate-adjacent label-creation path: a failed find here raises RuntimeError('Failed to create or find folder'), aborting the action. Not flagged in round 1 (Outlook provider was uncovered).

findings.title: $top=limit passed verbatim to Graph with no per-page cap or pagination loop
findings.description: params['$top'] = limit, where limit can be up to MAX_TRIAGE_LIMIT (1000). Microsoft Graph caps $top at 1000 for messages and returns 400 (InvalidArgument) for larger values; more importantly the method returns only a single page (next_page_token from @odata.nextLink) and the engine's limit semantics assume a page size, so a limit>page-size silently truncates results or errors. There is no clamp to Graph's max page size (typically 1000, default 10).

findings.title: MCP server's documented standalone HTTP run command needs uvicorn/starlette that requirements-mcp.txt does not declare
findings.description: requirements-mcp.txt declares only `mcp>=1.2,<2`. But mcp_server/server.py:20 documents the hosted run path as `uvicorn mcp_server.server:http_app`, and the Streamable-HTTP `http_app` is an ASGI app that needs an ASGI server (uvicorn) and Starlette to serve. uvicorn lives ONLY in requirements-api.txt (`uvicorn[standard]`), and Starlette is pulled in only via fastapi (requirements-api.txt). The file's own header says it is "ISOLATED from the core requirements" and instructs `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt` — but the isolation framing plus a separate file invites installing `requirements.txt + requirements-mcp.txt` alone (e.g. an MCP-only deployment), which yields a server with no uvicorn and no Starlette. The MCP HTTP endpoint would be undeployable. The Dockerfile/CI happen to install all three so the hosted deploy is safe, but the standalone-MCP install story is broken.

findings.title: `requests>=2.28.0` floor permits known-vuln versions (CVE-2023-32681 proxy cert leak, CVE-2024-35195 verify=False persistence)
findings.description: The floor `requests>=2.28.0` allows installing 2.28.x/2.29.x/2.30.x. requests <2.31.0 is affected by CVE-2023-32681 (Proxy-Authorization header leaked over HTTPS via redirects to an HTTP target), and requests <2.32.0 is affected by CVE-2024-35195 (a Session that made a request with verify=False keeps skipping verification for all subsequent requests to the same host). This code makes authenticated HTTPS calls to Gmail/Graph and proxies credentials; the floor should be `requests>=2.32.0`. A fresh `pip install -r requirements.txt` with a resolver that picks an old pinned-elsewhere version, or an existing environment, can run a vulnerable requests.

findings.title: `ends with dom` matching is unreliable and `contains` can over-match, risking wrong-folder moves
findings.description: `is_bulk_sender` matches if the sender `contains ("@" & dom)` OR `ends with dom`. The `ends with dom` branch rarely fires correctly because a Mail.app sender string is typically a display form like `Name <user@host>` ending in `>`, not the bare domain; meanwhile `contains` is a substring test, so a configured domain like `news` or a short domain could match unintended senders and `move` legitimate mail into the Newsletters folder. Moves are destructive (mail leaves inbox) and are silently wrapped in a bare try (lines 25-30), so misroutes happen with no diagnostics. Default domain list also contains placeholders (`newsletter.yourfav.com`).

findings.title: Default bulkDomains ships an example domain 'newsletter.yourfav.com' and runs unedited
findings.description: bulkDomains defaults to {"mailchimp.com", "substack.com", "newsletter.yourfav.com"}. The third entry is a placeholder, and the first two are live real domains. Unlike flag_important_senders (whose placeholder list round 1 noted is a no-op), this script's defaults are LIVE: run unedited it will immediately move all mailchimp.com and substack.com mail out of the inbox. Combined with the missing protected-sender gate and the unanchored `ends with`, running the shipped default is an active, destructive operation against real mail with no dry-run guard. Round 1 did not flag the live-default hazard for this file.

findings.title: set -e plus `source` of op env file aborts whole run if any `op read` fails
findings.description: With `set -euo pipefail` active, line 20 `source "$HOME/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh"` runs the op env file in the current shell. If that file performs `op read`/`op run` style command substitutions (the standard 1Password env pattern) and any single lookup returns non-zero (locked vault, expired session, renamed item), `set -e` causes the entire run_automation.sh to exit at the source line before any provider is processed. There is no fallback or partial-degradation path. Consider sourcing in a subshell or temporarily relaxing errexit around the source.

findings.title: Test never exercises tamper-detection on GET, and verification only passes because sign and verify share the same in-process ephemeral key
findings.description: The roundtrip test calls `receipts.verify(rec['signed_body'], rec['signature'])` in-process, where `verify` re-derives the SAME ephemeral key the server used to sign (same process). It therefore cannot catch the cross-process/cross-restart key-rotation defect at all — by construction it always shares the key. It also never asserts that a TAMPERED stored body is rejected by the GET endpoint (the endpoint doesn't recompute, so it couldn't), and never sets RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY, so the durable-key path is entirely uncovered. The suite thus gives false confidence that 'the signature verifies over the signed body' while the two real failure modes (server doesn't verify stored rows; key rotates per process) are untested. test_sign_then_verify_roundtrip (11-17) likewise only proves in-process sign/verify symmetry.

findings.title: No test covers negative-n on consume_credit/add_credits (overdraw-via-negative money bug uncovered)
findings.description: consume_credit issues 'SET run_credits = run_credits - ? WHERE id=? AND run_credits >= ?'. With a negative n (e.g. consume_credit(acct,-5)) the WHERE clause run_credits >= -5 is always true and run_credits - (-5) ADDS credits — a free-credit bug. add_credits(account,-100) similarly debits with no floor (can go negative). The test only exercises positive n and the simple overdraw-by-positive case. The money-path tests never feed negative or zero values, so this entire class of arithmetic-sign abuse is uncovered. Worth a regression test (and the source arguably should reject n<=0).

findings.title: Live audit counts interpolated into innerHTML without escaping (DOM-XSS sink)
findings.description: The dry-run preview renders the API response by interpolating a.protected_held, a.archived, a.moved, a.labeled, a.kept directly into innerHTML (`${a.protected_held ?? 0}` etc.) with NO escapeHtml call. The `?? 0` guard only catches null/undefined, not a string value. These fields come from the unauthenticated POST /v1/triage/preview response (data.audit). When fronted by the canonical FastAPI backend the AuditSummary schema (api/schemas.py:56-57) coerces them to int, mitigating it; but the client places blind trust in response shape. If API_BASE (window.__MAIL_AUTOMATION_API_BASE__, line 677) is ever pointed at a non-FastAPI surface (e.g. the Cloudflare worker mock, a proxy, or an attacker-controlled origin in a share/embed scenario), a string payload like '' is injected and executes. Every other innerHTML insertion in this file uses escapeHtml; these numeric fields are the lone unescaped sinks.

findings.title: categorization.tier interpolated into innerHTML unescaped; server schema does not coerce it
findings.description: In the sender-check handler, `const tier = cat.tier != null ? \`tier ${cat.tier}\` : ""` interpolates cat.tier with no escaping, then it is concatenated into `extra` and written via out.innerHTML (lines 814,816,818). Unlike cat.label (escaped on line 813), tier is raw. The /v1/senders/check response models categorization as `Optional[dict]` (api/schemas.py:36) — a free-form dict that FastAPI does NOT type-coerce — so whatever the categorization engine puts in `tier` passes straight through. A non-numeric/HTML tier value (or a compromised/alternate backend at API_BASE) yields stored/reflected DOM-XSS on the public, credential-free trust surface. The numeric audit counts at least pass through an int-typed schema; tier does not.

findings.title: Checkout redirect assigns server-supplied URL to window.location with no host validation
findings.description: On a successful /v1/billing/checkout response the code does `window.location.href = data.url` with no check that data.url points to the expected Stripe Checkout host. The URL is fully attacker-controlled if the backend is compromised, mis-deployed, or if API_BASE (line 677) is overridden to a hostile origin. A `javascript:` or off-domain URL would execute / phish. Standard Stripe flows return https://checkout.stripe.com/... but the client validates nothing (no scheme/host allowlist), so this is an unvalidated open-redirect sink on a money path.

findings.title: Charge treats only status=='succeeded' as ok, but a PaymentIntent in 'requires_capture' or 'processing' is neither charged-final nor refunded — fulfillment is skipped while funds may be held/captured later
findings.description: Only intent.status == 'succeeded' returns ok=True. With confirm=True and no capture_method specified, default is automatic capture, so 'succeeded' is expected on success. But async payment methods or 3DS can yield 'processing' or 'requires_action'; those return ok=False and the session stays READY, while the PaymentIntent may later transition to succeeded server-side (Stripe captures the money) with no webhook wired here to fulfill. Net: money can settle at Stripe while the buyer is never credited (no async-completion handler for ACP PaymentIntents). For SPT/card-only this is uncommon but the gap exists.

findings.title: ACP product feed 'price' is emitted in cents (100 / 900) which may be misread as dollars by an ACP consumer
findings.description: acp/feed.py:48 sets the feed 'price' = pack['amount_cents'] (api/plans.py: pack_100 amount_cents=100, pack_1000 amount_cents=900), so the committed product_feed.json shows price=100 for '100 verified-safe triage runs' and price=900 for '1,000 ... runs'. Internally this is consistent ($1.00 and $9.00, matching pricing.md lines 17-18). The risk is spec-faithfulness: an ACP/feed consumer that interprets 'price' as a major-unit decimal (a common feed convention, e.g. Google Merchant feeds use '100.00 USD' major units) would read $100 and $900. There is no explicit unit declaration in the feed entry. Because product titles literally read '100' and '1,000', a misinterpreting agent could conflate the run-count with the price. Confirm the ACP 2026-04-17 feed spec's price unit; if it is major units this is a money/pricing bug, if minor units it is correct but undocumented.

findings.title: A session can be re-priced via update after being READY, and amount charged at /complete is recomputed from line_items — but update does not re-validate that items still map to known packs against a tampered stored session
findings.description: update_session recomputes line_items/total_runs only when body.items is provided; otherwise it trusts current['line_items'] and row['data']['total_runs'] read back from the store. complete_session computes amount = grand_total(current['line_items']) from the stored response. Since the stored session data_json is fully under store control and line_item totals are persisted as plain ints, there is no re-derivation of price from the authoritative CREDIT_PACKS at completion time — the charge amount and the credited run count come from whatever was persisted at create/update. If the persisted total_runs and line_item totals ever diverge (e.g. via the update path keeping old line_items but new total_runs, or a future bug), the buyer is charged grand_total(line_items) but credited row total_runs, which can mismatch. The amount-to-fulfill linkage is not re-validated against the catalog at the charge moment.

findings.location: complete_session() lines 273-280 vs 332-337 (status persistence on failure)
findings.title: Payment-failed branch persists session as STATUS_READY but the response includes only the failure message; a concurrent successful /complete and a failed one both persist, last-write-wins can resurrect READY over COMPLETED
findings.description: Two /complete calls for one session (different Idempotency-Keys): call A charges ok, fulfills, persists COMPLETED; call B (slightly later, processing concurrently before A persisted) charges (Stripe dedups -> ok), fulfill_once returns False, persists... actually B would persist COMPLETED too. But if call B's charge fails (decline) while A succeeded, B persists STATUS_READY (line 278) which, if it lands AFTER A's COMPLETED persist, overwrites the session back to READY in the store (save_session is INSERT OR REPLACE). A subsequent GET shows READY for an already-completed+credited session, and another /complete would re-enter charge (Stripe dedup saves the charge, fulfill_once saves the credit, but the state machine is now incoherent). The session status transitions are not guarded by an atomic compare-and-set on current status.

findings.title: No guard against amount <= 0 before charging / fulfilling
findings.description: amount = models.grand_total(current['line_items']) is passed straight to the payment client and used in the order receipt with no check that amount > 0. grand_total sums li['total'] over line items; an empty/zero line-item set yields amount=0. The status guard requires STATUS_READY which requires valid=bool(items) (models.py:79) so a totally empty cart is normally NOT ready, but build_line_items marks valid=False only when an item names no known pack — it does not re-validate amount, and a future zero-priced pack or a pack edited to amount_cents=0 would produce a READY session with amount 0. StripeSPTPaymentClient.charge sends amount=int(0) which Stripe rejects, but NullPaymentClient and test clients would 'succeed,' fulfilling runs for $0. Defensive: reject amount <= 0 before charging. Currently relies entirely on the catalog never having a zero/negative price.

findings.title: On a charge-success-but-fulfill_once-False replay, the session is re-persisted bound to whichever bearer last called /complete, rewriting account_id ownership
findings.description: Every /complete that reaches the success branch calls _persist(..., account_id=account['id']) at line 337 with the CALLER's resolved/created account, even when fulfilled=False (already credited to a different account). Since there is no ownership check, a second bearer's idempotent-ish re-complete rewrites acp_sessions.account_id to the second account (store.save_session INSERT OR REPLACE, line 408) even though the credits and order receipt belong to the first account. The session's recorded owner and the actual fulfillment recipient (acp_fulfillments.account_id) can therefore disagree, corrupting any later reconciliation/audit keyed on acp_sessions.account_id.

findings.title: Checkout creates a brand-new free account when account_id absent but the created account_id is only returned in the JSON, not bound to any session/cookie
findings.description: When req.account_id is falsy, a new account is created and its id is used as client_reference_id and metadata. The new account_id is returned in the response body only. If the front-end ignores it (or the buyer's browser/session doesn't persist it), the grant lands on an orphaned account the user can never authenticate as — paid, but no usable entitlement binding. There is also no idempotency on checkout creation: repeated POSTs (double-click, retry) each create a fresh free account and a fresh Stripe Checkout session, multiplying orphan accounts. Lower severity than a charge bug but it is a money/UX-path correctness gap.

findings.title: Expanded Stripe 'customer'/'subscription' object (dict) would be used as a customer id, causing wrong lookups or a 500
findings.description: obj.get('customer') is assumed to be a string id everywhere (passed to get_account_by_customer and set_subscription(customer_id=...)). If the webhook endpoint's Stripe API version or an expand configuration delivers an expanded customer object, obj['customer'] is a dict. get_account_by_customer would then run SELECT ... WHERE stripe_customer_id = {dict} (always missing, so it falls through to orphan-account creation), and set_subscription(customer_id={dict}) attempts to bind a dict as a SQL parameter -> sqlite3.InterfaceError -> uncaught -> 500 -> the event is never marked processed -> infinite redelivery. Same risk applies to obj.get('subscription') at line 228. A type/shape guard (isinstance str) is absent.

findings.title: Receipt verification recomputes sign(body) from a body reconstructed out of the DB; type coercion on round-trip (dry_run, summary) could break signature match for some receipts
findings.description: sign() canonicalizes with json.dumps(sort_keys, separators). At persist time the signed body's 'summary' is triage_result.get('audit') (a dict possibly containing ints/bools/None). get_receipt reconstructs body from rec where summary = json.loads(summary_json) and dry_run = bool(rec['dry_run']). For the ACP order receipt, save_receipt is called with summary=order_receipt_body['summary'] but the SIGNATURE was computed by receipts.sign(order_receipt_body) over the FULL body {run_id, provider, dry_run, summary, receipt_line}. get_receipt rebuilds exactly those 5 keys, so it matches IF and only if json round-trip preserves every value type. Any float that serialized as e.g. 1.0 vs an int, or a tuple coerced to list in the original audit summary, will not round-trip identically and verify() will FAIL for a legitimately-issued receipt. The audit summary content is not constrained to JSON-stable scalar types, so this is a latent verification-mismatch risk for non-ACP receipts.

findings.title: Single shared sqlite3 connection with autocommit-per-statement and no transaction grouping means multi-statement money operations are not atomic if an exception occurs between statements
findings.description: fulfill_once (lines 365-389) does INSERT OR IGNORE then a separate UPDATE then a single commit, which is good. But the connection is in default isolation mode and each method calls commit() individually. There is no BEGIN/COMMIT wrapping across methods, so any operation composed of multiple store calls (e.g. billing webhook: set_subscription + later mark_event_processed) is not a single transaction — a crash between them leaves partial state. Also sqlite3's default isolation_level means an implicit transaction opens on the first DML; if an exception is raised after the INSERT in fulfill_once but before commit, the connection holds an open transaction that the next caller's commit could inadvertently commit. Given a single RLock-serialized connection this is bounded, but it is a latent atomicity hazard on the money path.

findings.title: idempotency_complete updates status='done' even if the key row was reclaimed by another request, with no compare-and-set on the original claim
findings.description: idempotency_complete blindly UPDATEs status='done' and response_json by key. If a stale 'processing' entry was reclaimed by a different request (the timeout path in idempotency_begin rebinds request_hash) the original (slow) request can still call idempotency_complete and overwrite the new claimant's row with its own (possibly different) response, then the new request also completes — last-writer-wins on the stored idempotent response. For the ACP money/credit path this can return the wrong cached response on a subsequent replay. Edge case but on the payment idempotency path.

findings.title: Replay of a key whose request completed without storing a response returns None as the replay body, which router returns as a JSON 'null' 200 response
findings.description: If status is 'done' but response_json is NULL (e.g. idempotency_complete never ran, or a code path marked done without a response), idempotency_begin returns {state:'replay', response: None}. router._begin_idempotency returns that None... actually returns state['response'] which is None; complete checks `if replay is not None` so None means proceed — but a None replay for a genuinely-completed key would re-execute the operation (re-charge) instead of replaying. Conversely create_session returns JSONResponse(replay) which for a non-None-but-empty dict is fine. The None-vs-proceed conflation means a 'done' key with no stored response is treated as 'proceed', defeating idempotency for that edge.

findings.title: save_receipt uses INSERT OR REPLACE — an id collision silently overwrites a prior signed receipt
findings.description: save_receipt does INSERT OR REPLACE INTO receipts (run_id PRIMARY KEY ...). run_id is the PK and is reused for both triage run ids (run_+12 hex, api/app.py:127) and ACP order ids (order_+12 hex, acp/router.py:296). If two runs/orders ever collide on id (different prefixes make cross-collision impossible, but a repeated run_ id from a reused secrets stream, or a deliberate replay of an order id, would), the OR REPLACE silently overwrites the earlier signed receipt and its signature/summary — destroying the tamper-evident audit artifact the product sells as its headline trust signal. A receipt ledger that is supposed to be append-only/immutable should use INSERT (and surface a conflict) rather than INSERT OR REPLACE. Collision probability is low (96-bit random), but the failure mode is silent data loss of a signed financial/audit record.

findings.location: build_agent_manifest lines 17-51 / agent_manifest line 78-80 (no Cache-Control, reflects request.base_url)
findings.title: Agent manifest/llms.txt reflect Host into absolute URLs AND set no Cache-Control, so a poisoned response can be cached and served to other agents
findings.description: Round 1 flagged the Host-header reflection into the manifest/llms.txt. The additional defect: these discovery responses (JSONResponse/PlainTextResponse, lines 80/85) set no Cache-Control: no-store. A self-describing surface that varies by Host but carries no cache directives is cacheable by intermediary/CDN keyed on path only, so a single attacker request with a spoofed Host can poison the cached manifest that subsequent agents fetch — turning the reflection into a persistent, shared mis-advertisement of attacker-controlled checkout/MCP URLs rather than a per-request-only issue.

findings.title: apply_category/remove_category operate on /me/messages/{id} but the categorize+move pipeline may move the message first, leaving categories applied to a now-stale-folder message id
findings.description: Outlook apply_label is a /move that physically relocates the message and Graph mints a NEW message id on move (the id is folder-scoped for some mailbox configurations; a moved message's id changes). In base.apply_actions the order is: apply_label (move) first, then star, then apply_category (lines 342-362). apply_category then PATCHes /me/messages/{ORIGINAL_id}. After a move, the original id may no longer resolve (404) — the category PATCH then hits the broad except at 362-364 and returns False silently, so tier-routing categories are dropped for any message that was also moved. Round 1 flagged the read-modify-write race (630/642/653) and that target_folder is a no-op (639), but not the move-then-category ordering hazard where the message id is invalidated by the prior move.

findings.title: CI never exercises the documented 3.9 core floor nor the stripe/mcp lower version bounds
findings.description: The version-split design is the whole point of three requirements files: core importable on 3.9, mcp requiring >=3.10, deploy on 3.11. But CI only runs 3.11 and 3.12 and always installs all three requirements files together, so (a) the claim that the core stays '3.9-importable without mcp' (Dockerfile comment L6-7, requirements-mcp.txt L2) is never tested — a 3.10+-only syntax could creep into core/ undetected; and (b) since pip resolves to the latest allowed versions, the lower bounds (stripe>=15.2, mcp>=1.2, fastapi>=0.110, pydantic>=2.0) are never validated. Add a core-only job on 3.9 installing just requirements.txt to defend the documented floor.

findings.title: Smoke test runs AFTER deploy with no rollback, so a broken worker is already live before the test can fail
findings.description: The 'Smoke test live share demo' step runs after the 'Deploy Worker' step (wrangler deploy) in the same job. wrangler deploy publishes immediately; the smoke test only runs afterward and, on failure, simply fails the workflow — there is no rollback to the prior worker version. A deploy that breaks /health or the protected-sender check is live on uma.4444j99.dev for the window until someone notices the red CI and manually reverts. The test is a post-hoc alarm, not a gate.

findings.title: CHANGELOG omits all of the commerce/API/MCP/ACP/Cloudflare work — stale vs current repo state
findings.description: The CHANGELOG's [Unreleased] section lists only 'Platinum Sprint: CI/CD workflow, standardized badge row, ADR documentation' and 'Initial CHANGELOG'. None of the substantial features present in the tree are recorded: the FastAPI app (api/), Stripe billing, the Agentic Commerce (ACP) surface, the MCP server (mcp_server/), the signed-receipt ledger, pricing tiers, or the Cloudflare share Worker. For a repo in PUBLIC_PROCESS status this is a notable doc-accuracy gap: the changelog materially understates what the project does. Keep-a-Changelog format is claimed but not followed for the actual changes.

findings.title: GEMINI.md contains only auto-generated ORGANVM system boilerplate — no project-specific guidance, contradicting AGENTS.md which calls it the 'overview'
findings.description: AGENTS.md:9 lists 'GEMINI.md (overview)' as a reference doc for the project overview, but GEMINI.md is entirely the auto-generated ORGANVM:AUTO block (System Context, Active Directives, Trivium, Logos, Conductor OS) plus a Google-prompting note — it contains zero description of universal-mail-automation itself (no mention of the CLI, providers, rules engine, or commerce surfaces). The cross-reference in AGENTS.md is therefore misleading: a reader directed to GEMINI.md for an 'overview' finds only system-wide boilerplate. PROCESS.md (also cited by AGENTS.md:9) was not verified here but the GEMINI.md mischaracterization stands on its own.

findings.title: Inconsistent minimum Python version across docs (3.10 vs 3.9)
findings.description: README badge (line 2) says 'Python-3.10+' and Prerequisites (line 241) says 'Python 3.10 or later'. CLAUDE.md Coding Conventions (line 278) says 'Python 3.9+'. api/app.py:33-34 and requirements-mcp.txt:2-3 clarify the real story: the core engine stays importable on 3.9 and only the optional MCP SDK requires >=3.10 (deploy image is 3.11). The README's blanket '3.10 or later' prerequisite is stricter than the actual core requirement and conflicts with CLAUDE.md's '3.9+'. Pick one and reconcile (likely: core 3.9+, MCP/hosted 3.10+/3.11).

findings.title: ACP feed hardcodes the spec version literal instead of importing API_VERSION — version drift risk
findings.description: acp/__init__.py defines the single source of truth `API_VERSION = "2026-04-17"` and acp/router.py correctly imports it (`from acp import API_VERSION`). acp/feed.py does NOT import API_VERSION; it hardcodes the duplicate literal `"2026-04-17"` in the feed payload (line 60). If the date-versioned spec is bumped in acp/__init__.py, the router will advertise/enforce the new version while the published feed.json keeps reporting the old one, silently diverging. Fix: `from acp import API_VERSION` and use it.

findings.title: Value-score penalty keys on bare 'off' and '%' substrings, over-penalizing legitimate subjects
findings.description: The noise penalty list includes 'off' and '%': `any(x in subject_lower for x in [..., 'off', '%'])`. 'off' matches 'office', 'offer', 'offsite', 'kickoff', 'cutoff', 'staff'(no), 'official' etc.; '%' matches any encoded/stat subject. This systematically drags down scores for many human/important subjects, skewing the 'high value / low value' percentages the report presents as decision data.

findings.location: line 28 (from cli import get_provider, run_labeler) -> cli.py:48 logging.basicConfig
findings.title: Importing the FastAPI app triggers logging.basicConfig() as an import-time side effect
findings.description: api/service.py does a module-scope `from cli import get_provider, run_labeler` (line 28). cli.py at line 48 calls `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format=...)` at module top level. Because api/app.py imports api.service (and mcp_server.server also imports api.service), simply importing the API/MCP app reconfigures the PROCESS root logger (level INFO, a stdout handler, a fixed format). This is the classic library-import-time-side-effect anti-pattern: it silently overrides or fights with the host application's / uvicorn's / pytest's logging configuration, and is a no-op-but-confusing if the root logger already has handlers. The level/format are also frozen at import, so any later log_level config (see the separate 'log_level loaded but never applied' issue) cannot take effect. Root-cause fix: move basicConfig into cli's __main__ entrypoint, not module scope.

findings.title: idempotency_keys and webhook_events grow unbounded — no TTL/cleanup, monotonic disk growth and ever-slower scans
findings.description: Neither idempotency_keys nor webhook_events is ever pruned. Every ACP POST (create/update/complete/cancel) inserts a row keyed by the client's Idempotency-Key (router.py _begin_idempotency, store.py 327-331), and every Stripe webhook inserts into webhook_events (mark_event_processed, lines 249-260). Rows are PRIMARY KEY/UNIQUE but never deleted. Over time the SQLite file grows without bound; with the single serialized connection (see other finding) any future ORDER/scan over these tables, and the file's page cache footprint, degrade steadily. There is no created_at index and no retention job. For a long-lived single-file commerce store this is a slow-burn durability/operability defect: the file backing customer ids and grants can balloon, and a restore/backup window lengthens. Severity low (no incorrectness, just growth), but worth a retention/vacuum policy.

findings.title: add_credits() is dead production code — ACP fulfillment inlines its own UPDATE, nothing else credits
findings.description: store.add_credits() (the documented 'Add n run-credits (ACP credit-pack fulfillment)' helper) has ZERO production callers. Grep across acp/, api/, mcp_server/ finds it referenced only in tests/test_store.py and tests/test_acp.py. The live ACP path (acp/router.py:290 -> store.fulfill_once) re-implements the same `UPDATE accounts SET run_credits = run_credits + ?` inline (store.py:383-387) and never calls add_credits. Round 1 flagged consume_credit and entitlements_for as dead; add_credits is a SEPARATE dead-code instance in the same crediting subsystem and is the canonical 'how an agent's pack is supposed to credit' entry point that the real code path bypasses — so the documented single crediting helper diverges from the actual crediting site, and any future caller using add_credits would credit OUTSIDE fulfill_once's exactly-once dedup.

findings.title: store_json_secret is dead code (no callers) — write-back actually goes through gmail_auth._op_item_edit, a duplicate implementation
findings.description: Grep shows store_json_secret has zero callers across the repo; the only live write-back is gmail_auth.store_token_info -> gmail_auth._op_item_edit (a near-identical private copy of op_item_edit). So auth/onepassword.py defines a full parallel OP credential layer (op_read/op_item_get/op_item_edit/store_json_secret) that is largely unused while gmail_auth.py reimplements the same logic. This duplication means any fix (e.g. adding `--`, stdin secret passing, field-path handling) must be applied in two places or it will be missed in the path that actually runs. The dead store_json_secret also gives a false sense that the store path is exercised when it is not.

findings.title: Bare `except:` swallows all errors in extract_domain
findings.description: `extract_domain` wraps `sender.split('@')[-1]...` in a bare `except:` returning None. `str.split` cannot actually raise here, so the except is dead defensiveness, but the bare `except:` would also swallow KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit if anything inside ever did raise. Minor, but it's a silent-failure smell and the function silently returns None for unparseable senders which are then dropped (line 128 `if dom`).

findings.title: Bare except swallows all errors and returns None domain
findings.description: `except:` (bare) catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit and returns None. The split/strip can't really raise for a str, but the bare except is a code smell that would also mask programming errors; combined with the downstream `if dom:` check it silently drops messages whose sender doesn't parse. Use `except Exception` at minimum, or just guard with a None check.

findings.title: Audit disabled silently when --dry-run, so dry-run apply previews bypass receipt unless audit object passed; _record_dry_run_intent only runs if audit is not None
findings.description: _make_audit returns None when dry_run is True (line 365). cmd_label/cmd_escalate therefore pass audit=None into run_labeler/escalate during dry-run. In run_labeler, the dry-run preview recorder _record_dry_run_intent is gated on `audit is not None` (line 289), so during dry-run it is never invoked — meaning the carefully-built dry-run intent receipt code path (lines 55-89, 289-290) is dead under normal CLI usage because the only caller (cmd_label) never supplies a non-None audit in dry-run. The 'preview records the intended disposition' feature documented in _record_dry_run_intent's docstring is effectively unreachable from the CLI.

findings.location: cmd_summary markdown lines 696 / cmd_pending markdown lines 693-696 / cmd_vip markdown lines 797-800, 793
findings.title: Markdown table/code-span injection from unescaped sender, subject, and VIP pattern
findings.description: The markdown output paths interpolate raw msg.sender / msg.subject / vip.pattern into Markdown tables and code spans without escaping. A subject like 'Re: payment | Click [here](http://evil) now' (sliced to 30 chars still retains the '|') injects extra table columns / breaks rendering and can smuggle Markdown links into a report a user may paste into an issue or wiki; a VIP pattern containing a backtick breaks the `Pattern: `...`` code span. Round 1's cli.py findings contain no output-format escaping issue. Affects cmd_summary (--format markdown), cmd_pending (--format markdown), cmd_vip (--format markdown).

findings.title: PROTECTED set is dead code and protected-sender logic is duplicated/inconsistent in the fallthrough branch
findings.description: `const PROTECTED = new Set(["courts.ca.gov", "chase.com", "1password.com"])` (line 1) is never referenced anywhere in the file. The actual protection decision is reimplemented ad hoc: the `.gov` broad match (line 106) protects ANY `.gov` address (much broader than the listed `courts.ca.gov`), `chase.com` is handled at line 128, and the final fallthrough at line 153 computes `protected: value.includes("chase.com") || value.includes("1password.com")` — the `chase.com` term there is unreachable (already returned above), so only `1password.com` is effectively honored in the tail. The result is a protected-sender list that does not match the declared PROTECTED set and is easy to drift. For a feature whose core promise is 'can't archive a protected sender', the source-of-truth being unused dead code is a real maintainability/correctness hazard.

findings.location: /v1/triage handler lines 260-263 vs /v1/triage/preview lines 255-258; cross-ref api/app.py real triage semantics
findings.title: /v1/triage (the apply path) returns dry_run:true and is byte-identical to /v1/triage/preview — the worker erases the preview-vs-apply distinction the whole product is built on
findings.description: The real product separates /v1/triage/preview (dry run, no mutation) from /v1/triage (live apply, consumes credits, mutates the mailbox). In the worker both call triagePreview() which always returns dry_run:true with the same fabricated body. On the live demo there is no apply semantic at all, and a client/agent that posts to /v1/triage expecting a live run gets a dry-run-labelled fake. This conflation is not a round-1 finding (round 1 listed no triage-endpoint worker issue).

findings.title: Marketing classifier matches bare substrings 'deal' and 'news' anywhere in the sender — 'newsletter@irs-deals.example', 'jdealer@gmail.com' misclassified
findings.description: marketing = value.includes('deal') || value.includes('news'). 'deal' matches 'idealist.org', 'jdealer@…', 'dealership'; 'news' matches 'newson@…', any 'newsletter'. Because this is computed on the whole lowercased sender string (no token boundaries), legitimate personal/transactional senders containing these substrings are demoted to Marketing tier 3. This mirrors the Python over-matching class but is a fresh instance in the JS worker's own classifier (round-1 worker findings did not cover the marketing substring matcher).

findings.location: senderCheck lines 154-165 fall-through tier_config color always 'amber'
findings.title: Fall-through tier_config.color is hardcoded 'amber' for BOTH Marketing (tier 3) and Misc/Other (tier 4) — Reference should be 'green' per the engine tier table
findings.description: In the fall-through branch the tier_config name correctly switches between 'Delegate'/'Reference' but color is always 'amber'. The canonical tier table (CLAUDE.md Priority Tier System; empty-sender branch line 96 itself uses color 'green' for Reference tier 4) maps tier 4 Reference -> green. So a non-marketing Misc/Other sender returns {number:4,name:'Reference',color:'amber'} while the empty-sender path returns {number:4,name:'Reference',color:'green'} — inconsistent color for the identical tier within the same function.

findings.title: Worker returns wrong tier color 'amber' for Reference (tier 4) and Delegate (tier 3); README/core define green and blue
findings.description: In senderCheck the generic branch hardcodes `color: "amber"` (line 162) for BOTH the marketing case (tier 3, Delegate) and the non-marketing case (tier 4, Reference). README.md tier table (lines 190-191) and core/rules.py PRIORITY_TIERS define Tier 3 Delegate = blue and Tier 4 Reference = green. The dashboard rendering from the share demo will therefore show colors that do not match the documented Eisenhower color scheme. The .gov/chase branches do return the correct 'red' for tier 1, so the divergence is only in the tier 3/4 fall-through.

findings.title: /v1/audit (no trailing slash) does not match the audit handler and silently serves static assets
findings.description: The audit handler matches `url.pathname.startsWith("/v1/audit/")` — note the trailing slash. A GET to `/v1/audit` (no slash, no run_id) does NOT match this prefix, so it falls through to `serveApp` and returns the static SPA / a 404 HTML page rather than a JSON 404/400. The real API exposes `GET /v1/audit/{run_id}` and would 404 with JSON. Divergent contract: the worker's audit route is reachable only with the trailing slash, and `/v1/audit` behaves like an unknown asset path.

findings.title: Worker /llms.txt diverges from the canonical builder and advertises non-existent worker routes
findings.description: api/well_known.py build_llms_txt is described as the single source generated by scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py (no drift). The worker hardcodes a DIFFERENT llms.txt (lines 307-318) with different content/links. It advertises `/v1/audit/{run_id}` and the MCP/ACP surfaces while the worker does not implement MCP, ACP, agent.json, or openapi. The two llms.txt outputs are out of sync (drift the comment in well_known.py explicitly promises cannot happen), and the worker's version points agents at endpoints (mcp/acp) that 404 on the share host.

findings.title: Marketing categorization is a naive substring on 'deal'/'news' producing false categorizations and an always-amber tier_config color
findings.description: `const marketing = value.includes("deal") || value.includes("news")` mis-categorizes any sender whose address contains those substrings (e.g. dealer@chase-not-protected, idealist@x.com -> 'deal', newsletter AND newsroom@whitehouse handled earlier). More notably the returned `tier_config.color` is hardcoded `"amber"` for BOTH the marketing (tier 3) and non-marketing (tier 4) cases (line 161), whereas the API's tier system uses distinct colors (tier 3 blue, tier 4 green per CLAUDE.md). The worker's categorization payload therefore reports an internally inconsistent tier/color pair (tier 4 'Reference' with color 'amber' instead of 'green').

findings.title: Hardcoded Mail version path 'V10' will silently fail on other macOS Mail versions
findings.description: `PLIST_PATH = ~/Library/Mail/V10/MailData/SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist`. The 'V10' directory is macOS-version-specific (V9/V10/V11 across releases). On a machine with a different version the file won't exist and the script SystemExits (line 99-100). Hardcoded path; no discovery of the actual V* directory.

findings.location: record() lines 181-184 and entry build 197-210 (entry['archived']/['moved'] vs entry['disposition'])
findings.title: Violation JSONL line is internally contradictory: disposition=protected_held while archived/moved=true on the same record
findings.description: When a protected sender is observed leaving the inbox (is_protected and left_inbox), record() sets `disposition = PROTECTED_HELD` (line 182) and appends the id to violations (line 184), but the same entry dict still writes `"archived": bool(archived)` and `"moved": bool(moved)` from the actual operation (lines 203-204), which are True in exactly this case. The persisted line therefore says disposition=protected_held AND archived=true (or moved=true) simultaneously — a self-contradiction. Any downstream JSONL consumer that trusts `disposition` to mean 'held in inbox' will miscount the breach, while one that trusts `archived/moved` will count a departure not reflected by the disposition label. This is distinct from the round-1 summary-count mismatch (round 1 reported summary counts over-reporting protected_held): here the defect is the per-line record carrying mutually exclusive fields both set, with no `violation:true` flag to disambiguate at the line level.

findings.title: Docstring claims 'CLI > env > config file' precedence but load_config never applies CLI args
findings.description: The module docstring (line 5) and load_config docstring (153-156) both promise precedence 'CLI > env > config file > defaults'. The implementation only ever calls _apply_yaml_config then _apply_env_config (172-177); there is no CLI-args parameter or application step. CLI overrides are handled separately in cli.py via argparse and do not flow through this function. The documented top-priority tier (CLI) is therefore not implemented here, which is misleading for any caller relying on the contract and means config.dry_run / config.batch_size set in config are not reconciled with CLI flags inside this function.

findings.title: frozen=True gives a false immutability guarantee: mutable Set fields can be mutated in place
findings.description: EmailMessage is declared @dataclass(frozen=True) and the docstring (line 30) advertises it as 'Immutable'. frozen=True only blocks attribute *reassignment* (msg.labels = x raises FrozenInstanceError); it does NOT freeze the contents of the mutable Set fields `labels` (line 49) and `categories` (line 53). Any holder of a reference can call msg.labels.add(...)/discard(...)/clear() and mutate the shared set in place, with no error. Because Gmail's get_message_details (providers/gmail.py:197-208) and other providers build these sets and the message may be cached (e.g. batch_get_details returns a Dict[str, EmailMessage]), a downstream caller mutating msg.labels would corrupt the cached/shared instance and silently change categorization inputs for other code paths. The frozen=True + 'Immutable' docstring lulls callers into assuming defensive copies are unnecessary. The test test_immutability (tests/test_models.py:57-63) only checks attribute reassignment, reinforcing the false guarantee. Fix: use frozenset for the field type or document that contents are mutable; at minimum the docstring overstates the guarantee.

findings.location: line 1163 (def calculate_email_age_hours(email_date: Optional["datetime"]) -> float) with no `from __future__ import annotations` at top (line 8) and `datetime` imported only locally at line 1176
findings.title: Forward-ref annotation Optional["datetime"] is unresolvable — NameError if type hints are ever introspected
findings.description: core/rules.py does not import `from __future__ import annotations` (module starts at line 8 with `import re`), and `datetime` is never imported at module scope — it is imported lazily inside the function body at line 1176 (`from datetime import datetime, timezone`). The parameter is annotated `Optional["datetime"]` as a string forward reference. Because it is a string, it does NOT crash at def time, so the import test passes. But any consumer that resolves annotations — `typing.get_type_hints(calculate_email_age_hours)`, pydantic model building referencing this, dataclass field resolution, or a doc/schema generator — raises `NameError: name 'datetime' is not defined`. Today nothing in-repo calls get_type_hints (verified by grep), so it is latent, but it is a real correctness defect and exactly the class of bug the missing 3.9/typing CI coverage would let rot. Fix: import datetime at module scope and annotate `Optional[datetime]`, or add `from __future__ import annotations`.

findings.title: com.user.gmail_labeler.plist is orphaned — committed but never installed by deploy.sh (only booted out)
findings.description: deploy.sh hardcodes PLIST_SRC/PLIST_DEST to com.user.mail_automation.plist only (lines 9-10) and copies/bootstraps just that one (48, 60). com.user.gmail_labeler.plist is only referenced as a stale job to bootout (deploy.sh:57). So the gmail_labeler plist file sits in the repo with no installer path and no consumer — dead weight that a reader will mistake for an active second scheduler. Either delete it or document it as legacy. (Reported as hygiene because it cannot run via the supported deploy path.)

findings.title: agent-commerce.md says 'Five endpoints' but then lists six ACP routes
findings.description: Line 61 states 'Five endpoints implement the verified Agentic Checkout spec:' and the fenced block immediately below lists six routes: POST create, GET retrieve, POST update, POST .../complete, POST .../cancel, and GET /acp/feed.json. Either feed.json is not meant to be counted among the 'verified Agentic Checkout spec' endpoints (in which case the five checkout-session endpoints is correct and feed.json should be separated) or the count is simply wrong. Minor numeric inconsistency in otherwise careful prose.

findings.title: Second frontend (docs/pitch) is an orphan: not deployed and not linked from anywhere, so its stale copy will drift unnoticed
findings.description: wrangler.toml serves `directory = "./web"` as the Cloudflare assets binding, and ci.yml's only deploy step deploys that worker. docs/pitch/index.html is therefore NOT published by the live deploy, and a repo-wide grep found zero references to `docs/pitch` from any workflow, doc, or the served web/index.html. It is a git-tracked auto-generated artifact with no consumer (orphan surface). Impact is two-fold: (1) it lowers the urgency of the link/copy issues above (they are not live-facing), but (2) because nothing consumes or validates it, its stale org link and placeholder copy will silently rot, and anyone who later wires it up (or finds it via the repo) gets the wrong picture. Either delete it, or wire the generator to publish a real version and keep it in sync with pricing.md / README.

findings.title: Legacy labeler start/finish banners stamp naive local time while audit.py stamps UTC — same local-vs-UTC inconsistency as state.py
findings.description: The legacy exhaustive labeler prints 'Time:' and 'Completed:' using naive `datetime.now()` (local, no tz, no zone suffix), whereas core/audit.py records every receipt entry with `datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()`. A run log therefore mixes local timestamps (banners) with UTC timestamps (receipts) with no marker distinguishing them, making cross-correlation of a run's wall-clock window against its receipt trail wrong by the local UTC offset. Round 1 flagged the analogous state.py line-87 naive-local-vs-UTC issue but did not cover these legacy banner sites, which are the same defect class in a separate file.

findings.title: IMAP connection leaked on exception; logout only on success path
findings.description: main() does `imap = connect_imap(...)`, `imap.select(...)`, search, then a processing loop, and only calls `imap.logout()` at the very end (line 158). Any exception during select/search/fetch/apply (e.g. server error, KeyboardInterrupt) skips logout, leaking the SSL socket. No try/finally around the connection. Same pattern would orphan a half-applied label run. icloud_triage.py does this correctly with try/finally — imap_rules.py does not.

findings.title: IMAP connection not closed on early return / exception (resource leak) and logout-only cleanup
findings.description: main() connects (line 131), selects, searches, and only calls imap.logout() at line 158 at the very end. Any early `return` (line 137 search failure, line 142 no messages) or any exception between connect and logout leaves the IMAP4_SSL socket open — no try/finally. Per-run leak is minor, but a scheduled job that errors mid-run accumulates half-open TLS sockets. icloud_triage.py does this correctly with try/finally + logout; imap_rules.py does not.

findings.title: Runtime state file with per-account mailbox statistics is committed to the repo
findings.description: labeler_state.json is tracked in git (confirmed via `git ls-files`) and contains real usage telemetry for the maintainer's own mailbox: total_processed=36470 and a per-category breakdown (Finance/Banking: 151, Tech/Security: 372, Professional/Jobs: 79, etc.). CLAUDE.md explicitly lists `*_state.json` under 'Files to Avoid Modifying ... runtime state' and the .gitignore intent is to keep PII/usage out, yet this state file slipped into version control. It is not a credential, but it leaks private mailbox composition/volume for a public ORGAN-III repo. The .gitignore does not list it (only gmail_labeler.log and generic *.log are ignored), so it will keep being re-committed.

findings.location: module docstring line 3 vs query line 41
findings.title: Docstring says '>30 days' but the query uses older_than:7d (and inline comment says 7 days)
findings.description: The header docstring (line 3) states it marks 'old (>30 days) emails ... as READ'. The actual query (line 41) is `label:{category} is:unread older_than:7d`, and the inline comment (line 39-40) says 7 days. The behavior is far more aggressive than the documented contract — recent (8-30 day) mail in GitHub/Infrastructure/etc. is force-marked-read, which can hide genuinely unread important notifications. Doc/behavior mismatch on a mutating operation.

findings.title: Gmail label_counts recorded before batchModify runs — over-counts on batch failure or protected-suppressed actions
findings.description: In the build loop, `for label in action.add_labels: result.add_label_stat(label)` increments label_counts for EVERY action up front, before any batchModify executes. If a batch later fails (HttpError at 469-472 increments error_count but label_counts is already incremented), or if a protected message is held, the reported per-label counts in ProcessingResult overstate what was actually applied. Unlike the base provider (which calls add_label_stat only after a successful apply_label at base.py:345), the Gmail override counts unconditionally at build time, decoupling the label report from reality. Round 1 flagged dry-run inflation in cli.py and success_count accounting, but not this build-time label_counts over-count specific to the Gmail batch path.

findings.title: O(labels) reverse lookup per label id per message — quadratic label resolution on large mailboxes
findings.description: For each message, every label id is resolved by scanning the entire _label_cache (`for name,id_ in self._label_cache.items(): if id_==lid`). With L cached labels and K labels per message this is O(K*L) per message, repeated for every message in a run. A reverse {id:name} dict built once at cache init would make this O(K). Performance smell, not a correctness bug, but it runs on the common path for every fetched message.

findings.title: No requires-python / [project] metadata — nothing authoritatively enforces the Python floor at install time
findings.description: pyproject.toml contains only `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` (testpaths, pythonpath). There is no `[project]` table and no `requires-python`. As a result, pip will happily install the package/deps on Python 3.9 even if the code actually needs 3.10+, or vice-versa, with no guardrail — the floor is asserted only in prose (CLAUDE.md, README, code comments) which already disagree with each other (see contradiction finding). Adding `requires-python = ">=3.x"` would make pip refuse mismatched interpreters and give a single source of truth that CI can mirror in its matrix.

findings.title: total_archived double-counts overlapping labels and miscounts because Gmail messages carry multiple labels
findings.description: recount sums messagesTotal across all user labels plus INBOX (excluding only INBOX and TRASH names at line 41). Since a single Gmail message can carry many labels (e.g. Work/Dev/GitHub AND Notification AND a parent label), summing messagesTotal across labels massively over-counts the 'TOTAL ORGANIZED ARCHIVE'. The figure presented is not a real message count. Also UNREAD is included in relevant_labels (line 12) and would be added into the archive total.

findings.title: httpx is a test-only dep shipped into the production image
findings.description: requirements-api.txt L6 pulls in httpx>=0.27 with the comment 'required by fastapi.testclient (Starlette TestClient) and for tests.' The deployed runtime (api/app.py and the routers) does not import httpx — it is only needed by Starlette's TestClient under tests/. Because the Dockerfile (L9-10) installs requirements-api.txt wholesale, httpx (and its transitive httpcore/anyio/sniffio surface) ships into the production image. Minor: a test-only dependency belongs in a requirements-dev.txt or pyproject test extra, not the runtime API requirements that the prod image installs. Increases image size and attack surface without runtime benefit.

findings.title: test_immutability asserts the wrong (overstated) guarantee and never exercises set-content mutation
findings.description: test_immutability only verifies that reassigning msg.id raises AttributeError. It does NOT test that msg.labels.add(...) is rejected — which it is NOT (see the frozen-with-mutable-Set finding).
