The test therefore validates and reinforces the misleading 'Immutable' claim while leaving the real mutability hole (in-place set mutation) completely uncovered. A reader trusting this test would assume EmailMessage contents are immutable. Fix: add a test documenting that labels/categories CAN be mutated in place (or, if frozenset is adopted, that they cannot).

findings.title: Test coverage gap: 'corrupt' test only exercises invalid JSON, never valid-JSON-wrong-shape
findings.description: The only corruption test writes syntactically invalid JSON ('NOT VALID JSON {{{') and asserts defaults are returned, which exercises the JSONDecodeError branch. It does NOT cover the actually-dangerous cases the gap report flags: a valid JSON value that is not the expected dict (list/string/null at top level), or a dict whose fields have wrong types (next_page_token as dict, total_processed as string, history as a list). Because no test pins this behavior, the schema-validation gap and the get_history/total_processed type-confusion crashes can regress or ship unnoticed. There is also no test for a failing save() (read-only path) asserting the failure is detectable, nor any concurrency/torn-write test.

findings.title: Store tests run against ':memory:' where WAL is silently ignored — the concurrency/durability properties the store advertises are never exercised by tests
findings.description: Every unit test in test_store.py uses Store(':memory:') (lines 7-8). On :memory: databases SQLite silently ignores journal_mode=WAL (the store's own comment at lines 131-132 acknowledges this), so none of the WAL behavior, the busy_timeout absence, file-based locking, or concurrent-connection semantics are covered by tests. test_idempotency_stale_processing_is_reclaimed (lines 91-98) manually UPDATEs created_at=0 and asserts a single-threaded reclaim returns 'new' — it does NOT test the dangerous case (a still-live original holder racing the reclaim), nor the idempotency_complete clobber race. There is no concurrency test at all (no threads), so the central guarantees the store is built to provide (serialized writers, exactly-once fulfillment under contention, idempotency under retry races) are asserted only single-threaded. This is a test-quality gap: the most failure-prone properties are unverified.

findings.title: Offline categorization uses naive substring match ('deal'/'news') that over-matches
findings.description: The fallback categorizes a sender as Marketing/tier-3 when `value.includes("deal") || value.includes("news")`. These bare substrings match inside unrelated addresses — e.g. news@idealcorp.com or info@dealership-legal.com — mislabeling legitimate or even protected-looking mail as movable Marketing. This is the same over-match defect class round 1 flagged for the Python core/rules.py keyword rules, here reproduced independently in the client JS (a separate, un-reviewed file).

findings.title: Green design tokens were referenced ~13 times but never defined until this dirty diff (pre-existing broken branding)
findings.description: git show HEAD:web/index.html confirms --green, --green-2, and --green-soft were used across lines 79,240,245,266,271,277,278,285,287 etc. but were NEVER declared in :root (only --accent/-2/-soft existed). Before this uncommitted change every var(--green*) reference fell back to the CSS-invalid/inherited value, so the committed page rendered with broken accent/branding colors (buttons, focus rings, 'Most popular' badge, protected-verdict chip). The pending diff fixes it by adding the definitions, but the bug shipped in the committed HEAD and was never caught — worth recording since round 1 never reviewed web/.

findings.location: line 52 (`mypy . --ignore-missing-imports`) with no mypy config in repo
findings.title: mypy invoked with no config and no exclude — scans everything from repo root, advisory result is near-meaningless
findings.description: `mypy .` is run with `--ignore-missing-imports` and NO mypy configuration (verified: no mypy.ini, setup.cfg, no [tool.mypy] in pyproject.toml — pyproject only has [tool.pytest.ini_options]). With `--ignore-missing-imports`, the majority of real type errors that arise from third-party stubs are suppressed, and with no `files`/`exclude`/`packages` config mypy scans the entire tree from root (legacy scripts, archive_*.py, etc.). The advisory output is therefore both noisy and weak — it neither enforces nor reliably surfaces type problems, reinforcing the prior finding that there is no effective type gate. (Note: in CI deps install to the runner site-packages so a stray local `.venv` is not scanned, but the lack of any config still makes the result unreliable.)

findings.location: line 31 (`pip install pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy`) and line 30 (`pip install -r requirements*.txt`)
findings.title: Tooling (pytest/ruff/mypy) installed unpinned alongside unpinned app deps — non-reproducible toolchain
findings.description: Line 31 installs `pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy` with no version pins, and line 30 installs the three requirements files which are themselves mostly `>=`. Ruff in particular is fast-moving and introduces/removes rules across minor releases; an unpinned ruff means the advisory lint output changes run-to-run for the same code, and an unpinned pytest/pytest-cov can shift fixture/coverage behavior. Combined with no lockfile, the entire CI environment is non-reproducible: nothing about a green run today guarantees a green run tomorrow. Pin the dev tools (and the app deps via constraints) so CI results are reproducible.

findings.title: codecov-action@v4 used without CODECOV_TOKEN — coverage upload can fail/skip silently on this private-org repo
findings.description: codecov-action@v4 generally requires a CODECOV_TOKEN for reliable uploads (tokenless upload was deprecated/rate-limited and is unreliable, especially for repos not under a public OSS Codecov plan). No `token:` input or `CODECOV_TOKEN` secret is referenced anywhere in ci.yml. Because `fail_ci_if_error: false` (line 74) is set, an upload failure is swallowed and CI stays green, so the coverage badge/report silently rots while everyone assumes coverage is being tracked (README.md:8 already shows a 'coverage-pending' badge, consistent with uploads not actually landing). Low blast radius (observability only), but the silent-failure pattern means the gap is invisible. Add the CODECOV_TOKEN secret and pass it via `token:`. allow-secret

findings.title: Post-deploy smoke test hits a live domain immediately after deploy with no retry/propagation wait — flaky failures and a non-blocking deploy
findings.description: After `cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4 deploy` (lines 100-106), the smoke step (lines 108-119) immediately curls https://uma.4444j99.dev for /health, /app/, /v1/billing/plans, and POSTs to /v1/senders/check. Two issues: (1) there is no wait/retry loop, so if Cloudflare edge propagation or DNS/route activation lags even briefly after deploy, the curls fail and the whole job goes red despite a successful deploy — a flaky gate. (2) The smoke step runs under `set -euo pipefail` and the curls use `-f`, so a transient 5xx/timeout fails the job; but this job only runs on push to main (line 82), so a red smoke turns a successful production deploy into a 'failed' CI run with no rollback — the deploy already happened. Consider a retry-with-backoff around the curls and decoupling deploy success from smoke success (or rolling back on smoke failure).

findings.title: .gitignore omits already-tracked PII artifacts (labeler_state.json, mail_export.tsv) and any *_state.json
findings.description: The .gitignore protects credentials.json, token.pickle, client_secret_*.json, data/, *.db, audit/*.jsonl, protected_senders.local.txt and *.log — good. But it does not cover *_state.json (the documented runtime/PII state files; only specific ones are produced) nor mail_export.tsv, both of which are already tracked (see separate findings). It also has no .dockerignore counterpart. Recommend adding `*_state.json`, `mail_export.tsv`, and `*token_cache*.json` and creating a .dockerignore, then `git rm --cached` the two already-tracked artifacts.

findings.location: lines 9-10 (pip install -r ...) — no constraint/hash file, no pip version pin
findings.title: Docker build installs unhashed, unpinned dependencies with `pip install` and no `--require-hashes`, allowing supply-chain drift between identical source builds
findings.description: The Dockerfile installs `requirements.txt requirements-api.txt requirements-mcp.txt` with plain `pip install --no-cache-dir` and no constraints file, no hash pinning (`--require-hashes`), and no pinned pip version. Because the requirements files use floors (`>=`) with no ceilings on the core deps, two builds of the SAME git commit on different days resolve different transitive versions (fastapi, starlette, pydantic-core, google-*, urllib3, etc.). A malicious or broken upstream release is pulled automatically on the next autoDeploy (render.yaml autoDeploy:true). For a service that handles Stripe charges and provider OAuth tokens, the absence of hash-pinned reproducible installs is a meaningful supply-chain weakness.

findings.title: README documents the non-standard ~/System/Logs path, propagating the convention error rather than ~/Library/Logs
findings.description: README.md instructs users to create the log dir at ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/ (line 261), says logs are written there (544), and tails ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/launchd.stdout.log (556). This matches the plist but documents a non-standard macOS location. On macOS, user logs belong under ~/Library/Logs (visible in Console.app, subject to system log rotation). Using ~/System/Logs means logs are invisible to Console.app and never rotated, so launchd.stdout.log grows unbounded over daily runs. Minor but a maintainability/hygiene smell consistent across plist + deploy.sh + README.

findings.title: README 'First Run' Outlook/IMAP examples inherit a Gmail-only default query
findings.description: The label command's --query defaults to 'has:nouserlabels' (cli.py:1014), which is Gmail search syntax. README First Run shows `python3 cli.py label --provider outlook` (line 285) and `--provider imap --host imap.mail.me.com` (line 289) with no --query override, so they run with the Gmail-specific 'has:nouserlabels' default against Outlook Graph / IMAP. The help text itself flags this: '(default: has:nouserlabels for Gmail)'. For non-Gmail providers this default query is meaningless/likely-broken, but the docs present these as working first-run commands without noting a provider-appropriate query is needed.

findings.title: Product image_url points to the dashboard HTML page (/app/), not an image
findings.description: `'image_url': f'{base}/app/'` (feed.py:47) sets the product image to the SPA/dashboard HTML route, identical to the product `url` (line 45). ACP/feed consumers expect image_url to resolve to an actual image asset; pointing it at an HTML page yields broken images or rejected feed entries in agents that validate the media type. Provide a real image asset URL (or omit the field if none exists) rather than reusing the app URL.

findings.title: Payment client is cached at module level keyed on first-call env; a STRIPE_SECRET_KEY set after first call (or rotated) is never picked up
findings.description: _CLIENT is memoized on first get_payment_client() call. If the process starts without STRIPE_SECRET_KEY (NullPaymentClient cached), then the key is later injected (e.g. lazy secret load, or a worker that reads secrets after import), the cached NullPaymentClient keeps refusing all charges — every ACP completion returns 402 'payment is not configured' despite valid creds. Conversely a rotated key is ignored until restart. The billing.py side reads the key at call time (good); this caching is inconsistent and can wedge the charge path.

findings.title: Payment client is cached process-wide on first resolution; a STRIPE_SECRET_KEY set after first call (or rotated) is never picked up
findings.description: get_payment_client caches `_CLIENT` on first call based on the env at that instant (lines 107-109). If the first ACP request arrives before STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is present in the environment, the NullPaymentClient is cached and every subsequent /complete fails-closed with 'payment is not configured' even after the key is later set — until set_payment_client(None) is called or the process restarts. This diverges from api/billing.py, which reads the key at call time (billing.py:68) and thus tolerates late/rotated keys. The fail-closed direction is safe (no free credits), but it can silently brick all agent purchases after a deploy ordering quirk. Consider resolving the key per call or invalidating the cache on key change.

findings.location: lines 100-110 (_CLIENT module global, get_payment_client lazy init)
findings.title: Module-global payment client lazy-init has a read-check-set race under the FastAPI threadpool (no lock)
findings.description: _CLIENT is a mutable module-level global initialized lazily in get_payment_client() with an unsynchronized `if _CLIENT is None: ... _CLIENT = ...` (lines 107-109). Unlike api/store.py's get_store(), which guards the identical pattern with a threading.Lock + double-check, acp/payment.py imports no threading primitive at all. FastAPI/Starlette dispatches sync endpoints on a threadpool, so two concurrent first-requests can both see None and each construct a separate StripeSPTPaymentClient/NullPaymentClient; the second assignment wins and the first instance is silently orphaned. (Round 1 noted only the env-rotation caching of this global, not the concurrency race.) Low likelihood window but it is a shared-mutable-global concurrency defect on the payment path. Fix: guard with a module lock like get_store().

findings.title: Static product_feed.json bakes the placeholder host mail.example.com into committed buyer-facing product/image/checkout/seller URLs
findings.description: The committed acp/product_feed.json (generated by acp/feed.py write_static_feed with default base_url="https://mail.example.com") hard-codes the placeholder host into every product `url`, `image_url`, `seller_url`, and the top-level `checkout_url`. Unlike the live /acp/feed.json route (which resolves the host from the request), this static artifact is what gets committed/reviewed and could be served by a static host (the Cloudflare demo) where there is no request-host resolution. Any agent consuming the committed feed is pointed at the non-existent mail.example.com for checkout and product pages. Round 1 noted the same placeholder for server.json's MCP remote and for gen_commerce_artifacts.py BASE; this is the product_feed.json instance specifically, which carries the checkout_url (the money entrypoint) and was not separately reported.

findings.title: Feed image_url points to an HTML app page (/app/), not an image asset
findings.description: acp/feed.py:47 sets image_url = f'{base}/app/', so both products in product_feed.json have image_url 'https://mail.example.com/app/'. api/app.py:162 mounts /app as a StaticFiles HTML directory, so this URL serves an HTML page, not an image. ACP/product-feed consumers that fetch image_url expecting an image (to render a product thumbnail) will get text/html. Minor feed-correctness/quality issue; supply a real image asset URL or omit the field.

findings.title: Shipped ACP product feed and discovery artifacts hardcode mail.example.com placeholder URLs
findings.description: acp/product_feed.json ships checkout_url, url, image_url, seller_url all as https://mail.example.com/... (placeholder). image_url even points at https://mail.example.com/app/ (an HTML page, not an image). Likewise .well-known/agent.json, server.json, and llms.txt all carry https://mail.example.com URLs (these are generated by scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:28 from PUBLIC_BASE_URL defaulting to mail.example.com). DEPLOY.md:77-79 documents regenerating with PUBLIC_BASE_URL=https://<host>, and well_known.py resolves URLs live per-request, so the SERVED artifacts are correct at runtime — but the COMMITTED static files (which ACP-compatible agents and the MCP registry may read directly, e.g. the committed acp/product_feed.json and server.json) point an agent at the non-existent mail.example.com. An ACP agent discovering this feed cannot actually check out. The image_url pointing at /app/ (non-image) is an additional feed-spec smell.

findings.title: On payment failure the failed response is stored via idempotency_complete, so a legitimate retry with the SAME Idempotency-Key replays the FAILURE instead of re-attempting payment
findings.description: When result.ok is False, the code builds a STATUS_READY response with a payment_failed message, persists it, calls _complete_idempotency(ctx, resp) (line 279) marking the idempotency key 'done' with the failure response, and returns 402. The comment at line 268 says 'the session stays completable on retry' — true at the session level, but the Idempotency-Key is now burned: a retry reusing that key hits the replay branch (_begin_idempotency returns the stored 402 body) and the buyer can never complete with that key, even though the underlying card decline was transient. The caller must know to use a fresh Idempotency-Key, which is unusual semantics (idempotency keys normally let you safely retry the SAME key). Storing a non-terminal failure as a 'done' idempotent result is questionable.

findings.title: GET /checkout_sessions/{id} returns the stored session to ANY valid-format bearer with no ownership check
findings.description: get_session only runs `_gate(require_idempotency=False)` (prefix/non-empty Bearer + API-Version) then returns `row['data']['response']` for the given id (router.py:195-197). There is no check that the presented bearer owns or created the session. The response includes buyer name/email and order/permalink. Confidentiality rests entirely on the 128-bit random session id being unguessable (router.py:134 secrets.token_hex(16)); if a session id leaks (logs, referer, error reports), any caller with any non-empty bearer can read the buyer PII and order. Add an ownership/api_key binding check on retrieval.

findings.title: STATUS_EXPIRED is defined but never assigned; sessions never expire and remain completable indefinitely
findings.description: models.STATUS_EXPIRED ('expired', models.py:22) is referenced as a terminal state in update_session's guard (router.py:211-212) but is never SET anywhere — no expiry job, no created_at TTL check in complete_session. A session that reached ready_for_payment can be completed at any later time. The delegated Stripe SPT is one-time and may itself expire (handled by Stripe returning a decline), but the application never enforces session expiry, so stale sessions accumulate in acp_sessions and the documented 'expired' lifecycle is dead. Either implement expiry (set the status on a TTL) or remove the unused state to avoid implying a guarantee that does not exist.

findings.location: complete_session lines 304-310 (receipt summary 'currency': CURRENCY ('usd')) vs acp/feed.py:50 / product_feed.json ('USD') and api/billing.py:102 ('usd')
findings.title: Currency casing is inconsistent across the money surfaces: charge+receipt+billing use 'usd', the product feed uses 'USD' — a consumer keying on exact case sees two currencies
findings.description: The charged PaymentIntent and the signed order receipt use CURRENCY='usd' (router.py:39, 306, 310). The Stripe billing catalog endpoint returns currency 'usd' (billing.py:102). But the product feed and committed product_feed.json declare 'USD' (feed.py:50, product_feed.json:15/35). An agent or downstream ledger that matches currency codes by exact string treats the feed's advertised currency as different from the receipt/charge currency, breaking reconciliation between the advertised price and the settled charge. (Round 1 noted the feed-vs-checkout case mismatch; this is the deeper instance: the SIGNED receipt body — the product's headline trust artifact — records 'usd' while the public feed advertises 'USD', so an independent verifier reconciling feed price to receipt amount sees a currency-code mismatch.)

findings.title: Receipt account_id is the auto-provisioned bearer account, not the buyer email on the session — receipt audit trail attributes the purchase to an unidentifiable orphan
findings.description: save_receipt is called with account_id=account['id'] (316) where account was just resolved/created from the bearer api_key (287-289), with plan='free' and no email/customer linkage. The session, however, carries a buyer dict (name/email/phone) from body.buyer. The signed receipt's summary deliberately excludes PII (good), but the ledger row's account_id points to an orphan account that is not reconcilable to the buyer's billing identity or the Stripe payment_id's customer. list_receipts(account_id) for the real buyer's billing account will never surface this purchase. Round 1 flagged the orphan-account auth issue and orphaned credits; the NEW angle is that the audit RECEIPT itself is filed under the orphan, so the verifiable-receipt feature cannot link an ACP purchase to the paying party even server-side.

findings.location: _links lines 125-130 / _shape line 144 — terms_of_use & privacy_policy URLs point at {base}/terms and {base}/privacy which are not routed
findings.title: ACP session links advertise /terms and /privacy endpoints that the app does not serve (404)
findings.description: _links builds links to f'{base}/terms' and f'{base}/privacy' (lines 128-129) included in every checkout session. api/app.py registers no /terms or /privacy route (the only static mount is /app, line 162), so these spec-required legal links 404. An ACP agent (or a compliance reviewer) following the terms_of_use/privacy_policy links the checkout session asserts gets a not-found, undermining the spec-faithful surface and any agent that gates purchase on readable terms.

findings.title: Batch callback registered twice (on the batch AND per-request) causing each callback to fire twice — double-counting
findings.description: `batch = service.new_batch_http_request(callback=callback)` (line 101) sets a batch-level callback, and then `batch.add(..., callback=callback)` (line 103) ALSO passes a per-request callback. In google-api-python-client, the per-request callback overrides the batch-level one for that request, so in practice it fires once — but the dual registration is confusing and if the library ever invoked both, every message would be appended to msg_data twice, doubling all reported statistics. At minimum the batch-level callback (line 101) is dead/misleading given every add overrides it.

findings.title: Division by zero / statistics.mean on empty list crashes report if all batch fetches fail
findings.description: If every batch GET errors (callback returns early on exception, line 82-83), msg_data and value_scores stay empty. Then `statistics.mean(value_scores)` (line 146) raises StatisticsError, and the `/len(msg_data)` divisions (lines 151-152) raise ZeroDivisionError. No guard for the empty-sample case after fetching (only the pre-fetch empty check in main).

findings.location: _run lines 127-129 (run_id minted + receipts.persist) — preview path via triage_preview line 88 / app calls _run(req, dry_run=True)
findings.title: triage_preview persists a durable receipt and mints a run_id for a no-op preview, polluting the audit ledger (REST preview path)
findings.description: Round 1 noted dry-run receipts are persisted from _run. The specific REST-surface instance: triage_preview (line 86-88) calls _run(req, dry_run=True), which unconditionally mints run_id and calls receipts.persist (lines 127-129) for a preview that touches nothing. Every preview an agent runs writes a durable receipt row. The MCP triage_preview (server.py line 105) is similar. Beyond ledger pollution this enables an unauthenticated, unmetered preview endpoint to grow the receipts table without bound (no auth, no cap) — a write-amplification DoS on the receipt store, compounding the round-1 'previews in the ledger' correctness note with an availability angle.

findings.title: invoice.paid sets status='active' unconditionally, but does not re-write current_period_end, so a past_due->active flip leaves a stale/expired period_end that nothing re-validates
findings.description: invoice.paid handler (265-270) calls set_subscription(account_id, status='active') with NO current_period_end. set_subscription only writes non-None fields (store.py 213), so the previously-stored current_period_end (which may be the OLD, expired period from the failed cycle) is retained while status flips back to active. Combined with the fact that entitlements_for never checks current_period_end (per round 1), this is internally consistent today — but it means the stored period_end can permanently lag the actual paid-through date, so any future enforcement built on current_period_end would mis-grant. The NEW defect vs round 1: round 1 flagged 'invoice.paid never refreshes plan'; this flags that it also never refreshes period_end while reactivating, producing a status=active row whose period_end indicates an already-elapsed window.

findings.title: subscription.created/updated with status=None writes nothing for status but still forces current_period_end and may demote plan, producing a row whose plan changed without a corresponding status change
findings.description: When obj.get('status') is None (malformed/partial event), set_subscription skips the status column (store.py 213) but STILL writes plan (from price) and current_period_end. So a price-change event with a missing status can rewrite plan (e.g. to a new tier or, via plan_id_for_price returning None, to NULL plan) while leaving the OLD status untouched — decoupling plan and status. If the old status was 'canceled'/'past_due' and the new plan is a paid one, the account now stores a paid plan with a stale negative status (or vice versa). Round 1 flagged None-status-skips-write and unknown-price->plan=None separately; the NEW interaction is plan being rewritten while status is NOT, yielding an internally inconsistent (plan,status) pair from a single event.

findings.location: METERED_ADDON line 133-134 ($0.01/run) vs CREDIT_PACKS line 146 (pack_1000: 1000 runs / 900 cents)
findings.title: Per-run price is inconsistent across the catalog: metered is $0.01/run but pack_1000 sells runs at $0.009/run, and neither price is ever charged
findings.description: METERED_ADDON advertises unit_amount_cents=1 ('$0.01 / triage run', lines 133-134), while CREDIT_PACKS['pack_1000'] sells 1000 runs for 900 cents (line 146) = $0.009/run, and pack_100 sells 100 runs for 100 cents = $0.01/run. So the same 'verified-safe triage run' has three notionally-different unit prices ($0.01 metered, $0.01 pack_100, $0.009 pack_1000) with no documented volume-discount rationale, and the metered unit_amount_cents=1 is never used to create a Stripe price or meter event (it is read nowhere outside the public catalog). Because no run is ever actually metered or debited (see the metering gap), this is currently a latent money-consistency/economics defect rather than a live mischarge, but it would surface as soon as metering is wired (a customer comparing metered vs pack_1000 is overcharged 11% on the metered path).

findings.title: run_credits coerced with int(account.get('run_credits',0)) can raise ValueError/TypeError on a non-numeric stored value, 500-ing the entitlement check
findings.description: int(account.get('run_credits', 0)) assumes the column is always an int; the schema defaults it to INTEGER but a manual edit, migration, or a None written by some path makes int(None)->TypeError or int('x')->ValueError, raising out of entitlements_for. Since entitlements is meant to be the safe runtime cap, an exception here should fail safe to the Free floor, not propagate. (Round1 noted the int() trust on the dead path; this flags the unhandled-exception fail-mode specifically.)

findings.title: Receipt ledger write failure swallowed (acceptable) but the API never surfaces that the run has no verifiable receipt
findings.description: persist() wraps save_receipt in 'except Exception' and logs a warning, then returns the signature anyway. The docstring justifies this (safety invariant already asserted upstream). However the API (_run in app.py) calls receipts.persist(run_id, result) and ignores the outcome, then returns result with run_id — so a client is told a run_id exists and points at GET /v1/audit/{run_id}, but if the ledger write failed that endpoint will 404. The product's headline feature ('independently verifiable receipt at /v1/audit/{id}') silently does not exist for that run with no signal to the caller. Consider returning a flag/header when persistence failed.

findings.title: No minimum-length/strength validation on RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY — a weak/short key is accepted silently
findings.description: `os.environ.get('RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY')` is `.encode('utf-8')`'d and used directly as the HMAC key with no length or entropy check. An operator can set RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY=x (1 byte) and the server happily signs tamper-evidence receipts with a trivially brute-forceable key, with no warning. For a security artifact whose entire value is tamper-evidence, the absence of even a minimum-length guard (the ephemeral fallback uses 32 bytes) is a config-security weakness. A short or low-entropy key undermines the HMAC guarantee silently.

findings.title: ACP order receipts are hand-built instead of reusing signed_body(), so the two receipt schemas can silently drift
findings.description: Regular triage receipts are produced by receipts.signed_body() ({run_id, provider, dry_run, summary, receipt_line}). The ACP /complete order receipt re-implements the same 5-key body inline in router.py (297-311) and signs it directly. Today the key sets match so get_receipt reconstruction verifies; but because the canonical body builder is duplicated rather than called, any change to signed_body's shape/ordering (e.g. adding a field, or _canonical's encoding) would break verification for ACP receipts only, with no shared test catching it. This is a maintainability/latent-correctness smell on the tamper-evidence path.

findings.title: prov.disconnect() exception is silently swallowed with bare except: pass, hiding resource-cleanup failures
findings.description: The finally block wraps prov.disconnect() in try/except Exception: pass with no logging at all. If disconnect leaks a socket/connection or fails, there is zero observability — unlike the connect() failure which is logged. On a long-running service this can mask provider connection leaks. Low severity but a genuine silent-failure smell on a resource-cleanup path.

findings.title: Broad 'except Exception' around categorize_with_tier silently degrades categorization to None with no logging
findings.description: check_sender catches every exception from categorize_with_tier and sets categorization=None without logging. The protected check is the safety-critical part and is intentionally kept separate, which is good, but a recurring categorization bug (e.g. a malformed LABEL_RULES regex throwing) would be completely invisible in production — the endpoint keeps returning 200 with categorization:null forever. At minimum log the swallowed exception.

findings.title: Provider disconnect failure is swallowed with bare 'except: pass', and assert_no_violations runs AFTER disconnect so a disconnect that masks state is invisible
findings.description: The finally block does 'try: prov.disconnect() except Exception: pass'. Swallowing disconnect errors is defensible (cleanup), but it is a fully bare swallow with no logging, so a disconnect that fails because the underlying session/connection died mid-run (which could indicate the triage itself partially failed) leaves no trace. Combined with run_labeler re-raising on its own internal exception (cli.py:324), the ordering is: run_labeler may raise -> finally disconnects (swallowed) -> exception propagates as a 500-mapped Exception in service (it is NOT caught here, only ProviderUnavailable/AuditInvariantError are caught downstream in app._run). A generic engine exception from run_labeler thus escapes run_triage uncaught and becomes an unhandled 500 in the API with a possibly sensitive message — the module docstring claims everything maps cleanly, but only connect-time errors are wrapped.

findings.title: Broad 'except Exception' silently nulls categorization, hiding rules-engine regressions on the public trust surface
findings.description: categorize_with_tier is wrapped in 'except Exception: categorization = None' with the comment 'never block the protect-check'. While not blocking the protected boolean is correct, swallowing ALL exceptions (including bugs/regressions in the regex rules, e.g. a catastrophic-backtracking pattern or a malformed config-loaded VIP regex) means the categorization silently returns null with no log line, so a broken rules engine looks like 'uncategorized' on the demo/trust surface and in MCP check_protected_sender. At minimum log the exception. Also re.search over user-controlled VIP patterns (check_vip_sender) compiles arbitrary regex from config — a bad pattern raises re.error here and is swallowed.

findings.location: current_period_end column (49); set_subscription (191-221); entitlements_for in plans.py (184-186)
findings.title: Subscription expiry (current_period_end) is stored but never checked — an expired-but-active row keeps paid limits
findings.description: entitlements_for (the would-be enforcement function) only inspects account['status'] in ('active','trialing') to decide active vs Free floor (plans.py:184-186); it ignores current_period_end entirely. If Stripe ever leaves status='active' while the period has lapsed (e.g. a missed customer.subscription.updated/deleted redelivery, or an invoice.payment_failed that is never received), the account would retain paid entitlements past expiry. This is latent today because entitlements_for is uncalled, but it becomes a real over-grant the moment enforcement is wired in without also comparing current_period_end against now(). Flagging now since the gap-targeted scope is the enforcement subsystem.

findings.title: consume_credit always debits exactly the requested n with no cap on n and no zero/negative guard at the caller boundary
findings.description: consume_credit(account_id, n) does int(n) and the WHERE clause guards run_credits >= n, so a negative n would pass the guard and ADD credits (run_credits - (negative) increases the balance) while returning True. There is no caller in production, but if/when enforcement is wired, passing an attacker-influenced or miscomputed negative n (e.g. derived from request data) would grant free credits rather than debit them. add_credits (223) similarly does no non-negativity check. Recommend asserting n > 0 in both. Low severity today only because the functions are currently unreachable from request input.

findings.title: WAL pragma failure is silently swallowed — store can silently run in rollback-journal mode on restricted/networked filesystems
findings.description: The PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL call is wrapped in `try: ... except sqlite3.Error: pass` (lines 133-136). On filesystems where WAL is unavailable or downgraded (network/NFS mounts, some container overlay FS, or when the OS lacks the required mmap/shared-memory support), SQLite either errors or silently returns a different journal mode (the PRAGMA returns the ACTUAL mode, which may be 'delete' not 'wal'). The code neither checks the returned value nor logs the downgrade, so an operator believes they have WAL's crash/concurrency properties when they may have rollback-journal semantics. There is no observability hook. Minor because correctness of single-writer serialization does not actually depend on WAL here (see the serialization finding), but the silent swallow hides a real environmental misconfiguration and the unverified assumption propagates into the docstring's durability claims.

findings.title: Module-singleton Store connection is never closed in production and not coupled to FastAPI lifespan — connection/handle leak across reloads
findings.description: get_store() lazily builds a process-wide Store holding an open sqlite3 connection (line 127) and there is no call to Store.close() anywhere in the app lifecycle (api/app.py lifespan at lines 48-53 starts only the MCP session manager; it never closes the store). In dev with uvicorn --reload, or in any harness that re-imports the module / calls set_store(new) (line 455), the previous Store's connection is dropped without close(), leaking the file handle and (in WAL mode) potentially leaving -wal/-shm files. set_store(None) in tests (conftest 26/33) also abandons the prior connection without closing it. Not a production data risk, but a file-descriptor / WAL-sidecar leak under reloads and in the test suite (every test gets a fresh store via the autouse fixture, none are closed). Severity low.

findings.title: WAL is never checkpointed and the connection is never closed in production, so the -wal/-shm sidecar files grow unbounded
findings.description: WAL mode is enabled but no PRAGMA wal_autocheckpoint is set to a non-default and, more importantly, the single long-lived connection is never closed in the production path (get_store() singleton with no FastAPI lifespan shutdown calling close()). SQLite only fully checkpoints/truncates the -wal file on the last connection close or an explicit checkpoint; with one immortal connection and a steady trickle of small writes (webhooks, receipts, idempotency keys), the -wal file can grow large and is never reset, and a crash leaves -wal/-shm on disk. Round 1 noted the connection 'is never closed' as a handle leak and noted unbounded idempotency_keys/webhook_events row growth, but did not identify the WAL-sidecar growth / missing checkpoint consequence specific to enabling WAL without lifecycle management.

findings.title: int() coercions on caller-supplied counts can raise ValueError/TypeError mid-transaction, becoming an unhandled 500 and (with the no-rollback bug) poisoning the connection
findings.description: add_credits, consume_credit, fulfill_once, and list_receipts wrap their numeric arguments in int(...) inside the locked section (int(n), int(runs), int(limit)). If a caller ever passes a non-numeric value (e.g., a string from JSON metadata, None, or a float-string), int() raises ValueError/TypeError. In add_credits the int(n) is evaluated as part of building the execute() args after the UPDATE string but, more dangerously, in fulfill_once int(runs) at line 386 runs AFTER the acp_fulfillments INSERT has already executed (line 378 used int(runs) too, but a TypeError there leaves the just-issued statement's implicit transaction open with no rollback). Any such raise is uncaught and (per the dangling-transaction finding) leaves the shared connection in an open transaction. Round 1 only noted the missing zero/negative guard on consume_credit's n; the type-coercion crash path on the shared connection is not covered.

findings.title: Reads run inside whatever transaction the shared connection currently holds, so a read can observe an uncommitted in-flight writer's data or a stale snapshot
findings.description: All reads go through the same single connection that does writes. With the default isolation_level, after a write method has started its implicit BEGIN but before commit() (the window is small but non-zero, and unbounded if an exception left it open per the no-rollback finding), a read on the same connection sees the connection's own uncommitted changes (read-your-writes within the open txn) rather than the committed state. Because the RLock serializes calls this is usually benign, but a method like fulfill_once that does INSERT then UPDATE then commit will, if interleaved by re-entrant logic or a future refactor that calls a _fetch_* helper between statements, read partially-applied state. The store advertises 'WAL keeps reads concurrent with the writer' but in reality reads share the writer's connection and transaction, so there is no reader/writer isolation at all. Round 1's WAL/concurrency findings covered the RLock serialization bottleneck and the false WAL-concurrency docstring; this is the distinct point that reads have no snapshot isolation from the in-process writer because they reuse the writer's connection+transaction.

findings.location: __init__ 133-136 (PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL try/except) + return value never checked
findings.title: journal_mode=WAL return value is never verified, so on a filesystem that silently downgrades to another mode (NFS, some FUSE) the store runs without WAL while the docstring assumes WAL
findings.description: PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL returns the actually-applied mode as a row; SQLite does not raise on a network filesystem that cannot support WAL, it returns a different mode string (e.g. 'memory' or 'delete'). The code executes the PRAGMA but never reads the result row, so a silent downgrade to rollback-journal mode goes undetected. Round 1 flagged that an EXCEPTION on the WAL PRAGMA is swallowed; this is the complementary and more likely case where there is NO exception but the mode silently is not WAL, invalidating the whole 'readers proceed during the single writer' premise and reintroducing 'database is locked' errors (especially given no busy_timeout, which Round 1 also flagged).

findings.title: save_receipt uses INSERT OR REPLACE on run_id, so a colliding run_id silently overwrites a prior signed receipt
findings.description: save_receipt does INSERT OR REPLACE INTO receipts (run_id PRIMARY KEY ...). run_id is minted as 'run_'+token_hex(12) (96 bits, collision-safe) but ACP order receipts reuse the SAME table with order_ ids and any caller-supplied run_id would silently clobber an existing audit receipt rather than failing. For an append-only audit ledger that the product markets as tamper-evident, REPLACE (vs INSERT and reject-on-conflict) means a second write under the same id destroys the original signed record with no error.

findings.title: Agent manifest advertises /mcp and ACP checkout unconditionally even when the MCP app failed to mount
findings.description: build_agent_manifest always lists protocols.mcp.url = base/mcp and tools, and llms.txt always advertises the MCP endpoint. But /mcp is mounted only when the optional mcp SDK imported successfully (api/app.py 38-45, 141-143); on Python <3.10 or missing dep, _MCP_AVAILABLE is False and /mcp returns 404. The self-describing surface therefore tells agents to use an endpoint that does not exist on that deployment, a discovery/correctness mismatch. Distinct from the round-1 Host-header reflection finding on the same builders.

findings.title: Age cutoff uses local 'current date' with no timezone normalization; boundary off-by-one vs the Python age engine
findings.description: cutoffDate is computed from `(current date) - (days_threshold * days)` using Mail.app's local-time `current date`, and compared against `date received of msg`. The Python escalation/age path (core/rules.calculate_email_age_hours and core/audit) operates in UTC (datetime.timezone.utc). The two 90-day boundaries can disagree by the local UTC offset, so a message the Python engine considers <90 days (and would keep) can be archived by the AppleScript, and vice versa — an inconsistency that matters precisely because the AppleScript has no protected-sender gate to backstop a wrong age decision. Not reported by round 1 (which covered iterate/try/mailbox issues only).

findings.title: ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES references labels that do not exist in the core taxonomy (Work/Dev/*, Work/RealEstate)
findings.description: ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES lists 'Work/Dev/Infrastructure', 'Work/Dev/GitHub', 'Work/Dev/Code-Review', 'Work/RealEstate'. core/rules.py LABEL_RULES defines these as 'Dev/Infrastructure', 'Dev/GitHub', 'Dev/Code-Review' (no Work/ prefix) and has no 'Work/RealEstate' rule at all. The Gmail query `label:Work/Dev/Infrastructure label:INBOX` will match only if a label by that exact name exists in the account (it may, from organize_labels.py renames), but if the active taxonomy is the core 'Dev/...' names, these archive passes silently no-op (return 0 messages) and the corresponding mail is never archived — a silent functional gap. Conversely it couples this script to a label scheme that the current rules engine does not produce.

findings.title: 1Password read failures are swallowed to a warning and fall through to default/None
findings.description: In load_secret, both the op_read and op_item_get paths catch RuntimeError, log a warning, and fall through — ultimately returning `default` (often None). A genuine secret-retrieval failure (op not signed in, vault locked, item renamed) thus degrades silently to 'no secret', which downstream code may interpret as 'not configured' rather than 'misconfigured/transient failure', masking the real cause. For credential loading, a transient op failure being indistinguishable from absence can lead to confusing auth failures far from the root cause.

findings.title: Secret value passed on 1Password CLI argv (visible in process list)
findings.description: op_item_edit builds `cmd = ['op', 'item', 'edit', item, f'{field}={value}']` where `value` is the secret (token JSON). Command-line arguments are visible to other users via `ps`/`/proc/<pid>/cmdline` on the host for the lifetime of the `op` process. The function is marked sensitive=True only to suppress stderr, but the secret is still on argv. Prefer passing the assignment via stdin or an `op` mechanism that doesn't expose the value on the command line.

findings.title: Warning logs include the full RuntimeError detail, which embeds op stderr that may contain ref/item/field identifiers
findings.description: logger.warning(f"Failed to read from 1Password ref: {e}") and the item variant log the RuntimeError, whose message (from _run_op non-sensitive path, line 46) interpolates op's stderr verbatim plus the description string f"reading secret {ref}" / f"reading field {field} from item {item}". This writes 1Password reference paths, item names, vault names, and field names into the log at WARNING level. These are not the secret values themselves, but they are recon-useful (they reveal vault/item/field topology and exact ref syntax) and persist in log files. Consider logging only a generic message and reserving identifiers for DEBUG. Note the read path is NOT marked sensitive=True, so op stderr (which can sometimes echo back the ref) is included.

findings.title: store_json_secret silently prefers op_ref over item/field and silently no-ops on partial config edge cases
findings.description: If op_ref_env is set but the env var is empty/unset, code falls through to the item/field branch — fine. But if op_ref_env points to a ref that fails parse_op_ref (returns None, e.g. ref not starting with op:// or <3 path parts), the `if parsed:` is False and control falls through to the item/field branch; if item/field env vars are also unset, it raises the generic 'not configured' error — masking the real cause (a malformed ref). The operator gets 'Token storage not configured' even though they DID configure a (malformed) ref. This makes misconfiguration hard to diagnose at the credential boundary. Consider raising a distinct error when a ref is present but unparseable.

findings.title: Batch callback silently swallows per-message exceptions
findings.description: The Gmail batch callback `def callback(...)` does `if exception: return` (line 122), silently dropping any message whose metadata GET failed. A partial/failed analysis sample then drives the bulk move decisions with no logging of how many were dropped — silent degradation that can change classification outcomes without any operator visibility.

findings.title: Analysis batch.execute() has no error handling; a failure aborts the whole drain with traceback and the print('\r') progress can hide errors
findings.description: The metadata batch.execute() at line 135 is unguarded — an HttpError (rate limit on the analysis fetch) raises and crashes drain_loop entirely (no state, no resume). Within the move loop, progress is printed with end='\r' (carriage return) which overwrites the line, so an interleaved logger.warning('Batch Error') from the except branch can be visually clobbered by the next '\r' progress print, masking failures in console output. Mixed print()+logging to the same stream compounds this.

findings.title: Batch callback swallows fetch exceptions silently and batch.execute has no error handling
findings.description: The metadata-fetch callback does `if exception: return` (line 122) discarding the message entirely with no logging, so failed fetches vanish from the domain analysis without trace. batch.execute() (line 135) is not wrapped, so a transport error aborts the iteration. Header access uses h['name']/h['value'] direct indexing (line 124-125) rather than .get(), which would KeyError on a malformed header dict, though Gmail metadata format normally guarantees these keys.

findings.title: Comment claims label auto-creation but code just `continue`s, dropping the domain when target label missing
findings.description: Line 149 comment says 'Create label if missing' but the body is just `continue` (line 150) — the domain is skipped, not created. If classify_domain returns a category whose label was never created in the account (target_id None), those messages are silently never processed, and since moves_performed stays 0 the outer loop's no-progress branch is reached. Dead/misleading comment plus a silent skip path.

findings.title: get_label_id uses results['labels'] without .get(), KeyError if no labels key
findings.description: Line 76 indexes results['labels'] directly. If the labels().list response lacks a 'labels' key (empty account, or a partial/error response that still returns 200), this raises KeyError instead of returning None. Other code in the repo (gmail_labeler.py line 102, legacy line 322) defensively uses results.get('labels', []). Minor robustness gap. Also get_label_id is called once per rule (line 88-89) re-fetching the full label list every time — N round trips for N rules — a minor inefficiency.

findings.title: get_label_id accesses results['labels'] without .get(), KeyError if labels key absent
findings.description: Line 76 iterates `results['labels']` via direct subscript. The Gmail labels().list response normally includes 'labels', but an empty account or an unexpected/error-shaped response (e.g. a 200 with no labels) raises KeyError instead of returning None, crashing run_sweep. Everywhere else in the codebase uses results.get('labels', []). Inconsistent and fragile.

findings.title: report command issues a separate API list call per label with no pagination/cost guard and swallows query into broad except
findings.description: For gmail, cmd_report loops over every key in LABEL_RULES and runs `provider.list_messages(f"label:{label}", limit=1)` (line 481). If a label name contains characters special to Gmail search (e.g., spaces, '/', or reserved operators) the query `label:Work/Dev/GitHub` is passed unquoted; Gmail requires `label:Work/Dev/GitHub` quoted or hyphen-escaped for multi-word labels, so counts for hierarchical/space-containing labels may be silently wrong (returns 0 or matches the wrong label) and the error is caught only as a generic Exception (line 486) printing 'Error (...)'. total_estimate is also just resultSizeEstimate which Gmail documents as approximate, so the report presents approximate values as exact counts.

findings.title: Broad except re-raises after saving but the surrounding cmd_label has no handler — provider __exit__ runs but process exits with traceback, no exit code mapping
findings.description: run_labeler catches Exception, logs, saves state, and re-raises (line 324). cmd_label calls run_labeler inside `with provider:` but does not catch — so an exception propagates out of main()'s `return args.func(args)` (line 1201) and crashes with a traceback and a Python default exit code (1) rather than the intended structured codes (0/1/2). The KeyboardInterrupt branch (316-319) by contrast swallows the interrupt and lets run_labeler return normally (falls through to `return result` at 332) — so Ctrl-C is reported as a SUCCESSFUL partial run with whatever counts accumulated, not as an interruption. Inconsistent error semantics between the two failure modes.

findings.title: VIP attribution re-runs regex over all VIP patterns and attributes a message to the FIRST matching pattern only; overlapping VIPs undercounted, import re inside loop
findings.description: After categorize_with_tier flags is_vip, cmd_vip re-derives which VIP matched by iterating vip_senders and using `re.search(vip.pattern, msg.sender, re.IGNORECASE)` then `break` on first match (line 765-772). If two VIP patterns both match a sender (e.g., a broad domain pattern and a specific address pattern), only the first dict-iteration match is credited; the others show 0 messages, misrepresenting activity. Also `import re` is inside the for-loop body (line 764) — harmless but repeated; and the regex pattern from config is used unanchored/uncompiled with no try/except, so an invalid user-supplied pattern raises re.error and aborts the whole command. The is_vip determination in categorize_with_tier and this re-match can also disagree (different matching logic), so a message can be is_vip yet match no pattern here and be silently dropped from all VIP buckets.

findings.location: run_labeler lines 197-201, cmd_summary 552-556, cmd_pending 655-659, cmd_vip 750-754, cmd_escalate 866-870
findings.title: batch_get_details detection via hasattr but fallback dict-comprehension calls get_message_details per id which may return None and partially populate; no error isolation
findings.description: All commands do `if hasattr(provider,'batch_get_details'): details = provider.batch_get_details(msg_ids) else: details = {m.id: provider.get_message_details(m.id) for m in ...}`. In the fallback, a single get_message_details raising (network error mid-loop) aborts the entire dict comprehension and bubbles up; there is no per-message isolation, so one bad message kills the whole page. Also `details` keys are msg.id but batch_get_details may return a dict keyed differently — the loop assumes id->msg mapping; if batch_get_details omits ids that errored, those messages are silently dropped (the `if not msg: continue` only handles None values, not missing keys). Inconsistent robustness between batch and non-batch paths.

findings.location: run_labeler line 311 time.sleep(1.0) inside while loop
findings.title: Fixed 1s throttle per batch is unconditional even in dry-run and even on the last (break) iteration
findings.description: time.sleep(1.0) (line 311) runs every iteration before the `if not page_token: break` check (line 313). On a dry run there is no API call to throttle, yet it still sleeps 1s per 100-message batch — a 10k message dry run wastes ~100s sleeping for no reason. Also the sleep happens even on the final page right before breaking. Minor performance/UX issue; throttle should be conditioned on not dry_run and skipped when about to break.

findings.title: Provider-specific flags (--host/--user/--password/--gmail-extensions/--account) accepted for every provider but only used by imap/mailapp; silently ignored otherwise
findings.description: provider_group defines --host/--user/--password/--account/--gmail-extensions for ALL subcommands (lines 982-1002) and get_provider receives them, but GmailProvider() and OutlookProvider() ignore them entirely (lines 116, 133). So `cli.py label --provider gmail --host imap.example.com --password X` silently ignores host/password rather than warning the user that those flags don't apply. A user could believe they're connecting to a custom host while Gmail OAuth is actually used. No validation that provider-appropriate flags are supplied (e.g., imap with no --host/--user gives None to IMAPProvider, deferring failure).

findings.location: cmd_pending sort line 680 key uses x['age_hours'] which is 0 for messages with no date
findings.title: Messages with missing date get age_hours=0 (calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 for None), sorting them as 'newest' regardless of true age
findings.description: calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 when date is None (rules.py line 1173). cmd_pending sorts by (tier, -age_hours) (line 680). A starred message with no parseable date gets age_hours=0 and sorts as the most-recent within its tier, pushing genuinely-old undated items to the bottom of the action list. Same 0-for-None is used in escalate's age computation (line 883) meaning an undated email never escalates (age 0 < 24). Silent mis-prioritization of messages lacking a date header.

findings.title: Negative or zero --limit is passed unvalidated straight to provider.list_messages
findings.description: argparse declares --limit as type=int with no lower bound, so '--limit -5' or '--limit 0' parse successfully. summary/pending/vip/escalate pass args.limit directly into provider.list_messages(query=..., limit=args.limit) with no clamping. A negative limit is provider-dependent undefined behavior (Gmail maxResults<0 errors; IMAP/Outlook slicing semantics vary), and a 0 limit silently fetches nothing while still reporting a clean run. run_labeler's 'while processed_this_run < limit' protects the label path, but the read-only triage commands have no equivalent guard. Round 1 line 293 covered '--limit counts built actions not messages scanned' for run_labeler only.

findings.title: report command presents Gmail resultSizeEstimate as an authoritative label count
findings.description: cmd_report sets count = result.total_estimate or 0 for gmail, which is providers/gmail.py:166 results.get('resultSizeEstimate') — a Gmail API estimate that is documented as approximate and can be off by a wide margin for large result sets. It is printed under a '## Label Counts' heading as if exact, and for all non-gmail providers the column is the literal string 'N/A' mixed into the same int-valued list. The output mixes estimated ints and 'N/A' strings under a heading that implies precision.

findings.location: cmd_vip lines 763-772 (import re inside loop) and 765 re.search(vip.pattern, ...)
findings.title: cmd_vip re-runs unvalidated user VIP regex with no try/except, crashing the command on a bad pattern
findings.description: cmd_vip iterates vip_senders and calls re.search(vip.pattern, msg.sender, re.IGNORECASE) directly. vip.pattern comes from user YAML via apply_vip_senders_from_config and is never compiled/validated (cross-ref round-1 core/config.py:343 and core/rules.py:378 check_vip_sender). An invalid regex here raises re.error and aborts the entire vip command with a traceback — a second, independent crash site for the same untrusted-regex class that round 1 flagged only in check_vip_sender/categorization. (Round 1 line 306 noted the in-loop import re and first-match attribution but not the missing error guard on the user-supplied pattern at this call site.)

findings.title: `.gov` suffix match over-protects every government TLD address, not just configured senders
findings.description: `if (value.includes("courts.ca.gov") || value.endsWith(".gov"))` treats ANY sender address ending in `.gov` as protected/Critical. While erring toward protection is the safe direction for this product, it means the share-demo's behavior diverges from the documented protected-sender model (an explicit list), and `endsWith('.gov')` on a raw lowercased string also matches a hostile address like `evil.gov` or a display string that merely ends in `.gov`. Note this is the exact path the CI smoke test exercises (`clerk@courts.ca.gov`), so the test passes via the broad `.gov` rule rather than the intended list membership, masking list-config errors.

findings.location: senderCheck line 84 empty-sender branch returns protected:true with label 'Misc/Other'
findings.title: Empty/missing sender is fail-closed protected but mislabeled Misc/Other tier-4 — protected verdict paired with an archive-eligible tier_config (keep_in_inbox:false)
findings.description: For an empty sender the worker returns protected:true (good, matches engine fail-closed) but the categorization is label 'Misc/Other', tier 4, tier_config.keep_in_inbox:false, star:false. A consumer that drives archive decisions off tier_config.keep_in_inbox would see keep_in_inbox=false for a sender the same response calls protected, an internally contradictory record (protected items should keep_in_inbox per the tier table in CLAUDE.md). The engine never emits a protected+archivable pair. Minor since the top-level `protected` flag is authoritative, but the embedded tier_config is misleading.

findings.location: fetch() dispatch lines 244-320; serveApp fall-through line 320
findings.title: Unknown POST paths and wrong-method requests fall through to serveApp/ASSETS instead of 404/405 — POST /v1/senders/check via GET, or POST to any unrouted path, silently serves static assets
findings.description: Each API route is gated on both pathname AND request.method (e.g. line 250 requires POST). A GET /v1/senders/check, a PUT /v1/triage, or a POST to an unrouted path does not match any branch and falls through to serveApp(request,env) -> env.ASSETS.fetch(request). So a method-mismatched API call returns a 200 HTML asset (or the SPA index) rather than 405 Method Not Allowed / 404. An agent probing the surface gets HTML where it expects JSON, and there is no method validation. The real FastAPI app returns proper 405/404. No round-1 worker finding covers method/route fall-through.

findings.title: startsWith('/v1/audit/') with split('/').pop() mis-handles trailing-slash and nested paths — '/v1/audit/' yields run_id 'demo', '/v1/audit/a/b' yields 'b'
findings.description: Line 282 does runId = url.pathname.split('/').pop() || 'demo'. For '/v1/audit/' (trailing slash) pop() returns '' so runId defaults to 'demo'; for '/v1/audit/x/y' pop() returns 'y', silently treating a malformed nested path as a lookup for 'y'. Combined with the fabricated constant body this is low impact, but the path parsing is loose and would mis-key a real lookup if the endpoint were ever wired to the store.

findings.title: Malformed/empty request body silently becomes {} so senderCheck/triage proceed with undefined sender — no 400 for invalid JSON
findings.description: readJson catches any JSON parse error and returns {}. POST /v1/senders/check with a non-JSON or empty body therefore calls senderCheck(undefined) -> value '' -> protected:true Misc/Other (the fail-closed empty branch). POST /v1/triage with garbage body yields a fabricated preview for provider 'demo'. The caller gets a 200 with a plausible-looking result instead of a 400 Bad Request, so client-side malformed payloads are silently masked rather than surfaced. Distinct from any round-1 worker finding.

findings.location: PLANS lines 3-59 vs api/plans.py free/pro/business definitions
findings.title: Plan catalog is a hardcoded duplicate of api/plans.py with no shared source — prices/caps drift silently between the live demo and the real billing engine
findings.description: PLANS hardcodes id/name/price_cents/monthly_run_cap/retained_receipt_days/features for free/pro/business, a fourth parallel copy of the plan taxonomy (Python api/plans.py is canonical; web/index.html SAMPLE_PLANS is a third). The worker is deployed independently of the Python package, so a price or cap change in api/plans.py does not propagate here; the live /v1/billing/plans can advertise stale pricing. The CI smoke test only asserts the literal 'plans' string is present, so drift is never caught. Round 1 did not flag the worker plan duplication.

findings.title: Wildcard CORS (access-control-allow-origin:*) lets any origin read the unsigned audit receipts and sender-classification API from the browser
findings.description: Round 1 flagged the wildcard CORS generally (lines 61-66, 240-242). The NEW concrete consequence not noted there: because /v1/audit/{run_id} (lines 281-305) and /v1/senders/check are GET/POST JSON with allow-origin:* and allow-headers content-type, any third-party website can fetch a victim's audit receipts / drive sender checks cross-origin without credentials. Even though the bodies are currently fabricated, the policy is shipped on the surface the product markets as the trust/audit boundary; if the audit endpoint is ever wired to the real ledger (per receipts.py) the wildcard becomes a cross-origin data-read. Flagging the audit-endpoint-specific exposure as distinct from the generic CORS note.

findings.title: Worker inlines a hand-written llms.txt that can drift from the canonical root llms.txt / gen_commerce_artifacts.py output
findings.description: The worker hardcodes the /llms.txt body inline (L307-318) instead of serving the repo's root llms.txt (which DEPLOY.md L77-79 says is regenerated by scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py alongside pricing.md/agent.json/server.json). Since the root llms.txt is not in the ASSETS dir (web/), the worker could never serve it anyway, so this inline copy is the share-demo's only llms.txt — and it will silently diverge from the canonical artifact whenever pricing or endpoints change. Fix bases-not-outputs: either generate the worker's inline text from the same source or move llms.txt into web/ and drop the hardcode.

findings.title: Worker senderCheck is a divergent re-implementation of the Python protected-sender gate (share-demo only)
findings.description: Not a packaging finding per se, but relevant to the deploy split: the Cloudflare worker re-implements the protected-sender / categorization logic in JS (PROTECTED set L1, .gov / chase / 1password substring checks L106-153) rather than calling the Python core/rules gate. The two can diverge — e.g. the worker's final branch (L151-153) computes `protected` from `value.includes('chase.com') || value.includes('1password.com')` but for a 1password.com sender it returns protected:true while categorizing it as Misc/Other tier 4 (not a protected tier), an internal inconsistency the Python gate would not produce. Since the wrangler share demo is marketed (smoke test asserts protected:true for courts.ca.gov), drift between this JS copy and the authoritative Python gate is a correctness risk for the public demo. Flagged for coverage; downstream may treat as out-of-scope vs the config-file mandate.

findings.title: serveApp special-cases /app but lets any other path hit ASSETS, so /docs, /openapi.json, /server.json resolve to the SPA fallback or a bare 404 with no API semantics
findings.description: serveApp only special-cases `/` (302 redirect) and `/app`,`/app/` (serve index.html). Everything else is `env.ASSETS.fetch(request)` verbatim. With Cloudflare assets `html` handling, an unknown deep path may resolve to a 404 asset or (depending on not_found_handling) to index.html with 200, meaning routes like `/openapi.json` or `/server.json` could return the HTML SPA with a 200 status and `content-type: text/html` while a JSON consumer expects a manifest. wrangler.toml [assets] sets no `not_found_handling`/`html_handling`, leaving this behavior to defaults — undefined contract for any non-enumerated path.

findings.title: Root redirect uses 302 to /app/ and constructs the target with new URL('/app/', url), which works but the 302 (temporary) plus lost query/fragment is a minor divergence
findings.description: `Response.redirect(new URL("/app/", url), 302)` redirects `/` to `/app/`. Any query string on `/` (e.g. the Stripe return `/?billing=success&session_id=...`) is dropped because the new URL only carries the path `/app/`. The frontend's billing-return handler (index.html ~967) reads `location.search` expecting `/app/?billing=success`; Stripe success_url is set to `/app/?...` directly (billing.py:129) so this specific flow is fine, but any link landing on `/?billing=...` loses its query on redirect. Also 302 vs the API's RedirectResponse default (307) is a behavioral difference for POSTs to `/` (302 may downgrade method), though POST / is not a real route. Low-impact edge.

findings.title: OPTIONS preflight returns Allow-Methods GET,POST,OPTIONS for ALL paths including ones that only accept one method, and never reflects requested headers
findings.description: The OPTIONS handler (240-242) returns COMMON_HEADERS unconditionally for every path, advertising `access-control-allow-methods: GET,POST,OPTIONS` even for paths that only support GET (e.g. /v1/billing/plans, /v1/audit/*) or only POST. `access-control-allow-headers` is the fixed string `content-type`; a cross-origin caller sending `authorization` or `x-...` headers will be blocked by the browser because they are not reflected. Combined with the wrong-method fall-through (separate finding), the advertised CORS method set does not match what each route actually accepts.

findings.title: Worker billing webhook returns 503 JSON for POST /v1/billing/webhook, masking the fail-closed 400 'invalid signature' contract of the real webhook
findings.description: The worker stubs /v1/billing/checkout, /v1/billing/portal, AND /v1/billing/webhook all to a 503 'billing is not configured on this share demo'. For checkout/portal this matches the API's not-configured 503. But the real webhook (api/billing.py:170-185) is fail-CLOSED: an unverified/garbage payload returns 400 'invalid signature', and a missing secret returns 503. By unconditionally returning 503 the worker accepts (does not reject) the request shape and a caller probing the webhook's signature-rejection behavior gets a config message instead of the security-relevant 400. Since the share demo never processes real Stripe events this is low impact, but it is a contract divergence on a security endpoint. Also: exposing a webhook stub at all on a public host invites probing.

findings.title: RunAtLoad=true on gmail_labeler plist would trigger a full multi-provider run immediately on load/login, in addition to the 9 AM schedule
findings.description: com.user.gmail_labeler.plist sets RunAtLoad true while com.user.mail_automation.plist sets it false. If gmail_labeler were ever loaded (or if a user copied this plist thinking it's the canonical one), the entire run_automation.sh (Gmail sweep + Outlook + iCloud, mutating labels/folders) fires at load/login time, not just at the scheduled 09:00. For a job that performs bulk label/archive mutations this is an unexpected immediate side effect at boot/login. The inconsistency (true vs false across two otherwise-identical plists) signals the RunAtLoad value was not deliberately chosen per-job.

findings.title: Plist provides no PATH/EnvironmentVariables; job depends entirely on run_automation.sh sourcing the 1Password env and on /bin/bash existing
findings.description: launchd runs jobs with a minimal environment (PATH typically /usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin, no user shell profile). The plist sets no EnvironmentVariables. This is mostly mitigated because run_automation.sh sources ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh (run_automation.sh:19-24) and uses the absolute venv python ($REPO_DIR/.venv/bin/python). However, if the op env file uses commands not on the minimal launchd PATH (e.g. 'op' the 1Password CLI under /usr/local/bin or /opt/homebrew/bin), the sourced env-fetch can fail under launchd even though it works in an interactive shell. No secrets are embedded in the plist (good), but the runtime-env assumption is implicit and undocumented for the launchd context.

findings.location: SELF_NAME default line 23 + PERSONAL def lines 38-40
findings.title: Default SELF_NAME 'your-name' creates a PERSONAL smart mailbox matching the literal substring 'your-name'
findings.description: If SELF_NAME env var is unset, SELF_NAME defaults to 'your-name' and the PERSONAL smart mailbox is configured to match From ContainsString 'your-name'. Running without setting the env var silently installs a non-functional/garbage smart mailbox rule into the user's live Mail config. No guard warns that the placeholder is still in effect.

findings.title: Public package API omits names that consumers actually use (apply_vip_senders_from_config, AuditLog, AuditInvariantError)
findings.description: `from core import ...` cannot reach several first-class core names. core/__init__.py never imports/re-exports `apply_vip_senders_from_config` (defined in core/config.py:300 and used by cli.py:44), nor anything from core/audit.py (`AuditLog`, `AuditInvariantError`) which api/service.py:21 and cli.py rely on. core/audit is not imported by the package __init__ at all. Consumers therefore reach around the package facade with deep imports (`from core.config import ...`, `from core.audit import ...`), so the package's curated __all__ is an incomplete and misleading public surface. Not a crash, but a re-export completeness defect for the documented 'shared components' package. Fix: re-export the audit types and apply_vip_senders_from_config (or document that core.audit is a separate surface).

findings.location: receipt_line() lines 264-273 (esp. 'protected held in inbox' at 267 vs 'leave inbox' at 268)
findings.title: Human receipt_line reports a violating (protected-but-departed) message as 'protected held in inbox', misstating the breach in the headline number
findings.description: receipt_line() prints `{s['protected_held']} protected held in inbox` (line 267) and separately `{archived+moved} ... leave inbox` (line 268). Because a protected violation is routed to PROTECTED_HELD (record line 182), the violating message is counted in the 'protected held in inbox' figure even though it ACTUALLY left the inbox. The only signal that it departed is the appended `⚠ GATE VIOLATION on N protected message(s)!` (line 272), but the main sentence affirmatively (and falsely) states those messages are 'held in inbox.' On the product's headline trust surface, the primary count actively misreports the safety state — a human reading 'N protected held in inbox' is told the opposite of what happened to the violating messages. Distinct from round-1's summary-count finding, which addressed the machine summary; this is the human-facing one-liner.

findings.location: record() line 178 (_independently_protected re-runs is_protected_sender per message) cross-ref providers/base.py:299/gmail.py:403 (_drop_if_protected already ran it)
findings.title: Audit re-runs the full protected-sender regex check on every recorded message, duplicating the gate's work per-message
findings.description: Every record() call invokes `_independently_protected(sender)` (line 178) which lazily imports core.rules and runs the full `is_protected_sender(sender)` regex/normalize pipeline AGAIN — for the same sender the provider gate already evaluated via `_drop_if_protected` (base.py:299 / gmail.py:403). For large batches this doubles the per-message protected-sender computation (regex compile/scan + normalize_sender) purely for the cross-check. The independence design intends a second evaluation, but it could short-circuit when protected is already True (the union only needs the audit's check when the gate said False), avoiding the redundant scan on the common protected path. Correctness is fine; this is an efficiency/scaling smell on the hot apply loop.

findings.title: Audit JSONL 'domain' field uses normalize_sender (first address only), under-reporting multi-address senders
findings.description: record() stores domain=_domain_of(sender), which calls normalize_sender returning only the FIRST/primary address's domain. The gate (is_protected_sender) correctly unions over EVERY address, but the persisted receipt records just one domain. For a multi-address From where the protected sender is second, the receipt line shows a non-protected domain even though disposition is protected_held — the human-facing receipt is misleading about WHICH sender was protected. Counts/violations remain correct; only the displayed domain is wrong.

findings.title: IMAP_HOST/IMAP_USER env vars ignore the configured env_prefix, inconsistent precedence
findings.description: Most env overrides use the f'{prefix}...' form (default 'MAIL_AUTO_'), but IMAP_HOST, IMAP_USER, OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID, OUTLOOK_TOKEN_CACHE are read WITHOUT the prefix (bare names). This is an undocumented inconsistency: a caller passing env_prefix='MYAPP_' to load_config() gets prefixed overrides for everything except IMAP host/user and Outlook, which still respond to the un-namespaced global names. This can cause surprising cross-contamination if those generic env names are set for other reasons, and breaks the stated 'MAIL_AUTO_*' precedence contract in the docstring.

findings.title: Sample config file written without atomicity or overwrite guard
findings.description: create_sample_config(path) does path.parent.mkdir(...) then open(path,'w') unconditionally, silently overwriting any existing config at that path. A user calling this against an existing populated config.yaml destroys their settings with no confirmation or backup. Given config files hold VIP/protected-sender definitions central to the safety guarantee, an overwrite guard (refuse if exists, or back up first) is warranted.

findings.title: Double os.getenv call (TOCTOU-style redundancy) on every env override
findings.description: Each env override calls os.getenv twice: once in the `if os.getenv(...)` guard and again in the assignment (e.g. 266-267, 268-269, 276-277, 282-285, etc.). Besides being wasteful, the guard uses truthiness so an explicitly-empty env var (`MAIL_AUTO_DEFAULT_PROVIDER=""`) is treated as unset and silently ignored rather than producing a clear error — and there is a latent inconsistency risk if the environment mutated between the two reads (not realistic single-threaded, but it is a smell). Capturing the value once into a local would be both correct and clearer.

findings.title: custom_rules and vip_senders YAML are assigned wholesale with no validation, then VIP patterns are fed to re.search unguarded
findings.description: _apply_yaml_config sets config.custom_rules = data['custom_rules'] and config.vip_senders = data['vip_senders'] with no schema/type check. apply_vip_senders_from_config then registers each vip['pattern'] and check_vip_sender runs re.search(pattern, ...) on it. A malformed regex in user config raises re.error at categorization time (not load time), and a catastrophic-backtracking pattern is a ReDoS vector against every sender. custom_rules are accepted but (notably) never merged into LABEL_RULES anywhere — they are dead config, silently ignored.

findings.title: MAIL_AUTO_BATCH_SIZE parsed with bare int() — a non-numeric env value crashes config load
findings.description: config.batch_size = int(os.getenv(prefix+'BATCH_SIZE')) with no try/except. A non-integer value (typo, e.g. '100 ' with stray text) raises ValueError during load_config, taking down every command that loads config rather than warning and keeping the default. Same unguarded pattern as DRY_RUN's tolerant parse just two lines above, inconsistently applied.

findings.title: Frozen EmailMessage holds mutable Set fields — frozen guarantee is shallow
