findings.description: EmailMessage is declared frozen=True (so the dataclass is hashable and treated as immutable), but labels and categories are `Set[str]` built via default_factory=set. frozen only blocks attribute reassignment, not mutation of the contained set — `msg.labels.add('x')` succeeds and mutates the 'immutable' message in place. More importantly, frozen dataclasses get an auto __hash__, but hashing an instance with a mutable set field will work yet the hash does not reflect set contents and two 'equal' messages with different set identity issues can arise; if EmailMessage instances are ever put in a set/dict (plausible for dedup), mutating labels afterward corrupts the container. The fields should be frozenset for a real immutability/hash guarantee.

findings.title: merge() routes ordered List[str] label fields through set(), destroying order and any intended duplicate/sequence semantics
findings.description: add_labels and remove_labels are declared List[str] (lines 86-87), but merge() collapses them with list(set(self.add_labels + other.add_labels)) (line 100) and the same for remove_labels (line 101). set() (a) discards insertion order non-deterministically across runs/interpreters, and (b) collapses any intentional duplicates. Label *order* can matter for folder providers where the FIRST label that is a move wins (providers/base.py:342-348 iterates add_labels and the first LABEL_IS_MOVE apply triggers did_label_move; Gmail builds batch keys from sorted ids so it is insulated, but the base/Outlook/Mailapp path is not). Non-deterministic ordering also makes the audit's labels_added list (cli.py:72, gmail.py:437) non-reproducible run-to-run for the same input. If two distinct labels were intended to both be applied in a defined sequence, merge loses that. The model's own contract (List) and the merge implementation (set) disagree. Fix: dedup while preserving order (e.g. dict.fromkeys) instead of set().

findings.title: verizon (a telecom) is in Finance/Banking, mislabeling carrier mail as a Critical financial-alert
findings.description: Finance/Banking is tier 1 (Critical, starred, kept in inbox, in PRIORITY_LABELS). `verizon` is a phone/internet carrier, not a bank; matching it here gives Verizon marketing/billing mail Critical tier and starring. Reproduced: 'bill from verizon' matches r'verizon'. (Note Verizon billing also overlaps with Finance/Payments tier 2 conceptually.) Likely a misplacement that inflates the Critical bucket.

findings.location: AI/Services patterns 113-124 (r'claude', r'openai', r'anthropic', r'ollama', r'perplexity'); AI/Grok r'grok' (129)
findings.title: Bare-substring AI/brand patterns over-match unrelated mail
findings.description: Unanchored substrings match inside unrelated words/senders: r'claude' matches 'Claude Monet art print' and 'claudette@x.com'; r'grok' matches 'grokking algorithms course'; r'openai'/'anthropic'/'ollama'/'perplexity' similarly match any host or subject containing the token. These are case-insensitive substrings with no word/host boundary, unlike the carefully-anchored finance/travel/shopping domains nearby (which use (^|@|\.)...\b). Inconsistent anchoring leads to AI/Services false positives (tier 3).

findings.location: Shopping patterns: r'uber' (243), r'target' (247), r'square' (252), r'spirit' (Travel, 310), r'discover' (Finance/Payments, 185), r'statement'/r'invoice' (188-189), Notification r'reminder'/r'alert' (414-415), Personal r'mom'/r'dad'/r'family' (488)
findings.title: Many bare-substring keyword rules over-match common English words and brand prefixes
findings.description: Numerous unanchored substrings produce false positives: r'uber' matches 'tuberculosis'; r'target' matches 'retargeting'; r'square' matches 'squarespace' (also separately listed) and any 'square' word; r'spirit' (Travel) matches 'spiritual'; r'discover' (Finance/Payments, tier 2) matches 'discovery'/'rediscovered'; r'statement'/r'invoice'/'payment.*due' match generic prose ('personal statement essay'); Notification r'reminder'/r'alert' match any subject containing those words; Personal r'mom'/r'dad'/r'family' match marketing copy ('family recipes'). Each is a low-severity misclassification individually, but collectively they erode categorization accuracy and some (Finance/Payments, Personal=tier1) push mail into elevated tiers. Recommend word-boundary or host-anchoring consistent with the hardened finance/travel patterns.

findings.title: Negative email age (future-dated email) bypasses every escalation guard and is treated as fresh
findings.description: calculate_email_age_hours returns `(now - email_date).total_seconds()/3600` (line 1184). If a message has a clock-skewed or maliciously future receivedDateTime (common with spam/spoofed Date headers, and Outlook DOES populate date), age becomes negative. In escalate_by_age, a negative value satisfies `email_age_hours < 24` (line 1122) and the message is never escalated — which is arguably fine, but it is silent: the function returns reason='Email is less than 24 hours old' for a clearly anomalous timestamp. More importantly, in cmd_pending the sort key -age_hours becomes a large positive number for negative ages, pushing future-dated items to the TOP of the pending list (above genuinely old items), distorting triage priority. Worth handling: clamp age to >= 0 or detect future timestamps.

findings.location: line 616 (_local_domains, _local_selfs = _load_local_protected()) executed on import of core
findings.title: Safety-gate dataset is populated by a filesystem read executed at import of the core package
findings.description: core/__init__.py imports core.rules, which at module top level (line 616) calls _load_local_protected() — opening and parsing $PROTECTED_SENDERS_FILE (or ./config/protected_senders.local.txt) every time the core package is imported, to build PROTECTED_SENDERS/SELF_LOCALPARTS. This is an import-time file I/O side effect on the safety-critical path: the protected-sender set is frozen at first import and cannot reflect a file edited later in the process; the path resolves relative to the module's grandparent dir (line 594) regardless of CWD; and (per round 1's separate non-UTF-8 finding) only FileNotFoundError/OSError are caught at line 611. Beyond the encoding crash already reported, the design itself — doing safety-config I/O implicitly at import rather than via an explicit load call — is a fragility: any consumer importing `core` for an unrelated symbol pays the I/O and inherits a frozen snapshot. Fix: lazy/explicit load with a refresh hook.

findings.title: Tech/Google bare substrings 'workspace'/'gcp' over-match unrelated mail
findings.description: Tech/Google (tier 2 Important, priority 9) lists r'workspace' and r'gcp' as bare substrings. 'workspace' matches 'coworkspace', 'workspaces', 'rent coworkspace downtown'; 'gcp' matches any 'gcp' run inside filenames/tokens. Verified re.search(r'workspace','rent coworkspace downtown') and re.search(r'gcp','logo.png gcp-archive') both True. These specific patterns were not in round 1's substring-over-match list (which named uber/target/square/spirit/discover/statement/invoice/reminder/alert/family/mom/dad).

findings.location: Finance/Payments r'plaid' (172); Finance/Banking r'chime' (149); Professional/Jobs r'monster' (380); Travel r'kayak' (321); Shopping r'nike'/'nordstrom'/'zara'/'macys' (250-264)
findings.title: Additional bare-brand substrings collide with common English / unrelated products
findings.description: Beyond round-1's named set, several more bare-substring brand patterns over-match: r'plaid' (Finance/Payments tier 2) matches 'plaid shirts on sale'; r'chime' (Finance/Banking tier 1 Critical) matches 'wind chime sale'; r'monster' (Professional/Jobs tier 2) matches 'monster energy drink'; r'kayak' (Travel tier 2) matches 'kayaking trip photos'; r'nike'/'zara'/'macys' match inside other words. Verified categorize_with_tier('a@b.com','plaid shirts on sale') -> Finance/Payments tier 2 and ('x@y.com','Your monster energy rewards') -> Professional/Jobs tier 2. r'chime' in Finance/Banking is the worst since it is Tier 1 Critical. These specific patterns were not enumerated in round 1.

findings.title: escalate_by_age does not validate current_tier range/type
findings.description: escalate_by_age accepts arbitrary current_tier. A tier of 0 or 5 (reachable when a VIP config sets tier outside 1-4, per round-1 line 380, which flows into cat_result.tier and then into cmd_escalate's escalate_by_age call) is treated as escalatable: escalate_by_age(0,100,True) and escalate_by_age(5,...) bypass the 'tier==1' guard and either escalate to tier 1 or return the bogus tier unchanged, after which get_tier_config(escalated_tier) silently defaults out-of-range tiers to 'Reference' (PRIORITY_TIERS[4]). A non-int current_tier (corrupt stored state / config) would raise a TypeError on the comparisons. Round 1 covered the VIP-tier-out-of-range case in categorize_with_tier but not in escalate_by_age.

findings.title: _find_best_label scans rules in dict order and cannot recover from any same-priority misorder
findings.description: _find_best_label iterates LABEL_RULES in insertion order and on the first pattern whose priority is strictly less than best_priority records it and breaks the inner loop, but never re-considers a later rule with EQUAL priority. Combined with the multiple priority ties present in the data (priority 3, 8, 9, 11, 12, 14, and the critical 17 = Marketing/Tech-Storage/Personal-Government), the function's result is silently order-dependent for every tied group, not just the gov/marketing case. Any future reordering of the LABEL_RULES dict literal changes categorization with no test catching it. Round 1 noted priority ties for the gov case specifically; this flags the structural fragility across all six tied priority groups (verified ties listed via LABEL_RULES priority histogram).

findings.location: Notification patterns lines 414-422 + Tech/Storage/Notification priority interplay
findings.title: Notification catch-all keywords 'notification'/'alert'/'reminder' shadow more specific tier-1 categories below them
findings.description: Notification (priority 16, tier 3 Delegate) sits ABOVE Personal/Government (priority 17, tier 1 Critical) in priority order, and its patterns are extremely broad bare substrings ('notification','alert','reminder'). Any government/health/security mail whose subject contains 'reminder' or 'notification' but whose sender keyword has priority >16 is captured by Notification first and demoted to Delegate. Verified categorize_with_tier('news@x','Reminder: your passport photo discount') -> Notification tier 3 (passport/Government, priority 17, never gets a chance). Round 1 flagged 'reminder'/'alert' as over-matching English words but not this specific priority-ordering shadow of the Critical Government/Health tiers.

findings.title: VIP label_override is not validated against LABEL_RULES; a bogus override yields default time_sensitive=True silently
findings.description: When a VIP has label_override set, categorize_with_tier uses it verbatim as the label without checking it exists in LABEL_RULES: 'rule = LABEL_RULES.get(label, {"time_sensitive": True})'. A typo'd or arbitrary override string (from user YAML, fully untrusted) becomes the returned CategorizationResult.label and is then appended to action.add_labels in run_labeler (cli.py:244) and applied by the provider, potentially creating a junk label/folder on the live account. The fallback also silently forces time_sensitive=True for any unknown override. Round 1 covered the VIP-tier-out-of-range and VIP-no-override branches but not the unvalidated label_override propagating to provider apply.

findings.title: Label selection iterates dict insertion order, so a higher-priority rule defined later than a same-text earlier rule can lose the tie incorrectly
findings.description: _find_best_label breaks the inner pattern loop on the FIRST matching pattern of a label and only updates best_match when priority is strictly less than the current best. Because it walks LABEL_RULES in insertion order and 'Misc/Other' (priority 999, pattern '.*') is last, this is mostly fine — but rules sharing identical priority values (e.g. Finance/Tax and Finance/Payments both priority 8; Tech/Security and Tech/Google both 9; several at 11/14/17) resolve by insertion order, not by any deterministic, documented precedence. A subject matching both a priority-9 security pattern and the priority-9 Google pattern is categorized by whichever appears first in the dict — fragile and silently order-dependent.

findings.title: calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 for a None date, which escalate_by_age treats as a fresh (<24h) email — undated mail never escalates
findings.description: calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 when email_date is None (providers like Gmail/IMAP/Mail.app do not populate EmailMessage.date on the list/triage path). escalate_by_age then sees age 0 < 24 and reports should_escalate=False. So for providers that don't supply a date, time-based escalation is silently a no-op for every message — the feature degrades to nothing without any signal that the date was missing. (cli.py round1 noted cmd_escalate trusts age_hours=0; this is the rules-layer root cause.)

findings.title: clear() resets in-memory state before removing the file, then swallows removal errors — leaves stale file
findings.description: clear() sets self.state to defaults first, then tries os.remove inside a broad try/except that only logs on failure. If the remove fails (permission, file locked by another process), the function returns normally and the caller believes the state was cleared, but the old state file is still on disk. A subsequent fresh StateManager(filename) will _load that stale file and resume from the supposedly-cleared token — a silent failure to reset. Either the removal should be attempted before mutating in-memory state, or the failure should be surfaced to the caller.

findings.location: line 87 (last_run = datetime.now().isoformat()) and the absence of any reader
findings.title: state.py last_run is written as a naive-local ISO string that no code ever parses back, so the isoformat round-trip is untested and would break a naive-vs-aware comparison if a reader is added
findings.description: last_run is persisted as `datetime.now().isoformat()` — a naive local ISO string with no offset. Round 1 flagged the naive-local-vs-UTC inconsistency. The additional, distinct defect: nothing in the repo ever calls datetime.fromisoformat on last_run, so the value is write-only dead state; and the moment a reader is added it will fromisoformat() a naive string and, if it ever compares against the UTC-aware now used everywhere else (audit.py, rules.py), raise `TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes`. The mixed naive(state)/aware(audit,rules) convention is a latent TypeError landmine for any future last_run-based staleness/resume-window check.

findings.title: State file written non-atomically — a crash mid-write corrupts the resume token and loses all progress
findings.description: save() opens the state file 'w' and json.dump directly over it. A crash or kill during the write leaves a truncated/invalid JSON file; _load then hits JSONDecodeError, logs, and returns _default_state() — silently discarding the saved page token and processed counts, so the next run restarts from scratch (or, for dry-run-polluted state per round1's cli finding, from a wrong token). Atomic write (temp file + os.replace) is the standard fix for a crash-recovery state file.

findings.title: Implemented conditions do not match documented intent (no OR / no Label conditions)
findings.description: The comment describes 'Label is Awaiting Reply OR To Do OR (Personal AND Unread)', but the actual conditions are sender-contains + message-is-unread with no 'match type' specified (Mail.app defaults to ALL/AND, not the OR the comment implies) and no label/mailbox condition. The built mailbox semantics diverge from the stated design.

findings.title: No idempotency — re-running duplicates smart mailboxes
findings.description: make new smart mailbox is called unconditionally (unlike the 'Ω Automation' group at line 4 which is wrapped in try). Running the script twice creates duplicate '1. Needs Action' / '2. Finance (Recent)' / '3. Dev Alerts' smart mailboxes. No existence check.

findings.title: launchctl bootstrap not idempotent / no error handling; can fail on re-deploy
findings.description: Lines 54-57 `bootout` old jobs with `|| true` (good), but line 60 `launchctl bootstrap "$AGENT_ID" "$PLIST_DEST"` has no `|| true` and is run under `set -e`. If the service is already loaded (bootout raced or a different label is still resident) bootstrap returns non-zero and aborts the whole deploy after the plist has already been copied, leaving a half-applied state. Bootstrap of an already-loaded label is a common non-zero case; guard it or check load state first.

findings.title: deploy.sh boots out old jobs then bootstraps, but never reloads gmail_labeler — re-running deploy after a rename leaves only mail_automation, fine, but bootout failures are swallowed
findings.description: deploy.sh:54 and :57 run 'launchctl bootout ... 2>/dev/null || true' to remove the prior mail_automation and gmail_labeler jobs, then :60 bootstraps mail_automation. The '|| true' swallows all errors, including the case where bootstrap on :60 fails because the old job is still loaded (bootout raced or was denied). There is no verification after bootstrap (no 'launchctl list | grep mail_automation' check, no exit-code check on bootstrap), so deploy.sh reports 'LaunchAgent installed' (line 62) unconditionally even if bootstrap failed. Combined with the absolutized paths, a failed install is reported as success.

findings.title: Demo doc lists /v1/triage/preview as verified but omits that POST /v1/triage is a silent no-op that always returns dry_run:true
findings.description: The worker maps both /v1/triage/preview (line 255) and /v1/triage (line 260) to triagePreview(), which hardcodes `dry_run: true` and `run_id: "demo_preview"` (worker lines 172-198). So an 'apply' triage against the share demo silently does nothing and reports dry_run:true, and reports a fabricated fixed audit (total 3, protected_held 2, archived 1) regardless of input. cloudflare-share-demo.md documents the billing-mutation endpoints as returning 503 but says nothing about /v1/triage being a no-op preview, and the 'Verified Demo Endpoints' list only mentions /v1/triage/preview (not /v1/triage) even though /v1/triage is wired up. Minor accuracy gap that could mislead a reviewer who POSTs to /v1/triage expecting either an action or a clear 'demo-only' signal.

findings.title: Nav-dot active-state indexing relies on data-section values being a 0-based dense sequence matching DOM order
findings.description: The active-dot logic builds one dot per `section[data-section]` in document order (lines 260-265), then on intersection does `const idx = e.target.dataset.section; ... if (dots[idx]) dots[idx].classList.add('active')` (lines 270-273). `dots[idx]` is an index lookup keyed by the raw `data-section` attribute string. It happens to work today only because the sections are authored with data-section = "0".."7" in the same order they appear in the DOM, so attribute value == array index. If the generator ever emits non-contiguous, reordered, or non-numeric data-section values (e.g. skips a section, or uses ids), the wrong dot — or no dot — highlights. The `if (dots[idx])` guard prevents a crash but the mapping is silently wrong. Lower-risk because it's cosmetic and the input is generator-controlled, but it's a latent correctness bug coupling attribute values to array positions. Safer to map by the section's own index (use the loop index or `Array.from(sections).indexOf(e.target)`).

findings.location: lines 5-11 (delivery web_app status: not_started; revenue subscription status: planned) vs implemented api/billing.py + web/index.html + acp/router.py
findings.title: ecosystem.yaml status fields are stale — claims web_app not_started and subscription revenue merely planned while both are implemented
findings.description: ecosystem.yaml records delivery `platform: web_app, status: not_started` and revenue `platform: subscription, status: planned`. The repo actually ships a built static web dashboard (web/index.html, mounted at /app and deployed to Cloudflare per the handoff docs) and a fully implemented Stripe subscription path (api/billing.py: checkout, portal, webhook, plans catalog) plus an ACP agent-commerce surface. The machine-readable ecosystem manifest that downstream organvm tooling consumes therefore reports the commerce surface as not-yet-started when it is in fact built and being deployed. Round 1 flagged only the ecosystem-vs-seed disagreement; this is the ecosystem-vs-implemented-reality staleness, which will misinform any roadmap/status aggregation reading this file.

findings.title: File handle leaks on error: close access is outside any try and the open path may not exist
findings.description: Line 2 opens the output file (`open for access ... with write permission`) and the matching `close access outFile` is only at line 41, outside any error handling. If the inner write loop throws an unhandled error (only the per-message body is wrapped in try; the outer `repeat with mbox` and the `name of mbox` / `messages of mbox` access are not fully guarded against all failure modes), the script can terminate with the file access handle still open, which can leave the file locked for the rest of the AppleScript session. Additionally, the output path `~/mail_automation/mail_export.tsv` (line 1) is opened directly; if the `~/mail_automation` directory does not exist, `open for access` fails immediately. There is no `mkdir`/folder-existence step.

findings.title: TSV delimiter/newline injection corrupts output rows
findings.description: Sender, subject, and date fields are written raw into a tab-delimited file (`rowText` built with `tab` separators, `linefeed` row terminator). Email subjects and sender display names routinely contain tab characters and embedded newlines/CRLF; writing them unescaped produces malformed rows that break any downstream TSV parser (column shift, phantom rows). No sanitization/escaping of tabs or linefeeds is performed.

findings.title: Hardcoded placeholder sender list ships as-is (no-op or wrong matches if run unedited)
findings.description: `importantSenders` is hardcoded to template placeholders (`boss@company.com`, `admissions@university.edu`, `familymember@example.com`). If a user runs the script without editing, it silently flags nothing (or, via the substring `contains` match at line 5, could over-match — e.g. `example.com` substring would flag any sender whose address contains `familymember@example.com`). The `contains` semantics mean a list entry like a bare domain would flag unrelated senders. There is no warning that the list is unconfigured.

findings.title: Iterating live inbox messages with bare try swallows errors; no progress/logging
findings.description: Same pattern as archive_old_inbox: iterates `messages of inbox` and the per-message body is wrapped in a bare `try` with no `on error`. Any failure to read `sender of msg` or set flagged status is silently discarded. Lower impact here (flagging is non-destructive) but the same silent-failure smell applies, and a user gets no signal if zero messages were flagged because of an error vs. no matches.

findings.title: No scope verification on loaded token; a narrower stored token is treated as valid
findings.description: Credentials.from_authorized_user_info(token_info, scopes=scopes) attaches the requested scopes but does not verify the *granted* scopes of the stored token. If a previously stored token was granted fewer scopes than the caller now requests (e.g. read-only token but caller asks for gmail.modify), creds.valid can still be True (unexpired), so no refresh/re-consent is triggered, and downstream Gmail mutate calls then fail at request time with 403 insufficientPermissions rather than re-prompting. There is no creds.has_scopes(scopes) check.

findings.title: Gmail OAuth token passed as 1Password CLI argv (process-list exposure)
findings.description: Same issue as auth/onepassword.op_item_edit: `cmd = ['op', 'item', 'edit', item, f'{field}={value}']` puts the compact token JSON (access+refresh token) on the `op` process argv, visible via ps/proc to other local users while the command runs. store_token_info passes the serialized credentials this way on every token refresh. Pass via stdin instead.

findings.title: Interactive run_local_server(port=0) silently triggered on a headless/automated run
findings.description: If the cached token is invalid/expired-refresh-failed, get_credentials falls through to InstalledAppFlow.run_local_server(port=0), which starts a local web server and blocks waiting for an interactive browser OAuth. Under the launchd/run_automation.sh unattended path this would hang indefinitely (no TTY/browser) rather than failing fast, blocking the daily job. Should detect non-interactive context and raise instead of launching an interactive flow.

findings.title: No timeout on op subprocess invocation
findings.description: subprocess.run for the `op` CLI has no timeout=. If `op` hangs (e.g. waiting on a desktop-app approval prompt, biometric, or a stuck network session), the calling thread blocks indefinitely. In the hosted MCP/REST path this ties up a request worker. A bounded timeout would fail fast and surface a clear error.

findings.title: Label auto-creation in _init_labels has no error handling; a create failure aborts the entire run before any processing
findings.description: _init_labels iterates LABEL_RULES.keys() and calls labels().create for any missing label (lines 110-113) with no try/except. core/rules has ~25 categories. If any single create fails (rate limit not handled here since this path does NOT use _execute_with_backoff, or a name collision/limit), the exception propagates out of __init__ and the whole labeler aborts before processing a single message. The legacy get_or_create_label at least wraps creation in try/except and uses execute_with_retry. The non-backoff list/create here is also vulnerable to startup rate-limit 429s.

findings.title: Messages that fail BOTH batch-get and one-by-one retry are silently dropped from categorization (never labeled/archived)
findings.description: If a message's metadata fetch fails in the batch (callback appends to failed_ids) and the single retry at lines 170-181 also raises HttpError, it is logged then never added to batch_results, so it is omitted from the modifications loop (line 194 iterates batch_results only). Such a message is neither labeled nor archived this pass. With page-token state advancing (run() saves nextPageToken), on the default 'has:nouserlabels' query it could be revisited later, but for a custom query started fresh it is simply skipped. Silent under-processing rather than a crash, but worth noting for a tool that claims 'exhaustive' labeling.

findings.title: max_emails limit is only enforced between pages, not within a page — can overshoot by up to a full batch
findings.description: The max_emails check (line 445) runs once per page AFTER the inner per-message loop (lines 415-443) has fully processed up to batch_size (default 500) messages. So with max_emails=10 and batch_size=500, the code labels up to 500 messages before the check fires and breaks. The intent of a max limit (e.g. a small test run) is defeated; it overshoots by nearly a full page. The check belongs inside the per-message loop.

findings.title: execute_with_retry has two redundant except blocks and can return None on exhaustion, propagating None to .get()/['id'] callers
findings.description: Lines 304-309 catch (HttpError, OSError, socket.error) and lines 310-315 catch bare Exception with IDENTICAL bodies — the first block is fully subsumed by the second (every exception type is an Exception), making the first dead/redundant. Worse, if the loop completes without returning (e.g. retries=0, or a code path that never raises but also never returns), the function implicitly returns None; callers like get_or_create_label do created_label['id'] (line 330) which would TypeError on None. The function also retries on ALL exceptions including non-retryable 4xx (400/401/403 invalid label), wasting time and money before re-raising.

findings.file: iCloud Mail Filtering Rules - Complete Guide.md
findings.location: iCloud Mail Filtering Rules - Complete Guide.md:23-24,70-71,216-217
findings.title: Committed guide has line-broken email addresses corrupting the documented rules
findings.description: This tracked doc contains only generic well-known brand sender addresses (banks, retailers, airlines) — NOT the owner's personal PII — so it is not a privacy leak. However several addresses are broken across lines and will copy-paste wrong: line 23-24 'service@american' / 'express.com' (should be service@americanexpress.com), line 70-71 a stray bullet then 'email@zara.com', and line 216-217 'scholar-alerts-noreply@google.com' split off its bullet. Anyone following the guide to build iCloud rules will create malformed sender conditions. Content-hygiene defect.

findings.location: main() lines 161-166; per-message archive with no rate-limit/error backoff
findings.title: Apply loop has no per-message error backoff and EXPUNGEs after every message; large applies are slow and a mid-loop server error aborts the rest
findings.description: The apply loop (lines 161-165) calls archive_uid per message; with the COPY fallback each call EXPUNGEs the whole mailbox (see separate finding), making N archives O(N) expunges. There is no try/except inside the loop, so an imaplib error on one uid raises out, aborting all remaining moves (the finally only logs out). The summary 'Moved X, failed Y' (line 166) therefore can never actually report a non-fatal failure for the MOVE path because archive_uid returns True/False only for the COPY-failure case, not for exceptions which propagate.

findings.location: main lines 131-158 (no try/finally around imap session)
findings.title: IMAP connection leaked on exception — logout only on the happy path
findings.description: connect_imap opens an IMAP4_SSL session (line 131); imap.logout() is only called at line 158 after the processing loop completes. Any exception during select, search, fetch_headers, ensure_label, or apply_label propagates out of main() without logging out, leaking the socket/session. Should be wrapped in try/finally to guarantee logout. Repeated failures could exhaust server connection limits.

findings.title: 1Password failure falls through to interactive getpass even in non-interactive/automation context, and swallows all errors
findings.description: load_password catches a bare `except Exception` (line 71) on the `op` subprocess and merely prints the error, then falls through to getpass.getpass() (line 74). In a launchd/cron/automation context there is no TTY, so getpass either raises (caught by the bare except at line 75 returning '') or reads from a non-interactive stream, causing the run to abort at the empty-password check (line 127) with a generic message rather than surfacing the real 1Password failure. The over-broad except hides the actual op error (auth expired, item renamed). The function also has unreachable `return ""` at line 77 after the try/except that already returns.

findings.title: Unguarded dict indexing on labels list and header lookups can KeyError/crash
findings.description: Line 8: `results['labels']` is indexed directly (vs .get). Line 27-28 inside cb: `response['payload']['headers']` and `h['name']`/`h['value']` are indexed without .get; a message with no payload (e.g. drafts, or chat) or a header dict missing keys raises KeyError inside the batch callback, which the batch framework may swallow or surface unpredictably. Read-only inspection tool so low blast radius, but it will crash on certain message shapes.

findings.title: Mail export TSV (PII sink: sender/subject/message_id columns) is committed
findings.description: mail_export.tsv is git-tracked and is the output target of export_mail_snapshot.applescript, with a header row declaring columns sender, subject, message_id, date_received, read, flagged, size_bytes. It currently holds only the header (no data rows), but committing this file means a future `export_mail_snapshot` run that populates it will, on the next commit, leak real sender addresses and subject lines (PII) into the public repo history. It should be in .gitignore (alongside the audit/*.jsonl and protected_senders.local.txt entries that already exist for the same reason) rather than tracked.

findings.title: No error handling on list/batchModify; pagination relies on label removal and len<1000 heuristic, can loop
findings.description: Like the other legacy scripts, mark_read_loop has no try/except around the Gmail API calls — any HttpError aborts with a traceback. It loops 'while True' on the same query 'label:X is:unread older_than:7d' and breaks when len(ids)<1000, relying on the UNREAD removal to shrink the result set. If batchModify silently fails to remove UNREAD (no exception but no effect) the same 1000 messages return next iteration and the loop never terminates. No backoff, no state. Marks-read-only so not a data-safety issue, but an unbounded loop / hard crash risk.

findings.title: No error handling on Gmail list/batchModify; service never closed
findings.description: mark_read_loop calls service.users().messages().list(...).execute() and batchModify(...).execute() with no try/except — any HttpError (rate limit, transient 5xx) crashes the whole script. The pagination uses no pageToken; it relies on removeLabelIds=['UNREAD'] shrinking the is:unread result set, breaking when len(ids)<1000. If a batchModify partially fails or the query keeps returning >=1000 (e.g. UNREAD removal silently not taking effect on some), the loop can spin. The gmail service object is never disconnected/closed. Lower severity (read-state only, documented legacy).

findings.title: Pagination re-queries first page; termination on len(ids)<1000 is unreliable, can loop or under-process
findings.description: Same broken pagination pattern: list page 1 of `label:X is:unread older_than:7d` (no pageToken, line 44-46), batchModify removes UNREAD, break only when messages empty (line 49) or len(ids)<1000 (line 63). It relies on the UNREAD removal shrinking the is:unread query. This generally works, but if batchModify partially fails or Gmail search index lag returns a still-unread message, the same full page can re-appear and the loop re-runs. There is no try/except around list() or batchModify() (lines 44, 60), so any HttpError (rate limit) crashes the whole run with no backoff — unlike gmail_labeler.py which has _execute_with_backoff.

findings.title: Marks-read targets reference label names that diverge from the core taxonomy (Work/Dev/* present, but core uses Dev/*)
findings.description: TARGET_CATEGORIES includes 'Work/Dev/GitHub' and 'Work/Dev/Infrastructure' while core/rules.py defines 'Dev/GitHub'/'Dev/Infrastructure'. If the account's labels follow the core scheme, those two queries match nothing and the mark-read silently no-ops for them; if they follow organize_labels.py's renamed scheme they match. Same taxonomy-drift fragility as archive_sorted.py — the standalone scripts hardcode a label vocabulary that is not derived from the single source of truth (LABEL_RULES).

findings.title: Heavy MCP objects (FastMCP server + full ASGI app) are constructed at module import time, and MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS is read at import
findings.description: Importing mcp_server.server eagerly executes three import-time side effects beyond the SDK import: (1) `_transport_security()` reads os.environ['MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS'] at line 79 during the FastMCP(...) construction, freezing the host allowlist at import time so a later env change is ignored; (2) the FastMCP server object is fully constructed at module scope (lines 73-80); (3) `http_app = mcp.streamable_http_app()` (line 151) builds the entire Streamable-HTTP ASGI app at import. This makes `import mcp_server.server` expensive and non-idempotent w.r.t. env, and means any import-time exception in FastMCP/streamable_http_app surfaces as a package import failure (api/app.py wraps it in try/except, but `python -m mcp_server` and direct uvicorn targets do not). Round 1 noted only the MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' policy, not the import-time construction/env-freeze. Fix: build the server/app inside a factory called from main()/uvicorn factory rather than at module top level.

findings.location: triage() provider param line 111; triage_preview() provider line 99; _triage line 132 vs api/schemas.py:45 (provider max_length=64)
findings.title: MCP provider argument is length-unbounded, bypassing the HTTP layer's documented anti-DoS max_length=64
findings.description: api/schemas.py:45 bounds TriageRequest.provider to max_length=64 (the comment at lines 26-28/12-15 frames these caps as anti-DoS/anti-abuse). The MCP tools accept provider as a free str and pass it straight into service.run_triage -> cli.get_provider with no length bound. _clamp_limit (lines 83-84) is the only restraint and only touches limit; check_protected_sender truncates to 4096 (line 94) but triage/triage_preview do not bound provider at all. Round 1 noted query and remove_label are unbounded on the MCP path but did not call out provider; this is the third unbounded field and the one that reaches the provider-factory dispatch.

findings.title: readOnlyHint=True on triage_preview masks a credentialed, quota-consuming scan of up to 1000 messages with a per-batch sleep
findings.description: triage_preview is annotated readOnlyHint=True (line 97), which signals to agents that the tool is side-effect-free and cheap to call freely. In reality it calls service.run_triage with real mailbox credentials, connects to the provider, and pages through up to MAX_TRIAGE_LIMIT=1000 messages (one provider round-trip plus a 1s throttle sleep per ~100-message batch — exactly the DoS vector schemas.py:12-16 documents for the HTTP path). With no metering on the MCP surface, an agent (or a prompt-injected loop) repeatedly invoking this 'read-only' tool can exhaust the server's Gmail/Graph API quota and hold connections open. The readOnlyHint advertisement actively encourages the unsafe pattern. NEW framing distinct from round 1's generic 'no metering' item.

findings.title: MCP error mapping collapses provider-unavailable and gate-violation to generic RuntimeError, losing the HTTP layer's status semantics and is-retriable signal
findings.description: On the HTTP path (api/app.py:108-122) ProviderUnavailable maps to HTTP 503 (transient/retriable) and AuditInvariantError maps to 500 with a loud generic message. On the MCP path both are re-raised as a bare RuntimeError (lines 139, 142). FastMCP surfaces any tool exception as a generic isError result, so the agent cannot distinguish 'provider temporarily unavailable, safe to retry' from 'SAFETY GATE VIOLATION, do NOT retry / a protected sender was nearly moved.' The retriability and severity signal that the HTTP contract carries is erased, so a driving agent may retry a rejected destructive run or give up on a transient one. Distinct from round 1's items (which note the message is generic, not that the two error classes are conflated into one type with no machine-distinguishable signal).

findings.title: base.apply_actions wraps the entire per-message body in one try/except, so a failure in apply_label aborts remove/archive/star for that message but still counts it as one processed
findings.description: The single try/except (340-367) means if apply_label raises midway, the subsequent remove_label/archive/star/apply_category for the SAME message are skipped, the message is counted in error_count, but partial side effects (e.g. a label that WAS applied before the exception) remain. More subtly, did_leave_inbox/did_label_move/applied_labels reflect only what ran before the exception, and the audit record at 383 is still written for the failed message with whatever partial state — potentially recording a message as labeled/moved when the operation actually errored. Broad try/except around multiple distinct side-effecting operations makes partial-failure auditing unreliable. Medium-confidence error-handling weakness.

findings.location: apply_actions lines 356-363 (star/category for protected senders on folder providers)
findings.title: Protected-sender gate suppresses label-moves but still applies star and category to protected mail
findings.description: For folder providers with LABEL_IS_MOVE (Outlook/Mail.app), when protected=True the code sets move_via_label=True and skips add_labels (correct — a move would eject protected mail). But action.star (line 356) and action.category (line 358) still execute for protected senders. apply_category on Outlook issues a PATCH adding a category tag (does not move the message, so the never-archive invariant holds), and star flags it — neither ejects from inbox, so this is not a safety-invariant breach. However it means protected senders that the user expected to be left entirely untouched still get mutated (flagged/categorized) with no gate consideration, contradicting the 'neutralize every out-of-inbox operation' framing and surprising users who treat protected as do-not-touch. Worth an explicit decision.

findings.title: list_messages caps maxResults at `limit` but caller paginates expecting full pages, conflating per-page limit with total limit
findings.description: page_size = min(limit, LIST_PAGE_SIZE). The Gmail API treats maxResults as a per-page cap, but the `limit` parameter is documented (base.py:127) as 'Maximum messages to return per page'. The CLI (cli.py:185) passes batch_limit = min(limit - processed_this_run, 100) as the per-call limit and loops on next_page_token, so the two interpretations happen to align there. But any caller that passes limit=1000 expecting 1000 total in one call will silently get only one page of up to 500 (LIST_PAGE_SIZE), with the rest only reachable via next_page_token. The single-call `limit` semantics are ambiguous and under-deliver vs. a naive reading. Medium-confidence API-contract ambiguity.

findings.title: Backoff has no jitter and a high 10s base, compounding to ~310s worst case while holding the call serialized
findings.description: BASE_BACKOFF_SECONDS=10 doubling for up to 5 retries gives sleeps of 10,20,40,80,160 = 310s total blocking on a single throttled call, with no jitter (thundering-herd risk if multiple workers retry in lockstep) and no respect for any Retry-After header Gmail may return. For an interactive CLI this is a long opaque hang. Also the retry only triggers on 403/429 with specific reason tags in str(e); a 503 backendError or transient 500 is re-raised immediately with no retry. Low/medium severity resilience gap.

findings.title: O(n*m) reverse label lookup per message and silent drop of labels not in cache
findings.description: For each message, every label id is reverse-mapped to a name by scanning the entire _label_cache (nested loop, O(labels_on_msg * total_labels)). More importantly, any label id present on the message but NOT in the cache (e.g. a label created by another client after connect(), or a system label like CATEGORY_PERSONAL/IMPORTANT not pre-cached) is silently dropped from the returned EmailMessage.labels set, so msg.labels under-reports the true label set. Categorization/keep-in-inbox decisions that depend on existing labels would see incomplete data. Low/medium-confidence correctness (stale-cache) issue plus a perf smell.

findings.title: Unconditional time.sleep(2.0) after every batch of 20 makes large fetches very slow and is not configurable
findings.description: After every BATCH_GET_SIZE (20) chunk, batch_get_details sleeps 2.0s unconditionally even when not rate-limited. Fetching 1000 messages = 50 batches = ~100s of pure sleep regardless of actual quota pressure. This throttle is hardcoded and not tied to observed rate limiting. Combined with the per-action 0.5s sleep in apply_actions (line 475) this dominates runtime. Perf/observability smell, low severity.

findings.title: connect() with injected service sets _connected but never initializes label cache, leaving _label_cache empty
findings.description: When a pre-built `service` is injected (testing path, line 82-84), connect() returns early WITHOUT calling _init_label_cache(). Subsequent apply_label/remove_label/get_message_details then operate against an empty _label_cache: apply_label will treat every label as missing and call ensure_label_exists (creating labels), remove_label falls back to using names as ids, and reverse-lookup returns no labels. This is a real divergence between the injected-service path and the OAuth path. While intended for tests, it means any production code that injects a service must remember to populate the cache. Medium-confidence latent bug.

findings.title: Rate-limit detection by string-matching the exception message is brittle and may mask non-rate-limit 403s as fatal or vice versa
findings.description: Retry is gated on status in (403,429) AND substring match of 'rateLimitExceeded'/'userRateLimitExceeded'/'quotaExceeded' in str(e). Google returns several other retryable reasons (e.g. 'backendError', 503, 500) that are NOT retried here and will propagate as hard failures on a transient blip. Conversely a permanent quota-exhausted 403 ('Daily Limit Exceeded') contains 'quotaExceeded' and will be retried 5x with exponential backoff (10,20,40,80s) — ~2.5 minutes of futile sleeping before failing. Matching on the structured reason field of the HttpError JSON rather than substring of the rendered message would be more robust. Note BASE_BACKOFF_SECONDS=10 with doubling is also quite long for the first retry.

findings.title: remove_label falls back to using raw label name as label ID when not cached
findings.description: remove_label does `label_id = self._label_cache.get(label) or label` and then sends it as removeLabelIds. For a non-system, non-cached label this sends the human label NAME (e.g. 'Misc/Other') to the API as if it were a label ID; Gmail will reject it (400) — caught and logged as failure. For system labels it works. The fallback masks a cache-miss as an API error rather than ensuring/looking up the id, so removals can silently fail. apply_actions remove_ids has the same fallback. Edge correctness.

findings.location: apply_actions audit loop, lines 434 (pending keyed by message_id) and 482-495
findings.title: Duplicate message_ids in the action list collide in the pending dict, losing/mis-recording audit receipts
findings.description: pending is a Dict keyed solely on action.message_id (line 434). If the action list contains two LabelAction entries for the same message_id (legitimately possible since LabelAction.merge exists precisely because callers may emit multiple actions per message, and nothing dedupes before apply_actions), the second write clobbers the first's audit intent (sender/protected/add_labels/left_inbox). Then the post-execution audit loop at 482-495 iterates the FULL actions list and calls audit.record once per action — so the same message_id is recorded multiple times, each time reading the single (last-written) pending entry. Result: duplicate audit receipts with possibly the wrong disposition for the earlier action. Round 1 did not cover this multi-action-same-id collision in the Gmail audit path.

findings.title: apply_label resolves a stale/missing label id and never re-validates the cache after ensure creates it
findings.description: apply_label does label_id = self._label_cache.get(label); if missing, label_id = self.ensure_label_exists(label). ensure_label_exists creates the label and populates the cache, returning the id — so this path is OK. But note the single-message apply_label is the path used when callers bypass apply_actions; combined with remove_label's fallback (self._label_cache.get(label) or label, line 317) the two methods disagree: apply_label always resolves to a real id (creating if needed), while remove_label silently sends the raw label NAME as a removeLabelIds value on a cache miss, which Gmail rejects (invalid label id) — making the removal silently fail. Round 1 (547/549/556) flagged the remove_label raw-name fallback generally; the NEW angle here is the asymmetry: apply_label creates-and-resolves while remove_label does not, so a label present for adds but absent from cache for removes produces inconsistent, silently-failing behavior within the same provider.

findings.title: 403 non-rate-limit errors (insufficientPermissions/forbidden) are only retried if message text matches, else immediately raised — but a 403 quota error whose body lacks the exact tag is treated as fatal
findings.description: rate_limited requires status in (403,429) AND one of the literal substrings 'rateLimitExceeded'/'userRateLimitExceeded'/'quotaExceeded' in str(e). Gmail also returns 403 'dailyLimitExceeded' and 'userRateLimitExceededUnreg' and sometimes 429 'rateLimitExceeded' with the reason only in the structured error.errors[].reason, not always in str(e). A 403 dailyLimitExceeded (which IS a rate condition) lacks the matched substrings, so it is re-raised as fatal instead of backing off, aborting the run. Conversely a genuine 403 forbidden won't match and is correctly raised. Round 1 (555) flagged the string-matching brittleness generically; the NEW concrete instance is 'dailyLimitExceeded' specifically falling through to fatal because it is not in the matched tag set.

findings.title: Rate-limit backoff sleeps up to 10+20+40+80+160s synchronously, blocking the request thread with no jitter or cap
findings.description: BASE_BACKOFF_SECONDS=10 doubling over 5 retries can sleep ~310s total in one call, all synchronous time.sleep on the worker thread. The API path (service.run_triage) is dispatched on FastAPI's threadpool; a rate-limited run holds a worker for minutes with no overall deadline and no jitter (thundering-herd on concurrent runs). Combined with the request having no timeout, this can exhaust the threadpool under provider throttling.

findings.title: COPY-to-folder does not verify ensure_label_exists succeeded; create('NO') swallowed so COPY to nonexistent folder fails silently-ish
findings.description: ensure_label_exists treats both 'OK' and 'NO' from create() as 'folder now usable' and caches it. But 'NO' from CREATE can mean failure for reasons other than 'already exists' (e.g. invalid name, permission). If create truly failed, the label is cached as created, and the subsequent COPY returns NO and apply_label returns False — but on a retry ensure_label_exists short-circuits (cached) so it never tries to create again. Mis-classification of CREATE 'NO' as success.

findings.title: Decoded header parts joined with space can corrupt RFC2047 multi-encoded-word values and ignores decode errors silently
findings.description: _decode_header_value joins all decoded parts with ' ' (space). RFC 2047 encoded-words that are adjacent should be concatenated WITHOUT inserting whitespace (whitespace between encoded words is to be removed). Joining with a space inserts spurious spaces into senders/subjects (e.g. a name split across two encoded words gets an extra space), subtly corrupting the value used for categorization. Also errors='ignore' silently drops undecodable bytes. Minor correctness for non-ASCII headers.

findings.title: IMAP pagination slice math can return overlapping or out-of-order pages and uses reverse-from-end indexing that breaks when total < limit
findings.description: uids = uids[max(0, total - start - limit):total - start]. For start=0,limit=100,total=50 this is uids[max(0,-50):50] = uids[0:50] (ok). For start=100 with total=50, the high index 'total-start' = -50, so uids[max(0,-150):-50] = uids[0:-50] = empty-ish but the negative stop index silently slices from the wrong end rather than returning empty. Negative stop indices (when start>total) yield Python's from-the-end slicing instead of an empty page, so a caller paging past the end can receive unexpected messages. next_token logic (next_start < total) partially guards forward paging but the slice itself is fragile. Also messages are taken 'most recent first (reverse order)' by tail-slicing but UIDs returned by SEARCH are ascending; the comment claims reverse but the slice does not reverse — ordering is ascending within the tail window, contradicting the doc.

findings.title: IMAP connection leaked if login() fails
findings.description: connect() assigns `self._connection = imaplib.IMAP4_SSL(...)` then calls `self._connection.login(...)`. If login raises (bad credentials, server error), the socket-backed IMAP4_SSL object is created and stored on self._connection but never logout()'d/closed, and the exception propagates. In api/service.run_triage, connect() failure is caught and mapped to ProviderUnavailable, and prov.disconnect() is in a finally — but disconnect() only logs out if self._connection is set, which it is here, so it may partially recover. However for direct/CLI use and any path that doesn't call disconnect, the SSL socket leaks. Wrap login in try/except that closes the connection and resets self._connection to None on failure.

findings.title: IMAP self._connection used without None-check (AttributeError if not connected)
findings.description: Every method dereferences self._connection.uid(...) / self._connection.select(...) but _connection defaults to None and is only set in connect(). If any method is called before connect() (e.g. health_check on base calls list_messages, or misuse), it raises AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'uid' rather than a clear 'not connected' error. Edge-case robustness, not a common-path crash given context-manager usage.

findings.title: ensure_label_exists swallows mailbox-create failure but still caches the name and returns it, so a later move silently targets a nonexistent mailbox
findings.description: On a RuntimeError from _run_applescript, the function logs and falls through WITHOUT adding to _created_mailboxes — but it still returns 'label'. More importantly, in the success branch it adds to _created_mailboxes on ANY non-error result, including when the AppleScript 'on error' path failed to actually create the mailbox but returned without raising. apply_label then does 'move targetMsg to mailbox "{label}"'; if the mailbox does not exist this raises and is caught at line 275 returning False — but in base.apply_actions a folder-move that returns False sets neither did_label_move nor an error in a way that distinguishes 'mailbox missing' from 'protected hold'. The net effect: a create failure is logged once and then the move silently fails per-message with the message LEFT in the inbox while the run still reports overall success. Acceptable for inbox-safety (it stays put) but the failure is masked from the result counts.

findings.title: Per-call limit not enforced when paginating via @odata.nextLink; can over-fetch beyond requested limit
findings.description: When page_token is provided, list_messages uses it as the URL verbatim with params=None (417-420). The @odata.nextLink carries the ORIGINAL $top value baked in by the first request. The CLI shrinks batch_limit each iteration (cli.py 185: min(limit - processed_this_run, 100)) and passes it as `limit`, but on every paginated call after the first, that smaller limit is ignored because params is None — Graph returns up to the original $top per page. The processed_this_run < limit while-loop still bounds the run, but a single page can return more messages than `limit - processed_this_run`, so processed_this_run can overshoot the user-requested --limit by up to (top-1) messages. Minor over-processing, but it violates the documented 'Maximum messages to return' contract.

findings.title: Category create-conflict fallback re-fetches but still raises generic RuntimeError, and masks non-conflict errors as 'race'
findings.description: On _api_post failure the code assumes a race condition ('Category might already exist'), refreshes the cache and re-checks (315-320). But the post can fail for many non-race reasons (auth, throttling, invalid color, network). All are treated identically as a race; if the re-fetch also misses (or itself fails — _init_category_cache swallows and leaves cache empty), it raises RuntimeError with no underlying error context (the original exception `e` is only logged at debug level, not chained). Diagnosing real failures becomes hard, and a throttling 429 is silently retried as a cache refresh once with no backoff.

findings.title: Folder-name resolution depends on a cache that is empty when connect() was skipped or folder caching failed; labels silently missing
findings.description: get_message_details resolves the message's parentFolderId to a folder display name by scanning self._folder_cache (497-499). If _init_folder_cache failed (its except at 261 leaves the cache empty/partial) or if the message is in a folder not in the cache (e.g. created after connect, or a well-known folder like 'archive' whose displayName differs), labels stays empty and the message appears to have no folder. Downstream categorization/audit that relies on current labels will be wrong. The linear scan is also O(folders) per message. Lower severity but a correctness gap for label/folder state.

findings.title: Corrupt/invalid token cache file aborts auth with an unhandled exception
findings.description: If the token cache file exists but contains invalid/truncated JSON (e.g. from an interrupted _save_token_cache write, disk-full, or manual edit), cache.deserialize(f.read()) at 154 raises and propagates out of _get_msal_app -> _acquire_token -> connect, aborting the whole run. There is no try/except to fall back to a fresh cache (deleting/ignoring the corrupt file and re-authenticating). Combined with the non-atomic write in _save_token_cache, this is a realistic crash path.

findings.title: Due-date formatting truncates time to midnight and forces UTC, mis-stating the due date across timezones
findings.description: star() formats due_date with strftime('%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z') (574) — it hard-codes midnight and 'Z' (UTC) regardless of the datetime's actual time or tzinfo, then ALSO sets timeZone='UTC' (577). If due_date is a timezone-aware local datetime (e.g. 2026-06-03 22:00 America/New_York), the produced value is 2026-06-03T00:00:00Z, silently shifting the To Do due date to the wrong day (off by up to a day depending on tz). Naive datetimes are likewise assumed UTC. Time component is always discarded. Date/timezone math bug.

findings.title: Pagination via @odata.nextLink loses $orderby/$select consistency only if server omits them, but more importantly limit/$top is silently dropped on every subsequent page
findings.description: On the first page params includes $top=limit, $select, $orderby. When page_token (the @odata.nextLink) is provided, params=None and the raw nextLink URL is used. Graph's nextLink normally re-encodes $top/$select/$orderby, so continuation is usually fine — BUT the caller's `limit` is completely ignored on continuation (the nextLink carries Graph's own $skiptoken and original $top, not the caller's current limit). A caller that passes a smaller limit on a resumed page (e.g. remaining budget) gets Graph's original page size instead. Combined with round 1's note (647) that per-call limit is not enforced across pages, this means limit is effectively first-page-only and cannot bound a resumed sweep. NEW emphasis: the limit parameter is structurally un-enforceable on any page driven by page_token because params is forced to None.

findings.title: ensure_category_exists ignores the requested color on the already-exists path, so tier color is silently wrong when category pre-exists
findings.description: apply_category calls ensure_category_exists(category, color) where color comes from tier_config.color (Critical=red, Important=yellow, etc.). ensure_category_exists returns the cached id immediately if the name exists (298-299) WITHOUT verifying the existing master category's color matches the requested one. If a category named 'Critical' already exists in the mailbox with a different color (e.g. blue from a prior misconfigured run), the tier's intended color (red) is never applied — the visual Eisenhower-matrix color coding documented in CLAUDE.md is silently wrong. There is no update path to reconcile color. Not in round 1.

findings.title: Unbounded recursive child-folder fetch with no depth guard or visited-set
findings.description: _init_folder_cache recurses into every child folder via _fetch_child_folders with no depth limit and no cycle/visited tracking. A deeply nested folder tree (or a Graph response anomaly) drives unbounded recursion and one HTTP round-trip per folder on every connect(), and on a large mailbox this connect-time fan-out can be very slow or hit recursion limits. Each level also only takes $top=100 children, silently dropping folders beyond 100 per parent (no nextLink follow).

findings.title: acquire_token_interactive on a headless/hosted server blocks forever (opens a local browser) instead of failing fast
findings.description: connect() -> _acquire_token() falls back to app.acquire_token_interactive(prompt='select_account') when no cached token exists. On the API server (api.service.run_triage calls prov.connect()) this is a headless context: acquire_token_interactive tries to launch a browser and bind a local redirect server, which on a server either blocks indefinitely or errors obscurely, holding the request thread. The service layer maps connect() failures to 503, but an interactive flow that hangs rather than raises is not caught by that path — it just stalls the worker.

findings.location: lines 3-7 (httpx>=0.27 with comment "required by fastapi.testclient ... and for tests")
findings.title: httpx is declared as a runtime dependency but is only used by the test suite (TestClient), bloating the production image
findings.description: requirements-api.txt lists `httpx>=0.27` with the justification "required by fastapi.testclient (Starlette TestClient) and for tests." The production code (api/, acp/, mcp_server/) makes no outbound HTTP calls via httpx — outbound calls go through `requests` (outlook/google) and `stripe`'s own client. The only httpx consumer is `fastapi.testclient.TestClient` (tests/test_api.py:3). Shipping a test-only dependency in the production requirements file installed by the Dockerfile (`COPY requirements*.txt; pip install -r ... requirements-api.txt`) bakes httpx into the image unnecessarily. It belongs in a dev/test requirements file. Not a crash, but a dependency-hygiene/attack-surface smell: more code in the prod image than the running service uses.

findings.location: header comment lines 1-6 ("Optional, ISOLATED") vs CI/Dockerfile which always install it
findings.title: `mcp` is framed as "Optional" but is mandatory for the advertised `/mcp` product surface on the deployed (3.11) image
findings.description: requirements-mcp.txt frames the `mcp` SDK as "Optional, ISOLATED from the core requirements." While the 3.9-isolation rationale is legitimate for the CLI engine, the README/api.app advertise `/mcp` as a first-class product endpoint and the Dockerfile/CI/render deploy a 3.11 image that always installs it. So on the actual production target `mcp` is NOT optional — it is a mandatory dependency of an advertised, mountable, potentially-paid surface. The 'optional' label, combined with the broad-except silent-degrade in api/app.py:41, means a deploy that drops requirements-mcp.txt (believing it optional) ships a product missing an advertised endpoint with only an INFO log. The mandatory-vs-optional classification is misleading for the real deploy target.

findings.location: lines 1-15 (whole file: no upper bounds on google-*/requests/pyyaml; floors as low as >=0.5.0)
findings.title: All core deps have unbounded version ceilings and very low floors (google-auth-oauthlib>=0.5.0), giving non-reproducible installs and major-version drift
findings.description: requirements.txt pins only loose floors with no upper bound: `google-api-python-client>=2.0.0`, `google-auth-httplib2>=0.1.0`, `google-auth-oauthlib>=0.5.0`, `requests>=2.28.0`, `pyyaml>=6.0`. The API file at least caps stripe (`<16`). With no ceiling, a future `pip install` pulls the latest major of each Google library, and google-auth-oauthlib has had breaking API changes between 0.x and 1.x. `>=0.5.0` is a 4-year-old floor. There is no lockfile (no requirements lock, no poetry.lock, no pip-tools hashes). Combined with the unpinned tooling already noted in round 1, every fresh build/deploy can resolve a different dependency set, so the deployed runtime is non-reproducible and a transitive breaking release can break Gmail auth on the next image rebuild with no code change.

findings.location: lines 13-15 (mypy/types-requests commented out) vs .github/workflows/ci.yml:31 (pip install ... mypy)
findings.title: requirements.txt comments out mypy/types-requests as dev deps, but CI installs an unpinned mypy and runs it with no stub packages — type-check is dependency-incomplete
findings.description: requirements.txt deliberately comments out `mypy>=1.0.0` and `types-requests>=2.28.0`. CI (ci.yml:31) instead does `pip install pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy` with no version and crucially WITHOUT types-requests (or any other type stubs). The codebase imports `requests` (providers/outlook.py) and `google-*`, none of which ship inline types compatible with a stub-less mypy run; combined with the round-1-noted `--ignore-missing-imports` and `set +e`, the type-check is doubly defanged AND missing its declared stub dependency. The commented-out stub line documents an intent (install types-requests) that no install path actually fulfills — a dependency-completeness gap in the dev/type-check toolchain.

findings.title: Move-during-iteration over live inbox messages skips messages (same Mail.app pattern)
findings.description: Like archive_old_inbox.applescript, this iterates `set inboxMessages to messages of inbox` (line 23) and calls `move msg to bulkMailbox` inside the loop. Moving messages out of the inbox during a forward `repeat with msg in inboxMessages` shifts indices and skips matching messages, so a single run only routes a fraction of bulk senders. Combined with the bare try (no on-error), failures are invisible.

findings.title: Resolving Newsletters mailbox is unguarded and can abort the whole script
findings.description: Lines 16-21 resolve `mailbox bulkMailboxName` ("Newsletters") outside any try. The comment on line 3 even warns 'create this mailbox in Mail first' — if the user has not, this line raises an unhandled error and the script dies. No graceful message is emitted.

findings.title: PUBLIC_BASE_URL is not validated; an unset/typo env var bakes a placeholder host into committed machine-readable artifacts
findings.description: BASE defaults to 'https://mail.example.com' when PUBLIC_BASE_URL is unset. Running the generator without the env var silently emits server.json/agent.json/llms.txt pointing at example.com (e.g. the MCP remote `{BASE}/mcp` at line 91). Since these are committed declarations consumed by agents/registries, an accidental placeholder publish is a real correctness issue with no guard/warning.

findings.title: Credit-pack price rendered via float division of cents (amount_cents / 100) — float-rounding idiom on the published price page
findings.description: The committed pricing.md credit-pack price is formatted as `${pk['amount_cents'] / 100:.2f}`, dividing integer cents by 100 in floating point then formatting to 2dp. For the current values (100, 900) this renders correctly ($1.00 / $9.00), but float division for money is the classic rounding-error pattern: a value like 9995 cents -> 99.95 is fine, but values that are not exactly representable in binary float can round the displayed price the wrong way at the 2dp boundary. Prefer integer formatting (e.g. f"{amt//100}.{amt%100:02d}"). This is the buyer-facing published price, so a rounding artifact here is a displayed-price defect.

findings.title: Org name in seed.yaml (labores-profani-crux) disagrees with actual git remote (a-organvm)
findings.description: seed.yaml declares `org: labores-profani-crux` and the same in its header comment. The actual git remote is `github.com/a-organvm/universal-mail--automation`, and server.json uses `io.github.a-organvm/...`. CLAUDE.md meanwhile says `organvm-iii-ergon`. Three different org identifiers across config surfaces; at most one matches reality (a-organvm). Stale/incorrect org metadata will misroute any tooling that consumes seed.yaml's org field.

findings.location: lines 14-17 (produces: 'Email workflow automation and scheduling' artifact; consumes: [])
findings.title: seed.yaml produces/consumes does not describe the implemented commerce product (subscriptions, ACP credit packs, MCP)
findings.description: seed.yaml declares the repo `produces` a single artifact 'Email workflow automation and scheduling' and `consumes: []`. The implemented surface is a paid SaaS + agent-commerce product: Stripe subscriptions (api/billing.py), ACP one-time credit packs (acp/router.py + product_feed.json), an MCP tool server (mcp_server), and a public pricing catalog. None of the revenue-bearing outputs (billing, agent-commerce, MCP) are represented in the seed contract that organvm governance/edge tooling uses to wire the system graph. This understates the repo's real produced surface and leaves its commerce edges invisible to the dependency model. Distinct from the round-1 org-name (labores-profani-crux) finding.

findings.title: Org identifier is inconsistent across surfaces: seed.yaml says 'labores-profani-crux', remote/server.json say 'a-organvm', CLAUDE.md says 'organvm-iii-ergon'
findings.description: seed.yaml line 8 declares org: labores-profani-crux. The actual GitHub org (git remote) is a-organvm, server.json:3 names the server 'io.github.a-organvm/universal-mail-automation', and CLAUDE.md system-context block says Org: organvm-iii-ergon. Three different org identifiers for the same repo. seed.yaml is a governance/automation contract surface, so the stale org slug can break any tooling that resolves the repo by its seed org field. At minimum it is drift between the contract and the deployed reality (Rule #6 fix-bases concern).

findings.title: MCP remote URL points to placeholder mail.example.com
findings.description: The `remotes[0].url` is `https://mail.example.com/mcp`, an example.com placeholder, not the real hosted endpoint (the actual share demo is uma.4444j99.dev and the Dockerfile/worker expose /mcp logic elsewhere). Any MCP client resolving this server.json will attempt to connect to a non-existent host. Either remove the remotes block until real or point it at the live host.

findings.title: Fixture's broad except binds _payment_mod=None and can skip payment-client reset, leaking state between tests
findings.description: The fixture does 'try: from acp import payment as _payment_mod; _payment_mod.set_payment_client(None) except Exception: _payment_mod = None'. The except covers BOTH the import AND the set_payment_client(None) call. If set_payment_client ever raised (or any later refactor makes it do so), _payment_mod is set to None and the teardown branch 'if _payment_mod is not None' is skipped — leaving an injected payment client (e.g. _OKPay from a prior ACP test) live for the next test. Since acp tests inject real-ish OK/Null clients, a leaked client could cause order-dependent passes/failures. The reset and the import should be in separate try blocks; the broad Exception also hides genuine import errors in the acp package.

findings.title: Test fake payment client ignores idempotency_key, so the per-session charge-dedup claim is never actually exercised
findings.description: _OKPay.charge ignores its idempotency_key argument and unconditionally returns ok=True. The docstrings and comments in acp/router.py (lines 258-285) lean heavily on the per-session Stripe idempotency key f'acp-charge:{session_id}' to prevent double-charge across retries with fresh endpoint Idempotency-Keys. But the test that asserts 'no double credit' (test_complete_is_idempotent_no_double_credit) relies ONLY on the session-status guard and fulfill_once — the charge-dedup behavior is mocked away. So the code path that would re-call charge() on a second /complete (if status weren't COMPLETED) and rely on Stripe to dedup is never tested, and a real regression where the status guard is bypassed but the charge key is wrong would not be caught. The double-charge protection is essentially untested at the charge layer.

findings.title: Gmail-override safety tests stub _execute_with_backoff/_drop_if_protected so the override path is partly mocked away
findings.description: These tests exercise the Gmail apply_actions override but replace ensure_label_exists, _execute_with_backoff (run inline, no retry), and in one case _drop_if_protected itself with lambdas, and feed a fake _Service. That is necessary to avoid live API calls, but it means the retry/backoff wrapper and the real label-resolution path are not under test — the test verifies the body assembled for batchModify, not that the production _execute_with_backoff actually invokes the function or that ensure_label_exists resolves correctly. A regression in _execute_with_backoff (e.g. swallowing the call, or a backoff loop that never executes fn) would not be caught here. Coverage note rather than a definite defect.

findings.title: Test asserts _independently_protected(12345) is False, locking in 'non-str silently swallowed' as correct
findings.description: The test asserts _independently_protected(12345) is False with the comment 'non-str swallowed, never raises'. This encodes as-correct the behavior that a non-string sender is treated as NOT protected by the independent audit re-derivation (because is_protected_sender raises and the except returns False). That is a fail-OPEN direction in the one component whose entire purpose is fail-closed independence: if a sender ever arrives as a non-str (bytes/int from a malformed provider payload) the audit's independent check silently declines to protect it, diverging from the gate (is_protected_sender, blank/None -> protected True per other cases). The test cements the divergence rather than flagging it. Worth scrutiny: should the independent helper also fail closed on non-str like the gate does for blank/None?

findings.title: Tests only exercise valid numeric/string inputs; no coverage for the int() crash or invalid provider/log_level
findings.description: The config tests assert happy-path behavior only: batch_size with a clean integer string (170-172), valid provider override (158-160), etc. There is no test feeding a non-numeric MAIL_AUTO_BATCH_SIZE (which would expose the unguarded int() ValueError at config.py:272-273), no test for an invalid default_provider or log_level, and no test verifying custom_rules actually gets applied (it never does). The suite gives false confidence that config loading is robust against malformed input. test_env_overrides_yaml (198-201) only proves env beats YAML for a valid value, not the documented full precedence chain.

findings.title: No test exercises the one provider that actually consumes due_date (Outlook); the sole due_date star test targets Mail.app which ignores it
findings.description: due_date only has real behavior in providers/outlook.py star() (strftime to a Graph dueDateTime). The only test touching due_date (test_mailapp_star_accepts_due_date_from_base_apply_actions) drives Mail.app, whose star() explicitly ignores due_date. So the Outlook strftime formatting — including the UTC-mislabel bug noted separately — has zero coverage. Additionally the naive `datetime(2026, 6, 1)` fixture (test_mailapp.py:19) bakes in the naive-datetime pattern that the Outlook path would mis-handle. This is a test-quality gap masking the timezone defect on the only provider where it matters.

findings.title: MCP destructive/readonly hint assertions silently skip on the Python 3.9 CI floor
findings.description: The whole module is gated by pytest.importorskip('mcp'); the file comment states mcp requires Python >=3.10 so 'the core suite still passes on a 3.9 floor'. That means the safety-relevant assertions (triage marked destructiveHint, triage_preview/check_protected_sender marked readOnlyHint, output-schemas present) run ONLY where mcp is installed. On the documented 3.9 floor (and in any CI lane without the optional dep) these never execute, so a regression that flips triage to readOnly or drops the destructive annotation would pass CI silently. importorskip turns a security-property assertion into a no-op on the supported floor — worth documenting/asserting elsewhere or running an mcp-enabled lane.

findings.title: merge tests normalize with set()/sorted(), so they cannot detect order loss or the List-vs-Set contract violation
findings.description: test_merge_combines_labels (line 101) asserts set(merged.add_labels) == {'A','B'} and test_merge_deduplicates (line 108) asserts sorted(merged.add_labels) == ['A','B','C']. Both wrap the result in set()/sorted() before comparing, which masks the exact defect in merge() (loss of insertion order from list(set(...))). These tests would still pass if merge produced a wrong order or a different sequence semantics, so they assert almost nothing about the ordered List[str] contract the field declares. They cannot fail on the real bug. Fix: assert on the concrete list value with a deterministic, order-preserving expectation.

findings.location: missing coverage for merge() message_id / sender
findings.title: No test covers merge() mismatched message_id or the sender coalescing rule
findings.description: The TestLabelAction suite (lines 74-127) tests label combine/dedup, or-flags, and target_folder preference, but never exercises (a) merge() of two actions with DIFFERENT message_ids (the unguarded 'assumed' invariant) nor (b) the sender = self.sender or other.sender coalescing (line 99) which interacts with the fail-closed protected-sender gate. Both are correctness/security-relevant behaviors of a public model method and are entirely uncovered. Fix: add tests pinning the intended behavior (ideally a guard that raises on mismatched message_id, and a test for sender coalescing semantics).

findings.title: No test for state-file write failure or concurrent/partial-write corruption
findings.description: StateManager.save() catches all exceptions from open()/json.dump and only logs (core/state.py:91-95), and writes non-atomically (open 'w' truncates before dump, so a crash mid-dump corrupts the file). The state tests cover load of an already-corrupt file (test_corrupted_file_returns_defaults) but never cover: (a) save() to an unwritable path silently degrading (no exception surfaced, no return value), or (b) a truncated/partial write leaving an unparseable file. Given state.py is the crash-recovery mechanism, the absence of any save-failure / atomicity test is a meaningful coverage gap — a regression that loses or corrupts resume state on a write error would pass the suite.
