findings.title: Partial-update test never exercises subscription_id, leaving the stripe_subscription_id no-clobber path uncovered
findings.description: set_subscription accepts subscription_id and writes stripe_subscription_id, and billing._handle_event passes subscription_id on checkout.session.completed and subscription.* events. The store test sets customer_id/plan/status/current_period_end and asserts those survive a partial update, but never sets or asserts stripe_subscription_id. So the 'partial event does not clobber subscription_id' guarantee (the same no-clobber contract the test exists to prove) is unverified for that one column. A regression that drops or overwrites stripe_subscription_id on a partial event would pass.

findings.title: Stale-processing reclaim test reaches into private _conn and sets created_at=0 — brittle and only covers the extreme stale case
findings.description: The test simulates a crash by doing s._conn.execute('UPDATE idempotency_keys SET created_at=0 ...'). created_at=0 is ~55 years in the past, far beyond IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s), so it only proves the reclaim fires for an extreme value, not at the actual 60s boundary. There is no test at created_at = now-59 (should still be 'processing'/'conflict') vs now-61 (should reclaim), so an off-by-sign or units bug in the timeout comparison (_now()-created_at > TIMEOUT) would not be caught. Reaching into the private connection also couples the test to the schema. Boundary coverage gap on the DoS-prevention window.

findings.title: No Content-Security-Policy and an overridable global API base, amplifying the innerHTML sinks
findings.description: The page ships with no CSP (no meta http-equiv, and being a static file served via Cloudflare/Firebase it likely has no CSP header either — grep finds zero CSP/script-src). Combined with multiple innerHTML insertions (lines 816,818,843,858,862,911,941) and a globally overridable request origin `window.__MAIL_AUTOMATION_API_BASE__`, any successful HTML injection (e.g. via the unescaped tier/count sinks) executes unconstrained inline script. A CSP with `default-src 'self'` plus `connect-src` allowlist would both blunt the XSS sinks and pin the API origin. This is defense-in-depth that the trust-centric product positioning specifically warrants.

findings.title: Limit input max=1000 is not enforced before sending to the unauthenticated preview endpoint
findings.description: The limit field declares max="1000" (line 594) but the handler sends `parseInt($("limit").value, 10) || 50` with no clamp. A user can type 9999999 and it is forwarded to POST /v1/triage/preview (an endpoint round 1 flagged as having NO authentication and NO metering). The HTML max attribute is advisory only for spinner UI; nothing caps the value the client transmits, so the client offers no mitigation for the unmetered preview cost. Also `parseInt` of a non-numeric string yields NaN -> `|| 50`, silently overriding a malformed entry rather than surfacing it.

findings.title: Email validation regex is trivially permissive
findings.description: Checkout gating validates the email with `/.+@.+\..+/`, which accepts values like 'x@y.z', 'a b@c .d ' (with embedded spaces around the dot it can still match), and '<svg/onload>@a.b'. The email is sent to /v1/billing/checkout and is later used for Stripe customer creation and (per the copy) audit-receipt delivery. Garbage that passes this regex is accepted and round-trips to Stripe. The address is escaped where rendered, so this is a data-quality / validation weakness rather than direct XSS.

findings.title: postJSON collapses all transport and JSON-parse errors to a single indistinguishable {ok:false,status:0} / {data:null}
findings.description: postJSON swallows fetch network errors into `{ ok:false, status:0, data:null }` (line 791) and swallows JSON parse failures into `data=null` (line 788). Callers then cannot distinguish 'server down', 'CORS blocked', 'non-JSON 200', and 'genuine 503 static mode'. The preview handler treats `!ok || !data` identically to a real 503 and shows 'Static share mode' (line 841-844), so a transient network blip or a malformed-but-200 backend response is misreported to the user as 'this share link is running in static mode' — masking real backend failures behind a benign-sounding message on the demo path.

findings.title: wranglerVersion pinned only to major "4" — non-reproducible builds, supply-chain drift
findings.description: The deploy step pins `wranglerVersion: "4"` (floating major) and `cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4` (floating major tag, not a SHA). Both float, so a new wrangler 4.x or a re-tagged action can silently change deploy behavior between identical commits. For a step that holds the CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and pushes to production, pinning to an exact version/SHA is the safer practice.

findings.title: Third-party actions pinned to mutable major tags instead of commit SHAs
findings.description: All actions are pinned to floating major tags: actions/checkout@v4, actions/setup-python@v5, codecov/codecov-action@v4, cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4. Tag pins are mutable (a tag can be force-moved to a malicious commit). Since the deploy job has access to CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN, a compromised re-tag of any action in that job could exfiltrate the token. Hardening guidance recommends SHA pinning for actions in workflows that handle secrets.

findings.title: pip cache keyed on default lockfiles may miss requirements-api/mcp changes
findings.description: setup-python `cache: 'pip'` with no explicit `cache-dependency-path` keys the cache on the default-detected requirements file. The install step (line 30) installs three requirements files (requirements.txt, requirements-api.txt, requirements-mcp.txt). If the cache key only tracks one of them, edits to requirements-api.txt or requirements-mcp.txt may not invalidate the cache, leading to stale/incorrect installed dependency sets in CI. Specify cache-dependency-path covering all three.

findings.location: lines 16-21 (agentic_commerce.spec_version "2026-04-17") and product_feed.json line 1 ("version": "2026-04-17") vs acp/payment.py STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION line 28 ("2026-04-22.preview")
findings.title: Advertised ACP spec_version (2026-04-17) trails the Stripe SPT preview version the charge path actually requires (2026-04-22.preview)
findings.description: agent.json and product_feed.json advertise `agentic_commerce.spec_version: "2026-04-17"` (matching acp/__init__.py API_VERSION, which the gate enforces exactly). But the actual completion/charge in acp/payment.py pins Stripe to `STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION = "2026-04-22.preview"` because Shared Payment Tokens require that newer preview surface. So the discovery surface declares one spec date while the money path depends on a later Stripe preview version. An agent that builds its SPT delegation strictly to the advertised 2026-04-17 spec may produce tokens/shapes the 2026-04-22.preview PaymentIntent path rejects, and the advertised version gives no signal that a newer preview is in play. At minimum the two version axes (ACP protocol date vs Stripe SPT preview date) are conflated under one number on the public surface.

findings.location: lines 22-28 (api block + oauth_scopes: []) vs api/app.py triage/preview/audit endpoints which require no auth (and acp surfaces accepting any bearer)
findings.title: agent.json declares oauth_scopes [] and no auth requirement, faithfully mirroring an API that enforces no auth — the manifest normalizes the unauthenticated surface
findings.description: agent.json lists `oauth_scopes: []` and an `api` block (triage via MCP tools, receipt_verification, pricing) with no declared authentication. This is technically consistent with the code (api/app.py /v1/triage and GET /v1/audit/{run_id} require no auth; acp/_gate accepts any non-empty bearer), but as the canonical machine-readable contract it advertises to every consuming agent that the paid triage surface is callable with zero credentials — codifying the unauthenticated-paid-surface problem rather than signaling a required key. If/when auth is added to the triage path (which round 1 flagged as critical for app.py), this manifest will silently keep telling agents no auth is needed. Flagging the data-surface angle distinct from the code-side auth findings.

findings.title: DEPLOY.md Fly.io instructions are generic and unverifiable; no fly.toml ships and secrets list is Gmail-only
findings.description: The Fly.io section gives `fly launch --dockerfile Dockerfile --internal-port 8000` and `fly secrets set GMAIL_OAUTH_OP_REF=... GMAIL_TOKEN_OP_REF=...`. No fly.toml is committed (consistent with 'generates fly.toml'), but the secrets example only sets the two Gmail OP refs and omits the Stripe/RECEIPT/MCP secrets that DEPLOY.md's own Commerce table (lines 56-65) says the same single app needs to enable billing/ACP/MCP. A user following the Fly section verbatim deploys an instance where every money/agent surface is in the 503 'not configured' state with no pointer back to the secrets table. Minor, but the deploy instructions across platforms are inconsistent about which secrets to set.

findings.location: line 19 (CMD uvicorn ...) relies on uvicorn CLI from requirements-api.txt
findings.title: Container start command `uvicorn api.app:app` has no dependency-presence guard; a requirements-api.txt install failure yields a non-obvious 'uvicorn: not found' rather than a build failure
findings.description: The Dockerfile CMD is `uvicorn api.app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT:-8000}`. The uvicorn binary comes only from `uvicorn[standard]` in requirements-api.txt. If the RUN pip layer (lines 9-10) partially succeeds — e.g. requirements-api.txt resolution fails for the API extras but the layer is cached, or a future edit reorders installs — the image still builds (COPY . . succeeds) and only fails at container start with `uvicorn: command not found`, which on Render manifests as a crash-looping service rather than a failed build. There is no `python -c "import uvicorn, fastapi, stripe, pydantic"` smoke import in the build to fail fast if a declared runtime dep is missing. Given the multi-file requirements split and the 'optional' framing, a build that omits requirements-api.txt would still produce a runnable-looking image that cannot start.

findings.title: Currency case mismatch between feed ('USD') and checkout/charge ('usd')
findings.description: The product feed advertises `'currency': 'USD'` (uppercase, feed.py:50) while the checkout session and the Stripe PaymentIntent use lowercase `usd` (router.py:39 CURRENCY, passed to build_session and payment.charge). Stripe requires lowercase ISO currency codes for charges; the session currency is correct, but the inconsistency between the discovery surface (USD) and the transactional surface (usd) can trip strict ACP feed/session validators that compare the two. Normalize to one canonical case.

findings.location: build_feed line 48 (price) and line 47 (image_url) vs ACP product-feed spec
findings.title: Product feed emits integer-cents price and a non-image image_url, likely failing strict ACP feed validation
findings.description: build_feed sets 'price': pack['amount_cents'] (an int like 900) and 'currency': 'USD'. ACP/commerce product feeds conventionally express price as a decimal string in major units (e.g. '9.00') or as a {amount,currency} object; an integer 900 with currency USD would be interpreted as $900.00 by a strict consumer, a 100x price error on the discovery surface. Separately image_url points at f"{base}/app/" (an HTML page, also flagged in round 1 for the url field) which is not an image. The charge path itself uses amount_cents correctly via grand_total, so this affects only the agent-facing discovery feed, but a 100x advertised-price discrepancy is a real correctness risk for any agent that trusts the feed.

findings.location: feed() line 67-69 (JSONResponse, no Cache-Control) + build_feed reflects base_url
findings.title: Product feed reflects Host and is served with no Cache-Control, enabling cached poisoning of checkout_url/seller_url
findings.description: build_feed embeds the request-derived base into checkout_url (line 62), seller_url (line 55), and product url/image_url (lines 45-47). The /acp/feed.json handler returns a plain JSONResponse with no Cache-Control: no-store (lines 67-69). As with the manifest, a Host-spoofed request can poison a path-keyed cache so other ACP agents read a feed whose checkout_url points at an attacker host. Distinct from the round-1 'Host reflected' finding by naming the missing cache-control that escalates it from per-request to persistent.

findings.title: An update/create with an item whose id is valid but quantity drives subtotal has no per-line or grand-total overflow/sanity cap beyond Item.quantity<=1000; many distinct valid items multiply unboundedly
findings.description: Item.quantity is capped at 1000 (models.py line 27), but CheckoutCreate.items is an unbounded list (default_factory=list, no max_items). A request with thousands of pack line items, each quantity 1000, produces a huge grand_total and total_runs with no ceiling. While integer math won't overflow in Python, the resulting charge amount (cents) and credited runs are unbounded, and a single accepted request could attempt a very large Stripe charge or credit a massive run balance. Add a max_items on the list and a sane grand_total ceiling.

findings.title: build_line_items indexes it['id'] with [] though Item guarantees id; but build_line_items is also called with raw model_dump dicts — a missing key would KeyError 500 instead of a clean invalid_items
findings.description: pack = packs.get(it['id']) uses subscript access. Items reach here via [i.model_dump() for i in body.items], and Item requires id, so id is present in practice. However build_line_items is a public helper typed to accept List[dict]; any caller passing a dict without 'id' (or a future code path) raises KeyError -> unhandled 500 rather than the intended valid=False / not_ready_for_payment. it.get('quantity',1) is used defensively two lines down, so the inconsistency (subscript for id, get for quantity) suggests the id access was meant to be defensive too.

findings.title: build_line_items indexes it['id'] and trusts pack['amount_cents']/['runs'] with bracket access; KeyError/TypeError on malformed input
findings.description: build_line_items does `packs.get(it['id'])` (line 81) and `int(it.get('quantity', 1))`, `int(pack['amount_cents'])`, `int(pack['runs'])` (lines 85-96). From the router the items are sanitized pydantic dumps, so it['id'] always exists; but this is a public helper typed `List[dict]`. A dict missing 'id' raises KeyError (not the intended valid=False path), and a pack dict missing 'amount_cents'/'runs' raises KeyError/TypeError. If the CREDIT_PACKS catalog is ever edited to omit a field, the failure is an uncaught 500 on the money path rather than a clean invalid-items rejection. Use it.get('id') and validate pack fields defensively.

findings.title: build_line_items reads it['id'] via dict access and it.get('quantity') without validating against the Pydantic-bounded model in the update path consistently
findings.description: build_line_items takes raw dicts (items = [i.model_dump() for i in body.items]) so id/quantity are already validated by Item (id<=128, quantity 1..1000) on the create/update routes — fine. But the function also tolerates it.get('quantity', 1) and int() coercion, and packs.get(it['id']) will KeyError if a caller ever passes a dict lacking 'id' (the model guarantees it today, but the function is a public-looking helper with a dict signature). Low risk given current callers, but the function silently trusts that every dict has 'id'; an item dict from any future caller without 'id' raises KeyError -> 500 rather than a clean invalid_items message. Defensive .get('id') would keep the not_ready_for_payment contract intact.

findings.title: Item quantity read via it.get('quantity',1) but Pydantic Item already bounds qty to 1..1000; raw dict path in router bypasses bound on update with current line_items reuse
findings.description: build_line_items takes List[dict] (items already model_dumped) so quantity is validated by Item (ge=1,le=1000). However update_session, when body.items is None, reuses current['line_items'] and row['data']['total_runs'] without re-deriving — and complete charges grand_total(current['line_items']). If a stored session's line_items were ever persisted with a tampered/larger subtotal (e.g. via a future code path or direct store write), complete charges that amount with no re-validation against the pack catalog. Round1 noted update doesn't re-validate tampered items; this highlights that grand_total trusts persisted subtotals verbatim at charge time.

findings.location: build_line_items line 85 (int(it.get('quantity',1))) on create path with raw model_dump dicts
findings.title: build_line_items re-derives qty via int(it.get('quantity',1)) ignoring the pydantic-validated value, so any non-Item caller bypasses the 1..1000 bound
findings.description: On the create/update path, items are i.model_dump() of validated Item models, so quantity is already an int in 1..1000. But build_line_items re-reads it.get('quantity',1) and int()-casts it (line 85) rather than trusting the validated bound. The function is public and also intended to accept raw dicts (round-1 noted the it['id'] KeyError risk). Any caller passing a raw dict with quantity outside 1..1000 (or a float/str) bypasses the Item bound entirely: a negative quantity yields negative subtotal/total and a negative grand_total/charge; a huge quantity blows past the 1000 cap. The validation lives only on the Pydantic model, not in the money-computing function itself, so the bound is not enforced where the multiplication happens.

findings.title: All charge exceptions are collapsed to a generic 'charge failed' that the session reports as retriable, masking permanent (non-retriable) declines
findings.description: The except block (lines 89-91) catches every Exception (decline, network, auth, invalid-token, API-version errors) and returns ChargeResult(ok=False, error='charge failed'). The router then puts the session back to ready_for_payment and returns 402 (router.py:267-280), inviting the agent to retry. For permanent failures (card_declined, expired/consumed SPT, invalid API version) retrying will never succeed, so the agent loops. The original exception is logged but the distinction between retriable and terminal is lost. Map Stripe error categories so terminal declines surface a non-retriable code/state.

findings.title: No guard that amount > 0 before calling Stripe; a zero/empty line-item session reaching charge would error from Stripe rather than be caught early
findings.description: complete_session computes `amount = models.grand_total(current['line_items'])` (router.py:257). For a session with empty line_items grand_total is 0. Such a session should be not_ready and blocked by the status check at router.py:252, so in normal flow amount is positive. But there is no defensive amount>0 assertion before payment.charge, and StripeSPTPaymentClient passes int(amount) straight to PaymentIntent.create (payment.py:72-74). If any path ever produces a ready session with amount 0 (e.g. a future pack priced at 0, or a state edit), Stripe rejects amount=0 and the generic 'charge failed' fires, looping the agent. Add an explicit amount>0 precondition on the money path.

findings.title: Charge currency is passed verbatim from CURRENCY='usd' while the feed/receipt advertise 'USD' — no normalization, and amount is trusted to already be minor units with no unit assertion
findings.description: charge() forwards `"amount": int(amount)` and `"currency": currency` directly to Stripe's PaymentIntent. Stripe requires amount in the smallest currency unit (cents) and a lowercase currency code. The router passes CURRENCY='usd' (lowercase, correct) and grand_total (cents, correct) — but there is no assertion or validation at this boundary that `amount` is in minor units or that `currency` is a Stripe-valid lowercase code. Because the rest of the system mixes 'USD' (feed.py:50, product_feed.json, receipt summary currency=CURRENCY='usd' at router 306 but feed says 'USD'), any future caller that passes the 'USD'-cased value or a dollars-denominated amount to this seam would silently create a wrong charge. The money seam trusts its inputs entirely with no unit/currency guard.

findings.title: If STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is empty-string (set but blank), NullPaymentClient is selected and every ACP completion silently fails closed with no operator signal
findings.description: get_payment_client does `StripeSPTPaymentClient(key) if key else NullPaymentClient()`. An env var that is present but empty ('') is falsy, so a misconfigured deploy (key var exported but blank) silently falls to NullPaymentClient, which returns ok=False 'payment is not configured' for EVERY /complete. The router surfaces this as a per-request 402, so an operator sees individual payment failures rather than a startup/config error — the whole ACP revenue path is dark with no aggregate signal, and because the client is cached at module level (round 1 noted rotation), the blank key is locked in for the process lifetime. The NEW angle vs round 1's rotation/caching note is the empty-string-is-falsy fail-closed-without-signal behavior.

findings.title: Charge passes currency unchanged but ACP currency constant is lowercase 'usd' while feed advertises 'USD' — no normalization before Stripe
findings.description: router.CURRENCY='usd' is passed straight into PaymentIntent.create params['currency']. Stripe accepts lowercase, so the charge works, but combined with the feed's 'USD' (feed.py:49) there is no single normalized currency source; a future feed/charge mismatch (e.g. someone aligning the feed to lowercase or the charge to a feed-derived value) has no guard. Also charge() does no amount>0 / amount-is-int validation beyond int(amount); a zero grand_total (empty/invalid line_items reaching complete) is sent to Stripe and errors there rather than being rejected locally. Round 1 noted the zero-amount gap on the router side; this adds the payment-client side has no defensive floor either.

findings.location: StripeSPTPaymentClient.charge line 74 (int(amount)) — no upper bound / no >0 guard before Stripe call
findings.title: Charge passes int(amount) to Stripe with no minimum or maximum bound, so a desynced/huge amount is sent verbatim
findings.description: charge() coerces int(amount) and forwards it to payment_intents.create (lines 74-83) with no validation that amount is within a sane range (e.g. > 0 and below a per-charge ceiling). Combined with the 1,000,000-run / $9,000 single-session ceiling and the absent reconciliation at /complete, a desynced or maliciously-inflated line_items total flows straight to a real Stripe charge. The payment seam is the last place a money sanity-bound could be enforced and it enforces none.

findings.title: ACP sessions are never expired; STATUS_EXPIRED is defined and checked but no code ever transitions a session to expired, so a READY session with a stale price can be completed indefinitely
findings.description: models.STATUS_EXPIRED exists and update_session refuses to mutate an EXPIRED session, but nothing in the codebase ever sets a session to expired (no TTL sweep, no created_at age check at complete). A session created at one price can be completed weeks later at the persisted amount even if CREDIT_PACKS pricing changed in the interim, because complete charges grand_total(stored line_items) rather than re-deriving from the live catalog. Sessions should expire (spec has expired status for a reason) and/or re-price at completion.

findings.title: On update without items, total_runs is read from row['data']['total_runs'] but line_items kept from current['line_items']; if persisted shapes ever diverge the charge amount and credited runs desynchronize
findings.description: When body.items is None, update keeps `line_items = current['line_items']` (from the response object) and `total_runs = row['data']['total_runs']` (from the stored data wrapper). These come from two different persisted structures (response.line_items vs data.total_runs). They are written together today by _persist, so they agree; but the charge amount is later recomputed from line_items (grand_total) while credited runs come from total_runs, so any future code path that updates one without the other (e.g. a migration, a manual data edit, or a partial save) silently desynchronizes money charged from runs credited. Deriving total_runs from the line items at completion (single source) would remove the coupling risk.

findings.title: Auto-created account on /complete is plan='free' with no email/customer link; credited runs attach to an orphan account unreachable via billing
findings.description: If no account matches the bearer, complete_session creates one with only api_key and plan='free' (router.py:289). This account has no email, no stripe_customer_id, no subscription. The purchased run-credits are deposited there, but the buyer has no way to recover/associate this account through the billing/portal flows (which key off account_id/customer_id/email). If the agent loses the bearer, the paid credits are stranded on an unrecoverable orphan account. Tie ACP fulfillment to an identifiable account or require the account to pre-exist.

findings.title: Idempotency replay can return a stored response of JSON null, and gate parses body before idempotency claim allowing partial side effects
findings.description: idempotency_begin returns {'state':'replay','response': json.loads(existing['response_json'] or 'null')}. If a prior request claimed the key ('processing' -> 'done') but idempotency_complete stored response=None or was never reached but status flipped, the replay returns Python None, and the endpoints do 'if replay is not None: return JSONResponse(replay, ...)'. A stored literal JSON null deserializes to None, so the replay branch is SKIPPED and the handler RE-RUNS — re-creating a session / re-attempting work under a key already marked done, breaking idempotency. Separately, FastAPI validates body (models.CheckoutCreate) BEFORE _gate/_begin_idempotency run for the body-typed endpoints, so a malformed body 422s before the idempotency key is claimed — generally fine, but means the replay/claim ordering depends on validation success.

findings.title: Idempotent already-completed return does not re-shape order/permalink and can return a session without the order on a fresh Idempotency-Key
findings.description: If current['status']==STATUS_COMPLETED, the handler returns current verbatim (router.py:248-251). current['order'] is only present if a previous /complete actually minted it (the fulfilled branch). In the path where the session was completed but the order was suppressed (the elif order is None 'already_fulfilled' branch persisted a response with no order key — router.py:323-336 stores resp via _persist), a later retry returns that order-less completed session as the canonical result, so an agent that completed-with-fulfillment-elsewhere can never retrieve an order/permalink for its purchase. The order receipt exists in the ledger but the session response the agent polls has no pointer to it. Observability/consistency gap on the receipt 0->1 retrieval path.

findings.title: Bearer parsing does not strip the scheme case-insensitively and ignores leading whitespace nuances; malformed-but-intended tokens slip through as opaque keys
findings.description: auth.startswith('Bearer ') is case-sensitive and assumes exactly one space. A header 'bearer testkey' or 'Bearer  testkey' (two spaces) would be rejected (401) or yield ' testkey' with leading space respectively. More importantly, because the parsed value is used verbatim as the account key, 'Bearer testkey' and 'Bearer  testkey' (extra space, .strip() removes it) resolve to the same account while 'bearer testkey' creates none — inconsistent identity derivation from a header. This is minor given the larger auth-absence issue, but it means the api_key space is the raw, unvalidated header tail, which is brittle and an additional reason a token store/lookup (not string-passthrough) is the right model.

findings.title: On payment failure the buyer field can be silently dropped/overwritten and the session is re-persisted as READY without re-validating items
findings.description: On a failed charge the code rebuilds the session via _shape with buyer=(body.buyer or current.get('buyer')) and status=READY, then _persist with the original total_runs. This is mostly fine, but note: the messages list overwrites any prior messages, and the line_items are taken from current['line_items'] without re-checking that the catalog still resolves them (a pack could be removed between create and complete). Low severity since packs are static, but the completion path trusts the persisted line_items/total_runs rather than re-deriving against plans.CREDIT_PACKS, so a stale or tampered persisted session (the store row is the only integrity boundary) would charge grand_total(current['line_items']) and credit row['data']['total_runs'] without revalidation.

findings.title: amount=0 (empty/zero-priced READY session) is charged and the response treated as fulfillable
findings.description: amount = grand_total(current['line_items']) can be 0 if line_items is empty (e.g. a session that became READY through an update path, or build_line_items returning valid=True with a zero-cost pack). The charge is still attempted with amount=0. StripeSPTPaymentClient.charge passes int(0) to PaymentIntent.create which Stripe rejects (amount must be >= 50 for USD), so charge returns ok=False -> 402, OK-ish. BUT the NullPaymentClient path is unaffected, and more importantly there is no early guard, so a zero-amount session interacts with the per-session charge idempotency key: the first zero-amount attempt 'claims' acp-charge:S as a failed/errored intent, and a later legitimately-priced retry (after update) reuses that poisoned key. Round 1 noted 'no guard against amount<=0'; this adds the idempotency-key-poisoning consequence specific to amount=0 reaching Stripe.

findings.title: update_session with items=None keeps line_items but re-reads total_runs from stored row, while complete recomputes amount from line_items — divergence risk if persisted shapes drift
findings.description: On update without items, line_items are taken from current['line_items'] and total_runs from row['data']['total_runs'] (two independently persisted values). complete_session charges grand_total(current['line_items']) but credits row['data']['total_runs']. These are only kept in sync at create/update-with-items time; any code path that mutates one without the other (or a partial save_session) causes the charged amount and credited runs to desynchronize — the buyer is charged for one quantity and credited for another. Round 1 noted the update re-pricing concern generally (lines 71, 78); the NEW specificity is the structural split between the charge basis (line_items) and the credit basis (separately stored total_runs) with no invariant binding them.

findings.title: Saved ACP session 'response' embeds links built from a per-request base_url, so a replay/get returns URLs from whichever Host first created the session
findings.description: create/update persist the full shaped response (including _links terms/privacy and any order permalink_url) keyed off the creating request's base_url. A later GET returns the stored response verbatim. Combined with the well_known Host-trust issue, a session created via a forged Host stores attacker-controlled terms_of_use/privacy_policy/permalink URLs that are then served to other agents on subsequent reads. Cross-references the Host-header trust weakness.

findings.location: complete_session lines 296-317 (order_receipt_body summary) vs api/receipts.py get_receipt lines 125-131 + store.save_receipt line 287 (json.dumps round-trip)
findings.title: ACP order-receipt summary contains an int amount/runs that round-trips through JSON; re-served signed_body may not re-verify if any value type coerces
findings.description: The ACP order receipt is signed over order_receipt_body (router lines 297-312) whose summary embeds amount, runs_credited, payment_id, checkout_session_id. save_receipt stores summary via json.dumps (store.py line 287) and get_receipt reloads via json.loads (store.py line 296), then receipts.get_receipt reconstructs signed_body from those columns (receipts.py lines 125-131) and the caller re-verifies with sign(). The signed body's top-level 'dry_run' is reconstructed from the SQLite integer column (store.py line 297 bool(row['dry_run'])) while the originally-signed body had a Python bool False (router line 300) — these match, but the round-trip also drops/reorders nothing only because _canonical sort_keys. The fragility is that the ACP receipt's summary is a richer dict than the triage summary signed_body assumes; any non-JSON-stable value (e.g. a future Decimal amount, or payment_id None vs absent) would break re-verification of a genuine, untampered ACP order receipt. This is an ACP-specific instance distinct from the round-1 generic triage-summary round-trip note.

findings.location: complete_session lines 273-280 (payment-failed re-shape uses current['line_items'] but does NOT re-persist total_runs from a re-derivation) + 278 _persist(total_runs)
findings.title: Payment-failed branch re-persists the session preserving the OLD total_runs/line_items without re-validating them against CREDIT_PACKS before the next retry
findings.description: On charge failure the session is re-persisted as READY carrying current['line_items'] and the unchanged total_runs (lines 273-278). No re-derivation against plans.CREDIT_PACKS occurs, so if the stored line_items/total_runs were already desynced (residual-gap scope) or stale (pack price changed since create), the next /complete retry charges grand_total(old line_items) and credits the old total_runs again — the failure path is a no-op on the invariant and perpetuates whatever desync existed. The retry should re-resolve items against the current catalog, not trust the persisted session.

findings.title: current_period_end parsed with int() but no validation it is a sane epoch; partial events can leave period_end stale
findings.description: _period_end returns int(val) from the top-level field or the item-level fallback. There is no sanity check; a malformed but truthy value other than int/numeric would raise inside int() (caught only for the item-level branch via the except, but the top-level 'if val: return int(val)' is NOT wrapped — a non-numeric current_period_end string raises ValueError that escapes _period_end and bubbles into _handle_event, which converts it to a 500 'webhook handler failed'). Minor robustness gap on the money path.

findings.title: Broad except over stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError attribute access can itself raise AttributeError on some stripe SDK versions
findings.description: The except clause references stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError. In some Stripe Python SDK major versions the error module layout changed (stripe.error vs stripe.SignatureVerificationError at top level). If the installed SDK does not expose stripe.error, evaluating the except tuple raises AttributeError, which is NOT caught here and would surface as a 500 instead of the intended fail-closed 400. Worth pinning the SDK or importing the specific exception defensively. (Verify against the pinned stripe version in requirements.)

findings.title: status from Stripe passed straight into set_subscription; an unexpected/None status can leave a paid plan with no status update while plan stays paid
findings.description: status = 'canceled' if deleted else obj.get('status'). For created/updated, obj.get('status') may be a status not in the cancel set (e.g. 'incomplete', 'paused') — plan_id stays the price-derived paid plan (not dropped to free) and status is written as e.g. 'incomplete'. entitlements_for only honors 'active'/'trialing', so this is mostly safe for access. BUT if obj.get('status') is None (missing in a malformed/partial event), set_subscription skips the status column (only non-None written) while still writing the PAID plan — leaving an account on a paid plan with a stale 'active' status. Combined with plan_id_for_price returning the paid plan, a partial event could upgrade plan without a corresponding active-status proof. Lower severity because entitlements re-checks status, but the stored state can be inconsistent.

findings.title: invoice.paid sets status=active without re-checking subscription validity; period_end parsing swallows malformed values to None
findings.description: invoice.paid looks up the account by customer and unconditionally set_subscription(status='active'). If a prior invoice.payment_failed had set past_due and a later spurious/duplicate invoice.paid arrives (or a partial-payment invoice marked paid), the account flips back to active without consulting the actual subscription status — Stripe's subscription.updated is the authoritative source, but invoice.paid here can override it out of order (webhooks are not ordered). _period_end swallows KeyError/IndexError/TypeError/ValueError to None — a malformed current_period_end silently yields no period end (acceptable) but combined with set_subscription only writing non-None, a None period_end never clears a stale far-future period_end from a previous event.

findings.title: Subscription status written verbatim from Stripe without normalization; non-active statuses other than the canceled set leave paid plan in place
findings.description: For customer.subscription.created/updated/deleted, status is taken as obj.get('status') (verbatim), and plan is only forced to 'free' when status in ('canceled','unpaid','incomplete_expired') (billing.py:248-254). Statuses like 'past_due', 'incomplete', or 'paused' are written through with the resolved paid plan_id still set. entitlements_for then treats anything not in ('active','trialing') as Free floor for limits, so limits are safe — BUT plan stays 'pro'/'business' in the row while status='past_due', which is inconsistent state and risks any future code that keys off plan alone (e.g. provider gating, feature flags) granting paid features to a non-paying account. The two-place encoding of 'what counts as entitled' (here vs entitlements_for) is fragile; a paused/past_due subscription keeps the paid plan label. Lower severity because the live entitlement resolver re-checks status, but it is a latent money-path inconsistency.

findings.title: Checkout can create an orphan free account that is never linked to the resulting Stripe customer
findings.description: When no account_id is supplied, create_checkout calls store.create_account(email=..., plan='free') and uses its id as client_reference_id/metadata (billing.py:119-133). The grant later relies on the webhook's checkout.session.completed carrying client_reference_id back to set the subscription on that same account. If the webhook is missed/misconfigured (the only access-grant source of truth per the docstring), the customer paid via Checkout but the account is never upgraded and the new free account is orphaned with no customer link. There is also no idempotency on checkout creation, so repeated POST /v1/billing/checkout with no account_id spawns multiple orphan free accounts per attempt. Minor abuse/cleanup concern; the real grant still depends entirely on webhook delivery.

findings.title: Unhandled DB exceptions from the event handler 500 and wedge the event into a permanent retry loop
findings.description: The webhook only marks an event processed AFTER _handle_event returns cleanly (lines 194-203). Any exception inside _handle_event — IntegrityError on the UNIQUE customer constraint, InterfaceError from a non-string customer, or a transient DB lock — produces a 500 and leaves the event unmarked. While this is correct for genuinely transient failures (the test at lines 88-130 relies on it), for a DETERMINISTIC failure (a payload that always violates the same constraint) it produces an event Stripe redelivers forever, each attempt 500ing identically. There is no poison-event / dead-letter handling or distinction between transient and permanent handler failures, so one malformed-but-signed event becomes unbounded retry/error-log noise.

findings.title: Orphan account created by webhook may be left without its customer link if set_subscription then fails, leaking unreferenced rows
findings.description: _resolve_account creates an account (line 218) and returns its id; the subsequent set_subscription call (231 or 255) is what actually attaches stripe_customer_id/subscription_id. These are two separate committed transactions, not one atomic unit. If set_subscription raises (e.g. UNIQUE collision or non-string customer), the create_account commit has already persisted a fresh account row (with a live api_key) that is never linked to any customer and is unreachable by get_account_by_customer — an accumulating orphan on every failed-and-retried event. Even on success the create+update being non-atomic means a crash between them strands the account.

findings.title: invoice.paid sets status='active' without touching plan, producing plan='free'+status='active' on accounts created by the orphan path
findings.description: invoice.paid resolves the account ONLY via get_account_by_customer (no metadata fallback) and, if found, calls set_subscription(status='active') with no plan. If that account previously reached the Free floor through a cancel (plan='free') but a late/duplicate invoice.paid arrives, the account becomes plan='free', status='active'. This is benign under current entitlements_for logic (free plan = free limits regardless of active), but it is an internally inconsistent state that defeats the cancellation and would mislead any direct plan/status reader or support tooling. Note invoice.paid also never records current_period_end, so the period window can go stale.

findings.title: Webhook uses bracket indexing on the verified event without guarding malformed-but-signed payloads
findings.description: After construct_event succeeds, the code does event['id'], event['type'], event['data']['object'] with direct subscription. construct_event validates the SIGNATURE, not the schema, so a signed payload missing 'id', 'type', or 'data'/'object' raises KeyError. event['id'] (line 187) and event['type'] (188) are read BEFORE the try/except that wraps _handle_event (195-202), so a KeyError there is uncaught and becomes a 500 rather than the intended 400 'invalid payload'. Anyone who can sign a payload with the webhook secret (the same trust boundary) could trigger an unhandled 500 instead of a clean rejection. Low blast radius since it requires the secret, but it is an unguarded shape assumption on the security-critical path.

findings.title: checkout.session.completed hard-codes status='active' regardless of actual subscription/payment state
findings.description: The checkout.session.completed branch always calls set_subscription(status='active', plan=plan_id) (line 236). A completed Checkout Session does not guarantee the subscription is active — for delayed payment methods or an incomplete subscription the session can complete while the subscription is 'incomplete'/'past_due'. Granting 'active' on session completion can briefly grant entitlements before the real subscription status arrives via a later customer.subscription.* event. Because plan_id here comes from metadata.plan (trusted from checkout creation) it is at least bounded to a real plan, but the active status is assumed rather than read from the subscription.

findings.location: lines 294-303 (_period_end) feeding store.current_period_end (INTEGER) at billing.py:261, store.py:49/199-211
findings.title: current_period_end stored as a bare Stripe epoch int with no semantic check; any consumer comparing it must use UTC fromtimestamp, but no such consumer/normalization exists
findings.description: _period_end returns `int(current_period_end)` (a Unix epoch in UTC by Stripe convention) and stores it as a raw INTEGER. There is no normalization to a timezone-aware datetime and no validation it is plausibly in seconds (Stripe uses seconds, but a mis-versioned/test value in ms would be silently accepted and would read as a year ~52000). Round 1 noted entitlement never enforces this value; the additional datetime-class risk is that the FIRST consumer to compare it against 'now' must use datetime.fromtimestamp(v, tz=timezone.utc) / time.time() consistently — there is no shared helper, so a naive `datetime.fromtimestamp(v)` (local) comparison would be off by the server's UTC offset at the period boundary. Flagging the missing epoch->aware normalization seam.

findings.title: construct_event ValueError on malformed JSON and SignatureVerificationError both map to 400, but a missing stripe-signature header yields empty string -> still a verification error, never a distinct 'no signature' path
findings.description: request.headers.get('stripe-signature', '') defaults to '' when the header is absent. construct_event('', secret) raises SignatureVerificationError, caught and mapped to 400 'invalid signature' (185). Functionally fail-closed (good), but it conflates 'no signature header at all' (likely a misrouted/non-Stripe caller or a proxy stripping headers) with 'forged signature' (an attack). Operationally there is no way to distinguish a deploy/proxy misconfiguration silently dropping the signature header (every legit webhook 400s, billing goes dark) from active forgery. Combined with not echoing the reason, a header-stripping proxy produces a total, silent webhook outage with only generic 400s. Round 1 flagged SDK-version attribute risk on stripe.error; this is the operational no-signature-vs-forged conflation.

findings.title: Reverse price->plan mapping is O(n) over plans reading env each iteration; if two plans share/misconfigure the same price env value, first match wins silently
findings.description: plan_id_for_price iterates PLANS and returns the first plan whose stripe_price_id matches. stripe_price_id reads os.environ each call. If STRIPE_PRICE_PRO and STRIPE_PRICE_BUSINESS are accidentally set to the same Stripe price id (copy-paste deploy error), a Business purchase maps to whichever plan appears first in dict order ('pro'), silently granting the wrong (cheaper) tier to a customer who paid for Business. No uniqueness check guards this.

findings.location: list_plans/public_dict: price_cents (plans.py:56) and credit_packs amount_cents (billing.py:100) returned without a unit field; metered unit_amount_cents (plans.py:134)
findings.title: Public billing catalog returns three differently-named integer-cent fields (price_cents, unit_amount_cents, amount_cents) with no per-amount unit declaration
findings.description: GET /v1/billing/plans returns subscription plans with `price_cents`, the metered addon with `unit_amount_cents`, and credit packs with `amount_cents` — three different field names for the same unit (integer USD cents), with only a top-level `"currency": "usd"`. A client integrating against this catalog must know out-of-band that each of these three keys is cents (not dollars) and that they are all the same currency. There is no `"unit": "minor"` or analogous marker. A consumer that renders any of these as dollars produces a 100x error; the heterogeneous field naming makes that mistake more likely (a reader may assume `amount_cents` is cents but `price_cents`/`unit_amount_cents` map differently). This is a money-unit clarity defect on the public pricing API.

findings.title: GET /v1/audit/{run_id} is unauthenticated and run_ids/order_ids enumerate the receipt ledger
findings.description: The audit receipt endpoint has no auth. While the signed body is documented as PII-free (counts only), it does expose provider name, dry_run flag, exact disposition counts, created_at timestamp, and the HMAC signature for any known run_id/order_id. ACP order receipts (acp/router.py line 296, order_ + token_hex(12)) and triage run_ids (run_ + token_hex(12)) are 96 bits of entropy so enumeration is impractical, but order permalink_url is returned to the agent and the endpoint leaks business-volume metadata (number of runs, counts) to anyone holding a permalink with no expiry or access control. Low-to-medium given the entropy; flag for an explicit access decision on a payment service.

findings.title: Receipt ledger write failure is swallowed to a warning, so a Pro/Business customer paying for 'retained receipt history' can silently lose receipts
findings.description: persist() catches all exceptions from save_receipt and logs a warning, returning the signature anyway. The justification (safety invariant already asserted) is valid for the SAFETY boundary, but retained_receipt_days is a PAID feature (90/365 days on Pro/Business). A persistent DB write failure means a paying customer's receipts are never stored and GET /v1/audit/{run_id} returns 404, silently degrading a billed feature with no surfaced error or metric. At minimum this should increment an error metric / alert, not just warn-and-continue.

findings.title: Ephemeral HMAC signing key fallback silently breaks receipt verification across restarts (integrity fallback)
findings.description: When RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is unset, _signing_key() generates a random per-process key via secrets.token_bytes(32) and only logs a warning. This is a reasonable dev convenience, but in a misconfigured production deploy (env var forgotten) every restart silently rotates the signing key, so previously issued 'tamper-evident' receipts can no longer be verified and the headline trust artifact becomes unverifiable without any hard failure. It is not a secret leak per se, but it is an inappropriate silent security fallback on the integrity/credential path. Consider failing closed (or at least a CRITICAL/once-per-process error) when running under a production flag.

findings.title: verify() treats a missing/empty signature as a normal non-match rather than distinguishing 'no signature present'
findings.description: `verify(body, signature)` does `hmac.compare_digest(expected, signature or '')`, so a None or empty stored signature simply returns False — indistinguishable from a tampered signature. Given receipts can be persisted with `signature=signature` where the ledger row could carry NULL/empty (the column is nullable: `signature TEXT` with no NOT NULL in store.py schema line 69), a receipt with a missing signature verifies as 'False/tampered' rather than surfacing 'this receipt was never signed.' Combined with the GET endpoint never recomputing, a client cannot tell an unsigned receipt from a tampered one. Minor, but it muddies the trust semantics of the headline feature.

findings.location: get_receipt, lines 122-141 (with core/audit.py summary 241-251 and api/store.py save_receipt 280-290 / get_receipt 292-298)
findings.title: Stored summary round-trips through JSON (SQLite) so signed body bytes may differ from re-served body, breaking verification even without tampering
findings.description: The signature is computed by `sign(signed_body(...))` over the in-memory `triage_result['audit']` (audit.summary(), which includes a `violations` list). It is persisted via `save_receipt` as `json.dumps(summary)` into `summary_json`, then `get_receipt` does `json.loads`. The GET endpoint rebuilds `body['summary'] = rec['summary']` (the round-tripped object) and pairs it with the stored signature. For the current summary shape (str keys, ints, list of str ids) the round-trip is faithful, so verification holds today. BUT this couples receipt verifiability to the JSON round-trip being byte-faithful: any future summary field that is a tuple, a set, an int-keyed dict, a float with non-canonical repr, or NaN/Infinity would round-trip to a different structure and the stored signature would no longer verify against the re-served body even with zero tampering. There is no test or guard pinning that summary must be JSON-round-trip-stable, and `verify` is never invoked server-side to catch the divergence. Latent correctness/maintainability hazard for the trust artifact.

findings.title: Ephemeral key lazy-init is not thread-safe (read-check-set race on _EPHEMERAL_KEY under FastAPI threadpool)
findings.description: `_EPHEMERAL_KEY` is a module global set via the classic non-atomic `if _EPHEMERAL_KEY is None: _EPHEMERAL_KEY = secrets.token_bytes(32)` without a lock. FastAPI/Starlette dispatches sync endpoints (and thus `persist` -> `sign`) on a threadpool (store.py line 124 explicitly notes threadpool dispatch). Two concurrent first-requests after startup can both observe None and both assign, so one request can sign with key A while a near-simultaneous request signs with key B; whichever assignment wins becomes the durable key, and the receipt signed with the losing key is persisted with a signature that will never verify thereafter. Narrow window (startup only, ephemeral-key path only) but it compounds the per-process-key trust problem. Compare store.py which guards its singleton with a double-checked lock; this one does not.

findings.title: Provider query string is length-bounded but not otherwise validated; passed straight to provider.list_messages enabling provider-side query abuse
findings.description: query defaults to has:nouserlabels and is bounded to 2048 chars, but its content is unvalidated and flows directly into provider.list_messages as a provider-native query (Gmail search syntax, IMAP search, Graph $filter). For an unauthenticated endpoint (see app.py finding) a caller controls exactly which messages are scanned across the configured mailbox. Even with auth, there is no allowlist of safe query forms; a crafted query could target specific senders. Lower severity if auth is added, but worth noting the request-validation gap.

findings.title: get_receipt does json.loads(summary_json) and pops it, but if summary_json is somehow NULL/invalid it raises uncaught
findings.description: summary_json column is NOT NULL by schema so this is mostly safe, but get_receipt unconditionally json.loads(row.pop('summary_json')). If a receipt was written by an older/buggy path with empty string (save_receipt writes json.dumps(summary) so it is at least 'null'/'{}'), this is fine; flagged as low-confidence robustness note — a corrupted row turns a 200 lookup into a 500. No try/except around the deserialization.

findings.title: Single shared sqlite3 connection with check_same_thread=False relies solely on an RLock; any code path that touches self._conn outside _lock corrupts serialization, and there is no transaction isolation across multi-statement methods
findings.description: The store guards every method with self._lock, but multi-statement methods (fulfill_once: INSERT then UPDATE; idempotency_begin: SELECT then INSERT/UPDATE) hold the RLock for the duration which is correct. However each method calls self._conn.commit() per statement-group and there is no BEGIN/rollback on failure: if the UPDATE in fulfill_once raises after the INSERT-OR-IGNORE committed-implicitly? (autocommit is off by default in sqlite3, so the INSERT is not committed until commit() at line 388). If the UPDATE raises, no commit() runs and the INSERT is rolled back at next operation — acceptable. The deeper risk: WAL pragma failure is swallowed (lines 133-136) so on a filesystem that rejects WAL the store silently runs in rollback-journal mode where the 'readers concurrent with writer' assumption in the docstring is false, potentially surfacing 'database is locked' under the threadpool dispatch the comment says is safe.

findings.title: Stale idempotency claim is re-bound after 60s even mid-charge, allowing a duplicate /complete to proceed concurrently with a slow-but-alive original
findings.description: If a /complete request legitimately takes longer than IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s, store.py:103) — e.g. a slow Stripe charge — a concurrent retry sees the claim as 'stale (crashed)', re-binds it, and returns state 'new' (store.py:337-344), letting BOTH requests proceed into the charge+fulfill path simultaneously. Double-charge is prevented by the per-session Stripe idempotency key and double-credit by fulfill_once's INSERT-OR-IGNORE, so the strong invariants hold; but two concurrent completions can race on receipt minting / response persistence and produce confusing state, and the 60s window assumes charges always finish in under a minute (Stripe charges can exceed that under retries/3DS-like flows for SPT). The timeout-based reclaim trades a stuck-key DoS for a concurrency window; document/verify the charge p99 stays well under 60s or gate the reclaim on a liveness signal.

findings.title: Stale-claim reclaim updates created_at but never resets status or clears a prior response_json, leaving a window where a re-claimed key could already hold a response
findings.description: The reclaim path (lines 338-343) does UPDATE idempotency_keys SET request_hash=?, created_at=? WHERE key=? — it does NOT touch status (still 'processing') nor response_json. This is fine for the documented crash case (a crashed 'processing' row has NULL response_json). But it assumes the only way to be in 'processing' past 60s is a crash; it never asserts response_json IS NULL. If a future code path or a manual/partial state ever leaves a 'processing' row with a stale response_json, the reclaim keeps that stale response, and a later idempotency_complete will overwrite it — but in the window between reclaim and complete, the row is 'processing' with a stale response that the replay branch (lines 350-353) would never reach (status is 'processing'), so a concurrent third caller with the matching new hash gets {'state':'processing'} and a 409. Net effect is a confusing state surface rather than a hard bug; flagging as a latent fragility in the reclaim's assumptions (it should defensively clear response_json and confirm status). Low severity / speculative.

findings.title: Stale-claim window uses wall-clock time.time(); a backward clock step (NTP correction) can make a fresh 'processing' claim appear stale or never expire
findings.description: The reclaim decision compares _now() (time.time(), line 106-107) minus the stored created_at (also time.time() at insert, line 331) against the 60s timeout. time.time() is wall-clock and non-monotonic: an NTP step or manual clock change between the INSERT and the check can either (a) make the delta negative or tiny so a genuinely-stuck claim never reclaims (prolonged 409 DoS — the exact thing the timeout exists to prevent), or (b) jump the delta past 60s so an actively in-flight legitimate claim is reclaimed and re-executed (the double-execute path above). On a server with aggressive NTP discipline or a VM resumed from suspend, clock jumps are realistic. A monotonic clock (time.monotonic) cannot be used across process restarts (its epoch resets), so the correct fix is a larger, well-chosen wall-clock timeout plus tolerance — but the use of bare wall-clock with a tight 60s threshold is fragile. Low/medium confidence; severity low because it requires a clock anomaly to trigger.

findings.title: Replay of a key whose response_json is NULL silently returns response=None, which the router forwards as a JSON 'null' body with HTTP 200
findings.description: On the replay branch, json.loads(existing['response_json'] or 'null') yields Python None when response_json is NULL (line 352). This can occur if a key reached status 'done' but response_json was never written (no current path does both, but idempotency_complete and the 'done' transition are separate from the INSERT, and any future/partial path could). The router's _begin_idempotency returns state['response'] (router.py 117), and create/complete return JSONResponse(replay, headers={'Idempotent-Replayed':'true'}) (e.g. router 172) — JSONResponse(None) emits the literal body `null` with status 200. A client replaying would receive a 200 with a null body instead of the original resource, a silent contract violation rather than an error. Defensive handling (treat None response on a 'done' key as a server error / re-derive) is absent. Low severity / requires an inconsistent stored state to trigger.

findings.location: add_credits lines 223-233 / consume_credit 235-246 (run_credits ledger) vs acp/router.py fulfill_once crediting total_runs (router 290)
findings.title: run_credits balance and the dollar amount charged are tracked in unrelated units with no linkage — a credited-runs vs amount-paid mismatch cannot be detected
findings.description: ACP fulfillment credits `total_runs` (a run count) to accounts.run_credits, while the money actually captured is `amount` (cents) recorded only inside the signed receipt summary. The two are written by separate statements (fulfill_once -> run_credits; save_receipt -> summary.amount) with no stored invariant tying credited runs to dollars paid. If total_runs and amount ever desynchronize (e.g. the update_session re-pricing path above, or a pack whose runs/amount_cents ratio is mis-edited), the account is credited a run count that does not correspond to the money captured, and nothing reconciles them. The receipt's runs_credited and amount are both derived from the same in-memory values at completion, so the receipt cannot serve as an independent cross-check of runs-credited against dollars-charged.

findings.title: json.dumps of caller-supplied dicts can raise TypeError on non-JSON-serializable values, raising inside the lock with no rollback
findings.description: save_receipt, idempotency_complete, and save_session all call json.dumps on a dict that originates from request/response/summary data. If that dict ever contains a non-serializable value (datetime, Decimal, bytes, set, a custom object) json.dumps raises TypeError. In save_receipt the json.dumps is evaluated while constructing the execute() arguments, so it raises before the INSERT and merely surfaces a 500; but in fulfill-style flows and save_session the raise occurs inside the held lock, and combined with the missing rollback can leave the connection in an unclean state. There is no default=str fallback on any of these dumps. Round 1 noted the receipt JSON round-trip can change signed bytes but did not flag the serialization-raise path.

findings.title: close() leaves the singleton pointing at a closed connection; any later call raises 'Cannot operate on a closed database' on every method
findings.description: close() closes self._conn but does not clear the module singleton _STORE and there is no reconnect logic. If anything (a test teardown that forgets set_store(None), a shutdown hook, or a future lifespan handler) calls store.close() while get_store() still returns that same instance, every subsequent store call executes on a closed connection and raises sqlite3.ProgrammingError. There is no guard (self._conn is checked nowhere) and no lazy reconnect. This is distinct from Round 1's 'never closed in production' finding — here the hazard is the opposite: when close() IS called the object is left in an unusable, undetected state.

findings.location: save_session lines 392-413 (INSERT OR REPLACE) vs fulfill_once 365-389
findings.title: save_session uses INSERT OR REPLACE keyed only on session id with no status/account guard — a concurrent cancel can overwrite a completed session
findings.description: save_session does INSERT OR REPLACE on acp_sessions(id ...) preserving only created_at. There is no compare-and-set on status. In complete_session the success path persists status=completed (router 337) while a racing cancel_session persists status=canceled (router 357), both via save_session — last-writer-wins, so a cancel arriving after completion can overwrite the completed row to canceled even though credits were already fulfilled (fulfill_once is a separate table and is not reverted). The displayed/stored session status then disagrees with the actual fulfillment. Round 1 noted last-write-wins for failed-vs-success complete; this adds the complete-vs-cancel race specifically resurrecting a non-completed status over a fulfilled session.

findings.title: Single shared sqlite connection with check_same_thread=False relies entirely on RLock; any code path touching _conn without the lock corrupts the connection
findings.description: The store guards _conn with an RLock and documents 'never touched concurrently', but the contract is unenforced: _fetch_one_nolock executes on _conn and is called from idempotency_begin/save_session WITHIN the lock (ok), yet there is no guard preventing future callers from using _conn outside the lock. sqlite3 connections are not safe to share across threads even serialized for some operations (e.g. an in-flight cursor). A more robust design uses a connection-per-thread or a queue. Architectural fragility note, low severity given current usage.

findings.location: add_credits lines 223-233 + consume_credit lines 235-246 (no overflow / SQLite INTEGER semantics)
findings.title: run_credits INTEGER accumulates unbounded via add_credits/fulfill_once; a 1M-run pack repeatedly fulfilled has no ceiling and consume_credit's n is uncapped
findings.description: add_credits and fulfill_once do run_credits = run_credits + ? with int() but no upper bound (store.py lines 227, 384). Given the 1,000,000-run single-session ceiling (models.py quantity le=1000 * pack runs), a few fulfillments push run_credits into the millions with no business cap. SQLite INTEGER is 64-bit so no numeric overflow, but the unbounded balance combined with consume_credit (lines 235-246) being never called means the balance is a monotonically-growing, never-debited number — the credits-sold-but-never-spent round-1 finding plus the absence of any per-account credit ceiling. There is no guard rejecting an implausible balance, so a desynced fulfillment that credits 1M runs is permanent and invisible.

findings.location: fulfill_once line 378 int(runs) — negative/huge runs accepted verbatim into balance
findings.title: fulfill_once trusts the runs argument with no >=0 guard, so a negative or oversized total_runs is credited (or debited) verbatim
findings.description: fulfill_once does int(runs) and UPDATE run_credits = run_credits + ? (lines 378, 384) with no guard that runs >= 0 or below a sane ceiling. complete_session passes total_runs straight from the stored session (router line 290). If the desync (residual-gap scope) or a malformed stored session yields a negative total_runs, fulfill_once silently DEBITS the account's balance while the charge still captured a positive amount; an oversized total_runs credits an implausible balance. The exactly-once primitive validates uniqueness but not the magnitude/sign of what it credits.

findings.title: Field name with a dot is reinterpreted by `op` as section.field; unescaped assignment
findings.description: op_item_edit builds the assignment string `f"{field}={value}"`. 1Password's `op item edit` parses the left side as `[section.]field`, so a field name containing a '.' (e.g. a misconfigured OP_GMAIL_TOKEN_FIELD='oauth.token_json') silently writes to a different section/field than intended, and any '=' the user puts in the field-name env var would shift the split point. Low likelihood (field names come from env config, not the network) but it is unescaped/unstructured assignment construction that can corrupt the wrong field. The newer `--field` / typed assignment form avoids this ambiguity.

findings.title: op stderr echoed into RuntimeError can leak vault/item names into error surfaces
findings.description: For non-sensitive ops (op_read, op_item_get), a CalledProcessError includes `exc.stderr.strip()` in the raised RuntimeError message. 1Password CLI stderr can contain the item/vault reference and account hints. These reads back load_secret (which only warns) but op_read is also called directly by gmail_auth and could surface in higher-level error strings/logs. The service layer (api/service.py) is careful to never echo provider exception text to clients, but op_read/op_item_get raised here are not guaranteed to be scrubbed everywhere they are used. Mild info-leak risk.

findings.title: Read paths do not set sensitive=True, so op CLI stderr is interpolated into error messages
findings.description: op_read and op_item_get call _run_op without sensitive=True. On CalledProcessError the non-sensitive branch (line 45-46) appends exc.stderr to the RuntimeError. 1Password's CLI is generally careful, but stderr on certain auth/session failures can include the requested reference or contextual data; this then propagates to the warning logs in load_secret. The write path correctly uses sensitive=True; the read paths are inconsistent. Low severity because op rarely echoes the secret value itself, but the inconsistency widens the disclosure surface at the credential boundary.

findings.title: Inconsistent/undocumented op CLI flag ordering for edit vs get; --vault placement after positional assignment
findings.description: For op item get, flags follow the item positional. For op item edit, the assignment token `field=value` is placed immediately after item and BEFORE --account/--vault. op generally accepts flags in any position, but mixing positional assignments with trailing flags is fragile and version-dependent; combined with the missing `--` sentinel (separate finding), an op CLI version that stops parsing flags after the first positional/assignment would silently ignore --vault, writing to the wrong vault (or the default vault) for the token. Given no tests assert argv, a behavior change here would be invisible. Recommend canonical ordering: `op item edit --account A --vault V -- item field=value`.

findings.title: Empty-string secret is treated as 'not present' and silently falls through to next source
findings.description: value = os.getenv(env_var); if value: -> an env var set to an empty string is falsy and treated as 'unset', so the function silently proceeds to 1Password lookups. If an operator intentionally set the var to empty (to disable a credential) or a deploy wrote an empty value, the system silently uses a different source instead of failing/honoring the explicit empty. Same pattern at lines 187/270 (`if item and field`) — an empty item or field is silently skipped. Minor, but at a credential boundary the difference between 'unset' and 'explicitly empty' can mask configuration mistakes.

findings.title: Credential write-back has no audit/success logging, so a wrong-field or wrong-vault write is undetectable
findings.description: op_item_edit returns None and store_json_secret returns silently on success. There is no log line confirming which item/field/vault was written. Combined with the field-path and flag-ordering issues above, a successful-but-misdirected write (wrong vault/field) leaves no trace; the operator only discovers it when the next auth read fails. A single INFO log ('stored token to item X field Y vault Z' — identifiers only, never the value) would make misconfiguration diagnosable. Observability gap at the most consequential boundary.

findings.title: batch.execute() unguarded; header lookup by exact-case 'From'/'Subject' may miss
findings.description: drain_loop's batch.execute() (line 135) has no try/except, so an HttpError aborts the whole drain. The callback matches headers with `h['name'] == 'From'` (exact case and KeyError-prone: uses h['name'] not h.get('name')); Gmail returns canonical 'From'/'Subject' so usually fine, but a header dict missing 'name' raises KeyError inside the callback which google-api-client may swallow, dropping the message. Minor robustness.

findings.location: cmd_summary line 564; cmd_pending line 680; cmd_summary tier_counts init line 535
findings.title: tier_counts indexed by cat_result.tier can introduce out-of-range tiers / KeyError-free silent growth
findings.description: tier_counts is initialized {1:0,2:0,3:0,4:0} (line 535) but line 564 does `tier_counts[cat_result.tier] = tier_counts.get(cat_result.tier, 0) + 1`, which silently creates a new key if categorize_with_tier ever returns a tier outside 1-4 (e.g., 0 or 5). The JSON/markdown/table output then iterates tier_counts including that bogus tier, and get_tier_config(tier) falls back to PRIORITY_TIERS[4] (rules.py line 1049) mislabeling it as 'Reference'. Low likelihood given current rules, but it is an unvalidated trust of upstream tier values; an out-of-range tier would be silently mis-summarized rather than flagged.

findings.title: escalate: when no actions built (e.g., all dry-run) result.success_count is set to escalated_count, but with dry_run escalated_count>0 and actions empty conflates 'previewed' with 'succeeded'
findings.description: In escalate, actions are only appended when `not args.dry_run` (line 902). So in a dry run, actions is empty, the else branch runs `result.success_count = escalated_count` (line 930), reporting escalations as successes though nothing was applied. Additionally, on a real run where escalated_count>0 but every action targeted a provider lacking categories/folder/star (so action has no effect), apply_actions is still called; that's provider-dependent. The dry-run success accounting (success_count = escalated_count) is the same dry-run-reports-success smell as cmd_label, and `--limit` here is messages to CHECK (line 1096) not escalate, which is at least consistent with its help.

findings.title: audit default path built from args.provider unsanitized; provider is choice-restricted so low risk, but audit_file custom path has no path-traversal guard
findings.description: default_path = f"audit/{args.provider}-{kind}.jsonl" — args.provider is constrained by argparse choices, so safe. However --audit-file (line 370 audit_path = getattr(args,'audit_file',None) or default) accepts an arbitrary user path with no validation; a path like '../../etc/x' or an absolute path is written verbatim by AuditLog. The code only WARNS (line 382-388) that a non-audit/ path isn't gitignored; it does not prevent writing PII outside the repo or to a traversal target. Combined with the un-redacted default (redact defaults False, line 371) real sender addresses (PII) are written to disk by default on every apply run.

findings.title: remove_label only removed when computed label differs from remove_label; equality check is exact-string and case-sensitive
findings.description: `if remove_label and label != remove_label: action.remove_labels.append(remove_label)` (line 274-275). The comparison is exact case-sensitive string equality. If a user passes --remove-label 'misc/other' but the rule produces 'Misc/Other', the labels differ by case so the (effectively same) label gets scheduled for removal AND the new (re-cased) label added — potentially churning the same logical label. Also if the newly computed label equals remove_label exactly, removal is skipped (correct), but there's no normalization, so the re-label sweep use-case (documented: `--query "label:Misc/Other" --remove-label "Misc/Other"`) depends on exact-case match to avoid removing the label it just re-applied.

findings.title: Edits SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist in place while Mail may be running, with a single .backup that prior runs overwrite
findings.description: The script rewrites the live Mail plist; if Mail.app is running it will overwrite the script's changes on quit (or the script's write races Mail's own write), and the backup path is fixed (.backup) so a second run clobbers the first backup — destroying the only good copy if the first run already corrupted the plist. There is also no validation that the dumped structure round-trips (plistlib.dump of arbitrary nested dicts can silently change types). macOS-only fragile-state mutation with weak recovery.

findings.title: Audit JSONL append is not flushed/fsynced; tail of receipt can be lost on crash
findings.description: _append opens in 'a' mode and writes one line per record, relying on the context manager to flush on close — but the file is reopened every call, so each record IS flushed on its own close. However there is no fsync, so on a power loss / kernel crash the last buffered lines may be lost even though the mail operations they recorded already happened — producing a receipt that under-reports actions, including potentially a violation line. For a module whose entire purpose is a trustworthy receipt of the safety invariant, the durability gap is worth noting. Also reopening the file per record is O(n) syscalls for large runs (perf).

findings.title: Fallback domain parser strips '>' but not '<' / whitespace inside, and differs from canonical normalize_sender
findings.description: When normalize_sender import/parse fails, _domain_of falls back to sender.rpartition('@')[2].strip().strip('>').lower(). For a raw header like 'Lawyer <a@firm.com>' this yields 'firm.com' correctly, but for 'a@firm.com>, b@bulk.com' (multi-address) it returns only the LAST address's domain ('bulk.com'), which is precisely the last-'@' escape the canonical _iter_sender_domains was hardened against. Since this only affects the 'domain' field stamped in the redacted receipt (not the protection decision, which uses _independently_protected), severity is low — but a redacted/shared receipt could record a misleading domain for a multi-address protected sender, undermining the receipt's evidentiary value.

findings.location: record() entry timestamp line 198 vs receipt; concurrency on shared path
findings.title: Concurrent AuditLog writers to same path can interleave/lose lines (no locking)
findings.description: Each record() reopens the path in append mode and writes a line. If two processes/threads run with the same audit path (e.g. two providers configured to the same default audit/ file, or overlapping scheduled runs), writes are not locked. POSIX append-mode writes of a single short line are usually atomic under PIPE_BUF, but JSON receipt lines containing full sender addresses can exceed PIPE_BUF (4096 bytes is the typical guarantee) for long headers, risking interleaved/garbled lines and a corrupt JSONL that won't parse on later analysis. Worth noting given runs can be scheduled (launchd daily job per CLAUDE.md).

findings.location: record() lines 152/184/200 (message_id typed str but accepted/stored unvalidated)
findings.title: message_id accepted and stored without type/empty validation; empty or non-str ids silently pollute the violations trail
findings.description: record() declares `message_id: str` but never validates it. It is appended raw to self.violations (line 184) and written as entry['message_id'] (line 200). Callers pass `action.message_id` (cli.py:79, base.py:384, gmail.py:489) which is typed str on LabelAction but is not guaranteed non-empty (LabelAction can be constructed with message_id=''). An empty-string id in a violation produces `violations: ['']`, which makes the breach message (receipt_line 272 / AuditInvariantError 256-259) report a violation it cannot pin to a real message, weakening the 'here is the receipt' forensic value precisely on the one path that matters most. Unlike sender (guarded by isinstance in _domain_of line 91), message_id has no defensive normalization anywhere in this module despite being the identifier the entire violations trail is built from.

findings.title: Path.expanduser() evaluated at import time freezes HOME
