findings.description: DEFAULT_CONFIG_PATHS is built at module import using Path('~/...').expanduser(). The expansion of ~ is bound once at import. If HOME/USERPROFILE changes after import (test harnesses, sudo -E, monkeypatched env), find_config_file() still looks at the original home directory. Minor, but it makes config discovery non-reactive to env changes and complicates testing. Prefer expanding inside find_config_file().

findings.title: DRY_RUN env var cannot be turned OFF and silently ignores invalid values
findings.description: `if os.getenv(f"{prefix}DRY_RUN")` only enters the branch when the var is set and truthy-as-string. Setting MAIL_AUTO_DRY_RUN=0 or =false still enters the branch (non-empty string) and correctly evaluates to False, so that part is fine — but a value like MAIL_AUTO_DRY_RUN=off or =no is silently treated as False with no warning, and if config.dry_run was True from YAML it gets overridden to False by any non-listed value. More importantly there is no validation/feedback for unrecognized truthiness tokens; the user gets silent behavior divergence. Low severity but worth noting as a silent-fallback.

findings.title: merge() reorders labels via set(), losing deterministic label order
findings.description: merge() computes add_labels=list(set(self.add_labels + other.add_labels)) and likewise for remove_labels. Converting through a set deduplicates (good) but also discards ordering non-deterministically (set iteration order for strings is not insertion order). If label application order matters anywhere downstream (e.g. first-match wins, or a provider that applies labels sequentially with side effects), merged actions become order-unstable across runs. Also note: if a label appears in BOTH add_labels and remove_labels after merge (one action adds, the other removes the same label), merge keeps both with no conflict resolution, leaving an inherently contradictory action.

findings.title: combined_text emits a leading/trailing space for empty fields, polluting the match string (e.g. ' ' for empty sender+subject)
findings.description: For an empty sender and subject, combined_text returns ' ' (a single space), confirmed by test_combined_text_empty (tests/test_models.py:69-71). More generally an empty sender yields ' subject' (leading space) and an empty subject yields 'sender ' (trailing space). A regex pattern with a start-anchor (^) intended to match the sender at the beginning of the string will instead see a leading space and fail to match, or a pattern using \s/word boundaries can match the synthetic separator space rather than real content. This silently degrades categorization for messages with a missing From or Subject (common for system/relay mail). Low severity because most patterns use re.search without anchors, but it is a latent correctness hazard given the field-conflation above. Fix: strip()/normalize, or join only the non-empty fields.

findings.title: Tier 2 emails in the 24-72h window never escalate, contradicting the documented 'Tier 2-4 -> Tier 1' intent and time-sensitivity
findings.description: The docstring/system rules describe escalation as raising stale mail in priority. In the 24-72h branch the code only escalates when `current_tier >= 3`, so a Tier 2 (Important) email aged 24-72h is never escalated even when time_sensitive=True -- it sits at Tier 2 until it crosses 72h. Reproduced: escalate_by_age(2, 50.0, True) -> escalated_tier=2, should_escalate=False with reason 'Not time-sensitive or already Important tier'. Whether this is intended is unclear, but the reason string conflates 'already Important' with 'not time-sensitive', and a time-sensitive Tier 2 bill/security item can languish 3 days before any escalation. Worth confirming against intended behavior.

findings.title: Negative / NaN email_age_hours mis-handled and final return is dead code
findings.description: email_age_hours is a float that can be negative (clock skew, future-dated email; calculate_email_age_hours can return a negative age if email_date is in the future). A negative age falls into `< 24` and is reported 'less than 24 hours old' (benign here, but a future-dated email is treated as fresh). More importantly the branches `age < 24`, `24 <= age < 72`, and `age >= 72` are exhaustive for any real number EXCEPT NaN; a NaN age (all comparisons false) falls through to the final `return EscalationResult(..., reason='No escalation rule matched')` -- so the final return is effectively dead code reachable only via NaN, and NaN is silently swallowed as 'no escalation'. No validation of the input.

findings.title: Relay senders resolve to email='' so the Gmail self-match can never fire for relayed self-mail
findings.description: When an iCloud relay local-part is decoded to an unprotected real domain, _resolve_addr returns ('', real_domain) -- the email is empty. _self_match(addr, domain) computes the local part via addr.rpartition('@')[0]; with addr='' it yields '' and can never match SELF_LOCALPARTS. So self mail forwarded through an iCloud relay (a common case for users protecting their address) would not be recognized as self by _self_match. Low impact because is_protected_sender still fails closed for relays it cannot decode and most self domains are gmail (non-relay), but the self-protection guarantee is weaker for relayed self-mail than the comments imply.

findings.location: categorize_with_tier VIP-no-override branch lines 991-995 vs normal branch 1006-1008
findings.title: VIP-without-override path re-categorizes on combined_text but defaults time_sensitive=True, diverging from non-VIP default of the rule's own value
findings.description: In the non-VIP path, time_sensitive comes from the matched rule (default False). In the VIP non-override path (line 995) the code does rule.get('time_sensitive', True) -- defaulting to True with a comment 'VIP is time-sensitive'. But it reads the matched rule's value when present, so a VIP whose mail matches e.g. Marketing (time_sensitive=False) gets time_sensitive=False, contradicting the 'VIP is time-sensitive' comment. The True default only applies when the label is absent from LABEL_RULES (essentially never, since _find_best_label always returns a known label). The intent (VIP => time-sensitive) is therefore not actually enforced.

findings.title: Personal rule's generic keywords (family/mom/dad) at tier 1 risk elevating marketing mail to Critical when it outranks competing rules
findings.description: The Personal rule is tier 1 (Critical, in KEEP_IN_INBOX) with bare substrings r'family', r'mom', r'dad' at priority 18. While priority 18 loses to most rules, any message that matches ONLY these (e.g. a 'family plan' or 'Mom-approved' marketing subject from an unrecognized sender) would be classified Personal/Critical/kept-in-inbox. In the reproduced case Marketing (priority 17) happened to win, but for a sender/subject lacking Marketing keywords these generic family words can wrongly mark commercial mail as Critical personal mail.

findings.location: _find_best_label() lines 1025-1038; LABEL_RULES catch-all pattern r'.*' line 502
findings.title: Catch-all regex r'.*' and broad substring patterns cause mojibake/over-matching; categorization is advisory but feeds archive decisions in tier_routing
findings.description: Misc/Other uses pattern r'.*' which matches everything; fine as a fallback at priority 999, but several rules use unanchored bare-word substrings against 'sender subject' combined text (e.g. Shopping r'uber' matches 'cyber'/'tuber'; r'target' matches 'targeting'; Marketing r'off' is avoided via \b but r'discount' substring matches 'discountinued'? no). The concern for THIS sweep: categorize feeds run_labeler which sets action.archive=True for non-keep labels. A false-positive category that is a tier 3/4 non-keep label results in a NON-protected message being archived/moved — the protected gate still shields protected senders, but an over-matched normal sender can be wrongly archived. Not an error-handling bug per se, but the categorization mis-fire is silently acted upon (archive) with only debug logging. Lower-confidence/severity since gate protects the sensitive class.

findings.title: Duplicate/overlapping flash-sale regexes — one alternation is dead, and broad keywords mislabel transactional mail
findings.description: The Marketing rule lists both r"(% ?off|limited[- ]time|exclusive offer|flash sale|use code|coupon)" and r"(flash sale|\d+% ?off|clearance sale)" — 'flash sale' is matched by both (the second is largely redundant). More importantly the bare token r"discount", r"statement" (in Finance/Payments), and r"invoice" are unanchored substrings that match inside legitimate transactional/legal subjects, dragging them to a lower-priority archive bucket. Low severity but a real precision defect in the shared taxonomy.

findings.title: appleid.com endswith check treats any '*.appleid.com' as a relay, mis-routing legitimate appleid subdomains
findings.description: _resolve_addr enters relay-decoding for `domain in RELAY_DOMAINS or domain.endswith('.appleid.com')`. appleid.com itself is in PROTECTED_SENDERS, but the relay branch then tries to recover a 'real' domain from the local part of ANY appleid.com subdomain (e.g. e.appleid.com marketing mail). For non-relay appleid subdomains the local part is not an encoded address, so _relay_domain_candidates yields junk and _best_relay_domain may return '' — falling through to keep the carrier domain, which is fine for protection but can mis-categorize. The endswith('.appleid.com') is also a subdomain-suffix match that would match a hypothetical 'evil.appleid.com.attacker' only if it literally ends in '.appleid.com' (it would not), so protection is safe — but categorization routing is muddied.

findings.title: Protected-sender local config loaded once at import; file is read with no size bound and merged unvalidated, and a domain line that is actually a comment-only or malformed entry is added verbatim
findings.description: _load_local_protected appends `line.lower()` for any non-self, non-comment line with no validation that it is a plausible domain (no '@', no spaces, has a dot). A stray line like 'my notes' becomes a protected 'entry' that _domain_matches will never match (harmless) — but a line like 'gmail.com' silently makes ALL Gmail senders protected (never-archive), a severe over-broad gate the user may not intend, with no warning. There is also no file-size cap, so a huge local file is fully read into memory at import. Over-protection fails safe but can render the whole product a no-op for a mailbox.

findings.title: last_run timestamp uses naive local datetime.now() (no tz) — inconsistent with audit's UTC
findings.description: StateManager.save records last_run via datetime.now().isoformat() with no timezone, producing a naive local-time string. core/audit.py deliberately uses datetime.now(timezone.utc). Mixing naive-local state timestamps with UTC audit timestamps makes cross-correlation and any age/escalation math built on last_run ambiguous across DST changes / machines in different zones, and a naive isoformat can't be safely compared to a tz-aware one (raises TypeError if subtracted). Recommend timezone-aware UTC for consistency.

findings.title: save() assumes history is mapping-like; a non-dict argument raises inside save
findings.description: save() does `dict(history)`. The type hint is Dict[str, int], but if a caller (or a future code path) passes a non-mapping iterable or a malformed value, dict() raises and — because save's try/except only wraps the file write (lines 91-95), not this line 86 — the exception propagates out of save() uncaught. This is inconsistent with the module's intent to never let state-keeping abort a run. Minor/defensive, but worth a guard given the rest of the module tries to be crash-safe.

findings.location: is_resumable, lines 131-133 vs get_token usage in cli.py:172
findings.title: is_resumable only null-checks the token; an empty-string or non-string token is reported resumable
findings.description: is_resumable() returns `self.state.get('next_page_token') is not None`. An empty string '' (which most provider APIs treat as 'no cursor' / first page) is `is not None` -> reports resumable True, and an empty/whitespace token would be replayed. More broadly, this predicate does not validate that the token is a usable non-empty string, so it can report a run as resumable when the stored token is actually unusable. Low impact but a correctness smell that pairs with the unvalidated-token replay finding.

findings.title: Date condition 'is less than value:30' is likely wrong units for a date-received filter
findings.description: condition type:date received, qualifier:is less than value:30 passes an integer 30 to a date-received condition. Mail.app date conditions expect a date value (or a relative day-count via a specific qualifier); an unqualified integer 'is less than 30' is unlikely to mean 'within the last 30 days' and may produce an empty or wrong 'Finance (Recent)' view. AppleScript Mail smart-mailbox date semantics are finicky and this is untested.

findings.title: 'message is in mailbox does contain Finance/Dev' depends on Gmail labels existing as Mail.app mailboxes
findings.description: The Finance and Dev smart mailboxes match on 'message is in mailbox does contain Finance'/'Dev'. If the hierarchical Gmail labels (Finance/Banking, Work/Dev/*) are not synced into Mail.app as mailboxes, these conditions match nothing. Cross-provider assumption is unstated and unverified.

findings.title: Query 'label:Uncategorized' likely targets a non-existent label (taxonomy uses 'Misc/Other')
findings.description: The sweep runs `app.run(query="label:Uncategorized")`. The repo's catch-all label is 'Misc/Other' (per CLAUDE.md and CATCH_ALL in icloud_triage.py); there is no 'Uncategorized' label in the documented taxonomy. If no such Gmail label exists, the query matches nothing and the script silently does nothing — a no-op masquerading as a sweep. Worth verifying the label name.

findings.title: is_important `contains s` iterates over a `text item` reference, not the string value (missing `contents of`)
findings.description: In the repeat over senderList, `s` is a list-item reference; the comparison `theSender contains s` relies on AppleScript implicit coercion of the reference to its string value. Contrast route_bulk_senders line 8 which correctly does `set dom to contents of d` before comparing. While AppleScript often coerces implicitly in `contains`, relying on it (rather than `contents of s`) is fragile and inconsistent with the sibling script; under some Mail/AppleScript versions a reference-vs-text comparison can behave unexpectedly, silently failing to flag an important sender. Low-confidence correctness smell not noted in round 1.

findings.title: Scope upgrade silently ignored when a cached token has narrower scopes
findings.description: get_credentials builds Credentials.from_authorized_user_info(token_info, scopes=scopes) but if the cached token was minted with narrower scopes than requested, google-auth does not re-consent automatically — creds.valid can be True with the old scope set, so the requested broader scope is silently not granted and later API calls fail with 403 insufficient permissions rather than triggering a fresh consent. There is no scope comparison before accepting the cached creds.

findings.location: process_batch retry block lines 168-182, lambda closure line 173
findings.title: Late-binding closure over loop variable in retry lambda (msg_id) — works here but fragile
findings.description: Inside the retry loop, the lambda at lines 173-176 captures msg_id by reference. It happens to be correct because the lambda is invoked synchronously within the same iteration (via _execute_with_backoff(...)), so msg_id has the expected value at call time. However this is the classic late-binding-closure footgun: any refactor that defers execution (collecting lambdas, threading, async) would make every retry use the last msg_id. Worth binding explicitly (lambda mid=msg_id: ...). Low risk as written.

findings.location: process_batch lines 247-261, batchModify lambda body closure line 258-260
findings.title: batchModify lambda closes over loop variable 'body' (rebuilt each iteration) — relies on synchronous invocation
findings.description: Same late-binding pattern as the retry lambda: the lambda at lines 258-260 references `body`, which is rebuilt each id_chunk iteration (line 252). It is correct only because _execute_with_backoff invokes it synchronously before the next iteration reassigns body. Fragile against refactor; consider binding body=body. Low risk as written.

findings.title: Batch HTTP retry re-executes an already-consumed BatchHttpRequest on rate limit, likely raising instead of retrying
findings.description: _execute_with_backoff(batch_get.execute, 'batch get') (line 164) passes the bound execute method of a googleapiclient BatchHttpRequest. If the batch execute raises an HttpError classified as rate-limited, the backoff loop calls batch_get.execute() again — but a BatchHttpRequest is generally single-use; re-executing an already-executed batch raises (e.g. 'BatchHttpRequest has already been executed' / empty request) rather than retrying the constituent gets. So the intended rate-limit backoff for the batch-get path is effectively non-functional; per-request rate limits inside the batch are instead surfaced via the per-request callback (failed_ids) and retried one-by-one (lines 168-182). The top-level batch retry is misleading dead/half-working code.

findings.location: run() exception handler lines 333-335 saving page_token
findings.title: On fatal mid-batch error, state is saved with the NEXT page token though the current batch was only partially modified
findings.description: If process_batch (line 311) raises partway through (some messages already modified, some not), the except handler at lines 333-335 saves page_token — but page_token at that point is still the token that produced the CURRENT page (it is only advanced at line 319 AFTER process_batch returns). So on the next resume the same page is reprocessed, which for the default draining query means already-labeled messages no longer match and are skipped — generally safe — but total_processed (line 312) was never incremented for the failed batch, so counts under-report. Conversely if the failure happens after line 319 advanced page_token but the partial batch's modifications are uncertain, the partially-processed page is skipped entirely. State-consistency on mid-batch crash is not transactional.

findings.location: process_batch stats line 204 vs continue at line 213
findings.title: Stats counter increments for messages that are then skipped (no label cached), over-reporting processed distribution
findings.description: self.stats[label_name] += 1 at line 204 runs BEFORE the `if not target_label_id: continue` guard at line 212-213. So when a categorized label has no cached ID (shouldn't normally happen since _init_labels creates all LABEL_RULES labels, but possible for a label not in LABEL_RULES, or a creation failure), the stat is counted even though no modification is applied. The session distribution printout (line 347-350) and persisted history then over-report. Minor reporting inaccuracy.

findings.location: process_batch() lines 257-262; batchModify lambda closure over `body`
findings.title: Lambda in batch-modify retry closes over loop variable `body`; safe today but a latent closure-capture bug
findings.description: Inside the `for (add, remove), msg_ids` / `for id_chunk` loops, `body` is rebound each iteration and passed to _execute_with_backoff via `lambda: ...batchModify(..., body=body)...` (line 259). Because _execute_with_backoff invokes the lambda synchronously within the same iteration, the late-binding closure happens to read the correct `body`. But this is fragile: any refactor that defers/collects these lambdas (e.g. batching retries) would have every lambda see the LAST body. Same late-binding pattern appears in the failed-fetch retry lambda at lines 173-176 (closes over msg_id, also synchronous-safe today).

findings.title: execute_with_retry can return None implicitly (no exception, no value) and retries non-retryable errors blindly
findings.description: execute_with_retry catches (HttpError, OSError, socket.error) AND a separate bare `except Exception` (line 310), retrying ALL exceptions with backoff regardless of whether they are transient — a permanent 400/permission error is retried 4 times with sleeps before finally re-raising. More subtly, the function only returns inside the try (line 303); there is no return after the loop, so if `retries` were ever 0 the function returns None and callers (e.g. line 321 results = execute_with_retry(...).get(...)) would crash with AttributeError on None. With the default retries=4 the loop always either returns or raises, so it is currently safe, but the structure is fragile and the catch-all retries waste time on hard failures.

findings.title: Legacy categorize_email accesses email_data['payload']['headers'] without guards (KeyError on missing payload)
findings.description: categorize_email (line 342) does headers = email_data['payload']['headers'] with hard indexing. A metadata-format get normally includes payload.headers, but a malformed/partial response (or a message with no headers) raises KeyError, which is then swallowed by the broad `except Exception` in the caller (line 441) and the message is skipped with a printed error. core/rules.categorize_message uses header.get(...) defensively. Also note line 348 `header['name']` and `header['value']` use hard indexing where core uses .get(). Minor robustness divergence in the legacy path.

findings.title: Same draining-query-with-page-token hazard as gmail_labeler.py; plus no state persistence so a crash loses all progress
findings.description: label_all_unlabeled_emails pages 'has:nouserlabels' using nextPageToken (lines 396,449) while simultaneously labeling those messages (removing them from the match set). The result set shifts under pagination, so messages can be skipped. Unlike gmail_labeler.py there is NO StateManager here, so a crash/interrupt loses page position entirely and a rerun starts from page 1 (acceptable since query drains, but combined with the shift-skip it can leave a residue of never-labeled messages). verify_labeling_complete (line 484) reports remaining count via resultSizeEstimate which is itself only an estimate, so 'SUCCESS: all labeled' can be falsely reported.

findings.title: resultSizeEstimate used as exact count; it is an ESTIMATE and unreliable for completion checks
findings.description: verify_labeling_complete reads results.get('resultSizeEstimate', 0) (line 485) and declares SUCCESS when it equals 0. The Gmail API documents resultSizeEstimate as an approximation, not exact; it can report 0 while messages remain, or non-zero when empty. Treating it as ground truth for 'all emails labeled' yields false SUCCESS / false WARNING messages. Cosmetic here (it only prints), but the same value drives operator trust.

findings.title: `--limit` slice keeps most-recent UIDs but archives them — opposite of typical 'archive old noise' intent, and limit semantics may surprise
findings.description: `uids = uids[max(0, len(uids) - args.limit):]` keeps the most-recent N UIDs (highest UID values, assuming UID order ~ arrival order). The archive then targets these most-recent messages. For a triage tool whose purpose is clearing noise, defaulting to operating on the newest 500 (rather than oldest) is a debatable choice and could archive freshly arrived mail the user hasn't seen. Worth confirming intent; at minimum it's undocumented in --limit help ('max most-recent messages to scan').

findings.title: IMAP fetch failure for a single message returns ('','') which then bypasses protected check via empty sender = protected (OK) but mislabels via empty domain
findings.description: If the per-UID fetch fails (res != OK or data malformed) it returns ('',''). is_protected_sender('') returns True (fail closed) so it is protected — safe. But this is a silent per-message failure with no logging/counter; a server hiccup could silently mark many messages 'protected' and skew the dry-run disposition numbers the operator relies on to decide whether to --apply.

findings.title: Mailbox name is wrapped in literal quotes but not escaped, breaking on names containing quotes/special chars
findings.description: Both MOVE and COPY wrap the destination as f'"{archive_mailbox}"'. A mailbox name containing a double-quote or backslash would produce a malformed IMAP command (and 'Archive' with non-ASCII would need modified-UTF-7 encoding, which isn't applied). Default 'Archive' is fine, but a user-supplied --archive-mailbox with a space-or-special name relies on naive quoting that can misfire or, worse, move to the wrong folder.

findings.title: IMAP archive-mailbox name injected unescaped into UID MOVE/COPY
findings.description: `imap.uid('MOVE', uid, f'"{archive_mailbox}"')` and `imap.uid('COPY', uid, f'"{archive_mailbox}"')` interpolate the `--archive-mailbox` CLI arg (default 'Archive') directly into the quoted IMAP argument with no escaping. A destination name containing a quote or CRLF could break the command. archive_mailbox is operator-supplied (argparse) so this is mostly a robustness/quoting bug rather than a remote-exploit, but a malformed name silently produces a malformed IMAP command rather than a clear error.

findings.title: Narrow except on MOVE only catches imaplib.IMAP4.error; MOVE returning non-OK without raising falls through to COPY duplicating the message
findings.description: archive_uid tries UID MOVE; if it returns res != 'OK' (server replied NO/BAD without raising), the code does not return and falls through to the COPY+Delete+EXPUNGE path. If the MOVE actually partially succeeded server-side (some servers move then report oddly) or the COPY then also succeeds, the message can be duplicated or the original mishandled. The except clause only catches imaplib.IMAP4.error, so a different exception (socket error) would not be caught and would abort mid-archive. Edge-case correctness around the MOVE/COPY dual path.

findings.title: data[0].split() on a failed/None search and most-recent-N slice assume sequential UID ordering
findings.description: Line 107 does data[0].split() after checking res=='OK', but if the server returns OK with an empty/None data[0] this could error; minor. More substantive: line 112 `uids = uids[max(0, len(uids)-args.limit):]` assumes the UID SEARCH result is returned in ascending UID order so the tail is 'most recent'. IMAP does not guarantee SEARCH returns UIDs sorted, and 'highest UID' is not strictly 'most recent by Date' anyway. The 'scan most-recent N' contract can silently scan an arbitrary subset, so a protected/important message outside that slice is simply never evaluated (it's left in inbox, which is safe, but the dry-run disposition counts are misleading).

findings.location: main() line 130; normalize_sender vs is_protected_sender domain basis
findings.title: Categorization uses normalize_sender's FIRST address only while protection uses the union; a multi-address From categorizes off one address
findings.description: Line 130 uses normalize_sender(frm) -> real_domain for categorization, which per core/rules.py returns only the FIRST/primary address's domain. The protected gate (is_protected_sender) correctly unions all addresses, so protection is sound. But categorization of a multi-address From (e.g. 'Bank <a@bank>, Marketing <b@bulk>') keys off the first address only; if the first is a relay/undecodable, real_domain may be '' and it falls to `categorize_from_strings('' or frm, subj)` (line 131) which then matches against the raw header again. Minor: protected mail is still safe; only the archive bucket label for non-protected multi-address senders can be off.

findings.title: ensure_label caches label as created on IMAP error responses other than OK/NO, and treats NO as success blindly
findings.description: ensure_label adds the label to created_cache only when res in ('OK','NO'). If create() returns 'BAD' or raises, the label is not cached and ensure_label will retry create() on every subsequent message with that label (minor inefficiency). More concerning: it treats 'NO' as 'already exists' (per the comment) and proceeds, but 'NO' can also mean the create genuinely failed for another reason (permission, invalid name); the subsequent apply_label COPY to a nonexistent folder then fails silently (see related finding). No result from create() is logged, so a systematically failing folder creation is invisible.

findings.title: Legacy IMAP label injection (unescaped quotes in STORE/COPY)
findings.description: Same IMAP quoted-string injection as providers/imap.py: `imap.uid('STORE', uid, '+X-GM-LABELS', f'"{label}"')` and `imap.uid('COPY', uid, f'"{label}"')` interpolate the categorized label without escaping. The label is produced by categorize_from_strings (currently a fixed taxonomy) so practical risk is low, but a label/folder containing `"` breaks the IMAP argument. No anchoring/escaping. Worth surfacing for defense-in-depth.

findings.title: load_password has unreachable final return and broad except hiding 1Password failures; can silently proceed with empty password
findings.description: The final `return ''` (line 77) is unreachable (the getpass try/except always returns). The op subprocess except (line 71-72) catches everything, prints, and falls THROUGH to getpass — but in a non-interactive run (cron/launchd) getpass either raises (caught, returns '') or reads EOF, yielding an empty password; main() then bails 'must be set' which is acceptable, but the broad except also masks a genuine op misconfiguration as a silent fallthrough to a password prompt that hangs a daemon. Also subprocess uses op item without --vault and trusts OP_ITEM/OP_FIELD env verbatim (no injection risk since not shell=True, but unvalidated).

findings.title: Route list omits POST /v1/triage and may drift from actual api/app.py routes
findings.description: llms.txt lists triage/preview but not the live mutating POST /v1/triage, and lists endpoints without methods. Worth reconciling the advertised route set against api/app.py + the worker.mjs route table so agents do not call wrong/missing paths.

findings.title: Label names with spaces/slashes are interpolated into Gmail query without quoting
findings.description: `query = f"label:{category} is:unread older_than:7d"`. Category names like 'Work/Dev/GitHub' work as Gmail label paths, but any category containing a space (none here, but the pattern is fragile and reused elsewhere) would break the query. More relevantly, no escaping/quoting is applied, so adding a category with spaces later silently malforms the query and clears the wrong (or no) messages.

findings.location: _clamp_limit() line 83-84; triage limit param typed int
findings.title: _clamp_limit assumes coercible limit; int(limit) can raise on bad MCP input
findings.description: _clamp_limit does int(limit). The MCP tool signature types limit as int, and FastMCP normally coerces, but if a client passes a non-coercible value (e.g. a string like 'all' or null that slips through), int(limit) raises ValueError/TypeError which is NOT caught in _triage (only ProviderUnavailable and AuditInvariantError are caught), producing an unhandled error rather than a clean validation message. Minor robustness gap given the file's careful error-mapping elsewhere.

findings.title: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' fully disables DNS-rebinding protection with no origin checks
findings.description: Setting MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS to '*' returns TransportSecuritySettings(enable_dns_rebinding_protection=False), disabling Host/Origin validation entirely. The docstring assumes a fronting proxy validates Host, but nothing enforces that assumption; a misconfiguration (or copy-paste of '*' for convenience) leaves the Streamable HTTP endpoint open to DNS-rebinding/cross-origin attacks against a local/hosted MCP server. Worth flagging as a foot-gun even though it is opt-in.

findings.title: DNS-rebinding protection fully disabled when MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*'
findings.description: Setting MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' returns TransportSecuritySettings(enable_dns_rebinding_protection=False), disabling Host/Origin validation entirely. The docstring says to use it only behind a validating proxy, but nothing enforces that; a misconfiguration exposes the hosted Streamable HTTP MCP to DNS-rebinding from browsers. Note positively: limit is clamped and sender/subject are truncated to 4096 before reaching service. Flagging the '*' escape hatch as a config-hardening risk.

findings.location: module import time line 151 (http_app = mcp.streamable_http_app()) and lines 38-45 in api/app.py
findings.title: _transport_security() reads MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS once at import time, freezing the host allowlist for the process
findings.description: _transport_security() is invoked exactly once when the FastMCP instance is constructed at module import (line 73-80), and http_app is built immediately after (line 151). The MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS env var is therefore snapshotted at first import and never re-read. Any orchestration that sets/updates the var after the api.app import chain has already pulled in mcp_server.server (api/app.py:39 imports it at module load) will silently run with the stale (likely localhost-only) allowlist, compounding the 421 outage above and making the failure non-obvious to operators who 'set the env var' but after process start / in a different layer. Low severity but a real operational footgun not noted in round 1.

findings.title: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS hardcodes :8000 dev hosts into the production allowlist and builds origins by string concat, admitting any scheme-host on port 8000
findings.description: _transport_security always appends localhost/127.0.0.1 AND localhost:8000/127.0.0.1:8000 to allowed_hosts even in production, and constructs allowed_origins as http://+host and https://+host for every entry. The :8000 dev entries widen the production DNS-rebinding allowlist permanently, and there is no way to run hardened without also trusting loopback:8000. A deployment intending strict Host validation cannot drop the dev hosts. Minor hardening gap.

findings.title: DNS-rebinding allowlist hardcodes only :8000 loopback variants; a hosted MCP on a non-8000 port loses loopback dev access and may push operators to set MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS=* (protection off)
findings.description: _transport_security always appends exactly ['localhost','127.0.0.1','localhost:8000','127.0.0.1:8000'] (line 52). If the hosted Streamable HTTP app runs on any other port (uvicorn default for this app is shown as :8000 but Render/Heroku inject $PORT), local/loopback testing against the real port fails Host validation, and the documented escape hatch is MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' (line 49) which disables DNS-rebinding protection entirely. The brittle hardcoded port list nudges operators toward turning the protection off. Also: the production host must be supplied without scheme, but operators copy a full https:// Render URL into MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS, which then becomes allowed_hosts=['https://x'] and never matches the Host header — silently breaking all hosted requests or, again, driving them to '*'.

findings.title: Rename conflict handling updates local index incorrectly, and rename can collide with existing Gmail label causing API error mid-pass
findings.description: After a successful rename, line 63 sets `name_to_meta[new] = meta` but leaves the old key present in name_to_meta. More importantly, the conflict check (line 50) only skips when a target with a DIFFERENT id exists; but Gmail's labels.update will fail if the new name duplicates an existing label name (case-insensitive) that wasn't in name_to_meta due to a prior rename in the same run — the local cache isn't fully consistent with server state, so a later rename in the loop can hit an unexpected 'label name exists' HttpError. It is caught and logged (line 64-65), so non-fatal, but renames can be silently skipped.

findings.title: Hardcoded label-rename map with no dry-run mutates live Gmail label taxonomy immediately
findings.description: main() performs labels().update() on the user's real Gmail account with no --dry-run or confirmation. Renaming a label rewrites it for every message carrying it. If any RENAMES source name was already partly migrated, or a target path's parent doesn't exist, results are surprising. For a bulk taxonomy mutation, lack of a preview mode is a blast-radius concern (lower than data deletion, but still).

findings.location: main() lines 49-63; rename conflict / mapping update
findings.title: Label rename updates name_to_meta[new] to the SAME meta object, so chained renames and conflict detection can misbehave
findings.description: After a successful rename, line 63 sets name_to_meta[new] = meta (the OLD meta dict, whose 'name' field is still the old name). The conflict check at line 50 compares target['id'] != meta['id']; because the stored meta still has the old id under the new key this is internally consistent, but the meta['name'] is now stale, and if RENAMES contained a chain (A->B then B->C) the second rename would read name_to_meta.get('B') = the A-meta and rename A's id again rather than a distinct B label. With the current static RENAMES (no chains) this is latent, but the bookkeeping is incorrect and would bite if the map grows. Non-destructive (renames only), low severity.

findings.title: health_check swallows ALL exceptions into a generic unhealthy status, masking auth vs network vs quota distinctions
findings.description: health_check does `try: self.list_messages(limit=1) ... except Exception as e: return False, str(e)`. Catching bare Exception conflates an expired/invalid token (AuthenticationError), a network outage (ConnectionError), and a rate-limit (which list_messages' backoff may turn into a RuntimeError after 310s of sleeping). The caller gets only a string; there is no way to distinguish a transient throttle (retryable) from a hard auth failure (needs re-login). Also, calling list_messages(limit=1) with an empty query against Gmail lists the entire mailbox's first message — fine, but for IMAP/others an empty query may behave differently. Low/medium-confidence observability weakness.

findings.title: Gate neutralizes archive + INBOX-removal but does NOT clear action.star / action.category / move-on-label for non-LABEL_IS_MOVE folder providers when archive flag is the only departure
findings.description: _drop_if_protected sets archive=False and strips INBOX/\INBOX from remove_labels, and apply_actions additionally suppresses label-as-move via move_via_label. But for a folder provider where LABEL_IS_MOVE is False yet apply_label still physically relocates (none ship today, but the base contract permits it), add_labels are still applied — the suppression depends solely on the LABEL_IS_MOVE class attribute being correct per provider. If a provider mis-declares LABEL_IS_MOVE=False while its apply_label moves, the gate leaks. The invariant is enforced by a per-provider boolean rather than by capability, a fragile coupling.

findings.title: batchModify body captured by late-binding closure inside loop
findings.description: The lambda passed to _execute_with_backoff closes over `body`, which is reassigned each loop iteration. Because _execute_with_backoff invokes the lambda synchronously within the same iteration, the currently-correct `body` is used, so this is NOT a live bug today. However, it is fragile: if _execute_with_backoff were ever changed to defer/schedule the callable, or if the loop variable `body` were reused, every retry/deferred call would operate on the last-assigned body. Recommend binding via default arg (lambda b=body: ...). Flagging for coverage as a latent closure-over-loop-variable smell.

findings.title: batch.execute retried wholesale on rate limit can double-process already-succeeded messages and accumulate state in closure
findings.description: batch_get_details wraps `batch.execute` in _execute_with_backoff. If the batch partially succeeds then hits a rate-limit HttpError, _execute_with_backoff retries the SAME batch object. The `callback` closure appends to `failed_ids` and writes into `results` on every invocation. On retry, google-api-client re-issues only unfinished requests in some versions but the failed_ids list is not reset between attempts, so a message that failed on attempt 1 then succeeded on retry would remain in failed_ids and be redundantly re-fetched individually at lines 252-255. This wastes quota but is not data-corrupting. Behavior depends on the client library's batch retry semantics; flagged as medium-confidence correctness/efficiency concern.

findings.title: left_inbox detection upper-cases label-cache INBOX id but real Gmail user-label ids are mixed-case opaque tokens
findings.description: inbox_id = str(self._label_cache.get('INBOX', 'INBOX')).upper() and _inbox_forms = {'INBOX','\\INBOX', inbox_id}. Then any(str(rid).upper() in _inbox_forms for rid in remove_ids). For Gmail the INBOX system label id IS literally 'INBOX', so this works. But the comment claims defense against 'non-literal/aliased' ids — the upper() canonicalization is applied to BOTH sides, so if INBOX ever had an opaque id like 'Label_99', comparing 'LABEL_99' against remove_ids works only because the same value flows into remove_ids via _label_cache.get('INBOX'). The audit flag is best-effort and only over-reports (never hides), as the comment states, so low severity — but worth noting the upper() of an opaque case-sensitive Gmail id could theoretically cause a missed match if the id is added to remove_ids in original case while inbox_id is upper-cased. Low-confidence edge case.

findings.title: get_message_details bypasses 404-to-None handling when the 404 is wrapped by backoff RuntimeError, and only treats status==404 specially
findings.description: get_message_details calls _execute_with_backoff and separately catches HttpError to map 404 -> None. But _execute_with_backoff itself catches HttpError internally: a 404 is not rate-limited so it is re-raised from inside the helper as the original HttpError (good, the outer except sees it). However, e.resp.status is accessed directly (line 182) without getattr guard — if e.resp is None (possible for some HttpError construction paths / network-layer errors), this raises AttributeError instead of returning None, crashing the caller. The backoff helper uses getattr(e.resp,'status',None) defensively but this site does not. Low-confidence robustness gap / inconsistency.

findings.title: is_read derived as 'UNREAD not in label_ids' — relies on UNREAD always being present in metadata labelIds
findings.description: is_read = 'UNREAD' not in label_ids. This is the correct Gmail convention (unread messages carry the UNREAD label). However if labelIds is missing/empty due to a partial metadata response, is_read defaults to True (read), which could cause an unread important message to be treated as already-seen. This is the safe-ish default but worth noting it fails toward 'read' on degraded responses. Low severity / low confidence.

findings.title: Late-binding closure over loop variable 'body' in batchModify lambda can apply the WRONG batch on retry
findings.description: Inside the per-chunk loop, 'body' is reassigned each iteration and captured by the lambda passed to _execute_with_backoff: lambda: ...batchModify(userId='me', body=body).execute(). _execute_with_backoff invokes func() multiple times across retry attempts (up to 5) within the same iteration, so the captured 'body' is correct for THAT call. However the lambda closes over the loop variable by reference; this is safe only because execution is synchronous within the iteration. The real latent bug is that 'body' (and the 'lambda' in apply_label/remove_label/get_message_details) capture mutable loop state — currently fine because each is executed before the next reassignment, but it is fragile and an easy regression vector if execution is ever deferred. Lower confidence as a live bug; flagging the pattern. The list-comprehension closures elsewhere (none) are fine.

findings.title: Batch callback records failures but the surrounding batch.execute via _execute_with_backoff can lose individual successes on a whole-batch retry
findings.description: batch_get_details runs the batch through _execute_with_backoff(batch.execute, ...). If the OVERALL batch.execute call raises a rate-limit HttpError, _execute_with_backoff re-invokes batch.execute() — but a google-api-client BatchHttpRequest is single-use; re-executing an already-executed/partially-executed batch object is undefined and may raise or silently no-op, while the callback may have already populated 'results'/'failed_ids' from the first attempt. The retry path for batched requests is not safe the way single requests are. failed_ids are retried individually afterward (good), but a transient whole-batch 429 mixed with the backoff retry can double-process or drop. Lower confidence pending the client's exact re-exec behavior.

findings.title: Reverse label-id->name map silently drops labels created after connect() (cache populated only at connect), under-reporting message.labels
findings.description: _init_label_cache runs once at connect() and the reverse lookup in get_message_details/_parse_message_response (196-202, 280-284) maps labelIds back to names via the cache. Labels created during the run (by ensure_label_exists, which DOES add to cache) are covered, but labels created out-of-band by another client, or Gmail system labels not pre-seeded (CATEGORY_*, IMPORTANT, CHAT), are absent from the cache and silently omitted from EmailMessage.labels. Since categorization and the protected-skip logic in cli.py can branch on msg.labels, a message carrying an unknown label id will appear to have FEWER labels than it does. Round 1 (552) noted the O(n*m) cost and silent drop generically; the NEW severity angle is that system/category label ids (e.g. CATEGORY_PROMOTIONS) are never in the user-label cache, so they are always dropped from labels — affecting any label-based skip/keep logic.

findings.location: apply_actions lines 459-466 (lambda over loop variable body)
findings.title: batchModify lambda captures loop-mutated 'body' by closure — safe today but a latent footgun if execution is ever deferred
findings.description: Inside the chunk loop, body is rebuilt each iteration and passed to _execute_with_backoff via lambda: self._service...batchModify(body=body). The lambda closes over body by reference. It is currently safe because _execute_with_backoff invokes the callable synchronously within the same iteration before body is reassigned. However, _execute_with_backoff retries by re-calling the same lambda; if any future change makes execution lazy/deferred or collects these lambdas, every call would use the last body. This is a fragile pattern on the destructive batchModify path; binding body as a default arg (lambda b=body: ...) would remove the hazard.

findings.title: INBOX 'left_inbox' detection upper-cases the cached INBOX id but compares against remove_ids that were built from raw label-cache ids, so a non-literal INBOX id mismatch can hide a real archive from the audit
findings.description: left_inbox is computed as `any(str(rid).upper() in _inbox_forms for rid in remove_ids)` where _inbox_forms={'INBOX','\INBOX',inbox_id.upper()}. remove_ids includes the literal 'INBOX' appended for action.archive (line 417) so the common path works. But remove_label ids come from `self._label_cache.get(label) or label`; if a label happened to alias to the INBOX id under a different string, or if Gmail ever returns a non-'INBOX' system id, the uppercase set-membership could miss it — and the comment explicitly worries about this. The detection is heuristic string-matching rather than comparing resolved ids, so the audit's archived flag for Gmail rests on the INBOX id being exactly the literal string.

findings.title: Read/starred status via substring match '\\Seen'/'\\Flagged' in raw FLAGS string can false-positive on label/user-flag names
findings.description: `is_read = "\\Seen" in raw_str` and `"\\Flagged" in raw_str` do a naive substring test over the entire raw FLAGS response string (which also contains the UID and command echo). A user keyword/label that contains the substring 'Seen' or 'Flagged' (e.g. a custom flag 'NotSeen', or a Gmail label 'Flagged-Later') would set is_read/is_starred incorrectly. Should parse the FLAGS list and match tokens exactly.

findings.title: 1Password subprocess uses check_output with broad except; password failures swallowed, and op CLI arg injection via env-controlled item name
findings.description: _load_password runs `op item get {op_item} --account {op_account} --field={op_field}` via subprocess (list form, so no shell injection), but: (a) the broad `except Exception` swallows ALL errors (including the op binary missing) and merely warns, then falls through to raise ValueError — losing the real cause; (b) op_item/op_field come from env and are passed as argv — if op_field is attacker-controlled it could inject additional op flags (argv-level option injection, e.g. op_field starting with '--'), not shell injection but flag injection. Lower severity since env is user-controlled, but worth flagging.

findings.title: Password potentially returned as None type leading to confusing login failure
findings.description: `if os.getenv("IMAP_PASS"): return os.getenv("IMAP_PASS")` calls getenv twice (minor TOCTOU/redundant), and if IMAP_PASS is set to empty string the truthiness check skips it and falls through to 1Password/ValueError — an explicitly-set empty password env var is treated as unset. Minor edge case.

findings.title: Methods dereference self._connection without verifying connect() ran -> AttributeError if used outside context manager
findings.description: _select_mailbox, get_message_details, apply_label, etc. all call self._connection.uid(...) directly. If a caller invokes these without entering the context manager / calling connect(), self._connection is None and raises AttributeError('NoneType' has no attribute 'uid') rather than a clear 'not connected' error. connect() guards via __enter__, but the methods themselves have no precondition check (unlike list_messages which calls _select_mailbox which would still NPE). Robustness/observability issue.

findings.title: logout() may leave socket open on certain server errors; no close()/shutdown fallback
findings.description: disconnect calls logout() inside try/except and sets _connection=None in finally, but if logout() raises (server already dropped the connection) the underlying SSL socket may not be closed (imaplib's logout both sends LOGOUT and shutscloses; on error the socket can leak). A belt-and-suspenders self._connection.shutdown()/close() in the except path would prevent FD leaks across many connect/disconnect cycles. Minor resource-leak risk.

findings.title: UID decoded to str for EmailMessage.id but downstream STORE/FETCH pass it back as str — fine, but no validation that data[0] split tokens are UIDs
findings.description: `data[0].split()` splits the raw search response bytes on whitespace into UID byte tokens. If the server prepends anything or returns an empty-but-nonNone data[0]=b'' the `if data[0]` guard handles empty. Edge: some servers return UIDs space-separated which is handled, but a server returning a multi-element data list (data[1:] ignored) could drop UIDs on servers that chunk the response. Low-confidence pagination/completeness note.

findings.title: Broad except Exception when loading IMAP password swallows real errors and falls through
findings.description: The 1Password load is wrapped in `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...)` then falls through to raise a generic ValueError. This masks distinguishable failures (op not installed vs auth-required vs item-not-found) behind a warning, making misconfiguration hard to diagnose, and continues as if no password source existed. Narrow the except and surface actionable errors.

findings.title: IMAP write operations assume the correct mailbox is selected, but apply_actions issues UID STOREs without re-selecting INBOX
findings.description: UID-based STORE/COPY operations act against the currently selected mailbox. _select_mailbox is only called from list_messages. In the run_labeler flow apply_actions runs after listing, so INBOX is usually still selected; but if any path lists a non-INBOX mailbox (list_messages accepts a mailbox arg) and then applies actions, the UIDs (which are mailbox-scoped) are interpreted against the wrong mailbox — UID validity is per-mailbox/UIDVALIDITY, so a STORE could target an unrelated message or fail. There is no assertion that the mailbox the UIDs came from is still selected before mutation.

findings.title: is_read/is_starred parsed from raw AppleScript boolean string is fragile to localization/format
findings.description: `is_read.lower() == "true"` and `is_flagged.lower() == "true"` assume `(msgRead as string)` yields exactly 'true'/'false'. AppleScript boolean coercion gives 'true'/'false' in English locales, but this is locale/version-sensitive; any other rendering (e.g. capitalized, localized) silently coerces to False, mis-marking read/flagged status. Low confidence but worth noting.

findings.title: Mail.app launch uses fixed `delay 2` race; subsequent operations may run before Mail is ready
findings.description: connect() activates Mail and waits a hardcoded `delay 2` seconds, then returns 'ok'. On a slow/cold start Mail.app may not have finished launching/loading accounts within 2s, so the first list_messages/move can fail. This is a timing race with no readiness check (e.g. polling that accounts are loaded).

findings.title: Pagination next_token off-by-one boundary when total is exactly a multiple of limit
findings.description: `next_token = str(next_offset) if next_offset < total else None`. With total==200, limit==100: page1 start=0 returns items 1..100, next_offset=100, 100<200 -> token '100'. page2 start=100 returns items 101..200, next_offset=200, 200<200 false -> stops. Correct here. But when total grows between calls (messages arrive), offsets drift and messages can be skipped or duplicated; more importantly the loop `repeat with i from start+1 to start+limit` with `if i > totalMsgs then exit repeat` uses the totalMsgs captured at page time, while the python next-token uses the python-side `total` from the same call — consistent within a page but not across concurrent mailbox mutation. Edge-case correctness note.

findings.title: AppleScript repeat upper bound uses string-interpolated arithmetic that breaks for non-int page_token
findings.description: `int(page_token) if page_token else 0` will raise ValueError if a non-numeric page_token is ever supplied (it shouldn't be from this provider's own tokens, but tokens are opaque strings and could be crossed from another provider's result in a misuse scenario). The exception is unhandled in list_messages (the try only wraps _run_applescript), so a bad token crashes rather than returning empty. Same applies to imap.py list_messages line 192 `int(page_token)`.

findings.title: Mail.app list pagination builds an O(N) AppleScript that re-indexes 'item i of allMsgs' and uses unescaped account name
findings.description: The list script interpolates self.account into 'of account "{account}"' unescaped (injection, same class as the id/label finding). Functionally, the repeat 'from {start_offset+1} to ({start_offset}+{limit})' indexes 'item i of allMsgs' each iteration; AppleScript item-access on a large message list is O(N) per access, so listing deep offsets is quadratic and the 30s osascript timeout (_run_applescript) will fire on large inboxes, causing list_messages to return an empty result (caught at 173-175) — silently truncating processing with no error to the caller. total parsing 'int(line.replace(...))' will raise ValueError (uncaught) if the AppleScript output is malformed/truncated by the timeout partial output.

findings.location: lines 191-228 (list_messages/get_message_details build EmailMessage with no date) cross-ref line 293-294 star ignores due_date
findings.title: Mail.app provider returns dates in local zone semantics nowhere normalized; combined with calculate_email_age_hours assume-UTC, any future date population would be tz-wrong
findings.description: Mail.app's AppleScript surface deals in the user's local time zone (Mail.app `date received` is local). The provider currently populates no date at all (round 1 flagged the escalate no-op). The deeper latent defect: if/when date is wired in via AppleScript it will be a naive local datetime, which calculate_email_age_hours (core/rules.py:1180-1182) will mislabel as UTC — there is no conversion layer between AppleScript local time and the UTC-assuming age math. Flagging the missing normalization seam so the eventual date wiring does not silently inherit the tz bug.

findings.title: Archive uses literal destinationId 'archive'; if the well-known alias is not accepted the message stays in inbox and only logs an error
findings.description: archive() POSTs /move with destinationId='archive' (548). Microsoft Graph's well-known folder name for archive is 'archive', which is generally accepted, but for some account types/locales the well-known name may not resolve and the move 400s. The failure is caught (553-554) and only logged, returning False. In apply_actions (base.py 353-355) a False from archive() means did_leave_inbox stays False — so the audit records the message as NOT archived even though the intent was to archive, and the message silently remains in the inbox with no retry. Worth verifying 'archive' resolves for consumer (Outlook.com/MSA) accounts specifically.

findings.title: Category read-modify-write PATCH is non-atomic and can lose concurrent category changes
findings.description: remove_category and apply_category both GET the full categories array and PATCH the modified array. If two operations (or two overlapping CLI runs / the launchd job overlapping a manual run) target the same message, the second PATCH overwrites the first's changes (lost update). There is no ETag/If-Match concurrency control on the PATCH (Graph supports optimistic concurrency via If-Match). Metadata-only, so severity is low, but it is a genuine race on shared mailbox state.

findings.title: Direct dict indexing msg['id'] in list_messages will KeyError if Graph omits id; not defensively handled like other fields
findings.description: In list_messages the EmailMessage is built with id=msg['id'] (461) using hard indexing, while every other field uses msg.get(...) with a default. The $select includes id so it normally returns, but if Graph returns a malformed/partial item (or a delta-style item without id), msg['id'] raises KeyError, which is NOT inside the try at 436-440 (that try only wraps the _api_get) — it is in the parsing loop, so it propagates uncaught and aborts the whole list_messages call. Same pattern in _init_folder_cache (255: folder['displayName'], folder['id']) and _fetch_child_folders. Inconsistent defensive handling.

findings.title: requests.Session never closed on the no-connect / health-check error paths; potential socket leak across repeated provider instantiation
findings.description: The session is closed only in disconnect() (243-245), which runs via the context manager __exit__. If code creates an OutlookProvider and calls _api_*/_get_session without using the context manager (or if connect() raises after _get_session is first invoked), disconnect() may not run and the underlying urllib3 connection pool is not closed. Minor in practice (GC reclaims it) but a resource-cleanup gap, especially if many short-lived providers are created (e.g. per-account loops).

findings.title: Interactive token result accessed without None guard; acquire_token_interactive returning None would raise TypeError instead of clean error
findings.description: After acquire_token_interactive (184), the code does `if 'access_token' not in result` (189) and result.get(...) (190) assuming result is a dict. MSAL normally returns a dict, but on certain failures/cancellations the contract should still be validated; if result is ever None (e.g. an internal MSAL path or a mocked test), `'access_token' not in None` raises TypeError, masking the real 'auth failed' condition with a confusing error. Defensive None-check is absent (unlike the silent path which guards `if result and ...`).

findings.title: Folder cache stores only displayName; well-known folders (Inbox, Archive, Sent) keyed by localized displayName can mismatch move targets
findings.description: _folder_cache keys are folder displayName (255). For consumer accounts the inbox/archive display names can be localized or differ from the well-known aliases ('inbox','archive') used in apply_label fallback (426) and archive() (548). A message's parentFolderId resolving to displayName 'Posteingang' (German inbox) won't match anything callers expect by the literal 'inbox', and ensure_label_exists could try to create a folder named after a localized well-known folder. Edge case but a real i18n correctness risk.

findings.title: Category create error is logged at debug and masked as 'race condition'; a real failure raises a generic RuntimeError losing the original cause
findings.description: On create failure it logs at DEBUG ('Category create for {name}: {e}'), re-inits the cache, and if still absent raises RuntimeError(f'Failed to create category: {name}') WITHOUT chaining the original exception (no 'from e'). So a genuine permission/throttling failure is hidden at debug level and surfaced as an opaque RuntimeError with no cause, making diagnosis hard. The 'race condition' comment assumes the only failure mode is concurrent-create, which is not guaranteed.

findings.title: requests.Session leaked on all error paths (no context manager / try-finally)
findings.description: The HTTP session is created lazily and only closed in disconnect(). Many internal flows construct the session and then call response.raise_for_status(); if an HTTPError/ConnectionError propagates and the caller never reaches disconnect() (e.g. connect() raises during _init_folder_cache before the with-block sets up, or any direct use without the context manager), the Session and its underlying urllib3 connection pool are never closed -> socket/fd leak. Also the Session's Authorization header embeds the bearer token and lives for the object lifetime. Recommend try/finally or closing on connect failure.

findings.title: OData filter injection via folder name single-quote
findings.description: When a folder create fails, the fallback search builds `params={'$filter': f"displayName eq '{part}'"}`. `part` is a path segment of the label. A label segment containing a single quote (e.g. `O'Brien`) breaks the OData string literal (OData escapes ' by doubling it) producing a malformed filter -> request error, or with crafted input could alter the filter semantics. Currently labels are a fixed taxonomy so practical risk is low, but the code accepts arbitrary labels. Escape single quotes (double them) or use a parameterized approach.

findings.location: list_messages lines 417-420 (page_token used as full URL)
findings.title: Pagination follows server-provided @odata.nextLink as the request URL (open-redirect / SSRF-shaped trust)
findings.description: When page_token is set, list_messages uses it directly as the request URL: `url = page_token`. page_token is the @odata.nextLink returned by Graph. If a malicious/compromised intermediary or a future code path supplies an attacker-controlled page_token, the authenticated session (which attaches the Bearer token via session.headers) would send the OAuth token to that arbitrary URL — a token-exfiltration/SSRF vector. Graph nextLinks are normally trusted, but blindly GETting a caller/response-supplied absolute URL with credentials attached is risky; validate the host is graph.microsoft.com before following.

findings.title: Unanchored .replace("Z", ...) on the ISO timestamp corrupts any string containing a stray 'Z'
findings.description: `.replace("Z", "+00:00")` is a global substring replace, not an end-anchor. Graph normally returns the Z only as the trailing UTC designator, but the replace would also rewrite any other literal 'Z' in the string (e.g. a malformed/spoofed value), producing a garbage ISO string and a swallowed ValueError -> date dropped. The robust form is `.rstrip()` + checking/stripping a trailing 'Z'. Minor/edge but a real correctness smell distinct from the precision issue.

findings.location: ensure_label_exists, lines 641-646 ($filter displayName eq '{part}' lookup) and 658-664 in __init__ folder cache by displayName
findings.title: Folder lookup by displayName eq '{part}' returns the FIRST match across the whole mailbox, ignoring parent scope when parent_id is None
findings.description: In the create-conflict fallback, when parent_id is None the find query is GRAPH_API_FOLDERS with $filter=displayName eq '{part}' — this searches ALL top-level folders and takes value[0]. If two top-level folders share a displayName (Graph allows duplicate displayNames), or a localized well-known folder collides, the wrong folder id is cached and subsequent moves target the wrong folder. When parent_id is set the search is correctly scoped to childFolders, so the bug is the top-level (parent_id is None) branch silently binding to an arbitrary first match. Round 1 (627/641) flagged the fallback returning the raw label and the OData single-quote injection, but not the unscoped first-match selection across duplicate-named top-level folders.

findings.title: Folder names containing a literal '/' collide with the hierarchical path separator, corrupting the folder cache and routing moves to the wrong folder
findings.description: The folder cache keys hierarchical folders by joining displayNames with '/' (line 270: full_name = f"{parent_name}/{folder['displayName']}"). Outlook permits a literal '/' inside a folder displayName. A top-level folder literally named 'Work/Dev' and a real hierarchy Work > Dev both map to cache key 'Work/Dev', so one overwrites the other. ensure_label_exists then splits the requested label on '/' (line 607) and will resolve/create against the wrong node, moving messages into the wrong physical folder. This separator-collision is distinct from round 1's recursion/duplicate-name note (636); the specific defect is that the '/' separator is not escaped or distinguished from '/' inside a displayName.

findings.location: apply_category, lines 346-356 (de-dupe is case- and whitespace-sensitive)
findings.title: Category de-dupe is exact-string; Graph treats category names case-insensitively, so repeated runs append duplicate-by-case categories
findings.description: apply_category fetches current categories and does `if category not in current_cats: current_cats.append(category)`. Graph master categories are matched by displayName but the membership test here is exact case-sensitive Python `in`. If a category was previously applied as 'Critical' and a later run requests 'critical' (or the tier_config name casing differs from what Graph stored/normalized), the membership check misses and a duplicate-by-case entry is appended, and ensure_category_exists may also attempt to create a colliding master category. Over repeated escalation runs this bloats the message's category list. Not reported in round 1 (which covered the read-failure race, not the case-sensitive de-dupe).

findings.title: Archive move and folder move share no idempotency/verification — re-running archive on an already-archived message issues a redundant move and a failed move is only logged
findings.description: archive() POSTs /move with destinationId='archive' unconditionally; there is no check whether the message is already in Archive, so re-processing (e.g. on resume after a crash, or escalate re-runs) issues redundant moves. More importantly, the only success signal is _api_post not throwing; if the well-known 'archive' alias is not honored (round 1 #652) the broad except returns False and the message silently stays in inbox. Combined with the lack of any read-back verification, the archive operation is not idempotent and not verified — there is no guard analogous to apply_category's 'if category not in current_cats'. NEW idempotency angle for the move/archive mutation specifically.

findings.title: Folder-name attribution is ambiguous: reverse lookup of parentFolderId returns the first cache entry, mis-naming same-named child folders
findings.description: _folder_cache maps full hierarchical path -> folder id, but get_message_details reverse-maps parentFolderId -> name by scanning the cache and taking the FIRST name whose id matches. If two distinct folders share the same Graph folder id collision is impossible, but the inverse problem is real: the cache stores BOTH the leaf displayName collisions and full paths, and child folders with identical displayName under different parents each get a distinct full-path key, yet the reverse scan returns whichever path dict-iteration hits first. The resulting labels set on EmailMessage can attribute a message to the wrong folder path, feeding categorization/audit with an incorrect current-folder. Edge-case, but wrong results.

findings.title: Gmail batch label-get with no error surfacing and no batch-size chunking — silently drops failed/over-limit entries
findings.description: recount builds one batch over ALL relevant labels with a callback that appends only on success and ignores exceptions entirely (no else/log). Gmail batch requests are capped (100 per batch); a taxonomy with >100 labels exceeds the limit and the tail is silently lost, and any per-label error vanishes, so the printed totals can be wrong with zero indication. Reporting-only script, low impact.

findings.location: lines 1-15 (whole blueprint; envVars only sets PYTHONUNBUFFERED)
findings.title: render.yaml declares no Python/runtime version and no dependency install step, fully delegating reproducibility to the unpinned Dockerfile base `python:3.11-slim`
findings.description: render.yaml uses `runtime: docker`, so all dependency resolution is inherited from the Dockerfile, whose base is `python:3.11-slim` (a moving tag — not digest-pinned). Combined with the unbounded requirements floors, the blueprint provides zero reproducibility guarantees: a rebuild can pick up a new 3.11.x patch AND new transitive dep majors. render.yaml also sets `autoDeploy: true`, so this drift ships automatically on every push with no human gate. For a billing+auth service this is a config/reproducibility weakness — the base image should be digest-pinned and deps hash-locked.

findings.title: ACP charge path depends on a Stripe PREVIEW API surface (Shared Payment Tokens) only loosely tied to the `stripe>=15.2,<16` SDK pin
findings.description: acp/payment.py pins a preview Stripe API version `2026-04-22.preview` (STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION) to charge Shared Payment Tokens, and requirements-api.txt pins `stripe>=15.2,<16`. Preview API surfaces are explicitly unstable and the SDK's preview-request mechanics (how `stripe_version`/preview headers are passed to `PaymentIntent.create`) can change across 15.x minors. The `<16` ceiling guards the major break, but ANY 15.x minor that alters preview handling — within the allowed range — can break the live charge path with no code change, surfacing as the round-1-noted generic 'charge failed' (acp/payment.py charge except). A money-moving path bound to a preview API plus a floating minor floor is a fragile dependency contract; it should be pinned to an exact tested stripe version.

findings.title: stripe major pinned <16 — billing code uses StripeClient/v1 namespace; verify floor matches the API surface used
findings.description: billing.py uses the newer stripe.StripeClient(key) + client.v1.checkout.sessions.create / client.v1.billing_portal.sessions.create resource-namespaced API (L71, L139, L158) and stripe.Webhook.construct_event / stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError (L182-183). The StripeClient + .v1.* service pattern is a relatively recent addition to the Python SDK; the >=15.2 floor likely covers it, but the lower bound is load-bearing and is not exercised by CI (CI installs the latest <16). If a deployer resolves to exactly 15.2 and that release predates the .v1.* service layout, checkout/portal calls would AttributeError at runtime (caught by the broad except -> 502). Recommend confirming 15.2 actually exposes client.v1.* or raising the floor. Lower severity because <16 generally resolves to a recent release.

findings.title: iCloud credentials passed as inline env assignments to python (visible in process list and unquoted expansion)
findings.description: The iCloud step sets IMAP_HOST/IMAP_USER/IMAP_PASS as inline command-prefix env assignments to the python invocation. With `set -euo pipefail` and unset ICLOUD_* vars this would error (good), but the IMAP_PASS value becomes part of the child process environment which is readable via /proc/<pid>/environ by the same user and may appear in audit logs. Sourcing into env vars at all (vs. letting the python read 1Password directly) widens secret exposure. Lower severity since it's same-user, but it is a secret-handling smell.

findings.title: Invalid-item test relies on Pydantic default quantity but does not assert charge is blocked
findings.description: The test posts {'id':'no_such_pack'} (no quantity, defaults to 1 via the Item model) and asserts status not_ready_for_payment + code invalid_items. It verifies the shaping but not the downstream invariant that a not_ready session cannot be completed/charged (complete_session raises invalid_state for non-ready, but no test drives complete on a not_ready session built from an invalid item). The 'charging for nothing' risk that build_line_items' valid=False exists to prevent is only half-covered: the status flag is asserted, the actual completion-block is not exercised from this entry point.

findings.title: Unwritable-audit tests rely on directory-as-path raising, an OS/behavior-specific trigger
findings.description: Both robustness tests create a directory and pass it as the audit path, expecting open(path,'a') to raise OSError so the log degrades to memory. _append only catches OSError (core/audit.py). On most POSIX systems open(dir,'a') raises IsADirectoryError (an OSError subclass) so this works, but the trigger is platform/behavior-dependent and the test does not assert the specific exception class — it infers degradation from write_error being non-None. If a platform allowed the open or raised a non-OSError, the test would either falsely pass (write_error still None but no record raise) or the uncaught exception would propagate from record(). Brittle trigger for a safety-critical degradation path; a more explicit fault injection (monkeypatch open to raise) would be more robust.

findings.title: current_period_end set in the test event is never asserted on the resulting account
findings.description: The test injects current_period_end=1893456000 into the subscription event and the handler passes it through _period_end -> store.set_subscription, but the assertions only check plan/status/stripe_customer_id. The period-end propagation (a field that gates entitlement expiry) is silently unverified despite being part of the event the test constructs. _period_end has non-trivial logic (top-level vs items[0] fallback, int() coercion) that no test asserts the output of. Minor, but the test sets up data it then ignores.

findings.title: Invalid-YAML test asserts silent {} fallback as correct, masking a swallow-all error path
findings.description: load_yaml_config on malformed YAML returns {} and the test cements this as desired behavior. This is the same silent-fallback pattern flagged elsewhere as risky: a user with a typo'd config gets default behavior with no error surfaced, and the test guarantees no exception/warning is asserted. No test verifies that a warning is logged or that the failure is observable. As written the test would keep passing even if the function swallowed, say, a permissions error or a partially-parsed dangerous config. Worth pairing with an assertion that the failure is logged.

findings.title: Mail.app star test mocks _run_applescript and so never exercises AppleScript message_id interpolation/injection surface
findings.description: The test monkeypatches provider._run_applescript to a capture lambda and asserts the script contains 'flagged status'. The real star() interpolates message_id directly into the AppleScript via f-string (providers/mailapp.py:297 'whose id is {message_id}'). The test uses a numeric '123' so it never probes a message_id containing AppleScript metacharacters/quotes (an injection-shaped input). Because _run_applescript is fully mocked, neither the escaping behavior nor the actual execution is under test — the test confirms the template string only. Coverage gap on a string-interpolation-into-a-scripting-language surface.

findings.title: Frozen-dataclass tests use try/except + assert False idiom that misclassifies non-AttributeError outcomes
findings.description: Both tests do: try: obj.field='x'; assert False, '...'; except AttributeError: pass. The pattern is correct for the happy path (FrozenInstanceError subclasses AttributeError), but it is brittle test hygiene: pytest.raises(AttributeError) (or dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError) is the idiomatic, self-documenting form. As written, if the assignment ever succeeded silently the 'assert False' would fire (good), but the assertion message is swallowed unless AttributeError is NOT raised — the test conflates 'mutation allowed' with 'wrong exception type' and relies on FrozenInstanceError being an AttributeError subclass (an implementation detail). Low impact but worth tightening to pytest.raises.

findings.title: Escalation boundary values (exactly 24h and exactly 72h) are untested
findings.description: escalate_by_age has branch boundaries at 24 (if email_age_hours < 24 / elif 24 <= ... < 72) and 72 (if >= 72). Tests use 12, 48, 50, 80, 100 — none hit the exact boundaries 24.0 or 72.0. An off-by-one flip of < to <= (or >= to >) at either boundary would not be caught. Given escalation drives whether stale mail gets re-prioritized, the missing boundary cases are a small but real coverage gap.

findings.title: Plan run-cap renders 'NaN' if the API returns a non-numeric monthly_run_cap
findings.description: Both the live and fallback plan renderers compute `${Number(p.monthly_run_cap).toLocaleString()} runs / mo` after only checking `== null` for the Unlimited case. If /v1/billing/plans returns monthly_run_cap as a non-numeric string (or the value is undefined rather than null — undefined != null is false so it passes the null check... actually `== null` catches undefined too), Number() yields NaN and the card shows 'NaN runs / mo'. The pricing surface would display a broken cap rather than failing gracefully.

findings.title: Lint and type-check steps are advisory-only and can never fail the build (assertion-that-cannot-fail smell)
findings.description: Both the ruff and mypy steps explicitly `set +e`, capture status, and at most emit `::warning::` to the step summary — they never propagate a non-zero exit.
