The comment states 'pytest remains the hard gate', so this is intentional, but it means lint/type regressions never block merges; the steps are effectively non-gating decoration. Worth flagging because a reviewer may assume these gate the PR. (Coverage upload also has fail_ci_if_error: false at line 74, so coverage problems are silent too.)

findings.title: Committed discovery artifacts are byte-in-sync with their generators (verified, no drift)
findings.description: Positive verification. I regenerated agent.json (build_agent_manifest), llms.txt (build_llms_txt) and product_feed.json (build_feed) at base_url https://mail.example.com using the in-repo builders and compared byte-for-byte to the committed files: all three reported IN SYNC. The manifest endpoints it advertises (/v1/billing/plans, /v1/audit/{run_id}, /v1/senders/check, /v1/triage/preview, /acp/checkout_sessions, /acp/feed.json, /mcp, /openapi.json) all map to real routes (api/billing.py:94, api/receipts.py:114, api/app.py:79/85/91, acp/router.py:166, acp/feed.py:67, api/app.py:143 mount, FastAPI auto /openapi.json). No endpoint drift found. Reported as info to record that this surface was checked and is clean.

findings.title: Bare `except:` in extract_domain swallows all exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt
findings.description: `extract_domain` uses bare `except:` returning 'unknown'. Same anti-pattern as auto_drain.py — masks any error and can swallow KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit. The split logic can't raise, so it's dead, but the bare except is a code smell repeated across scripts.

findings.title: Local protected-senders file with real PII exists on disk and is correctly gitignored / never committed (verified clean)
findings.description: Positive verification (no leak). config/protected_senders.local.txt contains substantial real protected-sender PII across legal, financial, government, and self-mailbox entries; specific values redacted for public review artifact safety. I confirmed via git ls-files and git log --all that this file is NOT tracked and has NEVER been in history; git check-ignore shows it is matched by .gitignore:8. The loader core/rules.py:_load_local_protected (line 583) reads it correctly and the format parses cleanly (35 domains + 1 self). This is the intended design working as documented. Reported as info so the downstream filter can confirm the privacy boundary holds and does not regress (e.g. if someone force-adds it).

findings.title: Scope assumption wrong: create_smart_mailboxes.scpt is plain UTF-8 AppleScript source, NOT a compiled binary — it IS reviewable (and unreviewed)
findings.description: The review scope stated this file is a compiled binary AppleScript that cannot be source-reviewed and should be logged as a coverage gap. Verification shows otherwise: 'file create_smart_mailboxes.scpt' reports 'Unicode text, UTF-8 text' and the first bytes are literal source ('tell application "Mail" -- Create a group for our automation tell ...'). It is 27 lines of readable AppleScript creating smart mailboxes (conditions reference mailbox 'Dev' and message-is-unread). So there is no binary-blob coverage gap; rather, this AppleScript is unreferenced by deploy.sh/run_automation.sh and is not exercised by the launchd path, and was not source-reviewed in earlier rounds. Flagging both the corrected scope fact and the residual gap: nothing in the scheduling pipeline invokes this script, and its smart-mailbox conditions (only 'Dev' + unread) look like an incomplete/placeholder rule set.

findings.location: imports line 23 (re) and line 22 (email) unused; stats dict not defaultdict
findings.title: Minor: unused imports and re-decode of UID per fetch
findings.description: imap_rules.py imports `re` (line 23) which is unused (categorization is delegated to core). fetch_headers and the loop call uid.decode() twice (lines 150 and 156) for the same uid. These are cosmetic but indicate the file was copied/edited without cleanup; flagging for coverage.

findings.location: lines 6-7 (docstring: 'The heavy mcp SDK is imported lazily by mcp_server.server')
findings.title: mcp_server package docstring misstates lazy-import behavior — server.py imports the mcp SDK eagerly at module top level
findings.description: The __init__ docstring asserts the heavy `mcp` SDK 'is imported lazily by :mod:`mcp_server.server`'. In fact mcp_server/server.py imports it eagerly at module top level: line 31 `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP` and line 32 `from mcp.server.streamable_http import TransportSecuritySettings`. Importing mcp_server.server therefore pulls the full SDK immediately (and transitively api.service -> cli with its basicConfig). The docstring's only true claim is that importing the *package* (__init__ alone) is cheap because __init__ does not import server. The 'lazily by server' wording is incorrect and could mislead someone reasoning about the 3.9 import-safety story. Fix: correct the docstring (the lazy boundary is api/app.py's try/except import of server, not server itself).

findings.title: Dead/confusing config: module-level DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID reads env but __init__ re-reads the same env, so DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID is never used
findings.description: DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID = os.getenv('OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID', '') (63) is computed at import time but __init__ does self.client_id = client_id or os.getenv('OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID') (120), re-reading the env directly and never referencing DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID. The constant is dead code. Worse, it is misleading: a reader might assume the default is wired up. If env is set after import (unlikely but possible in tests), the two would diverge. Cosmetic/maintainability.

findings.title: Unused module constant GRAPH_API_MESSAGES (inbox-only) — never referenced; list_messages builds its own URL
findings.description: GRAPH_API_MESSAGES = f'{GRAPH_API_BASE}/me/mailFolders/inbox/messages' (25) is defined but never used anywhere in the module (list_messages constructs urls from GRAPH_API_FOLDERS / mailFolders/{folder}/messages). Dead constant; also bakes in 'inbox' which could mislead. Cosmetic.

findings.title: fastapi/uvicorn/pydantic/stripe ARE properly declared (false-positive killer)
findings.description: Reconciliation: requirements-api.txt explicitly declares fastapi>=0.110 (L3), uvicorn[standard]>=0.29 (L4), httpx>=0.27 (L6), pydantic>=2.0 (L8), and stripe>=15.2,<16 (L13). The deployed api/ and acp/ code imports only pydantic, fastapi, and (lazily) stripe. The Dockerfile (L9-10) installs requirements.txt + requirements-api.txt + requirements-mcp.txt, and the CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml installs the same three). Therefore any earlier claim that 'fastapi/stripe/uvicorn/pydantic is an undeclared dependency' is a FALSE POSITIVE and should be dropped by the downstream filter.

findings.title: mcp SDK IS properly declared (false-positive killer)
findings.description: Reconciliation: requirements-mcp.txt declares mcp>=1.2,<2. mcp_server/server.py imports mcp.server.fastmcp and mcp.server.streamable_http; api/app.py imports it lazily inside a try/except and degrades gracefully if absent. Both the Dockerfile (Python 3.11) and CI install requirements-mcp.txt, so the 'mcp' dependency for the hosted /mcp endpoint is genuinely declared and installed. Any claim that 'mcp is an undeclared import' is a FALSE POSITIVE.

findings.title: Core deps (google-api-python-client, msal, requests, pyyaml) ARE declared
findings.description: Reconciliation: the core/provider runtime deps are declared in requirements.txt — google-api-python-client>=2.0.0, google-auth-httplib2, google-auth-oauthlib (Gmail), msal>=1.25.0 + requests>=2.28.0 (Outlook/Graph), pyyaml>=6.0 (YAML config). cli.py / providers / core import these. No undeclared core runtime dep. Drop any 'requests/msal/pyyaml undeclared' false positives.

findings.location: lines 52-53 (a { color: var(--green); } a { color: var(--accent); })
findings.title: Duplicate conflicting anchor color rule (dead CSS)
findings.description: Two consecutive `a {}` rules set color to var(--green) then var(--accent); the second always wins, making line 52 dead. Both currently resolve to the same hex (#0f766e), so the effect is invisible today, but the redundant pair is a maintenance hazard: changing --green will not affect link color despite the apparent intent, and a future de-dup of the accent/green tokens could silently shift link color.

findings.title: Smoke test asserts '"status":"ok"' as an exact substring, which is brittle against worker JSON.stringify spacing
findings.description: Line 113 greps the /health response for the literal `'"status":"ok"'` (no spaces). The worker builds this via `JSON.stringify({status:"ok",...})` which emits no spaces, so it passes today — but it is coupled to serializer formatting rather than parsing JSON. If /health were ever served by the FastAPI surface (which Pydantic/Starlette may serialize differently) or pretty-printed, the grep would fail despite a valid healthy response. Minor brittleness; prefer `jq` over substring grep on JSON.

findings.title: DB path default read from environment at module import time
findings.description: DEFAULT_DB_PATH is computed at import (line 39) from os.environ. get_store() at line 451 then reads os.environ.get('MAIL_DB_PATH', DEFAULT_DB_PATH) again, so the live env is honored for the override but the *fallback* default is captured at import. The practical effect is benign here (the literal 'data/app.db' fallback never changes), but it is a module-level env read at import — the documented anti-pattern for this review — and is redundant with the runtime read in get_store(). It also means the default is evaluated before any test or deploy that mutates the environment post-import has a chance to influence it. Fix: drop the import-time env read; compute the path entirely inside get_store().

findings.location: drain_loop line 180 print(...end='\r') interleaved with logging
findings.title: print with carriage-return progress interleaves badly with logging to stdout/file
findings.description: Progress is shown via `print(f'...', end='\r')` (line 180) while the logger also writes to StreamHandler (stdout) and a file. The \r progress lines and logger lines interleave and the \r control char is written to the log file via the console too, producing garbled logs. Cosmetic/observability.

findings.title: Worker /health version is hardcoded '0.1.0' and can drift from the API's __version__
findings.description: The worker returns `version: "0.1.0"` as a string literal at line 247. The real API returns `__version__` from api/__init__. If the package version is bumped, the worker's reported version silently stays 0.1.0, so the share-demo /health misreports the deployed version. Low severity, but it is an independent hardcoded constant that no test or build step keeps in sync with the Python package.

findings.location: calculate_email_age_hours line 1163 signature `Optional["datetime"]`; import inside body line 1176
findings.title: datetime type annotation is an unresolved forward-ref string and import is function-local
findings.description: The parameter is annotated Optional["datetime"] but `datetime` is never imported at module scope (only inside the function body, lines 1176). The string forward-ref will not resolve if anything calls typing.get_type_hints() on this function, and it relies on the runtime import each call. Cosmetic/maintainability, but the annotation is effectively broken for introspection and tooling.

findings.title: Group-creation failure silently swallowed by bare try
findings.description: make new mailbox {name:'Ω Automation'} is wrapped in try…end try with no error handler or logging; any failure other than already-exists is silently ignored, with no signal to the operator.

findings.title: Redundant unpinned pip install of msal/requests duplicates pinned requirements.txt entries
findings.description: Line 32 runs `pip install msal requests -q` separately, but both are already declared with version pins in requirements.txt (msal>=1.25.0, requests>=2.28.0) and installed at line 31. The separate unpinned install can resolve to a different (newer) version than the pin intends and is redundant. Minor, but it can mask the pin and slow the deploy.

findings.location: lines 19-26 packages_distributions shim; line 12 unused 'import re' is used; line 14 socket
findings.title: Dead/likely-unnecessary importlib_metadata shim with bare-except swallow
findings.description: Lines 19-26 monkeypatch importlib_metadata.packages_distributions for Python <3.10, importing a backport inside a try/except that swallows all exceptions (line 25). packages_distributions is never actually called anywhere in this module (grep shows no usage), so the entire shim is dead code. The bare `except Exception: pass` also hides any real import error. Cosmetic but flagged for coverage; it suggests copy-paste cruft.

findings.title: resultSizeEstimate used as a 'Total' count is unreliable/approximate
findings.description: The script prints `results.get('resultSizeEstimate', 'Unknown')` labeled as the total count of remaining items. Gmail's resultSizeEstimate is explicitly an estimate and can be wildly off for large mailboxes; presenting it as 'Total' is misleading for any operator decision-making.

findings.title: Direct subscripting of results['labels'] and response['payload']['headers'] / h['name'] with no guards (read-only, low impact)
findings.description: inspect_remaining.py uses results['labels'] (line 8), response['payload']['headers'] and h['name']/h['value'] (lines 26-28) without .get(). A malformed/empty response raises KeyError. This is a read-only diagnostic (no mutations) so impact is limited to a crash of the inspector, but it shares the brittle direct-indexing pattern flagged in the mutating scripts.

findings.title: providers package __all__ omits the public DTO ListMessagesResult
findings.description: providers/__init__.py exports only EmailProvider and ProviderCapabilities in __all__, but ListMessagesResult (providers/base.py:39) is a public return-type DTO used by callers and tests (tests/test_api.py:8 imports it). Consumers must reach into providers.base directly rather than `from providers import ListMessagesResult`. The package facade is incomplete relative to the actual public contract of providers.base. Fix: re-export ListMessagesResult (and any other public base classes) from the package __init__ or stop advertising base via the package.

findings.location: line 12 (from googleapiclient.errors import HttpError, module top level)
findings.title: providers.gmail imports googleapiclient at module top level, so direct import crashes without the optional Google dependency
findings.description: providers/__init__.py is explicitly structured with lazy getter functions (get_gmail_provider etc.) 'to avoid dependency issues', and importing the providers package does NOT import gmail. However providers/gmail.py itself imports `from googleapiclient.errors import HttpError` at module top level (line 12), so any code doing `import providers.gmail` (or `from providers.gmail import GmailProvider`) — which is exactly what the lazy getter and the CLI's get_provider do at call time — raises ModuleNotFoundError if google-api-python-client is absent. This is consistent with the intended deferral (the crash only happens when the gmail provider is actually requested), but the failure mode is a raw ModuleNotFoundError at the deep import rather than a friendly 'provider unavailable' message; combined with the package's 'avoid dependency issues' promise it is easy to misread the guarantee. Info-level: documents the real boundary of the lazy-import design.

findings.location: apply_actions, lines 419-421 and audit pending build 434-439
findings.title: Audit labels_added omits the STARRED label even though apply_actions adds it to addLabelIds
findings.description: When action.star is true, apply_actions appends the resolved STARRED id to add_ids (419-421) so the batchModify will physically add the STARRED label. But the audit pending record sets add_labels=list(action.add_labels) (line 437), which does NOT include STARRED (starring is driven by action.star, not action.add_labels). The trust receipt therefore under-reports the labels actually written to the message — labels_added will not reflect the STARRED mutation. Minor fidelity gap in the audit receipt; not reported in round 1.

findings.title: Batch callback silently drops failed label fetches; report omits them with no warning
findings.description: The callback appends to detailed_labels only on success (`if not exception`). Any label whose detail GET failed is silently omitted from the report and from the total, with no log. For a reporting tool this produces silently incomplete output.

findings.title: render.yaml relies entirely on the Dockerfile CMD; no PORT export and starter plan caveats are implicit
findings.description: render.yaml declares runtime: docker with only PYTHONUNBUFFERED set. It does not set PORT — that is fine because Render injects $PORT and the Dockerfile CMD honors ${PORT:-8000} (Dockerfile L19). healthCheckPath '/health' matches api/app.py health() (L70) and the worker /health. No COPY/secret hygiene problem in render.yaml itself (credentials are correctly left to the dashboard, L11-12). This is informational confirmation that the Render path is internally consistent; the only latent risk is that the 'starter' plan + a synchronous uvicorn single worker has no autoscaling and MCP stateless_http assumes horizontal scaling that this single-instance plan won't provide.

findings.title: uvicorn started via shell-form CMD as root with no worker/uvicorn pinning
findings.description: `CMD uvicorn api.app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT:-8000}` runs in shell form (needed for $PORT expansion, acknowledged) but the container runs as root (no USER directive) and binds 0.0.0.0. For a deployed API image this is a minor hardening gap — a non-root USER is the standard practice. Not a functional bug.

findings.title: Provider capability matrix claims Gmail batch 'Yes (1000/batch)' but text elsewhere references batchModify 1000 — verify against gmail.py batch size
findings.description: README Provider Capabilities Matrix (line 488) states Gmail 'Batch operations: Yes (1000/batch)' and line 493 repeats 'apply labels to up to 1,000 messages in a single API call'. The CLAUDE.md provider table marks Gmail Batch API as supported but states no number. This is a specific numeric claim (1000) that should be cross-checked against the actual batch size constant in providers/gmail.py; Gmail's batchModify limit is 1000 ids per call, so the claim is plausible but was not verified against the provider code in this round. Flagging as a low-confidence numeric-accuracy item for the filter step.

findings.title: Idempotency-Key claimed (begin) before request body validation cost is bounded; large body is read via await request.body() with no explicit size limit at this layer
findings.description: Each POST does `raw = await request.body()` (router.py:169,204,239,345) and hashes the full body (store.py request_hash -> sha256 of all bytes). There is no explicit max-body-size guard in this layer; protection relies on the ASGI server / upstream proxy. A large body forces a full read + sha256 + json parse on every replay attempt and is stored as request_hash. Combined with the pre-gate pydantic parsing, an unauthenticated caller can drive body-read/parse work before auth is fully established. Enforce a max content-length on ACP POSTs.

findings.location: create_checkout exception handler line 140-142; create_portal line 164-166
findings.title: Stripe error logged with exc_info=True may capture sensitive request internals server-side (low risk, log-only)
findings.description: On a Stripe failure the handlers do `logger.warning("stripe checkout create failed: %s", e, exc_info=True)`. The client response is correctly generic (502, no leak). The exception/traceback can however include the params dict echoed in some Stripe error reprs and internal identifiers in the server log. This is log-side only (not returned to clients) and the secret key itself is not in the params, so the exposure is limited to whoever can read server logs — noting it for completeness given the scope is credential/secret leakage into logs.

findings.location: METERED_ADDON lines 130-139 (unit_amount_cents: 1 => $0.01/run) vs CREDIT_PACKS lines 143-148 (pack_100: 100 runs/100c = $0.01/run; pack_1000: 1000 runs/900c = $0.009/run)
findings.title: Per-run pricing is internally inconsistent across the three agent pay paths (metered $0.01, pack_100 $0.01, pack_1000 $0.009)
findings.description: Three agent-facing pay mechanisms advertise different effective per-run prices: the metered add-on bills $0.01/run (unit_amount_cents:1), pack_100 is 100 runs for 100c = $0.01/run, and pack_1000 is 1000 runs for 900c = $0.009/run. So pack_100 offers zero bulk discount over metered while pack_1000 does — an agent optimizing cost has no reason to ever buy pack_100 (identical unit price to pay-as-you-go but loses the flexibility), and the catalog presents an inconsistent price ladder. Whether intended or a copy/units oversight, the per-unit economics across the credit packs and the metered rate are not coherent and the feed exposes all three to agents simultaneously. Worth a deliberate decision rather than the current accidental shape.

findings.title: verify() returns True for an empty body+empty signature edge and accepts None signature as empty string
findings.description: verify(body, signature) computes expected=sign(body) then compares with `signature or ""`. If a caller passes signature=None it is coerced to "" and compared (always False vs a real hex digest, fine) — but the coercion masks the type error rather than rejecting it. More subtly, verify never validates that signature is a hex string of expected length before compare_digest; a caller treating verify() as input validation gets no shape check. Minor robustness gap on the public verifier that is advertised as standalone/open-source.

findings.title: Header name match for 'From' is case-insensitive but value extraction takes first match only; OK, but fail-closed empty-string relies on is_protected_sender semantics
findings.description: Minor/defensive: senders_for sets out[rid]='' on fetch error (fail closed -> treated as protected). That is correct per is_protected_sender (empty -> True). However if a message simply has no From header (rare but possible), it also yields '' and is treated as protected and never archived — a benign over-protect, but worth noting it can leave items stuck in inbox forever with no diagnostic.

findings.title: store_json_secret ignores account override and is dead/divergent relative to gmail_auth
findings.description: store_json_secret calls op_item_edit(item, field, compact, vault) without ever passing `account`, so a non-default OP_ACCOUNT is honored on reads (op_read/op_item_get fetch OP_ACCOUNT) but writes via this helper still resolve account only inside op_item_edit's own os.getenv fallback — consistent here, but note gmail_auth.py reimplements the entire op/secret stack separately (store_token_info). The two parallel implementations (auth/onepassword.py and gmail_auth.py) can drift: a fix to one (e.g. the stdin-secret fix) will not protect the other. The Gmail OAuth path actually used by build_gmail_service goes through gmail_auth.py, so auth/onepassword.store_json_secret may be effectively unused for tokens. Maintainability/dead-code risk that hides security fixes.

findings.title: Direct dict indexing on profile fields can KeyError; assumes keys always present
findings.description: `profile['messagesTotal']` (line 13) and `profile['emailAddress']` (line 22) are indexed directly. If the Gmail getProfile response is missing a field (or the call returns an error object), this raises KeyError and crashes. Read-only tool, low impact, but unguarded.

findings.title: Health-check batch callback uses parameter name 'id' shadowing builtin and drops errors silently
findings.description: `def cb(id, resp, exc)` shadows the builtin `id`; minor. More relevant: `if not exc: label_stats[resp['name']] = resp` silently drops any label whose fetch errored, so the unread report can be incomplete without any indication. Also `resp['name']` indexed directly.

findings.title: in_audit_dir check uses normpath string-prefix and misses './audit' equivalence edge but flags absolute audit/ paths as outside
findings.description: in_audit_dir = norm == 'audit' or norm.startswith('audit' + os.sep) (line 375). If a user passes an absolute path that IS inside the repo's audit dir (e.g. /repo/audit/x.jsonl) normpath keeps it absolute so it does NOT match 'audit/...' and the code emits the 'OUTSIDE gitignored audit/' warning incorrectly (false alarm). Conversely a path like 'audit/../secrets.jsonl' normalizes to 'secrets.jsonl' (correctly flagged outside) — that case is fine. The classification is purely lexical and can misreport for absolute or symlinked audit paths; only a warning so impact is cosmetic, but the safety messaging can mislead.

findings.location: cmd_summary line 596/611 and cmd_pending — percentage/division and bar math
findings.title: Summary table bar uses int(pct/5) which can exceed the 20-cell width only if pct>100, but '░'*(20-int(pct/5)) goes negative-safe; verify pct cap
findings.description: Line 612: bar = '█'*int(pct/5) + '░'*(20 - int(pct/5)). pct = count/total*100 is bounded 0-100 when count<=total, so int(pct/5) is 0-20 and (20-int(pct/5)) is 0-20 — fine. But tier_counts can sum to MORE than `total` is impossible here (total increments once per msg, each msg adds to exactly one tier), so pct<=100 holds. Low risk; noting because if the tier_counts ever gains an extra out-of-range key (see other finding) the per-tier pct still <=100 but the sum of displayed percentages could exceed 100, and '░'*(negative) would silently produce '' rather than error if pct>100. Mostly defensive observation.

findings.location: cmd_label exit code lines 454-457 vs cmd_escalate 945-947
findings.title: apply error_count>1 returns generic exit 1 but partial-success runs with some errors are indistinguishable from total failure
findings.description: cmd_label returns 0 if error_count==0 else 1 (line 457), and 2 on audit violation. A run that processed 1000 messages with 1 error returns the same exit code (1) as a run where everything failed. Scripts/launchd consuming the exit code cannot distinguish partial from total failure. Minor observability gap, consistent across label/escalate, but worth noting for automation that gates on exit status.

findings.title: subparsers not marked required; no-command path returns 1 but args.func may be missing for unknown handling
findings.description: add_subparsers(dest='command') is not required=True (line 1004). The code guards with `if not args.command: parser.print_help(); return 1` (line 1197). That handles the no-subcommand case. However, args.func is only set via set_defaults on each subparser; if argparse ever reaches line 1201 with a command set but (hypothetically) no func default it would AttributeError. All current subparsers set func, so safe today — noting the implicit coupling: any future subparser added without set_defaults(func=...) will crash at line 1201 rather than print help.

findings.title: CORS allow-origin is wildcard `*` on all API responses
findings.description: COMMON_HEADERS sets `access-control-allow-origin: *` and is applied to every JSON response including the billing and triage endpoints. For a public share demo this is likely intentional, but it means any origin can invoke the sender-check / triage-preview / billing-plans endpoints from a browser. Worth a deliberate decision rather than a blanket wildcard, especially before non-demo billing endpoints are wired up.

findings.title: Subclass dataclasses redeclare 'name' with a default while base has it without — fragile field ordering
findings.description: ProviderConfig declares `name: str` (no default) as its first field. The subclasses (GmailConfig etc.) redeclare `name: str = 'gmail'` to give it a default. This works in current Python only because the override supplies a default for the previously-defaultless field, but it is a well-known dataclass-inheritance footgun: if anyone adds a no-default field to ProviderConfig after `enabled`, or reorders, you get 'non-default argument follows default argument' TypeError at class-definition time. It is also semantically odd that the base ProviderConfig is effectively unusable without passing name positionally while subclasses ignore that. Worth flagging as a maintainability/correctness hazard.

findings.title: DRY_RUN env var only togglable to True, never explicit False over a config-file True
findings.description: config.dry_run is only touched when os.getenv(f'{prefix}DRY_RUN') is truthy-present, and then set to (value.lower() in ('1','true','yes')). So setting MAIL_AUTO_DRY_RUN=0 to override a config file's `dry_run: true` does correctly set False — but MAIL_AUTO_DRY_RUN='' (empty string) is falsy so the whole branch is skipped and the config-file True stands, while MAIL_AUTO_DRY_RUN='false' enters the branch and correctly sets False. The inconsistency is the empty-string case: a user who exports the var empty to 'clear' it instead leaves the config value in force. Minor precedence surprise; the same empty-string-skip pattern applies to every os.getenv branch here.

findings.title: label_counts annotated as bare 'dict' not Dict[str,int]
findings.description: label_counts: dict = field(default_factory=dict) loses the value type. add_label_stat assumes int values (self.label_counts.get(label,0)+1). If something else populates label_counts with non-int values (e.g. merging a label_counts that came from JSON state where a value is a string), the +1 raises TypeError. Minor type-hygiene/observation; the bare-dict annotation also weakens static checking.

findings.title: add_label_stat is not thread-safe (read-modify-write on shared dict) — lost counts if apply_actions is ever parallelized
findings.description: add_label_stat does self.label_counts[label] = self.label_counts.get(label, 0) + 1 — a non-atomic read-modify-write. The base apply_actions loop (providers/base.py:340-368) and gmail apply (providers/gmail.py:444-446) call this per label, and a single ProcessingResult is shared across the whole batch. Today the loops are sequential so this is safe, but the base docstring explicitly invites overriding for batch/parallel APIs ('Providers with batch APIs should override for better performance', providers/base.py:315-316). If any provider parallelizes action application (or accumulates from multiple threads), concurrent add_label_stat calls will lose increments, under-reporting the label counts that feed the report/audit. Also success_count/error_count/processed_count are incremented directly on the shared result with the same hazard. Informational/latent. Fix: document single-threaded contract, or guard with a lock / use collections.Counter under a lock if parallel paths are added.

findings.title: Inner `break` after first matching pattern is correct but masks intent; combined with strict < it cannot pick the highest-priority within scanning
findings.description: The `break` exits the pattern loop on the first pattern that both matches AND improves best_priority -- but because all patterns within a single rule share one priority, the break is only safe if the match also satisfied `rule_config['priority'] < best_priority`. The break is INSIDE that `if`, so if a pattern matches but the rule's priority is NOT better than an earlier match, the loop continues to the next pattern of the SAME rule (wasted work, no behavior change) rather than skipping the rule. Minor inefficiency / readability smell; no correctness bug, but the control flow is non-obvious and a future edit that moves the break could change tie behavior.

findings.title: TLD heuristic for relay domain recovery uses 2<=len<=18 all-alpha terminal label, can pick a wrong real domain for categorization
findings.description: _best_relay_domain chooses the candidate with the fewest dots whose terminal label is all-alpha and length 2-18. This is a heuristic that can mis-recover the real domain for categorization when the relay token itself decodes to something alpha (e.g. a numeric or hyphenated TLD-like junk segment), causing relayed mail to be categorized under the wrong sender. Only affects categorization (not the fail-closed protection decision, which iterates all candidates), so impact is limited to mislabeling, but the heuristic is brittle and undocumented in its failure modes.

findings.title: Catch-all pattern r".*" combined with re.search makes the LABEL_RULES priority scan order-fragile; only priority 999 saves it
findings.description: The 'Misc/Other' rule pattern is r".*" (line 502) which re.search matches against ANY text (including empty). _find_best_label (lines 1025-1038) iterates LABEL_RULES and keeps the lowest priority value; Misc/Other is priority 999 so it only wins when nothing else matched. This is correct ONLY because every other rule has priority < 999 AND dict insertion keeps Misc/Other last. If a future rule were added with priority >= 999, or the r".*" rule were duplicated/moved, the catch-all could shadow real categories. Fragile reliance on a magic priority number plus the unanchored r".*". Low risk today; flagged as a maintainability/correctness trap in the shared module all scripts depend on.

findings.title: Relay local-part decoding can fabricate non-protected real domains for categorization, but never lowers protection — verify over-recovery cannot mis-skip
findings.description: _relay_domain_candidates generates every progressive truncation of underscore-separated segments (line 689) and _resolve_addr returns ('', real) for a non-protected best candidate (line 732-734). For categorization this is intentional. Confirm there is no path where a recovered candidate that IS protected is discarded in favor of a non-protected _best_relay_domain: is_protected_sender iterates _iter_sender_domains which yields only the single resolved domain from _resolve_addr (line 771-774), and _resolve_addr returns the protected candidate as soon as one matches (line 723-728), so protection wins. This appears sound, but the gate's correctness hinges entirely on _resolve_addr checking protection BEFORE picking _best_relay_domain — a single reordering of lines 722-734 would silently open a fail-open relay bypass. Flagged for the verification step given it is the headline safety guarantee.

findings.title: deploy.sh writes a LaunchAgent plist and bootstraps it (matches the user's hard-blocked plist/LaunchAgent rule)
findings.description: deploy.sh copies com.user.mail_automation.plist to ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ and runs launchctl bootout/bootstrap. Per the user's environment rules a LaunchAgent-Write hook hard-blocks */LaunchAgents/* and *.plist writes; this script performs exactly that write at runtime. Not a code-injection per se, but flag as a config/governance concern: the deployed job runs run_automation.sh which sources ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh and processes mail unattended. Also `cp` over an existing PLIST_DEST is non-atomic and unconditional.

findings.title: No Content-Security-Policy meta tag; inline script/style only (defense-in-depth gap, currently low risk)
findings.description: The page ships an inline `<style>` and inline `<script>` with no `Content-Security-Policy` meta tag and no security headers (the file is static HTML; the serving worker for ./web is separate and does not serve this file). There are currently no script injection vectors here (all content is static, no fetch, no innerHTML, no user input), so this is defense-in-depth only. Worth noting for completeness: if this page is ever promoted to a served surface and later gains any dynamic content, the absence of a CSP plus the inline-script pattern would remove a mitigation layer. Info-level hygiene given the current static, no-input nature.

findings.title: Nav-dot links get their accessible name from section id, then are visually hidden via text-indent:-9999px
findings.description: Each nav dot is an `<a>` whose `textContent` is set to the section id like "hero", "problem", etc. (line 264), then CSS hides that text off-screen with `text-indent:-9999px` (line 45). This is functionally an accessible name for screen readers, which is fine, but the names are raw ids ("cta", "market") rather than human labels, and the off-screen text-indent technique is a dated a11y pattern. Minor: the nav `<a>` elements also have no `aria-current` to convey the active section to assistive tech (only a visual `.active` class). Low/info hygiene; not a functional bug.

findings.title: ecosystem.yaml delivery/revenue status disagrees with seed.yaml metadata
findings.description: ecosystem.yaml declares delivery web_app status `not_started` and revenue subscription `planned`, while seed.yaml says `implementation_status: ACTIVE` / `promotion_status: PUBLIC_PROCESS` and the repo clearly has a live billing surface (api/billing.py, Stripe in requirements, a deployed worker). The 'not_started' delivery status is stale/contradictory with the rest of the repo state; tooling consuming ecosystem.yaml will report the project as not started.

findings.title: Token re-serialized via creds.to_json then re-dumped — redundant parse/dump, and full token (incl refresh_token) persisted
findings.description: store_token_info does json.loads(creds.to_json()) then json.dumps(..., sort_keys=True). This is benign re-serialization, but worth noting the stored blob includes the long-lived refresh_token written into 1Password via a command-line argument (see the argv-leak finding). No functional bug, but the round-trip is unnecessary and the combination amplifies the argv secret-exposure surface each refresh.

findings.location: process_batch lines 149-154 callback closure over failed_ids per-chunk
findings.title: Batch callback appends to a per-chunk failed_ids but batch_results is shared across chunks (correct) — note time.sleep(2.0) fixed delay regardless of size
findings.description: Observation/low-risk: the per-chunk callback (lines 149-154) is redefined each chunk and closes over a fresh failed_ids list, which is correct. However process_batch sleeps a fixed 2.0s after every batch-get chunk (line 165) and 0.5s after every modify (line 265) and 2.0s per list page (line 325); with BATCH_GET_SIZE=20 and up to 500 messages that is 25 chunks * 2s = 50s of fixed sleep per page just for gets, making large runs extremely slow. Not a correctness bug but a notable throughput issue and divergent from the cli.py provider path.

findings.title: Broad `except Exception` swallows logout errors (acceptable) but no close of select state / no NOOP error surfacing
findings.description: The finally block wraps imap.logout() in bare `except Exception: pass`. This is acceptable for cleanup, but note that if the connection died mid-apply, the swallow hides it entirely and the 'DONE. Moved X' summary may already be wrong. Low severity observability concern.

findings.title: apply_category default returns False even when CATEGORIES capability is set, with comment 'Subclasses override' — silent no-op if a subclass forgets
findings.description: The base apply_category checks the CATEGORIES flag then unconditionally `return False` with a comment that subclasses override. If a provider sets the CATEGORIES capability flag but fails to override apply_category, calls silently return False (no error, no log) and categories are silently dropped — a silent-failure trap. A NotImplementedError would surface the mistake. Low severity, low confidence (design choice), flagged for coverage.

findings.title: Base batch_get_details default has no throttling and could hammer the API; not an abstractmethod so a provider can silently inherit a slow/unsafe sequential path
findings.description: The default batch_get_details loops get_message_details with no rate limiting. Providers that declare BATCH_OPERATIONS capability but do not override this will use the slow sequential path while the capability flag advertises batch support — capability flag and actual implementation can diverge with no check. Informational/maintainability.

findings.title: star() silently ignores due_date and does not check STAR capability before applying
findings.description: Gmail's star() ignores due_date (documented) and unconditionally calls apply_label(message_id,'STARRED'). The base class star() (base.py:230) guards with `if not (self.capabilities & STAR): return False`; the Gmail override drops that guard. Gmail does declare STAR capability so it is harmless today, but the override diverges from the base contract — if STAR were ever removed from Gmail's capability flags, star() would still apply. Info-level consistency smell.

findings.title: _init_label_cache does not paginate labels().list (assumes all labels fit in one response)
findings.description: labels().list returns all labels in a single response with no nextPageToken in the standard Gmail API (labels are not paginated), so this is generally fine. Flagged only because it reads results.get('labels',[]) with no pagination guard — if the API behavior ever changes or a very large label set is truncated, the cache would be incomplete, which (per the remove_label finding) cascades into failed removes. Low confidence, informational.

findings.location: 417, 441 (archive via INBOX in remove_ids + batch key grouping)
findings.title: Star id resolution uses cache 'STARRED' but ensure_label_exists never adds STARRED to cache, so star always appends literal 'STARRED' (benign) while add label dedup may be defeated
findings.description: In apply_actions, add_ids may contain both a real label id (from ensure_label_exists) and 'STARRED' (line 420-421). The batch grouping key is (tuple(sorted(add_ids)), tuple(sorted(remove_ids))). Mixing opaque ids ('Label_42') and literal system names ('STARRED','INBOX') in the same sorted tuple is correct for batchModify (which accepts both), so functionally fine. Noting only that ensure_label_exists for system labels caches name->name (line 350-353) but STARRED is resolved via _label_cache.get('STARRED','STARRED') at 420 without going through ensure_label_exists, so STARRED is never cached — harmless duplication. Info-level.

findings.location: 248 (batch.execute not wrapped to reset between backoff retries)
findings.title: On batch.execute rate-limit retry, results dict may receive duplicate parses but request_id keys dedupe — no corruption, but failed_ids not cleared
findings.description: Related to the earlier batch retry finding: `results[request_id] = msg` is idempotent (dict overwrite) so re-parsing the same message on retry does not corrupt results. However failed_ids is defined per-chunk (line 225) and not reset across _execute_with_backoff retries of the same chunk, so a message that fails on attempt 1 and succeeds on retry stays in failed_ids and is fetched a third time individually (lines 252-255), overwriting the good result with an identical good result — wasted quota only. Confirming low severity, no data corruption.

findings.location: _execute_with_backoff lines 97-127 (lambda closure over loop var in apply_actions line 460)
findings.title: Late-binding closure: batch-modify lambda captures loop variable `body` by reference
findings.description: In apply_actions, the loop builds `body` per chunk and passes `lambda: self._service.users().messages().batchModify(userId='me', body=body).execute()` to _execute_with_backoff. Since _execute_with_backoff invokes the lambda synchronously within the same iteration (before `body` is reassigned), this happens to be correct today. But it is fragile: any refactor that defers/queues these lambdas (or the existing get_message_details/apply_label lambdas) would bind to the final loop value. Worth noting as a latent closure-capture hazard. Confidence that it is currently-broken: low.

findings.title: Star-only actions with no add/remove labels still create an empty batchModify body, and protected senders retaining star can be grouped with non-protected stars
findings.description: When an action has no add_labels/remove_labels and is not archive but only star=True, add_ids=[STARRED], remove_ids=[]. That is a valid batch. But an action that is fully neutralized (protected sender: archive cleared, INBOX stripped) yet still has star=True and empty add_labels yields add_ids=[STARRED], remove_ids=[] — grouping it (line 441) with non-protected star-only actions into one batch. That is functionally fine for stars, but the audit pending for the protected message records left_inbox via remove_ids (empty -> False) and labels_added=[] (since add_labels is empty) — so a protected, starred message is recorded with no labels_added even though STARRED was applied. Confirms the star-omission audit gap compounds for protected senders. Minor receipt-fidelity issue, not reported in round 1.

findings.title: IMAP star() ignores capability gating and always attempts \\Flagged even when STAR capability absent (non-gmail mode)
findings.description: In __init__, STAR capability is only added when use_gmail_extensions is True (line 98). But IMAPProvider overrides star()/unstar() directly and unconditionally issues +FLAGS (\Flagged) regardless of capability, bypassing the base-class capability guard. Generic IMAP servers support \Flagged so this is usually fine, but it diverges from the declared capabilities (a caller checking ProviderCapabilities.STAR would be told STAR is unsupported in non-gmail mode, yet star() still works/claims success). Inconsistency between declared capabilities and behavior.

findings.title: IMAP password resolved from env returns os.getenv twice (TOCTOU/None edge) — minor
findings.description: _load_password does `if os.getenv('IMAP_PASS'): return os.getenv('IMAP_PASS')`, calling getenv twice. Functionally fine for env vars, but it is a smell: between the two reads the value is assumed stable, and it returns the second read. Not a leak, but worth flattening to a single read for correctness/clarity. The 1Password subprocess fallback (lines 114-121) correctly catches and logs only the exception text (not the value) on failure.

findings.title: os.getenv('IMAP_PASS') checked then re-read, and an empty-string IMAP_PASS bypasses the 1Password fallback returning ''
findings.description: `if os.getenv('IMAP_PASS'): return os.getenv('IMAP_PASS')` — an explicitly-empty IMAP_PASS ('') is falsy so it correctly falls through; but the double getenv is a TOCTOU smell (env could change between calls) and, more practically, a whitespace-only password ('  ') is truthy and returned verbatim, which then fails login() with an opaque IMAP error rather than the clear 'not configured' ValueError. Minor.

findings.title: Mail.app auto-launch + fixed 2s delay race
findings.description: connect() runs AppleScript that, if Mail isn't running, activates it and `delay 2`, then returns 'ok'. On a slow machine Mail may not be ready to answer AppleScript queries within 2s, so the subsequent list_messages can fail or return partial data. Fixed sleep is a race; should poll for readiness. Minor.

findings.title: remove_label always returns False, so base.apply_actions never registers INBOX-removal as leaving the inbox for Outlook
findings.description: remove_label is a hard no-op returning False (logs a warning). In base.apply_actions, INBOX-removal is detected via `if self.remove_label(...) and label.upper() in ('INBOX',...)` (base.py 350-352). Because remove_label always returns False, did_leave_inbox via removal never becomes True for Outlook. This is consistent with Outlook being folder/move based (it relies on did_label_move + archive instead), so it is likely intentional — but it means any caller that adds 'INBOX' to remove_labels for Outlook silently has no effect, and the audit will not reflect the intent. Worth confirming this is the designed behavior rather than a latent gap.

findings.title: Optional deps (msal/requests/pyyaml) listed as hard requirements despite being documented optional
findings.description: requirements.txt includes msal, requests, and pyyaml under 'Optional:' comments, but they are unconditional install lines, so `pip install -r requirements.txt` always pulls them. Meanwhile the code imports them lazily (core/config.py imports yaml inside a function; providers/outlook.py imports msal/requests lazily), implying they are meant to be optional. This is inconsistent: either they are required (drop the 'Optional' comments) or they belong in an extras group. Low impact, mostly a footprint/clarity issue, but it contradicts the stated 3.9-minimal-core design.

findings.title: iCloud IMAP password passed via inline env assignment on the command (process-listing exposure)
findings.description: The runner invokes the labeler with IMAP_PASS="$ICLOUD_IMAP_PASS" prefixed inline on the command line. Inline env-var assignment is generally safer than argv (it is in the child's environment, not argv), but on Linux a process's environment is readable via /proc/<pid>/environ by the same user and can appear in some audit/accounting contexts. Lower risk than argv exposure and the value comes from a 1Password-sourced env, but flagged for the credential-handling sweep: prefer `export`-ing once in the sourced op env file rather than re-templating the secret onto each invocation.

findings.title: Generated files written non-atomically; a crash mid-write leaves committed artifacts truncated
findings.description: Each artifact (pricing.md, llms.txt, agent.json, server.json) is written by opening the final path directly for 'w'. If generation of build_llms_txt / build_agent_manifest raises after pricing.md is already written, the repo is left with a partially-regenerated, inconsistent artifact set. Low severity for a dev-time generator, but writing to temp + os.replace would keep the committed outputs consistent.

findings.title: Money formatting uses float division for cents -> dollars (rounding risk)
findings.description: `${pk['amount_cents'] / 100:.2f}` divides integer cents by 100 in float then formats to 2dp. For typical pack prices this is fine, but float division for money is the classic rounding-error pattern; values like 1999 cents render fine, but the idiom invites bugs if amounts grow or are summed. Prefer integer/Decimal cents formatting (e.g. f"{amt//100}.{amt%100:02d}").

findings.title: Triage tests stub run_labeler so the real audit chokepoint wiring inside run_labeler is bypassed
findings.description: Both triage tests monkeypatch service.run_labeler with a fake that directly calls audit.record(...) with hand-chosen protected/archived flags. This validates the API's translation of audit summary -> HTTP status (clean=200, violation=500), which is valuable, but it means the production run_labeler -> provider.apply_actions -> audit.record path (where protected re-derivation and real disposition recording happen) is NOT exercised by these API-level tests. The fake can assert 'protected=True, archived=False' regardless of whether the real code would compute that. The integration is covered in test_audit.py's TestRunLabelerReceipt, but the API tests give a false impression of end-to-end coverage. Note rather than a defect.

findings.title: Redirect test accepts any 3xx and only checks Location header, not that the dashboard is actually reachable via the redirect
findings.description: The test asserts status_code in (301,302,307,308) and Location=='/app/'. It does not follow the redirect to confirm /app/ returns 200 in the same flow (that is covered separately by test_dashboard_served, but only for a direct GET). Accepting four different status codes also means a regression that changes 307->301 (which alters method/caching semantics for POSTs) passes unnoticed. Low severity; the assertion is broad enough that it cannot fail on a semantically-wrong-but-still-3xx redirect.

findings.title: Sender/subject sent to API with no client-side length bound
findings.description: The sender-check posts `{ sender, subject: $("subject").value }` with no length cap (the TriageRequest.query field is server-bounded to 2048 per round 1, but SenderCheckRequest's subject/sender bounds are not enforced client-side). A user (or automation pointed at this form) can submit arbitrarily large sender/subject strings to the unauthenticated /v1/senders/check endpoint. Minor on its own but compounds the no-auth/no-rate-limit posture round 1 flagged on these endpoints.
